topics.title: Firewalls: Types of Firewalls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Firewalls: Types of Firewalls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standardized):**
> A **Firewall** is a network security device — implemented in hardware, software, or a hybrid form — that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on a pre-defined set of **security rules**, establishing a **security perimeter** between an internal trusted network and external untrusted networks (such as the Internet) or between distinct security zones within an industrial control system (ICS) / SCADA architecture.

In the context of **Industrial Cyber Security**, firewalls are the **first line of defense** in the **Purdue Model** (Levels 0–5), particularly deployed at the boundaries of:
- **Level 3.5 (DMZ)** between the Operational Technology (OT) network and the Information Technology (IT) corporate network.
- **Cell/Area Zone boundaries** within Level 0–2 to enforce micro-segmentation.

The **Types of Firewalls** differ primarily in the **OSI Layer** at which they inspect traffic, the **state-awareness** they possess, and the **contextual intelligence** they apply to traffic flows.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bouncer at a Club

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for B.Tech Students:**
> Imagine a popular nightclub with a strict bouncer at the door.

| Real-World Bouncer | Firewall Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Checks the **guest list** against the ID | **Access Control List (ACL)** / Whitelist |
| Checks **age and attire** (basic rules) | **Packet Filtering** (Stateless Firewall) |
| Remembers if the guest is **drunk or misbehaving** | **Stateful Inspection** |
| Inspects the **contents of pockets/bags** for weapons | **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** |
| **Escorts guests** to a private table | **Proxy / Application Gateway** |
| Monitors for **suspicious behavior patterns over time** | **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) with IDS/IPS** |

Just as a bouncer protects the club from trouble, a firewall protects the **trusted internal network** from malicious external traffic, unauthorized access, and protocol-level exploits.

## 1.3 The Fundamental Working Principle — The 4 Verbs

Every firewall, regardless of type, performs only four logical operations:

1. **ALLOW** — Permit the traffic to pass.
2. **DENY / DROP** — Silently discard the traffic.
3. **REJECT** — Discard the traffic *and* send an ICMP unreachable response to the sender.
4. **LOG** — Record the event for audit and forensic analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Aid:** "**A**llow, **D**eny, **D**rop, **R**eject, **L**og" → **ADDRL** is the universal action set of every firewall.

## 1.4 Why Firewalls Are Mandatory in Industrial Cyber Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECCT524 Module 1):**
> Modern Industrial Control Systems (ICS), SCADA, and IIoT environments demand **defense-in-depth** (DiD). Firewalls form the **perimeter enforcement layer** of this strategy. The 2024 scheme emphasizes that a single firewall type is **insufficient** — organizations must deploy a **layered firewall architecture** combining multiple types for OT-IT convergence security.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Classification Framework — The Three Primary Dimensions

Firewalls are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Dimension | Categories |
|---|---|
| **1. Deployment Mode** | Hardware Firewall / Software Firewall / Virtual Firewall / Cloud-Native Firewall |
| **2. OSI Inspection Layer** | Layer 3 (Network) / Layer 4 (Transport) / Layer 7 (Application) |
| **3. State Awareness** | Stateless / Stateful / Application-Aware |

## 2.2 Type 1 — Packet Filtering Firewall (Stateless Firewall)

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Operates at **OSI Layer 3 & 4** (Network and Transport layers).
- Examines each packet **in isolation**, with no memory of previous packets.
- Inspects the **header fields** only — does NOT look at the payload.
- Decision is made using a static **rule table (ACL)**.

**Header fields typically inspected:**
- Source IP Address
- Destination IP Address
- Source Port
- Destination Port
- Protocol (TCP / UDP / ICMP)
- Direction (Inbound / Outbound)

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
The first line of defense in routers (e.g., Cisco ACLs, Linux `iptables` `INPUT` chain). **Cheapest and fastest** firewall type, ideal for high-throughput industrial backbones where latency is critical (e.g., Modbus/TCP traffic on a Level 2 process network).

## 2.3 Type 2 — Stateful Inspection Firewall

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Operates at **OSI Layer 3, 4 & 5** (Session layer awareness).
- Maintains a **State Table (Connection Table)** that tracks every active TCP/UDP session.
- Uses the **three-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)** to verify legitimate sessions.
- Performs **context-based packet filtering** — a packet is allowed only if it belongs to an existing legitimate session.

**State Table Entry (Conceptual):**
```
{ Source IP, Source Port, Dest IP, Dest Port, Protocol, State, Timeout }
```

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
The industry workhorse for OT/IT boundary protection. Detects **half-open scans**, **SYN floods**, and **session hijacking**. Commonly deployed as industrial-grade appliances (e.g., Hirschmann, Fortinet, Palo Alto).

## 2.4 Type 3 — Application-Level Gateway (ALG) / Proxy Firewall

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Operates at **OSI Layer 7** (Application Layer).
- Acts as an **intermediary (proxy)** between the client and the server.
- The client **never speaks directly** to the server — the proxy terminates and re-establishes the connection.
- Can inspect the **entire payload**, including commands, URLs, file content, and application-specific protocols.

**Two Sub-Variants:**
- **Forward Proxy** — Sits in front of the internal client; controls outbound access.
- **Reverse Proxy** — Sits in front of the internal server; controls inbound access (used for DMZ servers, web servers).

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
Critical for **SCADA HMI** protection — can parse Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, and OPC UA payloads to detect malicious function codes.

## 2.5 Type 4 — Circuit-Level Gateway

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Operates at **OSI Layer 5** (Session Layer).
- Does **NOT inspect the payload**.
- Only verifies that the **TCP handshake** is legitimate (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK).
- Once the session is established, packets are **tunneled** through.
- Lightweight and fast — used when only session validity matters.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
Commonly combined with SOCKS proxies for remote vendor access into OT networks (e.g., remote maintenance of PLCs).

## 2.6 Type 5 — Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Combines **Stateful Inspection + DPI + IPS + Application Awareness + Threat Intelligence**.
- Identifies applications regardless of port (e.g., detects BitTorrent on port 80).
- Integrates with **SIEM** for log correlation.
- Supports **SSL/TLS decryption** to inspect encrypted traffic.
- Signature and behavior-based **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** engine embedded.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
Deployed at the **IT-OT DMZ (Purdue Level 3.5)** for high-fidelity traffic analysis. Industry leaders: Palo Alto Networks, Fortinet FortiGate, Check Point.

## 2.7 Type 6 — Web Application Firewall (WAF)

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Specialized firewall for **HTTP/HTTPS traffic** (Layer 7).
- Protects against **OWASP Top 10**: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, etc.
- Modes: **Block**, **Monitor**, **Challenge (CAPTCHA)**.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
Protects industrial web-based HMIs and SCADA web servers exposed in the DMZ.

## 2.8 Type 7 — Cloud-Native / Virtual Firewall (NGFW-V)

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Software-defined, runs as a **VM or container** (e.g., AWS Security Group, Azure Firewall, Kubernetes Network Policy).
- Horizontally scalable, API-driven.
- Critical for **IIoT and Industry 4.0** cloud-connected factories.

## 2.9 Type 8 — Industrial / ICS / OT Firewalls

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- Purpose-built for **OT protocols** (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP).
- **Deep protocol parsing** of industrial function codes.
- Supports **deny-by-default** whitelisting — only known-good commands pass.
- Often certified to **IEC 62443** standards.

**Real-World Examples:** Claroty, Nozomi Networks, Dragos, Tofino (by Hirschmann/Belden).

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Comparison Table for KTU 2024 Exam (Memorize This)**

| Firewall Type | OSI Layer | Stateful? | Inspects Payload? | Speed | Cost | OT Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Packet Filtering (Stateless) | 3–4 | ❌ No | ❌ No | ⚡⚡⚡ Very Fast | 💲 Low | Basic cell-level |
| Stateful Inspection | 3–5 | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚡⚡ Fast | 💲💲 Medium | IT-OT DMZ |
| Circuit-Level Gateway | 5 | ✅ Partial | ❌ No | ⚡⚡ Fast | 💲 Low | Remote vendor access |
| Application-Level Gateway (Proxy) | 7 | ✅ Yes | ✅ Full | ⚡ Slow | 💲💲💲 High | HMI / SCADA server protection |
| NGFW | 3–7 | ✅ Yes | ✅ Deep | ⚡⚡ Medium | 💲💲💲 High | IT-OT boundary (Purdue 3.5) |
| WAF | 7 | ✅ Yes | ✅ HTTP/S only | ⚡⚡ Medium | 💲💲 Medium | Industrial web HMIs |
| ICS / OT Firewall | 3–7 | ✅ Yes | ✅ Protocol-aware | ⚡⚡ Medium | 💲💲💲💲 Very High | Level 0–2 cell protection |
| Cloud-Native Firewall | 3–7 | ✅ Yes | ✅ Deep | ⚡⚡ Elastic | 💲 Pay-per-use | IIoT / Industry 4.0 |

## 2.11 Key Formulas / Numerical Models for Firewall Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Formula Sheet (Cybersecurity Engineering Metrics):**

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **T (Throughput)** | $$T = \frac{P \times 8}{t}$$ | Throughput in **bps** where **P** = packets processed, **t** = time in seconds |
| **D (Drop Rate)** | $$D = \frac{N_{drop}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$ | Percentage of packets dropped |
| **L (Latency)** | $$L = t_{out} - t_{in}$$ | Time delay added by the firewall |
| **BP (Bandwidth Penalty)** | $$BP = \frac{B_{raw} - B_{effective}}{B_{raw}} \times 100\%$$ | Throughput loss due to DPI/IPS |
| **FPR (False Positive Rate)** | $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ | Legitimate traffic incorrectly blocked |
| **FNR (False Negative Rate)** | $$FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$$ | Malicious traffic incorrectly allowed |
| **MTBF (Reliability)** | $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ | Probability firewall survives beyond time **t**, where **$\lambda$** = failure rate |

**Numerical Worked Example (For Exam Practice):**

A firewall processes **$P = 250,000$** packets in **$t = 10$ seconds**. Each packet averages **1500 bytes**.

$$T = \frac{250{,}000 \times 1500 \times 8}{10}$$

$$T = \frac{3{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10}$$

$$T = 3 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps} = \mathbf{3 \text{ Gbps}}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Logic

## 3.1 Decision Flow: How a Firewall Processes a Single Packet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked *"How does a firewall work?"*, examiners expect a **layered decision flowchart** — not a one-line answer. Present this flow.

**Step 1 — Packet Arrival:** A packet arrives at the firewall interface. The firewall extracts the **Layer 2, 3, and 4 headers**.

**Step 2 — Rule Lookup:** The packet header fields (Source IP, Dest IP, Source Port, Dest Port, Protocol) are compared against the **Access Control List (ACL)** sequentially from **Rule #1** to **Rule #N**.

**Step 3 — Stateless Check (If applicable):**
- If the firewall is stateless, it checks **only the current packet header** against the rules.
- Match found → **ACTION (ALLOW / DENY / DROP / REJECT)**.
- No match → Apply the **default rule** (usually DENY ALL in OT environments — **deny-by-default principle**).

**Step 4 — Stateful Check (If applicable):**
- The firewall checks the **State Table** for an existing matching session.
- If a matching entry exists with state = **ESTABLISHED**, the packet is allowed.
- If no entry exists but it's a **SYN** packet, a new session is initiated (state = **NEW**).
- If it's an unsolicited packet with no session → **DROP** (prevents port scanning).

**Step 5 — Application-Layer Inspection (For NGFW / Proxy / WAF):**
- The firewall **reassembles the packet stream** into a complete application-layer message.
- **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** analyzes the payload against:
  - Signature database (Snort, Suricata rules).
  - Protocol anomaly detection.
  - Behavioral baselines.
- Malicious patterns → **DROP + LOG + ALERT**.

**Step 6 — Logging & Forwarding:**
- Every decision is logged to a **syslog server** or **SIEM**.
- Allowed packets are forwarded to the destination; denied packets are dropped silently or rejected with ICMP.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Stateful Firewall State Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic KTU Exam Question Pattern:** *"Construct a state table for the following TCP session..."*

Consider three packets of a TCP handshake between **HMI (10.0.1.50:5000)** and **PLC (10.0.1.100:502)**:

| Packet # | Flag | Source | Dest | State Table Entry Created | State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SYN | 10.0.1.50:5000 | 10.0.1.100:502 | {10.0.1.50, 5000, 10.0.1.100, 502, TCP, NEW} | SYN_SENT |
| 2 | SYN-ACK | 10.0.1.100:502 | 10.0.1.50:5000 | Matches entry, transitions | SYN_RECEIVED |
| 3 | ACK | 10.0.1.50:5000 | 10.0.1.100:502 | Matches entry, transitions | ESTABLISHED |

**Post-handshake state:**
$$\text{State} = \text{ESTABLISHED} \implies \text{All subsequent packets in this session are ALLOWED}$$

**Key Insight:** A **stateless firewall** would have to evaluate **each of the 3 packets separately** against ACL rules. A **stateful firewall** only needs to check the **state table** for packets 2 and 3, making it **computationally more efficient and more secure** (resistant to session spoofing).

## 3.3 Firewall Rule Conflict Resolution — The First Match Wins Principle

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students lose marks by explaining "the most specific rule wins." This is **WRONG** for most firewalls. The correct principle is **FIRST MATCH WINS** — rules are evaluated **top-to-bottom** and the first match is applied.

**Worked Example (Common 7-mark question):**

A firewall has the following ACL (in this order):
1. **ALLOW** TCP from 10.0.1.0/24 to 10.0.2.0/24 on port 502
2. **DENY** TCP from 10.0.1.50 to 10.0.2.0/24 on port 502

**Question:** A packet from **10.0.1.50 → 10.0.2.10:502 (Modbus TCP)** arrives. What is the action?

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Inspect packet: Source = 10.0.1.50, Dest = 10.0.2.10, Port = 502, Protocol = TCP.

**Step 2:** Evaluate **Rule 1**: Does the source 10.0.1.50 belong to the subnet 10.0.1.0/24?

$$10.0.1.50 \in [10.0.1.0, 10.0.1.255] \rightarrow \text{YES}$$

**Step 3:** Does destination 10.0.2.10 belong to 10.0.2.0/24? **YES**.

**Step 4:** Does port 502 match? **YES**.

**Step 5:** Rule 1 **MATCHES** → Apply action **ALLOW**. Rule 2 is never evaluated.

**Conclusion:** The packet is **ALLOWED**, even though Rule 2 was intended to block the HMI specifically. **To block 10.0.1.50, Rule 2 MUST be placed BEFORE Rule 1** in the ACL.

$$\boxed{\text{Always place more specific rules BEFORE general rules in an ACL.}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Stateless vs. Stateful vs. NGFW

| Attack Vector | Stateless Defense | Stateful Defense | NGFW Defense |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IP Spoofing** | Limited (header check only) | Moderate (state verification) | Strong (DPI + behavioral) |
| **SYN Flood (DoS)** | ❌ Cannot detect | ✅ Detects half-open sessions | ✅ Auto-mitigates with SYN cookies |
| **Port Scanning** | ❌ Cannot correlate | ✅ Detects scan patterns | ✅ Integrates with IPS signatures |
| **Application-Layer Exploit (e.g., SQLi)** | ❌ Blind to payload | ❌ Blind to payload | ✅ DPI + WAF signatures |
| **Encrypted Malware (TLS)** | ❌ Blind | ❌ Blind (SSL bypass) | ✅ SSL inspection + sandboxing |
| **Zero-Day Exploit** | ❌ No detection | ❌ No detection | ⚠ Partial (behavioral anomaly) |

## 3.5 Defense-in-Depth Firewall Placement in Purdue Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industrial Focus:** The question *"Where are firewalls placed in an ICS architecture?"* appears frequently. Memorize this layered placement:

| Purdue Level | Zone | Recommended Firewall Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 5** (Enterprise / Internet) | Internet Edge | **NGFW** (Palo Alto, Fortinet) |
| **Level 4** (Site IT Network) | Corporate LAN | **Stateful + NGFW** |
| **Level 3.5 (DMZ)** | IT-OT Boundary | **NGFW + Reverse Proxy** (jump server for vendor access) |
| **Level 3** (Site Operations) | OT Operations | **ICS / OT Firewall** (Claroty, Nozomi) |
| **Level 2** (Area Supervisory) | SCADA / HMI | **ICS Firewall** (DPI on Modbus/DNP3) |
| **Level 1** (Basic Control) | PLCs / RTUs | **Cell-level Industrial Firewall** (e.g., Tofino) |
| **Level 0** (Process) | Sensors / Actuators | **Inline protocol filter / Diode** |

**Key Design Principle:** Each firewall is configured with **deny-by-default** policy — only explicitly allowed traffic is permitted. This is the cornerstone of **IEC 62443 Zone & Conduit** model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Statement

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node identifiers are alphanumeric with letter prefix (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`). Labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text only. Reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are never used as standalone node IDs.

## 4.2 Diagram A — Generic Firewall Placement Architecture (Purdue-Aligned)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[LEVEL 5 INTERNET EDGE] -->|Untrusted Traffic| nodeB[NGFW CLUSTER]
    nodeB -->|Inspected Traffic| nodeC[LEVEL 4 CORPORATE LAN]
    nodeC -->|DMZ Boundary| nodeD[NGFW PLUS REVERSE PROXY]
    nodeD -->|Filtered IT to OT| nodeE[LEVEL 3.5 DMZ]
    nodeE -->|Vendor Remote Access Only| nodeF[ICS FIREWALL OT AWARE]
    nodeF -->|Whitelisted Protocols| nodeG[LEVEL 3 OPERATIONS SCADA]
    nodeG -->|Cell Segmentation| nodeH[ICS FIREWALL MODBUS DNP3 AWARE]
    nodeH -->|Production Traffic| nodeI[LEVEL 1 PLC RTU]
    nodeI -->|Process Signals| nodeJ[LEVEL 0 SENSORS ACTUATORS]
    nodeK[SIEM LOG ANALYTICS PLATFORM] -.->|Log Feed| nodeB
    nodeK -.->|Log Feed| nodeD
    nodeK -.->|Log Feed| nodeF
    nodeK -.->|Log Feed| nodeH
```

## 4.3 Diagram B — Firewall Decision Flowchart (Packet Lifecycle)

```mermaid
graph TD
    pktA[INCOMING PACKET ARRIVES] --> pktB[EXTRACT L3 L4 HEADERS]
    pktB --> pktC{STATELESS OR STATEFUL}
    pktC -->|STATELESS| pktD[CHECK ACL RULES TOP TO BOTTOM]
    pktC -->|STATEFUL| pktE[QUERY STATE TABLE FOR SESSION]
    pktE -->|SESSION EXISTS| pktF{STATE IS ESTABLISHED}
    pktE -->|NO SESSION| pktG{IS IT A NEW SYN PACKET}
    pktG -->|YES| pktH[CREATE NEW STATE ENTRY]
    pktG -->|NO| pktI[DROP PACKET UNSOLICITED]
    pktF -->|YES| pktJ[ALLOW PACKET FORWARD]
    pktF -->|NO| pktI
    pktD -->|MATCH FOUND| pktK{ACTION FROM MATCH}
    pktD -->|NO MATCH| pktL[APPLY DEFAULT DENY RULE]
    pktH --> pktJ
    pktK -->|ALLOW| pktJ
    pktK -->|DENY OR DROP| pktI
    pktJ --> pktM[LOG EVENT TO SYSLOG]
    pktI --> pktM
    pktL --> pktI
    pktM --> pktN[PACKET PROCESSING COMPLETE]
```

## 4.4 Diagram C — OSI Layer Mapping of Firewall Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    osiA[OSI LAYER 7 APPLICATION] --> ftypeA[APPLICATION PROXY WAF NGFW]
    osiB[OSI LAYER 5 SESSION] --> ftypeB[CIRCUIT LEVEL GATEWAY]
    osiC[OSI LAYER 4 TRANSPORT] --> ftypeC[STATEFUL INSPECTION]
    osiD[OSI LAYER 3 NETWORK] --> ftypeD[PACKET FILTERING STATELESS]
    osiE[OSI LAYER 2 DATA LINK] --> ftypeE[MAC FILTERING BRIDGE FIREWALL]
    ftypeA --> zoneA[DMZ HMI WEB SERVERS]
    ftypeB --> zoneB[REMOTE VENDOR ACCESS TUNNELS]
    ftypeC --> zoneC[IT OT BOUNDARY]
    ftypeD --> zoneD[ROUTER PERIMETER LEVEL 1 TO 2]
    ftypeE --> zoneE[CELL LEVEL SEGMENTATION]
```

## 4.5 Diagram D — Defense in Depth Layered Firewall Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    diaA[EXTERNAL ATTACKER] --> diaB[EDGE NGFW LAYER 1]
    diaB --> diaC[DMZ PROXY WAF LAYER 2]
    diaC --> diaD[CORE NGFW LAYER 3]
    diaD --> diaE[OT BOUNDARY ICS FIREWALL LAYER 4]
    diaE --> diaF[CELL LEVEL INDUSTRIAL FIREWALL LAYER 5]
    diaF --> diaG[PLC RTU INLINE PROTOCOL FILTER LAYER 6]
    diaH[ZERO TRUST POLICY ENGINE] -.->|Dynamic Rule Updates| diaB
    diaH -.->|Dynamic Rule Updates| diaD
    diaH -.->|Dynamic Rule Updates| diaE
    diaI[THREAT INTELLIGENCE FEED] -.->|IP Domain Hash Blocklists| diaB
    diaI -.->|IOC Signatures| diaD
    diaI -.->|OT Specific Threat Signatures| diaE
```

## 4.6 Diagram E — Firewall Type Evolution Timeline (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    evoA[1980s PACKET FILTER STATELESS] --> evoB[1990s STATEFUL INSPECTION]
    evoB --> evoC[2000s APPLICATION PROXY AND WAF]
    evoC --> evoD[2010s NGFW WITH IPS AND DPI]
    evoD --> evoE[2020s CLOUD NATIVE AND ZERO TRUST]
    evoE --> evoF[2024 PLUS OT ICS AWARE FIREWALL IEC 62443]
    evoA -.->|Limitation No State Awareness| evoB
    evoB -.->|Limitation No Payload Visibility| evoC
    evoC -.->|Limitation Speed Bottleneck| evoD
    evoD -.->|Limitation Cloud Native Demands| evoE
    evoE -.->|Limitation OT Protocol Blindness| evoF
```

## 4.7 Diagram F — Proxy Firewall Operation (Request Mediation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    proxyA[INTERNAL CLIENT] -->|Request to External Server| proxyB[FORWARD PROXY FIREWALL]
    proxyB -->|Inspect URL Headers Payload| proxyC{ALLOW OR DENY}
    proxyC -->|ALLOW| proxyD[RE ESTABLISH CONNECTION TO SERVER]
    proxyC -->|DENY| proxyE[BLOCK AND LOG]
    proxyD -->|Response Inspection| proxyF[RECEIVE SERVER RESPONSE]
    proxyF -->|Scan For Malicious Content| proxyG{CLEAN OR MALICIOUS}
    proxyG -->|CLEAN| proxyH[FORWARD TO CLIENT]
    proxyG -->|MALICIOUS| proxyE
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]
**Q: Differentiate between a Stateless Packet Filtering Firewall and a Stateful Inspection Firewall. State one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Stateless Packet Filtering Firewall** examines each packet **independently** based on header fields (Source IP, Destination IP, Port, Protocol) using a static ACL. It has **no memory** of previous packets.
- **Limitation:** It cannot prevent **SYN flood attacks** or detect **session-based exploits** because it has no concept of an ongoing session.

A **Stateful Inspection Firewall** maintains a **State Table** that tracks the state of every active TCP/UDP session (NEW, ESTABLISHED, RELATED, INVALID). It verifies that each packet **belongs to a legitimate session** based on the three-way handshake.
- **Limitation:** It **cannot inspect the application-layer payload**, making it blind to malware, SQL injection, and other Layer 7 attacks.

**Key Differentiator:**
$$\boxed{\text{Stateless} = \text{Header Only, No Memory} \quad | \quad \text{Stateful} = \text{Header + Session Memory}}$$

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Q: What is a Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)? List four features that distinguish it from a traditional stateful firewall.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)** is an advanced network security device that combines traditional stateful filtering with **deep packet inspection (DPI)**, **intrusion prevention (IPS)**, **application awareness**, and **threat intelligence integration**.

**Four Distinguishing Features:**

1. **Application-Layer Awareness** — Identifies applications regardless of port/protocol (e.g., detects BitTorrent on port 80).
2. **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** — Examines packet payload, not just headers, to detect malware and exploits.
3. **Integrated Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** — Signature and behavior-based attack detection and blocking.
4. **SSL/TLS Decryption** — Inspects encrypted traffic by terminating and re-encrypting sessions.
5. **Threat Intelligence Feed Integration** — Real-time updates from global threat databases.
6. **User Identity Awareness** — Ties traffic to specific users (via AD/LDAP), not just IP addresses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the different types of firewalls based on the OSI layer they operate at. Compare their suitability for Industrial Control System (ICS) environments.** **[14 Marks]**

**Mark Distribution Hint:**
- (a) Classification & Diagram — 7 Marks
- (b) ICS Suitability Comparison — 7 Marks

---

#### Part (a) — Classification with Diagram **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Firewalls can be classified based on the **OSI Layer** at which they inspect and filter traffic:

**1. Packet Filtering Firewall (OSI Layer 3 & 4):**
- Inspects IP header and TCP/UDP header fields.
- Uses Access Control Lists (ACLs).
- **Example:** Cisco IOS ACL, Linux `iptables`.
- Stateless — no session memory.

**2. Circuit-Level Gateway (OSI Layer 5):**
- Validates TCP handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK).
- Creates a session relay once handshake is verified.
- **Example:** SOCKS proxy.

**3. Stateful Inspection Firewall (OSI Layer 3, 4 & 5):**
- Maintains a State Table to track active sessions.
- Allows only packets belonging to established sessions.
- **Example:** Check Point FireWall-1, pfSense.

**4. Application-Level Gateway / Proxy Firewall (OSI Layer 7):**
- Terminates and re-establishes client connections.
- Inspects the entire application payload.
- **Example:** Squid (HTTP proxy), HAProxy.

**5. Next-Generation Firewall (OSI Layer 3 to 7):**
- Combines stateful filtering, DPI, IPS, and application awareness.
- **Example:** Palo Alto PA-Series, Fortinet FortiGate.

**Neat Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    osiA[OSI LAYER 7 APPLICATION] --> proxyA[APPLICATION PROXY FIREWALL]
    osiB[OSI LAYER 5 SESSION] --> circuitA[CIRCUIT LEVEL GATEWAY]
    osiC[OSI LAYER 4 TRANSPORT] --> stateA[STATEFUL INSPECTION FIREWALL]
    osiD[OSI LAYER 3 NETWORK] --> pktA[PACKET FILTER STATELESS FIREWALL]
    proxyA -->|Higher Inspection| ngfwA[NGFW INTEGRATES ALL LAYERS]
    circuitA --> ngfwA
    stateA --> ngfwA
    pktA --> ngfwA
```

---

#### Part (b) — ICS Suitability Comparison **[7 Marks]**

| OSI Layer | Firewall Type | ICS Suitability | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer 3–4** | Packet Filtering | ✅ **Highly Suitable** for Level 1–2 cell segmentation | High throughput, low latency critical for Modbus/TCP, PROFINET |
| **Layer 5** | Circuit-Level Gateway | ⚠ **Moderate** for remote vendor access | Lightweight, good for jump-server tunnels |
| **Layer 3–5** | Stateful Inspection | ✅ **Suitable** for IT-OT boundary | Detects session-based attacks, reasonable latency |
| **Layer 7** | Application Proxy | ⚠ **Limited** — adds latency | Use only for critical HMI / Engineering Workstation protection |
| **Layer 3–7** | NGFW | ✅ **Ideal** for Purdue Level 3.5 DMZ | DPI detects OT protocol anomalies (Modbus function code misuse) |
| **Layer 7 (HTTP)** | WAF | ✅ **Essential** for industrial web HMIs | Blocks OWASP Top 10 attacks on SCADA web interfaces |
| **OT-Aware** | ICS Firewall | ✅✅ **Best for OT** (IEC 62443 compliant) | Native parsing of Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, PROFINET |

**Conclusion:** No single firewall type is sufficient for an ICS. A **defense-in-depth architecture** combining **NGFW at the IT-OT DMZ**, **ICS-aware firewalls at Level 0–3**, and **cell-level packet filters at Level 1–2** is the recommended approach per **IEC 62443-3-3** standards.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]

**Q: Explain the working of a Stateful Inspection Firewall with a suitable diagram. Construct a state table for a sample TCP session and explain how it prevents a SYN flood attack.** **[14 Marks]**

**Mark Distribution Hint:**
- (a) Working & State Table — 7 Marks
- (b) SYN Flood Prevention — 7 Marks

---

#### Part (a) — Working of Stateful Inspection Firewall **[7 Marks]**

A **Stateful Inspection Firewall** (also called a **Stateful Packet Filter**) maintains a **State Table** that records the state of every active network session. It verifies each incoming and outgoing packet against this table.

**Working Steps:**

1. **Initial SYN Packet:** When a client (e.g., HMI at 10.0.1.50:5000) sends a **SYN** packet to a server (PLC at 10.0.1.100:502), the firewall creates a new entry in the state table with state = **NEW**.

2. **SYN-ACK Response:** The server responds with **SYN-ACK**. The firewall matches the packet's 5-tuple against the state table, finds the existing entry, and transitions the state to **SYN_RECEIVED**.

3. **ACK Completion:** The client sends **ACK**. The firewall updates the state to **ESTABLISHED**. Only from this point onward, packets in both directions of this session are allowed.

4. **Session Monitoring:** All subsequent packets in this session are checked against the state table entry. If the state is **ESTABLISHED** and the packet matches, it is allowed.

5. **Session Termination:** When **FIN** is exchanged, the state transitions to **FIN_WAIT** and eventually the entry is purged after a timeout (e.g., 60 seconds of inactivity).

**State Table for Sample TCP Session:**

| Step | Packet | Direction | Source | Dest | State Created | State Transition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SYN | HMI → PLC | 10.0.1.50:5000 | 10.0.1.100:502 | NEW | SYN_SENT |
| 2 | SYN-ACK | PLC → HMI | 10.0.1.100:502 | 10.0.1.50:5000 | Match found | SYN_RECEIVED |
| 3 | ACK | HMI → PLC | 10.0.1.50:5000 | 10.0.1.100:502 | Match found | ESTABLISHED |
| 4 | DATA | HMI → PLC | 10.0.1.50:5000 | 10.0.1.100:502 | Match found | ESTABLISHED |
| 5 | FIN | HMI → PLC | 10.0.1.50:5000 | 10.0.1.100:502 | Match found | FIN_WAIT_1 |
| 6 | FIN-ACK | PLC → HMI | 10.0.1.100:502 | 10.0.1.50:5000 | Match found | CLOSED |

**Working Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    sfA[HMI CLIENT] -->|SYN| sfB[STATEFUL FIREWALL]
    sfB -->|Check State Table| sfC{NEW SESSION}
    sfC -->|YES CREATE ENTRY| sfD[FORWARD SYN TO PLC]
    sfD -->|SYN ACK| sfE[STATEFUL FIREWALL VERIFIES STATE]
    sfE -->|State Matches| sfF[FORWARD TO HMI]
    sfF -->|ACK| sfG[STATE TRANSITIONS TO ESTABLISHED]
    sfG -->|Data Flow Allowed| sfH[ONGOING SESSION]
    sfH -->|FIN| sfI[SESSION CLOSED ENTRY PURGED]
```

---

#### Part (b) — SYN Flood Attack Prevention **[7 Marks]**

**The Attack:**

In a **SYN Flood attack**, an attacker sends a massive number of **SYN packets** from **spoofed source IP addresses** and **never sends the final ACK**. This causes the server to keep **half-open connections**, exhausting its memory and preventing legitimate users from connecting.

**Number of half-open connections:**
$$N_{half\_open} = R_{syn} \times T_{timeout}$$

where $R_{syn}$ = rate of SYN packets/sec and $T_{timeout}$ = connection timeout (typically 75 seconds).

**How Stateful Firewall Prevents It:**

1. **SYN Rate Limiting:** The firewall enforces a **maximum threshold** of half-open (NEW) connections per source IP per second.
$$R_{syn,max} = 100 \text{ connections per second (configurable)}$$

2. **SYN Cookies:** Instead of allocating full session state for every SYN, the firewall sends a **cryptographic cookie** in the SYN-ACK sequence number. State is only allocated upon receiving a valid ACK.

$$\text{Cookie} = \text{Hash}(\text{Source IP}, \text{Source Port}, \text{Secret Key}, \text{Time Stamp})$$

3. **State Table Threshold:** The firewall sets a **cap on total state table entries**. If exceeded:
$$N_{entries} > N_{max} \rightarrow \text{Drop new SYNs}$$
$$N_{max} = 65{,}536 \text{ (typical default)}$$

4. **IP Reputation Filtering:** Spoofed source IPs are checked against threat intelligence feeds; known-bad IPs are dropped immediately.

5. **Idle Timeout for Half-Open Sessions:** A short timeout (e.g., 30 seconds) is enforced for connections stuck in SYN_RECEIVED state.

**Result:** The attacker's spoofed SYNs are either dropped (no state allocated) or rate-limited, while legitimate user sessions proceed normally.

**Numerical Example (Bonus Marks):**

If an attacker sends $R_{syn} = 10{,}000$ SYNs/sec and the state table has $N_{max} = 50{,}000$ entries with $T_{timeout} = 30$ sec:

$$N_{attack} = 10{,}000 \times 30 = 300{,}000 \text{ half-open states}$$

$$300{,}000 \gg 50{,}000 = N_{max} \rightarrow \text{Firewall drops new SYNs after 5 seconds.}$$

The server is **protected** because the firewall never allowed the attacker's state to consume server resources.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Based on Actual KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **❌ WRONG:** "A firewall is just an antivirus." → **No marks.** Firewalls filter **network traffic**; antivirus scans **files/endpoints**.
>
> 2. **❌ WRONG:** "Stateful firewalls are always better than stateless." → **No marks.** Stateful firewalls add **latency**; in latency-critical OT protocols (PROFINET, EtherCAT), stateless filtering at Level 1 may be preferable.
>
> 3. **❌ WRONG:** "NGFW can detect zero-day attacks perfectly." → **Partial marks only.** NGFW uses **behavioral analysis** for zero-days, which generates **false positives**. State this nuance in your answer.
>
> 4. **❌ WRONG:** Confusing **DMZ** with **NGFW**. DMZ is a **network zone**; NGFW is a **device type**.
>
> 5. **❌ WRONG:** Forgetting the **deny-by-default principle** in ICS — always mention this in OT-related answers for full marks.
>
> 6. **❌ WRONG:** Drawing firewall architecture without mentioning **Purdue Model levels or IEC 62443 zones** — for an Industrial Cyber Security course, this is mandatory context.
>
> 7. **❌ WRONG:** Writing "First match wins" without showing a **worked numerical example** of rule ordering — examiners expect this for full marks on 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These Key Points:**

- ✅ A **firewall** is a network security device (hardware/software/hybrid) that enforces an access control policy between security zones.
- ✅ **Eight types of firewalls:** Packet Filtering, Stateful Inspection, Circuit-Level Gateway, Application-Level Proxy, NGFW, WAF, Cloud-Native, ICS/OT-Aware.
- ✅ **Stateless** = Layer 3/4, header-only, ACL-based, no session memory, fastest.
- ✅ **Stateful** = Layer 3/5, maintains **State Table** with session states (NEW, ESTABLISHED, RELATED, INVALID).
- ✅ **Application Proxy** = Layer 7, terminates & re-establishes connections, full payload inspection, slower.
- ✅ **NGFW** = Stateful + DPI + IPS + Application Awareness + Threat Intelligence — the modern enterprise standard.
- ✅ **WAF** = Specialized for HTTP/S, protects against **OWASP Top 10**.
- ✅ **ICS/OT Firewalls** = Protocol-aware (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850), **deny-by-default**, **IEC 62443 compliant**.
- ✅ **First Match Wins** principle for ACL rule evaluation — order matters.
- ✅ **Deny-by-default** is the cornerstone of industrial firewall policy.
- ✅ Firewalls are deployed at **Purdue Level 3.5 (DMZ)** as the IT-OT boundary.
- ✅ **Defense-in-depth** requires **layered firewall types**, not a single device.
- ✅ **Defense Formulae:** Throughput $T = \frac{P \times 8}{t}$, Drop Rate $D = \frac{N_{drop}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$, Latency $L = t_{out} - t_{in}$.
- ✅ **SYN Flood Mitigation:** SYN cookies, rate limiting, state table cap, IP reputation.
- ✅ Always mention **IEC 62443** standard for any OT firewall question.
- ✅ Always draw a **diagram** (Purdue Model or block diagram) for full marks on 7+ mark questions.

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Firewalls: Types of Firewalls**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PECCT524 — Advanced Industrial Cyber Security*
