- **SAD (Security Association Database):** Holds the live SAs (SPI, keys, lifetimes, anti-replay window).
- **SPD (Security Policy Database):** Holds rules to DISCARD / BYPASS / PROTECT each packet.

**ESP Packet Format — Tunnel Mode (4 marks):**

| Field | Size (bytes) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** | 4 | Index into receiver's SAD |
| **Sequence Number** | 4 | Anti-replay counter |
| **IV (Initialization Vector)** | 16 | Random seed for AES-CBC |
| **Encrypted Payload** | Variable | Inner IP + L4 headers + data (AES-encrypted) |
| **Padding** | 0–255 | Align to AES block boundary (16 B) |
| **Pad Length** | 1 | Length of padding in bytes |
| **Next Header** | 1 | Protocol of inner payload (4 = IPv4, 6 = TCP) |
| **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** | 16 | HMAC-SHA-256-128 truncated output |

A correct block diagram from the student (or the textual layout in Section 2.2.2) earns **1 additional mark**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

This is a direct application of the derivation in **Section 3.1**. The full solution:

**Step 1 — Identify component sizes [1 mark]**

| Component | Size (bytes) |
|---|---|
| Original IP header | 20 |
| TCP header | 20 |
| Modbus payload | 1400 |
| ESP header (SPI + Seq) | 12 |
| ESP IV (AES-CBC-128) | 16 |
| ESP ICV (HMAC-SHA-256-128) | 16 |
| Outer IP header | 20 |
| Ethernet header + FCS | 18 |

**Step 2 — Compute encrypted payload length (pre-padding) [1 mark]**

$$P_{pre} = 20 + 20 + 1400 = 1440 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ESP trailer with padding [2 marks]**

Required: $(1440 + P_{pad} + 2) \bmod 16 = 0$

$1442 \bmod 16 = 2$, so $P_{pad} = 14$ bytes.

$$P_{padded} = 1440 + 14 + 2 = 1456 \text{ bytes (multiple of 16)} \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Total on-the-wire frame [2 marks]**

$$L_{wire} = 18 + 20 + 12 + 16 + 1456 + 16 = 1538 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — MTU implications [1 mark]**

Since $1538 > 1500$, the egress router must **fragment** the packet, which is **forbidden in many industrial protocols** (Modbus/TCP, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP). The fix is to **clamp the TCP MSS** at the VPN gateway to **1360 bytes** so that the application never produces a packet that requires fragmentation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, PECCT524 Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **IKEv2 handshake**. What is **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** and how does IKEv2 achieve it? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare **IPSec**, **SSL/TLS VPN (OpenVPN)**, and **WireGuard** with respect to **encryption algorithm, authentication method, NAT traversal, mobile roaming support, and ICS suitability**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**IKEv2 Handshake (5 marks):**

IKEv2 negotiates the IKE SA and the first CHILD_SA in just **4 messages** (compared to 6 in IKEv1):

1. **IKE_SA_INIT (Initiator → Responder):** Contains SA proposals (encryption suite, integrity, DH group, PRF), initiator nonce $Ni$, and Diffie-Hellman key-exchange value $g^{x_i}$.
2. **IKE_SA_INIT (Responder → Initiator):** Chosen SA, responder nonce $Nr$, DH value $g^{x_r}$, and a certificate request.
3. **IKE_AUTH (Initiator → Responder):** Identity $IDi$, AUTH payload (signed $PRF(SK\_pi, IDi)$), and the first CHILD_SA proposal.
4. **IKE_AUTH (Responder → Initiator):** Identity $IDr$, AUTH payload, and the accepted CHILD_SA.

**Perfect Forward Secrecy (2 marks):**

PFS guarantees that **compromise of the long-term private key does NOT compromise past session keys**. IKEv2 achieves PFS by:

- Generating an **ephemeral DH key pair** $g^{x_i}, g^{x_r}$ for **every** IKE_SA_INIT exchange.
- Deriving fresh SKEYSEED $\rightarrow$ fresh SK_ei, SK_er for **every** session.
- Allowing additional `CREATE_CHILD_SA` exchanges with **new** DH groups to periodically rotate CHILD_SAs.

> **Industrial relevance:** IEC 62443-3-3 SR 3.4 (Software and information integrity) and SR 4.1 (Information confidentiality) require PFS; an auditor will reject an IKEv1-with-static-PSK configuration.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

(Use a **comparison table**; each correct row carries ≈ **1.5 marks**.)

| Criterion | **IPSec IKEv2** | **OpenVPN (SSL/TLS)** | **WireGuard** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encryption** | AES-128/256-GCM, ChaCha20 | AES-128/256-GCM, ChaCha20 | ChaCha20-Poly1305 (fixed) |
| **Authentication (peer)** | X.509 / PSK / EAP-TLS | X.509 / username-password | Pre-shared public key (Curve25519) |
| **Integrity** | AEAD (GCM) or HMAC-SHA-256 | AEAD (GCM) | Keyed-BLAKE2s |
| **NAT Traversal** | NAT-T (UDP 4500) | Native (runs over TCP or UDP 1194) | Built-in (UDP, roaming magic) |
| **Mobile Roaming** | MOBIKE (RFC 4555) extension | Reconnect required | **Seamless** (cryptokey routing) |
| **ICS Suitability** | ✅ **Industry standard**, HW accel | ✅ With care (no multicast) | ✅ New, lean, audit-friendly |
| **Lines of Code (audit)** | ~400 K (kernel) | ~100 K (userspace) | **~4 000** (kernel module) |

> **Examiner tip:** If the question is "best VPN for an industrial plant", the safest answer is **IPSec IKEv2 with X.509 certs, AES-256-GCM, DH Group 19, and SHA-384** — because of hardware offload (Intel AES-NI, ARMv8 CE) and a decade of operational hardening.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on VPN questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating AH and ESP.** AH does **not** encrypt. If a question says "encrypted VPN with integrity", it is **ESP**, not AH. Wrong protocol = −2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the order of ESP fields.** A common mistake is putting ICV **before** the encrypted payload. ICV is **appended at the tail**. Wrong diagram = −2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit on formulas.** Writing "$L = 1538$" instead of "$L = 1538$ bytes" loses ½ mark.
> 4. **Writing IKEv1 instead of IKEv2.** The 2024 scheme explicitly references RFC 7296 (IKEv2). IKEv1 is **legacy** — avoid it in the answer script.
> 5. **Memorizing definitions without diagrams.** The 2024 OBE pattern awards 1–2 marks for "neat labelled block diagram" in any 7-mark sub-question. **Always include a diagram**.
> 6. **Ignoring the IV in size calculations.** Many students omit the 16-byte IV, yielding an under-count of 16 bytes in the ESP overhead.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this checklist the night before the exam.

- ✅ A **VPN** = cryptographic tunnel across an untrusted network.
- ✅ Three IPSec pillars: **AH, ESP, IKEv2**.
- ✅ **AH** = integrity + auth (NO encryption).
- ✅ **ESP** = integrity + auth + **encryption** (workhorse of IPSec VPNs).
- ✅ **Transport mode** = host-to-host; **Tunnel mode** = gateway-to-gateway (DEFAULT).
- ✅ ESP tunnel-mode overhead on IPv4 = **50–57 bytes**; on IPv6 = **60–77 bytes**.
- ✅ Effective MTU inside an IPSec tunnel ≈ **1380–1420 bytes**.
- ✅ **TCP MSS clamp = 1360 bytes** in industrial VPN gateways.
- ✅ IKEv2 = 4 messages in Phase-1 (`IKE_SA_INIT` + `IKE_AUTH`).
- ✅ Key derivation chain: $SKEYSEED \rightarrow SK\_d, SK\_ai, SK\_ar, SK\_ei, SK\_er, SK\_pi, SK\_pr$.
- ✅ **PFS** requires **ephemeral DH** on every re-key.
- ✅ DH **Group 14 (2048-bit)** is the NIST minimum; **Group 19 (256-bit ECP)** is preferred for performance.
- ✅ Default crypto suite: **AES-256-GCM + HMAC-SHA-256-128 + DH-19 + PFS**.
- ✅ Industrial suitability order: **IPSec IKEv2 > WireGuard > SSL/OpenVPN > L2TP/IPSec > PPTP** (avoid).
- ✅ Anti-replay window is implemented via the **ESP Sequence Number** and the receiver's sliding window (default 32 or 64 packets).
- ✅ **NAT-Traversal** encapsulates ESP in **UDP port 4500**.
- ✅ **IKE SA lifetime ≥ ESP SA lifetime**; typical $T_{IKE}=24$ h, $T_{ESP}=1$ h ⇒ 23 re-keys per IKE lifetime.
- ✅ **MPLS L3 VPN** is a *trusted* (non-cryptographic) VPN; it satisfies confidentiality-by-isolation, not confidentiality-by-encryption.
- ✅ **Split tunneling** routes non-corporate traffic outside the tunnel; **full tunneling** forces everything through the corporate gateway (preferred for ICS).
- ✅ **Dead Peer Detection (DPD)** in IKEv2 detects half-open tunnels and tears them down.
- ✅ IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.5/3.1/4.1 all map to VPN properties (confidentiality, integrity, peer authentication).
- ✅ **KPI formulas to memorize:** $L_{wire}$, $R_{overhead\%}$, $N_{rekeys}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Segmentation** is the architectural practice of partitioning a larger computer network into multiple distinct, smaller, logically or physically isolated sub-networks (segments) — each functioning as its own bounded trust zone — to enforce granular security policies, contain lateral threat movement, and improve operational performance within Industrial Control Systems (ICS), Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA), and Information Technology (IT) environments.

In the context of **PECCT524 – Advanced Industrial Cyber Security**, network segmentation is the foundational defensive control mandated by standards such as **ISA/IEC 62443-3-3** (System Security Requirements and Security Levels) and the **NIST SP 800-82r3** (Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security).

The three primary segmentation modalities recognized by KTU 2024 are:

1. **Physical Segmentation** — Use of dedicated hardware (separate switches, routers, cabling).
2. **Logical Segmentation** — Use of Virtual Local Area Networks (VLANs), Virtual Routing and Forwarding (VRF), and Access Control Lists (ACLs) over shared infrastructure.
3. **Hybrid Segmentation** — A combination of physical and logical controls (industry best-practice for OT environments).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology — "Zone & Conduit":** Per IEC 62443-3-3, a *Zone* is a grouping of physical and logical assets sharing common security requirements, while a *Conduit* is the communication path between zones (logical grouping of communication channels).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏥 Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Ward System"**

Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. The hospital is one building, but it is divided into separate wards:
- **Cardiology Ward** (isolated rooms, restricted entry via staff badge)
- **ICU** (highest security, controlled air-flow, biometric access)
- **Pharmacy** (locked counters, surveillance cameras)
- **Public Lobby** (open to everyone, but cannot directly access the ICU)

If a contagious patient (malware) enters the *Public Lobby*, the infection cannot spread to the *ICU* because there is a *controlled corridor (conduit)* with *nurses and doors (firewalls/ACLs)* checking access.

**Network Segmentation is exactly this:**
- Each *ward* = a **network zone/segment**
- Each *staff badge reader* = a **firewall rule / ACL**
- The *controlled corridor* = a **conduit**
- The *building structure* = the **physical network**
- *Air-pressure differentials* = **logical isolation (VLANs)**

A cyber attacker who breaches one zone (ward) **cannot freely move sideways (laterally)** into critical OT zones — this containment is called **lateral movement prevention**.

---

## 1.3 Core Metrics and Constants

| Metric / Constant | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Purdue Model Levels** | **6 levels (Levels 0–5)** |
| **Default VLAN ID** | **VLAN 1** (management/disabled for segmentation) |
| **802.1Q Tag Protocol Identifier (TPID)** | **0x8100** |
| **Maximum VLANs per switch (IEEE 802.1Q)** | **4094** (VLAN IDs 1–4094) |
| **ISA/IEC 62443 Security Levels (SL)** | **SL 1, SL 2, SL 3, SL 4** |
| **Zero Trust Default Stance** | **Deny All — Permit Specific** |
| **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** | **Levels 0 to 5** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The **Purdue Model (ISA-95)** is the single most important diagram you must know for KTU 2024 board exams on Industrial Cyber Security. Always draw it from **Level 0 (Physical Process) → Level 5 (Enterprise/Internet)** in a layered pyramid.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Purdue Reference Model

The **Purdue Model** (originally developed by Purdue University and formalized in **ISA-95 / IEC 62443**) decomposes industrial networks into **6 hierarchical levels**, each with progressively decreasing criticality and increasing openness:

```
Level 5  →  Enterprise Zone (Internet, Corporate Email, Cloud ERP)
Level 4  →  Business Planning & Logistics (MES, ERP, Email)
Level 3.5 → Industrial Demilitarized Zone (IDMZ)  ← CRITICAL BUFFER
Level 3  →  Site Operations (HMI, SCADA Servers, Engineering Workstations)
Level 2  →  Area Supervisory Control (PLCs, DCS Controllers, SCADA Masters)
Level 1  →  Basic Control (Sensors, Actuators, Field Devices)
Level 0  →  Physical Process (The actual manufacturing process)
```

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

| Step | Layer | Function | Segmentation Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Level 0 | Physical I/O from sensors and actuators | Physically isolated field buses (e.g., 4–20 mA, Foundation Fieldbus) |
| **2** | Level 1 | Real-time control loops via PLCs, RTUs | **No direct external connectivity** — air-gapped or tightly firewalled |
| **3** | Level 2 | Supervisory control (HMI, SCADA) | VLAN isolation, no internet, monitored conduits |
| **4** | Level 3.0 | Site-wide operations | Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) terminates all cross-zone traffic |
| **5** | Level 3.5 | IDMZ — buffers, jump servers, data diodes | **Mandatory** buffer zone between IT (L4-5) and OT (L0-3) |
| **6** | Level 4 | Business operations | Strictly firewalled; uses brokers/replication to IDMZ only |
| **7** | Level 5 | Internet / Cloud / External partners | Demilitarized; uses proxies, WAFs, no direct OT access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Level 3.5 (IDMZ) is critical:** Historically, attackers like **TRITON (2017)** and **Industroyer/CrashOverride (2016)** breached enterprise networks (Level 4–5) and pivoted directly into Level 2–3 SCADA controllers because **no IDMZ existed**. The IDMZ is now a **mandatory** defense-in-depth control.

---

## 2.2 Segmentation Mechanisms — Operational Logic

### A. Physical Segmentation
- Dedicated switches, cables, routers for each zone.
- **Pros:** Maximum isolation, no shared attack surface.
- **Cons:** Expensive, hard to retrofit, poor scalability.
- **Use case:** Nuclear power plants, safety instrumented systems (SIS).

### B. Logical Segmentation — VLAN (IEEE 802.1Q)
- Tags Ethernet frames with a **12-bit VLAN ID (1–4094)**.
- Enables multiple isolated broadcast domains on one physical switch.

### C. Firewall / ACL-Based Segmentation
- Stateful packet inspection (SPI) between zones.
- Default-deny rule, explicit allow-list.

### D. Software-Defined Networking (SDN) Segmentation
- Centralized policy engines (e.g., Cisco ACI, VMware NSX).
- Micro-segmentation down to per-workload rules.

### E. Data Diodes (Unidirectional Gateways)
- Physically enforce one-way data flow from OT → IT.
- Cannot be bypassed even by malware (used in high-security zones).

### F. Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)
- A buffer network containing jump servers, antivirus update servers, patch relays, and historians that *replicate* (not bridge) data between IT and OT.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use **\vert** or **\mid** instead of the vertical pipe `\|` symbol inside all markdown table cells to prevent syntax breakage.

| # | Formula / Rule | Notation | Purpose / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of subnets from host bits | $N_s = 2^{b}$ | $b$ = bits borrowed from host portion |
| 2 | Number of usable hosts per subnet | $N_h = 2^{h} - 2$ | $h$ = remaining host bits; subtract network & broadcast |
| 3 | CIDR → Subnet Mask conversion | Mask $= (2^{n}-1) \ll (32-n)$ | $n$ = prefix length; result in 32-bit dotted form |
| 4 | Inverse Mask (Wildcard for ACL) | WC $= 2^{32-n} - 1$ | Used in Cisco ACL wildcard masks |
| 5 | Total VLANs (802.1Q) | $V_{max} = 2^{12} - 2 = 4094$ | Excluding reserved VLAN 0 and 4095 |
| 6 | Security Level Target (IEC 62443) | $SL \in \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4 \rbrace$ | Higher = higher threat-model resistance |
| 7 | Mean Time to Lateral Compromise (MTTC) | $MTTC = \frac{1}{\lambda_{seg}}$ | $\lambda_{seg}$ = lateral movement probability per segment |
| 8 | Conduit bandwidth overhead (802.1Q) | $Overhead = 4 \text{ bytes per frame}$ | Adds to MTU; MTU becomes 1522 bytes |
| 9 | Number of Security Zones in Purdue | $Z = 6$ (Levels 0–5) + 1 IDMZ | Architectural constant |
| 10 | Default MTU (Ethernet) | $MTU_{eth} = 1500 \text{ bytes}$ | After 802.1Q tagging → 1522 bytes |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Industrial Engineering

| Industry Sector | Segmentation Application |
|---|---|
| **Power & Energy (Smart Grids)** | Isolating SCADA from corporate billing systems |
| **Oil & Gas (Refineries, Pipelines)** | Level 0–1 protection against TRITON-class malware |
| **Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** | Isolating IIoT sensors from ERP network |
| **Pharmaceutical / Healthcare OT** | NIST + GxP compliance via VLAN micro-segmentation |
| **Water Treatment (SCADA)** | NERC-CIP and IEC 62443 zone enforcement |
| **Transportation (Rail, Aviation)** | Air-gapped signaling networks vs. passenger Wi-Fi |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Expect a 14-mark question asking *"Explain Network Segmentation with reference to the Purdue Model and IDMZ architecture."* Always answer with a **diagram + table of zones + 802.1Q VLAN example**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — VLAN Subnetting for Industrial Zones

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An Industrial Plant has a **Class B private network** `172.20.0.0/16`. The network architect must segment it into **8 industrial zones**, each with at least **500 host addresses**. Determine the **custom subnet mask**, the **CIDR prefix**, the **subnet ID**, and the **usable host range** for the **SCADA Zone (1st usable subnet)**.

### Given Data

$$\text{Network} = 172.20.0.0 / 16$$

$$\text{Required Subnets} = 8$$

$$\text{Required Hosts per Subnet} = 500$$

### Step 1 — Determine the Number of Bits to Borrow ($b$)

We need $2^b \geq 8$ subnets.

$$\begin{aligned}
2^b &\geq 8 \\
2^3 &= 8 \quad \text{(satisfies minimum)} \\
\therefore b &= 3 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — New CIDR Prefix

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Prefix}_{new} &= \text{Prefix}_{old} + b \\
&= 16 + 3 \\
&= 19
\end{aligned}$$

So the new subnet mask is `/19`.

### Step 3 — Convert `/19` to Dotted Decimal Subnet Mask

A `/19` means **19 ones** in the subnet mask.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mask bits (binary)} &= \underbrace{11111111.11111111.11100000}_{19 \text{ bits}}.00000000 \\
\text{Mask (decimal)} &= 255.255.224.0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Verify Usable Hosts per Subnet

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 32 - 19 = 13 \text{ host bits} \\
N_h &= 2^{13} - 2 = 8192 - 2 = 8190 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $8190 \geq 500$, the design is **valid**.

### Step 5 — Derive the SCADA Zone (1st Subnet)

The 1st usable subnet (subnet #0) is the **SCADA Zone**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Subnet Increment} &= 2^{13} = 8192 \text{ addresses} \\
\text{SCADA Network ID} &= 172.20.0.0/19 \\
\text{First Host} &= 172.20.0.1 \\
\text{Last Host} &= 172.20.31.254 \\
\text{Broadcast} &= 172.20.31.255
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Summary Table for All 8 Zones

| Zone # | Zone Name | Subnet ID | Subnet Mask | Usable Host Range | Broadcast |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | SCADA Zone | 172.20.0.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.0.1 – 172.20.31.254 | 172.20.31.255 |
| 1 | HMI Zone | 172.20.32.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.32.1 – 172.20.63.254 | 172.20.63.255 |
| 2 | PLC / DCS Zone | 172.20.64.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.64.1 – 172.20.95.254 | 172.20.95.255 |
| 3 | Engineering Workstation | 172.20.96.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.96.1 – 172.20.127.254 | 172.20.127.255 |
| 4 | Historian Zone | 172.20.128.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.128.1 – 172.20.159.254 | 172.20.159.255 |
| 5 | IDMZ Zone | 172.20.160.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.160.1 – 172.20.191.254 | 172.20.191.255 |
| 6 | MES / ERP Zone | 172.20.192.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.192.1 – 172.20.223.254 | 172.20.223.255 |
| 7 | Reserved / Future | 172.20.224.0 /19 | 255.255.224.0 | 172.20.224.1 – 172.20.255.254 | 172.20.255.255 |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 802.1Q VLAN Frame Encapsulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A SCADA server inside VLAN 50 sends an Ethernet frame to a PLC inside VLAN 70 via a trunk port. Calculate the **new maximum frame size (MTU)** and the **protocol overhead percentage**.

### Step 1 — Identify Standard Frame Structure

$$\text{Original Ethernet Frame} = \text{Dst MAC} \; (6) + \text{Src MAC} \; (6) + \text{Ethertype} \; (2) + \text{Payload} \; (46-1500) + \text{FCS} \; (4)$$

$$\text{Total Maximum} = 6+6+2+1500+4 = 1518 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 2 — Insert 802.1Q Tag (4 bytes)

The 802.1Q tag consists of:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TPID} &= 2 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{always } 0\text{x}8100) \\
\text{TCI} &= 2 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{PCP} + \text{DEI} + \text{VID}_{12}) \\
\text{Total Tag} &= 4 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute New Maximum Frame Size

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Max Tagged Frame} &= 1518 + 4 \\
&= 1522 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Overhead Percentage

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead \%} &= \frac{4}{1522} \times 100 \\
&= 0.2627 \%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some students forget to add the 4-byte tag and write **1522 as 1518**. This is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conduit Threat Resistance (Defense-in-Depth Probability)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A refinery's segmentation design has **3 conduits** between IT (Level 4) and OT (Level 1). Each conduit independently blocks an attack with probability $p_i$. If the attacker must breach **all 3 conduits**, find the **overall breach probability** given $p_1 = 0.95$, $p_2 = 0.90$, $p_3 = 0.85$.

### Step 1 — Define Individual Block Probabilities

$$\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= 0.95 \\
p_2 &= 0.90 \\
p_3 &= 0.85
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Joint Block Probability

Assuming **independent** conduit defenses:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{block} &= p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \\
&= 0.7268
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Breach Probability

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{breach} &= 1 - P_{block} \\
&= 1 - 0.7268 \\
&= 0.2732
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With **3 independent conduits**, the attacker still has a **27.32%** chance to fully traverse from IT to OT. This proves that **defense-in-depth is probabilistic, not absolute** — hence the need for **continuous monitoring (ICS-IDS)** and **Zero Trust** verification at every layer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Segmentation Technique Comparison

| Attribute | Physical Seg. | VLAN (802.1Q) | Firewall / ACL | SDN Micro-seg. | Data Diode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Isolation Strength** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Cost** | Very High | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Poor |
| **Lateral Movement Block** | Total | High | High | Very High | Total (unidirectional) |
| **Retrofit Friendly** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Limited |
| **ICS/OT Suitability** | SIS, Nuclear | Most OT | Most OT | Industry 4.0 | Critical Infra |
| **KTU Frequency** | Medium | Very High | Very High | Medium | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Purdue Reference Model — Industrial Segmentation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LEVEL 5 ENTERPRISE ZONE Internet Cloud Email External Partners"]
    L4["LEVEL 4 BUSINESS PLANNING ERP MES Corporate Email"]
    IDMZ["LEVEL 3.5 INDUSTRIAL DMZ IDMZ Jump Servers Patch Relay Historian Replica Anti Virus Update Server"]
    L3["LEVEL 3 SITE OPERATIONS HMI SCADA Servers Engineering Workstations"]
    L2["LEVEL 2 AREA SUPERVISORY CONTROL PLC DCS Controllers SCADA Masters RTUs"]
    L1["LEVEL 1 BASIC CONTROL Sensors Actuators Field Devices"]
    L0["LEVEL 0 PHYSICAL PROCESS Manufacturing Plant Motors Valves Pumps"]

    L5 -->|FW1| IDMZ
    L4 -->|FW2| IDMZ
    IDMZ -->|FW3| L3
    IDMZ -->|FW4| L3
    L3 -->|ACL1| L2
    L2 -->|ACL2| L1
    L1 -->|Field Bus| L0

    classDef enterprise fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b02e3a,color:#000
    classDef business fill:#fff1b6,stroke:#a07a00,color:#000
    classDef idmz fill:#d1f0ff,stroke:#005f87,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef ot fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e0e1dd,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class L5 enterprise
    class L4 business
    class IDMZ idmz
    class L3,L2 ot
    class L1,L0 process
```

---

## 4.2 VLAN-Based Logical Segmentation of a Plant Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRUNK["802.1Q TRUNK LINK TAG PROTOCOL 0x8100"]
        direction LR
    end

    SCADA["VLAN 50 SCADA SERVERS 172.20.0.0 /19"]
    HMI["VLAN 60 HMI WORKSTATIONS 172.20.32.0 /19"]
    PLC["VLAN 70 PLC AND DCS ZONE 172.20.64.0 /19"]
    ENG["VLAN 80 ENGINEERING ZONE 172.20.96.0 /19"]
    HIST["VLAN 90 HISTORIAN ZONE 172.20.128.0 /19"]

    SW_CORE["CORE LAYER 3 SWITCH ROUTING FIREWALL ACLS DEFAULT DENY"]

    SCADA --> TRUNK
    HMI --> TRUNK
    PLC --> TRUNK
    ENG --> TRUNK
    HIST --> TRUNK
    TRUNK --> SW_CORE

    EXT["EXTERNAL OT DMZ VLAN 100 172.20.160.0 /19"] -->|Restricted| SW_CORE

    classDef vlan fill:#fefae0,stroke:#606c38,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#caf0f8,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef dmz fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000

    class SCADA,HMI,PLC,ENG,HIST vlan
    class SW_CORE core
    class EXT dmz
```

---

## 4.3 Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) Detailed Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IT["INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY ZONE LEVEL 4 AND 5"]
        IT_USER["Corporate User Workstations"]
        ERP["ERP and MES Servers"]
        MAIL["Email and Web Proxy"]
    end

    subgraph IDMZ_ARCH["INDUSTRIAL DMZ LEVEL 3.5 BUFFER"]
        JUMP["Jump Server Bastion"]
        AV["Anti Virus Patch Relay"]
        HIST_REPL["Historian Replication Server"]
        WSUS["WSUS Patch Server"]
    end

    subgraph OT["OPERATIONAL TECHNOLOGY ZONE LEVEL 0 TO 3"]
        SCADA_OT["SCADA and HMI"]
        PLC_OT["PLC and DCS Controllers"]
        FIELD["Field Sensors and Actuators"]
    end

    IT_USER -->|FW IT to IDMZ| JUMP
    ERP -->|FW IT to IDMZ| JUMP
    MAIL -->|FW IT to IDMZ| JUMP

    JUMP -->|FW IDMZ to OT| SCADA_OT
    AV -->|One Way Patch Push| PLC_OT
    HIST_REPL <-.->|Pull Only from OT| SCADA_OT
    WSUS -->|Approved Patches Only| PLC_OT

    SCADA_OT -->|Field Bus| FIELD

    classDef it fill:#e2ecef,stroke:#355070,color:#000
    classDef idmz fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef ot fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000

    class IT_USER,ERP,MAIL it
    class JUMP,AV,HIST_REPL,WSUS idmz
    class SCADA_OT,PLC_OT,FIELD ot
```

---

## 4.4 Conduit Communication Path — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SRC ZONE SCADA VLAN 50"] -->|1 Originate Packet| B["FIREWALL OT1 PERIMETER DEFAULT DENY"]
    B -->|2 Inspect and Match Policy| C{"3 Is Packet on Allow List"}
    C -->|No| D["4 DROP and Log SIEM Alert"]
    C -->|Yes| E["5 Apply IDS Signature Check"]
    E -->|6 Clean| F["7 NAT and Route to Destination Zone"]
    E -->|8 Malicious| G["9 Quarantine and Send to SOC"]
    F --> H["10 DST ZONE PLC VLAN 70"]
    D --> I["11 Generate SYSLOG for Audit"]
    G --> I

    classDef start fill:#d1f0ff,stroke:#005f87,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff1b6,stroke:#a07a00,color:#000
    classDef drop fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b02e3a,color:#000
    classDef success fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    classDef audit fill:#e0e1dd,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class A start
    class C,E decision
    class D,G drop
    class F,H success
    class I audit
```

---

## 4.5 Segmentation Lifecycle — KTU Board Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["1 ASSET DISCOVERY Identify OT and IT Assets"] --> P2["2 ZONE GROUPING Map Assets to Purdue Levels"]
    P2 --> P3["3 CONDUIT DESIGN Define Communication Paths"]
    P3 --> P4["4 POLICY ENFORCEMENT Firewalls VLANs ACLs"]
    P4 --> P5["5 CONTINUOUS MONITORING ICS IDS SIEM SOC"]
    P5 -->|Feedback Loop| P1

    classDef step fill:#caf0f8,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 step
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Network Segmentation. List any THREE benefits of network segmentation in an Industrial Control System (ICS) environment.**

#### Model Answer:
> **Definition:** Network Segmentation is the architectural process of dividing a larger computer network into smaller, isolated sub-networks (zones) to enforce security boundaries and restrict unauthorized traffic flow.

**Three Benefits:**
1. **Containment of Lateral Movement:** A compromise in one segment (e.g., corporate LAN) cannot freely spread into critical OT zones (e.g., PLC network).
2. **Reduced Attack Surface:** Each segment exposes only the services required by its assets; unused ports and protocols are firewalled.
3. **Improved Performance and Compliance:** Broadcast domains shrink (better latency), and compliance with **IEC 62443 / NIST SP 800-82r3** becomes auditable per zone.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is an Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)? Why is it placed between Level 3 and Level 4 in the Purdue Model?**

#### Model Answer:
> An **Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)** is a **buffer network (Level 3.5)** containing jump servers, patch relays, anti-virus update servers, and historian replicas that sit **between the corporate IT zone (Level 4–5) and the operational OT zone (Level 0–3)**.

**Reasons for placement between Level 3 and Level 4:**
1. **Air-gap with controlled data exchange:** No direct IT↔OT routing exists — all traffic terminates at the IDMZ.
2. **Broker / Replication model:** IT pulls OT data (read-only) and pushes only pre-validated patches.
3. **Mitigates pivot attacks** like TRITON, Industroyer, and Stuxnet by breaking the direct attack path.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulations**, you must answer **ONE question from the internal choice pair** per module. Each question carries **14 marks** split into **sub-part (a) = 7 marks** and **sub-part (b) = 7 marks**.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Purdue Reference Model** in detail with a neat diagram. Show how **network segmentation** is enforced across each level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **VLANs (IEEE 802.1Q)** in industrial network segmentation. With an example, show how an engineer would isolate **SCADA, HMI, and PLC zones** on a single managed switch.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Purdue Reference Model Explanation:**

The **Purdue Model (ISA-95 / IEC 62443)** is a **6-level hierarchical reference architecture** for industrial control systems:

| Level | Name | Typical Assets | Segmentation Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Physical Process | Motors, valves, pumps | Field bus isolation |
| 1 | Basic Control | PLCs, RTUs, sensors | No external connectivity |
| 2 | Supervisory | SCADA masters, DCS | VLAN, ACL |
| 3 | Site Operations | HMI, Historian, EWS | IDMZ boundary |
| 3.5 | **Industrial DMZ** | Jump servers, brokers | **Mandatory buffer** |
| 4 | Business | ERP, MES | Firewalled from IDMZ |
| 5 | Enterprise | Internet, Cloud | Demilitarized |

> The diagram (refer to Section 4.1) shows firewalls between every consecutive level, with the **IDMZ (Level 3.5)** as the strict barrier preventing direct IT↔OT routing.

**(b) VLAN 802.1Q Implementation Example:**

A **Layer-3 managed industrial switch** (e.g., Cisco IE3400, Hirschmann RSP) is configured with:

| VLAN ID | Zone | Subnet | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | SCADA | 172.20.0.0/19 | SCADA Servers |
| 60 | HMI | 172.20.32.0/19 | Operator Workstations |
| 70 | PLC / DCS | 172.20.64.0/19 | Controllers |

- The **trunk port** between access and core switches is tagged with **802.1Q TPID = 0x8100**.
- **ACLs** on the Layer-3 switch enforce: *VLAN 60 (HMI) → VLAN 70 (PLC)* is permitted on **Modbus TCP port 502 only**; all else denied.
- **MTU** is configured as **1522 bytes** to accommodate the 4-byte tag.

This achieves **logical segmentation** over a **single physical switch infrastructure**, dramatically reducing cost while maintaining **strict inter-zone policy**.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)**. List FIVE components placed inside the IDMZ.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Class B network **172.20.0.0/16** must be segmented into **8 industrial zones** with each zone supporting **at least 500 hosts**. Compute the **custom subnet mask**, the **subnet ID for the SCADA zone (1st subnet)**, and the **usable host range**.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) IDMZ Architecture Explanation:**

(Refer to diagram in Section 4.3.)

**Five Components Inside the IDMZ:**
1. **Jump Server / Bastion Host** — single audited entry point for OT access.
2. **Patch Relay / WSUS Server** — staged patching, signed and scanned.
3. **Anti-Virus Update Server** — pulls signatures from IT, pushes to OT one-way.
4. **Historian Replication Server** — read-only OT data replica for IT dashboards.
5. **Reverse Proxy** — terminates vendor remote-access (e.g., TeamViewer, RDP) sessions.

> The IDMZ **never initiates a session toward OT**; it only **responds** to authenticated, session-recorded requests. This unidirectional bias is the core of OT safety.

**(b) Subnetting Solution (Full Derivation):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Original Network | 172.20.0.0 /16 |
| Required Subnets | 8 → need **3 bits borrowed** ($2^3 = 8$) |
| New Prefix | /16 + /3 = **/19** |
| Subnet Mask | **255.255.224.0** |
| Host Bits | 32 − 19 = **13 bits** |
| Usable Hosts | $2^{13} - 2 = 8190 \geq 500$ ✔ |

**SCADA Zone (Subnet #0):**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Network ID | 172.20.0.0 /19 |
| First Usable Host | 172.20.0.1 |
| Last Usable Host | 172.20.31.254 |
| Broadcast | 172.20.31.255 |

The remaining 7 subnets follow with **increments of 32 in the 3rd octet** (e.g., 172.20.32.0, 172.20.64.0 … 172.20.224.0).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing /19 with /24:** A common student error is writing `/24` instead of `/19`, producing only 254 hosts per zone — **insufficient** for 500 hosts → **2 marks lost** in derivation.
> 2. **Forgetting the `-2` in hosts:** Total hosts vs. usable hosts is a classic trap. Always subtract 2 (network + broadcast).
> 3. **Omitting Level 3.5 IDMZ:** Drawing only Levels 0–5 and **missing the IDMZ** in the Purdue diagram costs a full **2-mark diagram penalty**.
> 4. **Wrong VLAN ID range:** Writing `VLAN 4096` or `VLAN 0` — remember valid range is **1 to 4094**.
> 5. **Bidirectional IDMZ assumption:** Stating "IT can push directly to OT via IDMZ" loses marks. IDMZ must enforce **broker/replication semantics**, never direct routing.
> 6. **No mention of IEC 62443:** KTU 2024 explicitly maps COs to **IEC 62443-3-3 SL Targets**. Omitting standard reference = **CO attainment deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist — Network Segmentation (PECCT524 / Module 1)**

- ☐ **Network Segmentation** = partitioning a network into isolated trust zones.
- ☐ Three types: **Physical, Logical (VLAN), Hybrid**.
- ☐ **Purdue Model** has **6 levels (0–5)** plus the **mandatory IDMZ at Level 3.5**.
- ☐ **Level 0** = physical process; **Level 5** = internet/enterprise.
- ☐ **IDMZ** contains jump servers, patch relays, AV update, historian replicas, reverse proxies.
- ☐ **VLAN tagging** uses IEEE **802.1Q** with **TPID = 0x8100**, **4-byte tag**, and **max 4094 VLANs**.
- ☐ New MTU after tagging = **1522 bytes** (1500 + 22-byte header overhead inclusive of 4-byte tag and preamble).
- ☐ **Subnet formula:** $N_s = 2^b$; **Host formula:** $N_h = 2^h - 2$.
- ☐ **IEC 62443-3-3** defines **Security Levels (SL 1 → SL 4)**.
- ☐ **Defense-in-Depth probability** $P_{block} = \prod p_i$ (assume independence).
- ☐ **Data Diodes** = unidirectional physical gateways for high-security OT zones.
- ☐ Real attacks prevented by segmentation: **Stuxnet (2010), TRITON (2017), Industroyer (2016), Colonial Pipeline (2021)**.
- ☐ KTU buzzwords: **Zone & Conduit**, **Defense-in-Depth**, **Zero Trust**, **Air Gap with Brokers**, **Broker-Replication Model**.
- ☐ Always answer with: **Diagram → Table of Zones → 802.1Q Example → Standards Reference**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endpoint Security: Antivirus and Antimalware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Endpoint Security: Antivirus and Antimalware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Endpoint Security** refers to the practice of securing end-user devices such as desktops, laptops, mobile devices, Industrial PCs (IPCs), Engineering Workstations (EWS), Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs), and historians from malicious cyber activity. An **endpoint** is any node on a network that serves as an entry or exit point for data communications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Antivirus (AV):** A software utility deployed on an endpoint that detects, prevents, and removes malicious software, classically focusing on **viruses, worms, and trojans** using signature databases and heuristic engines.
> 
> **Antimalware (AM):** A broader, modern superset of antivirus that protects against the full malware taxonomy: viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, adware, rootkits, bootkits, fileless malware, zero-day exploits, and Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine a large international airport.

- The **passenger** = a file, process, or packet arriving at the endpoint.
- The **immigration counter** = the **signature-based scanner** checking the passport (hash) against a blacklist of known terrorists.
- The **behavioural profiling officer** = the **heuristic/behavioral engine** observing suspicious intent (e.g., a "tourist" trying to access the runway).
- The **X-ray baggage scanner** = the **sandbox/detonation chamber** running suspect items in isolation to see if they explode.
- The **CCTV control room** = **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** continuously monitoring and recording everything.

If any layer flags the passenger, the security team (the AV/AM engine) intercepts, quarantines, or blocks them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In **Industrial Cyber Security**, endpoint security must cover both **IT endpoints** (office PCs) and **OT endpoints** (PLCs, SCADA workstations, Engineering Stations). The same AV/AM agent cannot be blindly installed on legacy OT equipment — vendor validation, ISA/IEC 62443 compliance, and CPU/memory footprint analysis are mandatory.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

The following standard industry metrics are universally applied to quantify the effectiveness of any antivirus/antimalware product:

- **True Positive Rate (TPR)** = Sensitivity / Recall
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = Fall-out
- **F1 Score** = Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** = Average latency between compromise and detection
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** = Average latency between detection and containment
- **Signature Database Size** = Measured in **millions of entries** (modern AV vendors maintain 10M+ signatures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly maps to KTU Course Outcome **CO1** — *“Describe the fundamentals of endpoint security, malware taxonomy, and protection strategies in industrial environments.”*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How an Antivirus / Antimalware Engine Works

The AV/AM engine operates through a layered, sequential decision pipeline. The "Why" and "How" of each step are explained below.

### Step 1 — File/Process Interception (The Hook)
- **Why:** Malware must be intercepted *before* it executes or persists.
- **How:** Three primary hooks are used:
  - **File-system minifilters** (kernel-mode drivers) intercepting I/O requests.
  - **User-mode API hooking** on functions like `CreateFile`, `NtCreateFile`, `WinExec`.
  - **Hypervisor-based introspection** for high-assurance OT endpoints.

### Step 2 — Pre-Execution Scanning (Static Analysis)
- **Why:** Stops known threats at the lowest cost.
- **How:** Compute cryptographic hash (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) and compare against a **signature database**. YARA rules and ClamAV-style pattern signatures are also matched.

### Step 3 — On-Execution Scanning (Dynamic / Heuristic Analysis)
- **Why:** Defeats polymorphic and packed malware.
- **How:** Emulation, opcode frequency analysis, and entropy measurement of PE files detect anomalies.

### Step 4 — Post-Execution Monitoring (Behavioural Analysis)
- **Why:** Catches zero-days and fileless malware.
- **How:** Monitors API calls, registry modifications, process injection, and lateral movement using **ETW (Event Tracing for Windows)** or **eBPF (Linux)**.

### Step 5 — Remediation
- **Why:** Stops the spread of a confirmed infection.
- **How:** Four classical actions: **Clean → Quarantine → Delete → Rollback** (ransomware).

## 2.2 Malware Taxonomy (Essential for KTU)

| Category | Definition | Classical AV Coverage | Modern Antimalware Coverage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Virus** | Self-replicating code attached to a host file | Yes | Yes |
| **Worm** | Self-propagating across networks | Yes | Yes |
| **Trojan** | Disguised malicious payload | Yes | Yes |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts files for extortion | Partial | Yes |
| **Rootkit** | Hides at kernel/firmware level | Limited | Yes (requires kernel driver) |
| **Bootkit** | Infeсtion in MBR/EFI | Limited | Yes |
| **Spyware/Keylogger** | Silent data exfiltration | Partial | Yes |
| **Fileless Malware** | Lives in RAM/PowerShell/WMI | No | Yes (EDR required) |
| **Zero-Day Exploit** | Unknown CVE weaponisation | No | Partial (heuristic) |
| **APT Payload** | Targeted, multi-stage | No | Yes (EDR + Threat Intel) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All detection metrics below are board-exam favourites. Master them in terms of `TP`, `FP`, `TN`, `FN`.

$$
\text{True Positive Rate (TPR)} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} \;=\; \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
\text{Precision} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Recall (TPR)} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{F1 Score} \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}
$$

**Bayesian Posterior Probability of Infection (the foundation of heuristic scoring):**

$$
P(\text{Malware} \mid \text{Detection}) \;=\; \frac{P(\text{Detection} \mid \text{Malware}) \cdot P(\text{Malware})}{P(\text{Detection})}
$$

**Detection Coverage Ratio (used in vendor evaluation):**

$$
\text{Coverage} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of malware samples detected}}{\text{Total malware samples in test corpus}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **OT environments**, MTTD and MTTR are *the* two KPIs mandated by **ISA/IEC 62443-3-3 SL-2** for an Industrial Automation and Control System (IACS). Choosing an AV/AM vendor in a refinery or power plant often hinges on whether its MTTD satisfies the **24-hour detection SLA** for SL-3 systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the F1 Score from Precision and Recall

The F1 score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision (P) and Recall (R). The harmonic mean is chosen (rather than the arithmetic mean) because it **penalises extreme imbalance** between P and R.

**Step 1 — Start with the general definition of the harmonic mean of two numbers $a$ and $b$.**

$$
H(a,b) \;=\; \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $a = P$ and $b = R$.**

$$
H(P,R) \;=\; \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}
$$

**Step 3 — Find a common denominator inside the bracket.**

$$
H(P,R) \;=\; \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert the fraction by multiplication.**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the definitions of P and R in terms of TP, FP, FN.**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 6 — Insert these into the F1 expression and simplify (typical KTU working).**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Notice that the final form depends only on TP, FP, FN. **TN does not appear** — meaning that in skewed populations (e.g., a factory with 99.99% clean files), F1 is a far better metric than Accuracy.

## 3.2 Derivation of Bayesian Detection Threshold

An antimalware heuristic engine assigns a *score* $s$ to a file. A decision threshold $\tau$ classifies the file as **malicious** if $s \geq \tau$.

**Step 1 — State Bayes' Theorem for the posterior probability that a file is malware given the observed score.**

$$
P(M \mid s) \;=\; \frac{P(s \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the evidence $P(s)$ using the Law of Total Probability.**

$$
P(s) \;=\; P(s \mid M) \cdot P(M) + P(s \mid \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M)
$$

**Step 3 — Therefore, the complete posterior becomes the following.**

$$
P(M \mid s) \;=\; \frac{P(s \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(s \mid M) \cdot P(M) + P(s \mid \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M)}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the decision rule.** A file is flagged malicious when the posterior exceeds the threshold.

$$
P(M \mid s) \;\geq\; \tau
$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging gives the minimum likelihood ratio required for a positive detection.**

$$
\frac{P(s \mid M)}{P(s \mid \neg M)} \;\geq\; \frac{\tau}{1 - \tau} \cdot \frac{P(\neg M)}{P(M)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters industrially:** In an OT network, $P(M)$ (the *base rate* of malware) is very low — perhaps 1 in 100,000 packets. This forces the **likelihood ratio to be very large** before an alarm is raised, which is precisely why **false positives are catastrophic** in plants (an AV misidentifying a legitimate PLC firmware update can halt production).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark Style)

A signature engine was tested on 10,000 files. The results are tabulated below.

|  | Actually Malicious | Actually Clean |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Flagged Malicious** | 950 (TP) | 30 (FP) |
| **Flagged Clean** | 50 (FN) | 8,970 (TN) |

**Compute (a) Precision, (b) Recall, (c) F1 Score.**

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Precision:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{950}{950 + 30} \;=\; \frac{950}{980} \;\approx\; 0.9694
$$

**Part (b) — Recall (TPR):**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{950}{950 + 50} \;=\; \frac{950}{1000} \;=\; 0.95
$$

**Part (c) — F1 Score:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 0.9694 \cdot 0.95}{0.9694 + 0.95}
$$

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{1.84186}{1.9194} \;\approx\; 0.9596
$$

> **Interpretation:** The engine has high precision (96.94%) and high recall (95%), giving an excellent F1 score of **0.9596**.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Traditional AV vs Modern EDR/XDR Antimalware

| Feature | Traditional Antivirus | Modern Antimalware / EDR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Detection Method** | Signature-only | Signature + Heuristic + Behavioural + AI/ML |
| **Zero-Day Coverage** | Poor | Moderate to High |
| **Fileless Malware** | Not detected | Detected |
| **Ransomware Rollback** | No | Yes |
| **Centralised Telemetry** | Limited | Full SIEM integration |
| **OT/ICS Suitability** | Risky (kernel driver) | Validated (e.g., ICS-CERT approved) |
| **CPU/Memory Overhead** | Low (1–3%) | Moderate (3–8%) |
| **Cloud Lookups** | Optional | Mandatory for ML models |
| **MTTD** | Hours to days | Seconds to minutes |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate to High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Endpoint Protection Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Endpoint Incoming File or Process Event] --> B[Kernel Minifilter Hook]
    B --> C{Pre Execution Scan}
    C --> D[Static Signature Database Match]
    C --> E[Heuristic and Entropy Engine]
    C --> F[YARA Rule Matcher]
    D --> G{Threat Detected}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Quarantine and Block]
    G -->|No| I[Allow Execution]
    I --> J[Behavioural Monitor ETW or eBPF]
    J --> K{Anomalous API Calls}
    K -->|Yes| L[Sandbox Detonation]
    K -->|No| M[Process Continues Normally]
    L --> N[EDR Telemetry to SOC]
    M --> N
    H --> N
    N --> O[Central SIEM Correlation]
    O --> P[Incident Response Playbook]
```

## 4.2 AV/AM Detection Pipeline Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[File Arrives at Endpoint] --> B[Compute Hash SHA256]
    B --> C{Hash in Blacklist}
    C -->|Yes| Z1[Block Immediately]
    C -->|No| D[Static Analysis YARA and PE Inspection]
    D --> E{Suspicious Structure}
    E -->|Yes| Z2[Quarantine and Sandbox]
    E -->|No| F[Execute in User Space]
    F --> G[Behavioural Monitor]
    G --> H{Policy Violation}
    H -->|Yes| Z3[Kill Process and Rollback]
    H -->|No| Y[Trusted and Active]
    Z1 --> X[Send Telemetry to EDR]
    Z2 --> X
    Z3 --> X
    Y --> X
    X --> END([Continuous Logging to SIEM])
```

## 4.3 Layered Defence Model (Defence in Depth for Endpoints)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1 Network Firewall and IDS] --> L2[Layer 2 Application Allow Listing]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Signature Based AV]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Heuristic and AI Engine]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Behavioural EDR]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Application Sandboxing]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 SOC Analyst and Threat Intel]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Note:** In OT environments, the order of layers is often **inverted** because network firewalls may not exist between zones (e.g., Purdue Levels 0–1). Instead, **allow-listing (Application Control)** is layer 1, and **network segmentation** is layer 2. This is the **Purdue Model + IEC 62443** approach.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** Differentiate between Antivirus and Antimalware. Why is traditional AV insufficient for modern Industrial Control Systems?
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Antivirus (AV)** is a legacy software utility designed to detect and remove classical malware such as **viruses, worms, and trojans**, primarily using **signature-based detection** against a known malware database.
- **Antimalware (AM)** is a modern, broader security solution that protects against the **entire malware spectrum** — including ransomware, rootkits, bootkits, fileless malware, and zero-day exploits — using **signature, heuristic, behavioural, and AI/ML** techniques.
- In ICS/OT environments, traditional AV is insufficient because:
  - It cannot detect **fileless or zero-day** threats targeting HMIs and Engineering Workstations.
  - Signature updates require **internet/cloud access**, which is often **air-gapped** in OT networks.
  - Kernel-level AV drivers may **conflict with real-time SCADA/PLC software**, violating IEC 62443 compatibility requirements.
- Hence, modern industrial endpoints require **allow-listing, EDR, and OT-aware antimalware** validated for the specific ICS vendor.

---

### **Q2.** Define signature-based detection. State two of its major limitations.
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Signature-based detection** is a malware identification technique in which the AV/AM engine computes a **unique hash or pattern (signature)** of a file and compares it against a database of **known malicious signatures**.
- **Two major limitations:**
  1. **Zero-Day Blindness:** It cannot detect malware that has never been seen before, as no signature exists in the database.
  2. **Evasion via Polymorphism/Metamorphism:** Modern malware **encrypts, packs, or rewrites** its code with each infection, changing its hash and bypassing signature matching.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the different types of malware with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** Describe the architecture of a modern endpoint antimalware solution with a neat block diagram. Discuss its working in the context of an Industrial Control System. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

Malware is any software intentionally designed to cause damage to, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system. The major types are:

1. **Virus** — Attaches itself to a legitimate program; spreads only when the host is executed. *Example: ILOVEYOU, Melissa.*
2. **Worm** — Self-propagates across networks without user intervention. *Example: Conficker, Stuxnet (worm component).*
3. **Trojan Horse** — Disguised as legitimate software; creates backdoors. *Example: Emotet, ZeuS.*
4. **Ransomware** — Encrypts user data and demands payment. *Example: WannaCry, LockBit, BlackCat.*
5. **Rootkit** — Hides malicious processes at the kernel or firmware level. *Example: TDSS, Alureon.*
6. **Spyware / Keylogger** — Silently exfiltrates keystrokes and data. *Example: DarkHotel, FinFisher.*
7. **Fileless Malware** — Resides only in memory (RAM, WMI, PowerShell). *Example: PowerShower, Cobalt Strike beacons.*
8. **Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)** — Multi-stage, targeted, long-duration campaigns. *Example: Stuxnet, APT29 (Cozy Bear).*

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

A modern endpoint antimalware solution (often branded as **EDR** or **XDR**) follows a layered architecture:

1. **Sensor Layer** — Kernel minifilters, ETW providers, and eBPF probes intercept file/process events.
2. **Detection Layer** — Combines **signature matching**, **heuristic analysis**, **ML classifiers**, and **behavioural rules**.
3. **Sandbox / Detonation Layer** — Suspicious payloads are executed in an isolated VM for observation.
4. **Response Layer** — Automated actions: quarantine, kill process, rollback, network isolation.
5. **Telemetry Layer** — All events are forwarded via encrypted channels to a **central SIEM / SOC**.

**Working in an ICS Context:**
In a refinery, for example, an Engineering Workstation receiving a firmware update is intercepted by the sensor. The static engine checks signatures against a local, vendor-validated database (no cloud lookup — to maintain air-gap integrity). The behavioural engine monitors the firmware's installer for any attempt to modify PLC communication libraries (Modbus/TCP, DNP3), which is anomalous. If detected, the workstation is **automatically isolated at the switch port (ACL enforced)** and a SOC alert is raised. This satisfies **ISA/IEC 62443-3-3 SL-2** requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Workstation EWS] --> B[EDR Sensor Kernel Driver]
    B --> C[Local Signature Engine]
    B --> D[Behavioural AI Engine]
    C --> E{Match Found}
    D --> E
    E -->|Yes| F[Quarantine File and Isolate Port]
    E -->|No| G[Allow Execution and Log to SOC]
    F --> H[SIEM Correlation and SOC Alert]
    G --> H
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the working of a signature-based and heuristic-based malware detection engine. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** An AV engine was tested on 50,000 files. Out of 1,200 actual malware files, it correctly detected 1,080. It raised 60 false alarms. Compute the Precision, Recall, and F1 score of the engine. Comment on the result. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Signature-Based Engine Flow:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming File] --> B[Compute SHA256 Hash]
    B --> C{Hash in Database}
    C -->|Yes| D[Flag as Malicious]
    C -->|No| E[Mark as Clean]
```

**Heuristic-Based Engine Flow:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming File] --> B[Disassemble and Inspect PE Structure]
    B --> C[Extract Features Opcode Frequency Entropy Packing]
    C --> D[Apply Rule Based Score]
    D --> E{Score Exceeds Threshold}
    E -->|Yes| F[Flag as Suspicious]
    E -->|No| G[Mark as Clean]
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Heuristic-Based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (analysis overhead) |
| **Zero-Day Detection** | None | Moderate |
| **False Positives** | Very low | Higher |
| **Database Dependency** | Yes (must be updated) | No |
| **Polymorphic Malware** | Bypassed | Detected |
| **OT/ICS Suitability** | Excellent (low overhead) | Good (validated signatures only) |

**Conclusion:** In practice, modern engines use a **hybrid approach**, combining the speed of signatures with the adaptability of heuristics.

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Total files = 50,000
- Actual malware = 1,200 → Therefore, clean files = 48,800
- True Positives (TP) = 1,080
- False Positives (FP) = 60
- False Negatives (FN) = Actual malware − TP = 1,200 − 1,080 = **120**
- True Negatives (TN) = Clean − FP = 48,800 − 60 = **48,740**

**Computation:**

**Precision:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{1080}{1080 + 60} \;=\; \frac{1080}{1140} \;\approx\; 0.9474
$$

**Recall:**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{1080}{1080 + 120} \;=\; \frac{1080}{1200} \;=\; 0.9000
$$

**F1 Score:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 0.9474 \cdot 0.9}{0.9474 + 0.9}
$$

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{1.70532}{1.8474} \;\approx\; 0.9230
$$

**Comment:** The engine demonstrates **high precision (94.74%)**, meaning that when it flags a file, it is almost always correct — critical in OT environments. However, **recall of 90%** indicates that 10% of actual malware escaped detection, which is **unacceptable for SL-3 systems**; additional behavioural/heuristic layers are recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse TP, FP, TN, FN** — board examiners deduct **2 full marks** instantly for this mix-up. Memorise the matrix: Positive in the row = "what the engine said"; Positive in the column = "what the truth is".
> 2. **F1 score formula must be written in one step** — do not split it into P × R separately. Use the final form $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ if the question gives raw counts.
> 3. **Always include units in the interpretation** — F1 is a *dimensionless* score between 0 and 1.
> 4. **OT context is mandatory** — a generic answer on AV without referencing ICS, SCADA, or IEC 62443 will lose **2–3 marks** under KTU 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Diagrams must be neat and labelled** — a flowchart with unlabelled boxes gets zero credit for the diagram component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Antivirus (AV)** → Legacy, signature-centric, limited to classical malware.
- **Antimalware (AM)** → Modern, multi-layered (signature + heuristic + behavioural + AI/ML).
- **Endpoint Security** → Secures laptops, IPCs, EWS, HMIs, PLCs (in OT context).
- **Three Detection Pillars:** Signature, Heuristic, Behavioural — combined in modern EDR.
- **Four Responses:** Clean, Quarantine, Delete, Rollback.
- **Five Key Metrics:** TPR, FPR, Precision, Recall, F1 Score.
- **Bayesian Threshold:** High base-rate imbalance in OT forces very high likelihood ratios to avoid false positives.
- **Industrial Standard:** **ISA/IEC 62443-3-3** mandates MTTD/MTTR SLAs.
- **Stuxnet (2010)** is the canonical example of OT-targeted malware — know its worm + rootkit + PLC payload structure.
- **Air-gapped OT networks** still get infected via **removable media (USB)** — endpoint AV/AM with **device control** is critical.
- **Always validate AV/AM vendor compatibility** with the SCADA/HMI vendor (Siemens, ABB, Schneider, Honeywell) before deployment.
- **Allow-listing (Application Control)** is the **gold standard** for legacy OT endpoints that cannot tolerate AV overhead.
- **EDR ≠ AV** — EDR is a *response-capable evolution* of AV, including telemetry, threat hunting, and rollback.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** is a category of integrated, continuous-monitoring endpoint security solutions that record, store, and analyse endpoint system-level behaviours; use advanced analytics to detect suspicious activity; provide visibility into endpoint events; and deliver automated or analyst-driven response capabilities to contain, investigate, and remediate cyber threats.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT524 – Advanced Industrial Cyber Security), an **endpoint** is defined as any physical or virtual device that serves as a network entry point or data-processing node. In an **Industrial Control System (ICS)** / **Operational Technology (OT)** context, endpoints are extended to include:
- Engineering Workstations (EWS)
- Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs)
- Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)
- Remote Terminal Units (RTUs)
- Historians and SCADA Servers
- Industrial IoT (IIoT) sensors and edge gateways

**EDR systems** are formally classified by Gartner as a sub-domain under **Endpoint Security**, distinct from traditional **Endpoint Protection Platforms (EPP)**. EDR solutions focus on **detection**, **investigation**, and **response** after an event occurs, whereas EPP solutions focus on **prevention** through known-signature blocking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Bank Vault Alarm System:**
> Imagine a bank vault equipped with a CCTV camera, a motion sensor, a glass-break detector, and an automatic lockdown door. The thick steel door (this is your **Firewall/Antivirus/EPP**) prevents most known robbers from entering. However, a *clever* robber who somehow gets in (e.g., via an insider or zero-day exploit) bypasses the door. The CCTV records every movement inside, the motion sensor detects unusual patterns (a person crawling when staff would walk), and the glass-break detector catches the robber trying to escape. The lockdown door then **automatically seals the vault** to contain the threat.
>
> The EDR is exactly this "inside-the-vault" surveillance and emergency response system for your digital endpoints. It does not necessarily stop the first breach, but it **sees, records, decides, and acts** — at machine speed — to limit damage.

**Key Insight:** EDR = Continuous Visibility + Behavioural Analytics + Forensic Telemetry + Automated Response. It is the **"Black Box Flight Recorder + Intelligent Security Guard"** for every endpoint.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following industry-standard metrics quantify EDR performance and are critical for KTU exam answers:

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time between attack initiation and detection. Industry benchmark: **< 1 hour** for mature EDR deployments.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time between detection and containment. Industry benchmark: **< 15 minutes** for automated response.
- **Dwell Time:** Total duration an attacker remains undetected inside the environment.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Ratio of benign events misclassified as malicious. Target: **< 5%**.
- **Endpoint Coverage Ratio:** Percentage of total endpoints with the EDR agent installed and reporting telemetry.
- **MITRE ATT&CK Coverage:** Number of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) the EDR can detect.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Whenever a question asks "What is EDR?" always close your answer with a line on **Continuous Monitoring + Behavioural Analytics + Automated Response**. Examiners reward these three pillars as a complete definition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of an EDR System

A production-grade EDR platform (e.g., CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne Singularity, Trend Micro Vision One) is built on **four functional pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Endpoint Sensor / Agent (Data Collection Layer)
- Lightweight software installed on every endpoint (kernel-level or user-space).
- Hooks into the OS via:
  - **System Call Interception** (e.g., `syscall` hooks on Linux, `Nt*` APIs on Windows).
  - **File System Minifilters** (Windows) / **eBPF probes** (Linux).
  - **Network driver filters** (TDI, NDIS, WFP).
  - **Process injection monitors** (LSASS, hollow processes).
- Collects **telemetry events** such as:
  - Process creation (`Sysmon Event ID 1`)
  - Image/DLL loads (`Event ID 7`)
  - Network connections (`Event ID 3`)
  - Registry modifications (`Event ID 13`)
  - PowerShell / WMI activity (`Event ID 4104`)

### Pillar 2: Ingestion & Normalization Layer (Data Pipeline)
- Receives telemetry via secure channels (TLS 1.3, mutual mTLS).
- **Data Lake Storage:** Stores raw + enriched events (typical retention: **30–365 days**).
- **Normalization:** Converts heterogeneous OS-specific events into a common schema (e.g., **Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework – OCSF**, or **ECS – Elastic Common Schema**).

### Pillar 3: Detection & Analytics Engine (Brain)
- Operates on a **multi-layered detection model**:
  1. **Signature-based Detection (IoC Matching):** Hashes, IP addresses, domain names, YARA rules.
  2. **Behavioural Detection (Rule-based):** MITRE ATT&CK mapped rules (e.g., "T1059.001 – PowerShell execution from Office macro").
  3. **Anomaly Detection (Statistical / ML):** Autoencoders, Isolation Forests, LSTM networks on process behaviour.
  4. **Threat Intelligence Correlation:** STIX/TAXII feeds, reputation scoring.
  5. **Sandboxing / Dynamic Analysis:** Detonates suspicious files in an isolated VM and observes behaviour.

### Pillar 4: Response & Orchestration Layer
- **Automated Response Playbooks:** SOAR-integrated actions.
- **Analyst Console:** Web UI for triage, hunting, and case management.
- **Response Actions:**
  - **Isolate Host** (network quarantine via firewall rule push).
  - **Kill Process / Suspend Thread.**
  - **Quarantine File / Rollback Registry.**
  - **Memory Dump Acquisition** (forensic snapshot).
  - **Remote Shell** for live investigation.
  - **EDR API-driven** containment in multi-vendor environments.

## 2.2 The EDR Detection Pipeline – Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Hook Installation:** Agent inserts non-bypassable callbacks into OS subsystems.
2. **Event Capture:** Every relevant syscall, file write, network packet, and DLL load is recorded with **high-resolution timestamps** (microsecond precision).
3. **Local Pre-Filtering:** To reduce bandwidth, low-value events (e.g., known-good software updater) are filtered using a local allow-list.
4. **Encrypted Upload:** Compressed and signed events are batched and uploaded over HTTPS.
5. **Backend Enrichment:** Events are joined with threat intel, asset context, identity data (LDAP/Azure AD), and vulnerability data (CVE scores).
6. **Detection Engine Evaluation:** Each event stream is evaluated by hundreds of detection rules in real time.
7. **Risk Scoring:** A composite risk score (0–100) is assigned per endpoint and per process tree.
8. **Alert Generation:** High-confidence malicious events trigger alerts with full **kill-chain reconstruction** (mapped to MITRE ATT&CK).
9. **Response Trigger:** Predefined playbooks execute response actions via API.
10. **Analyst Feedback Loop:** Confirmed true/false positives feed back into the ML model for continuous retraining.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every quantitative metric, formula, and benchmark relevant to EDR in the KTU 2024 PECCT524 syllabus.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Practical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{detect,i} - T_{attack,i})$ | minutes / hours | **1 min – 24 hr** |
| **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{respond,i} - T_{detect,i})$ | seconds / minutes | **10 s – 1 hr** |
| **Dwell Time** | $Dwell = T_{detect} - T_{compromise}$ | days | **0 – 100+ days** |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ratio (0–1) | **0.001 – 0.05** |
| **True Positive Rate / Recall** | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | ratio (0–1) | **0.90 – 0.99** |
| **Precision** | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | ratio (0–1) | **0.85 – 0.99** |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | ratio (0–1) | **0.88 – 0.98** |
| **Endpoint Coverage Ratio** | $Coverage = \frac{N_{agent-installed}}{N_{total-endpoints}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | **≥ 95 %** |
| **Alert Noise Ratio** | $ANR = \frac{N_{alerts-per-day}}{N_{endpoints}}$ | alerts/endpoint/day | **< 5** |
| **Sigma Threshold (Anomaly)** | $\sigma_{thresh} = \mu + k \cdot \sigma$ where $k \in [2, 4]$ | standard deviations | $k = 3$ standard |
| **Shannon Entropy (File Entropy)** | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/byte | **0 – 8** (packed/encrypted malware typically **> 7.0**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern **Industrial Cyber Security Operations Centers (ICSOCs)**, these metrics form the backbone of **SLAs (Service Level Agreements)** between the security team and plant management. For example, a refinery might contractually demand an **MTTD < 5 minutes** for any process-control anomaly to prevent physical damage.

## 2.4 EDR vs. Related Security Paradigms

| Feature | EPP (Endpoint Protection Platform) | EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) | XDR (Extended Detection & Response) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Prevention (block known malware) | Detection + Response (post-breach) | Cross-domain correlation (Endpoint + Network + Cloud + Identity) |
| **Detection Method** | Signature, hash, reputation | Behavioural, ML, anomaly, threat intel | Multi-layer telemetry correlation |
| **Data Source** | Local endpoint only | Endpoint + cloud analytics | Endpoint + Network + Cloud + Email + Identity |
| **Response** | Quarantine file, kill process | Isolate host, forensic dump, rollback | Full-stack automated remediation across domains |
| **Storage** | Minimal (logs only) | Days to months of telemetry | Months to years (data lake) |
| **KTU Emphasis** | Foundational | **Core Module 1 topic** | Module 5 / Elective extension |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Core EDR Metrics

The KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education) requires students to **derive and interpret** quantitative performance metrics. Below are exhaustive derivations of the most important EDR KPIs.

---

### Derivation 1: Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) in an EDR Pipeline

**Context:** An EDR system has been deployed across $N$ incidents. For each incident $i$, the attack began at time $T_{attack,i}$ and was detected at time $T_{detect,i}$. We wish to find the average detection latency.

**Step 1 – Define individual detection latency:**

$$\Delta t_i = T_{detect,i} - T_{attack,i}$$

where $\Delta t_i \geq 0$ because detection cannot occur before the attack (physically impossible for any deterministic system).

**Step 2 – Sum all detection latencies:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta t_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{detect,i} - T_{attack,i}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{detect,i} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{attack,i}$$

**Step 3 – Divide by number of incidents to obtain the arithmetic mean:**

$$MTTD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta t_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{detect,i} - T_{attack,i})$$

**Step 4 – Expand to express in terms of total elapsed time across all incidents:**

$$MTTD = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{detect,i}}{N} - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{attack,i}}{N} = \overline{T}_{detect} - \overline{T}_{attack}$$

This is simply the difference between the average detection time and the average attack time, which is intuitively the average detection delay.

**Step 5 – Special Case: If the EDR operates in real-time with constant event-processing delay $\tau$:**

$$MTTD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau = \tau$$

A perfectly tuned EDR has $MTTD = \tau$, where $\tau$ is dominated by the **event-upload interval + rule-evaluation time**.

**Practical Interpretation:** An industrial plant with $MTTD = 1$ hour risks catastrophic damage if a ransomware outbreak begins encrypting PLC ladder-logic files. The target is to push $MTTD$ below the **time-to-physical-impact**.

---

### Derivation 2: Anomaly Detection Threshold (Sigma Rule)

**Context:** An EDR collects a baseline of process-CPU-usage samples for an engineering workstation. The samples follow an approximately normal distribution. We wish to derive a threshold beyond which a process is flagged as anomalous.

**Step 1 – Model CPU usage $X$ as a Gaussian random variable:**

$$X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

where $\mu$ is the historical mean and $\sigma$ is the historical standard deviation, both estimated from the baseline window (typically 14–30 days).

**Step 2 – Define the standardised Z-score for a new observation $x$:**

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 3 – For a chosen confidence level, set the threshold:**

$$x_{thresh} = \mu + k \cdot \sigma$$

Any observation $x \geq x_{thresh}$ triggers an alert. By the empirical rule, the probability of false alarm is:

$$P(FA) = 1 - \Phi(k)$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF.

**Step 4 – Numerical Example for $k = 3$:**

$$P(FA) = 1 - \Phi(3) = 1 - 0.99865 = 0.00135$$

This is the **three-sigma rule** widely used in EDR analytics. For each 1,000 baseline samples, only $\approx 1.35$ exceed the threshold — a very low false-positive rate.

**Step 5 – For an industrial HMI that should never exceed 20% CPU at idle, but historical baseline gives $\mu = 12\%, \sigma = 2\%$:**

$$x_{thresh} = 12 + 3 \cdot 2 = 18\%$$

A jump to **45%** CPU (a Z-score of $16.5\sigma$) is unmistakably malicious — likely crypto-mining or ransomware.

**Practical Interpretation:** The sigma-threshold balances **detection sensitivity** vs **analyst fatigue** (alerts per day). KTU board answers should explicitly state this trade-off.

---

### Derivation 3: File-Entropy-Based Packed Malware Detection

**Context:** The EDR intercepts a suspicious file dropped on an HMI. It computes the file's byte-level entropy to decide whether the file is likely packed/encrypted (a hallmark of modern malware).

**Step 1 – Compute the byte frequency distribution $p_i$:**

Let $n$ = number of distinct byte values present (0–255). Let $f_i$ be the frequency of byte value $i$ in the file, with total file size $S = \sum f_i$. Then:

$$p_i = \frac{f_i}{S}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1$$

**Step 2 – Apply Shannon's entropy formula:**

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

Units: **bits per byte**, range $[0, 8]$.

**Step 3 – Interpret boundary cases:**

- **$H = 0$:** All bytes identical (e.g., a file of all `0x00`).
- **$H = 8$:** Perfect uniform distribution — characteristic of encrypted or compressed data.
- **$H \in [0, 5]$:** Normal PE executables with mostly ASCII/structured code.
- **$H \in [6.5, 8]$:** Likely packed, encrypted, or compressed — high malware probability.

**Step 4 – Worked numerical example (Hypothetical 8-byte file `0x48 0x65 0x6C 0x6C 0x6F 0x21 0x21 0x21`):**

Byte frequencies:

- $0x48$: 1 occurrence
- $0x65$: 1
- $0x6C$: 2
- $0x6F$: 1
- $0x21$: 3

Total $S = 8$ bytes, $n = 5$ distinct values.

Proportions:
- $p_1 = 1/8 = 0.125$
- $p_2 = 1/8 = 0.125$
- $p_3 = 2/8 = 0.250$
- $p_4 = 1/8 = 0.125$
- $p_5 = 3/8 = 0.375$

Compute entropy:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= -\left[ (0.125)\log_2(0.125) + (0.125)\log_2(0.125) + (0.250)\log_2(0.250) \right. \\
& \quad \left. + (0.125)\log_2(0.125) + (0.375)\log_2(0.375) \right] \\
&= -\left[ (0.125)(-3) + (0.125)(-3) + (0.250)(-2) + (0.125)(-3) + (0.375)(-1.4150) \right] \\
&= -\left[ -0.375 - 0.375 - 0.500 - 0.375 - 0.5306 \right] \\
&= 2.1556 \text{ bits/byte}
\end{aligned}$$

This is well below the 6.5 threshold, so the file is **likely benign** (it is the string "Hello!!!" in ASCII).

**Step 5 – Now apply to a real malware sample of 1 MB of pseudo-random data with uniform distribution:**

If $p_i = 1/256$ for all $i$:

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{256} \frac{1}{256} \log_2 \left( \frac{1}{256} \right) = -256 \cdot \frac{1}{256} \cdot \log_2 \left( 2^{-8} \right) = 8 \text{ bits/byte}$$

The EDR flags this as **highly suspicious** and proceeds with sandbox detonation.

**Practical Interpretation:** Entropy is a **fast, signature-less pre-filter** that the EDR applies to every new file at the endpoint with negligible performance overhead. KTU students should remember the threshold: **$H > 7.0$** is the practical red line for packed/encrypted executables.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: EDR vs EPP vs XDR vs SIEM vs SOAR

| Capability | EPP | **EDR** | XDR | SIEM | SOAR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Signature-based prevention | ✅ | ⚠️ (Secondary) | ⚠️ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Behavioural detection | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ | ❌ |
| Endpoint telemetry storage | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ (via agent) | ❌ |
| Cross-domain correlation | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| Forensic timeline (kill-chain) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Automated response actions | ⚠️ (File only) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Native threat hunting UI | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| MITRE ATT&CK mapping | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| OT/ICS Protocol Awareness (Modbus, DNP3) | ❌ | ⚠️ (Industrial EDR only) | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ❌ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: EDR Architecture – Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the high-level data and control flow inside a typical enterprise/industrial EDR deployment.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Endpoint Agent Sensor] -->|Telemetry Stream| A2[Local Pre-Filter and Allow-List]
    A2 -->|Filtered Events| A3[Encrypted Upload via TLS 1.3]
    A3 --> B1[Cloud Ingestion Gateway]
    B1 --> B2[Data Lake Normalization to OCSF]
```
