```mermaid
    WAF -->|Allowed Request| LB[Load Balancer]
    WAF -->|Blocked 403| Logger[SIEM and Log Aggregator]
    LB --> AppServer1[Web Application Server 1]
    LB --> AppServer2[Web Application Server 2]
    AppServer1 --> DB[(Database Tier)]
    AppServer2 --> DB
    Logger --> SOC[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
    WAF -. Logs .-> Logger
    DDoS -. Logs .-> Logger
    EdgeFW -. Logs .-> Logger
    style Internet fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style WAF fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style DB fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style SOC fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

**Reading guide:** The blue WAF block sits in the *critical path* between traffic sanitization (DDoS scrubber) and the load balancer. Every request must pass through the WAF. The WAF and adjacent devices all forward logs to the SOC for correlation.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — WAF Rule Evaluation Pipeline (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming HTTP Request] --> B[TLS Termination]
    B --> C[Protocol Normalization and Decoding]
    C --> D{Request Parsing}
    D --> E[Header Inspector]
    D --> F[URL and Path Analyzer]
    D --> G[Body and Payload Scanner]
    E --> H[Rule Engine Core]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Signature DB Match}
    I -->|Yes| J[Anomaly Scoring Module]
    I -->|No| J
    J --> K{State and Session Tracker}
    K --> L[Policy Decision Point]
    L -->|Allow| M[Forward to Backend]
    L -->|Block| N[Return 403 to Client]
    L -->|Challenge| O[Issue CAPTCHA or JS Test]
    L -->|Log| P[Write to SIEM]
    style H fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600
    style L fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style O fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style N fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Reading guide:** The orange Rule Engine Core is the heart. All parsers feed into it. The blue Policy Decision Point is the final arbiter that maps engine output to one of four actions. The green Challenge branch implements bot mitigation.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — WAF Detection Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Request Arrives at WAF] --> Check1{Blacklist Signature Match}
    Check1 -->|Match Hit| Sev1[High Severity Score]
    Check1 -->|No Match| Check2{Whitelist Anomaly Detected}
    Sev1 --> Action1[Block or Challenge]
    Check2 -->|Anomaly Detected| Sev2[Medium Severity Score]
    Check2 -->|Baseline OK| Check3{Stateful Behavior Anomaly}
    Sev2 --> Action2[Log and Rate Limit]
    Check3 -->|Suspicious| Sev3[Low Severity Score]
    Check3 -->|Normal| Allow[Allow Request]
    Sev3 --> Action3[Log Only]
    style Sev1 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff
    style Sev2 fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600
    style Sev3 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366
    style Allow fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

**Reading guide:** The flowchart implements a *cascading evaluation* — the request is checked against the cheapest filter first (blacklist), then the more expensive ones (anomaly, stateful). This minimizes latency for legitimate traffic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q: Define Web Application Firewall (WAF). Mention any two attacks that a WAF can mitigate which a traditional network firewall cannot.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Web Application Firewall (WAF) is a Layer 7 security control that monitors, filters, and blocks HTTP/HTTPS traffic between a web application and the Internet by inspecting the full request and response payload. *(1 mark)*
>
> Unlike a network firewall that operates at OSI Layers 3–4 and inspects only IP addresses and ports, a WAF understands HTTP semantics and can therefore detect application-layer attacks. *(1 mark)*
>
> Two such attacks are: **(i) SQL Injection** — where malicious SQL code is embedded in form fields, detectable via regex patterns like `union\s+select`; and **(ii) Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — where malicious JavaScript is injected into response pages, detectable via patterns like `<script>` or `onerror=`. *(1 mark)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q: Differentiate between positive (whitelist) and negative (blacklist) security models used in a WAF.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Negative model (blacklist):** The WAF is pre-loaded with a database of *known-bad* patterns (signatures). Any request matching a signature is blocked. It is effective against known attacks but offers no protection against zero-day threats. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Positive model (whitelist):** The WAF is configured with a profile of *known-good* behavior — accepted URLs, parameter types, lengths, and character sets. Any deviation from this baseline is flagged. It is effective against zero-day attacks but requires an initial learning phase and is prone to false positives until tuned. *(1.5 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the architecture and operational modes of a Web Application Firewall. Compare the three deployment models: **Network-based**, **Host-based**, and **Cloud-based**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the WAF rule evaluation pipeline. How does a WAF decide between *allow, block, challenge,* and *log* actions? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for A(a)

A Web Application Firewall (WAF) is a Layer 7 security appliance or software that sits between a web client and a web server, intercepting HTTP/HTTPS traffic and applying a policy of inspection, filtering, and enforcement. *(1 mark)*

**Architectural components of a WAF:** *(3 marks)*

1. **TLS/SSL Inspection Module** — Terminates the encrypted session so the WAF can inspect the plaintext payload. In bridged mode, the WAF may use the upstream device to terminate TLS.
2. **Protocol Normalizer** — Decodes percent-encoding, multi-byte characters, and chunked transfer encodings so attackers cannot bypass rules via obfuscation (e.g., `%73elect` instead of `select`).
3. **Request Parser** — Decomposes the HTTP request into its constituent parts: request line, headers, query string, body, cookies, and multipart boundaries.
4. **Rule Engine** — The core of the WAF; it applies a stack of rules (signature, anomaly, behavioral) to the parsed request.
5. **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** — Aggregates rule outputs and maps them to actions.
6. **Logging and Forwarding Module** — Sends alerts and audit logs to a SIEM.

**Comparison of deployment models:** *(3 marks)*

| Parameter | Network-based WAF | Host-based WAF | Cloud-based WAF |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Form Factor** | Hardware appliance | Software module on web server | SaaS / virtual appliance |
| **Latency** | < 5 ms | 0–2 ms (in-process) | 5–20 ms (geographic) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by appliance | Scales with servers | **Elastic (auto-scales)** |
| **TLS Termination** | Native | Depends on server | Native (CDN integrated) |
| **Examples** | F5 ASM, Imperva WAF | ModSecurity, NAXSI | AWS WAF, Cloudflare WAF, Azure WAF |
| **Best Use Case** | Large enterprise, low latency | Single server, low cost | **Multi-cloud, DDoS-resilient** |

**Operational modes:** A network-based WAF can be deployed as a **reverse proxy** (preferred for centralized inspection), a **transparent bridge** (transparent L2, no IP changes), or a **transparent forward proxy**. *(inline at end of solution)*

#### Model Solution for A(b)

The WAF rule evaluation pipeline is shown in the diagram below. *(3 marks for diagram and pipeline explanation)*

**Pipeline steps:**

1. **TLS Termination** — Decrypts the incoming HTTPS request.
2. **Protocol Normalization** — Decodes percent-encoding, unicode escapes, and chunked bodies.
3. **Request Parsing** — Splits the request into headers, URL, query string, body, and cookies.
4. **Header Inspection** — Checks for missing or malformed security headers (e.g., `Host` header injection, `X-Forwarded-For` spoofing).
5. **URL and Path Analysis** — Scans the URL for path traversal, RFI patterns, and suspicious file extensions.
6. **Body and Payload Scanning** — Inspects form data, JSON, XML for SQLi, XSS, XXE patterns.
7. **Stateful Session Tracking** — Validates CSRF tokens, session cookies, and request sequences.
8. **Rule Engine** — Aggregates findings and computes a cumulative severity score $S$.
9. **Policy Decision Point** — Maps $S$ to an action.

**Decision logic for actions:** *(4 marks)*

$$
\text{Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Block (HTTP 403)} & \text{if } S \geq 8 \\
\text{Challenge (CAPTCHA or JS test)} & \text{if } 4 \leq S < 8 \\
\text{Log only (forward to backend)} & \text{if } 1 \leq S < 4 \\
\text{Allow} & \text{if } S = 0
\end{cases}
$$

A **high score** indicates a confident match of multiple attack indicators. A **medium score** triggers a challenge to verify the client is human. A **low score** is suspicious but not conclusive — the request is forwarded, but an alert is raised. A **zero score** indicates a clean request. This tiered model balances security with user experience, since an aggressive block policy on every anomaly would produce unacceptably high false-positive rates.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities** and explain how a WAF provides a *virtual patch* against each. Why is a WAF considered a *compensating control* rather than a complete solution? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A WAF is deployed for 30 days and the following metrics are observed: $T_p = 9{,}000$, $F_p = 500$, $T_n = 2{,}988{,}000$, $F_n = 2{,}500$. Compute the **Detection Rate**, **False Positive Rate**, **Precision**, and **F1-Score**. Comment on the operational health of this WAF. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for B(a)

The **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** represents the ten most critical web application security risks. A WAF provides a **virtual patch** — a rule-based mitigation that protects vulnerable code without modifying the source. *(1 mark)*

**Coverage table:** *(4 marks)*

| OWASP ID | Vulnerability | WAF Virtual Patch |
|---|---|---|
| A01 | Broken Access Control | URL-based access enforcement; deny direct access to admin paths |
| A02 | Cryptographic Failures | Force HTTPS; inject HSTS, CSP headers |
| A03 | Injection (SQLi, XSS) | Regex and parser-based rules; input sanitization |
| A04 | Insecure Design | Block requests violating business rules (e.g., negative quantity) |
| A05 | Security Misconfiguration | Strip `Server`, `X-Powered-By` headers; enforce cookies as `HttpOnly` and `Secure` |
| A06 | Vulnerable Components | Match against CVE signatures in HTTP responses (limited) |
| A07 | Auth Failures | Rate-limit /login; enforce strong cookie flags |
| A08 | Data Integrity Failures | SRI enforcement on linked JS/CSS |
| A09 | Logging Failures | Forward WAF logs to SIEM (compensates for app's poor logging) |
| A10 | SSRF | Egress filtering; block requests to private IP ranges |

**Why a WAF is a compensating control, not a complete solution:** *(2 marks)*

1. **A WAF cannot fix the underlying vulnerability** — the SQLi flaw in the code still exists. An attacker who finds an *unseen* channel (e.g., a mobile API not routed through the WAF) bypasses the protection entirely.
2. **Signature evasion** — sophisticated attackers use encoding, polymorphism, and time-based blind SQLi to evade WAF rules.
3. **Encrypted payloads** — if the WAF cannot terminate TLS, it cannot inspect the body.
4. **Business logic flaws** — A WAF cannot reason about whether a $0.01 price in a checkout API is a manipulation attempt.
5. **Insider threats** — A WAF is a perimeter control; it cannot stop authenticated abuse.

**Conclusion:** A WAF is a critical layer in defense-in-depth, but it **must be combined with secure coding (SDLC), input validation, parameterized queries, and regular penetration testing**.

#### Model Solution for B(b)

**Given:** $T_p = 9{,}000$, $F_p = 500$, $T_n = 2{,}988{,}000$, $F_n = 2{,}500$. Total = 3,000,000. *(verification step)*

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (Recall):** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DR} &= \frac{T_p}{T_p + F_n} = \frac{9{,}000}{9{,}000 + 2{,}500} = \frac{9{,}000}{11{,}500} \\
\text{DR} &= 0.7826 \approx \mathbf{78.26\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate:** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FPR} &= \frac{F_p}{F_p + T_n} = \frac{500}{500 + 2{,}988{,}000} = \frac{500}{2{,}988{,}500} \\
\text{FPR} &= 0.000167 \approx \mathbf{0.017\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Precision:** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre} &= \frac{T_p}{T_p + F_p} = \frac{9{,}000}{9{,}000 + 500} = \frac{9{,}000}{9{,}500} \\
\text{Pre} &= 0.9474 \approx \mathbf{94.74\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Pre} \cdot \text{DR}}{\text{Pre} + \text{DR}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9474 \cdot 0.7826}{0.9474 + 0.7826} \\
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.7415}{1.7300} = 2 \cdot 0.4286 \\
F_1 &= \mathbf{0.8572}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operational Health Commentary:** *(1 mark for commentary)*

The WAF has an **F1-score of 0.857**, which meets the industry-accepted production threshold of **F1 ≥ 0.85**. The Detection Rate of 78.26% is **moderate** — about 22% of attacks bypass the WAF and reach the application, indicating that signature coverage should be expanded. The False Positive Rate of 0.017% is **excellent** — legitimate users are rarely inconvenienced. Precision of 94.74% is **high** — when the WAF blocks, it is almost always correct. **Recommendation:** Add more anomaly-based rules and behavioral analysis to lift DR above 90% without sacrificing the low FPR.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing pitfalls in WAF questions:**
> 1. **Confusing WAF with network firewall** — Examiners will deduct marks if you describe WAF as a "L3/L4 device." Always state **L7 / Application Layer**.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative-model vs positive-model distinction** — A 7-mark question on WAF detection *will* test this. Memorize both.
> 3. **Skipping the confusion matrix verification step** — Always verify $T_p + F_p + T_n + F_n = \text{Total}$ before computing any metric. Examiners allot 1 mark for this consistency check.
> 4. **Mixing up Precision and Recall** — Precision is "of those blocked, how many were real attacks." Recall (DR) is "of all real attacks, how many were blocked." Use the formulas, not intuition.
> 5. **Ignoring WAF limitations** — Any question that says "explain WAF" or "is WAF sufficient" **must** include the limitations: encrypted payloads, business-logic flaws, evasion techniques, and insider threats. A "WAF solves all web security problems" answer will be marked down.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — In 14-mark questions, a labeled WAF architecture diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **WAF = Layer 7 (Application Layer) security control** that inspects HTTP/S payloads, unlike network firewalls (L3/L4) and generic IDS/IPS.
- **Three deployment models:** Network-based (hardware), Host-based (software on server), Cloud-based (SaaS). Cloud-based dominates modern deployments due to elasticity.
- **Three operational modes:** Transparent reverse proxy, transparent bridge, host-embedded.
- **Three detection methodologies:** Signature (blacklist), Anomaly (whitelist), Stateful (behavioral). Production WAFs combine all three.
- **Key actions:** Allow, Block (403), Drop, Challenge (CAPTCHA), Log. Use the severity score to triage.
- **OWASP Top 10 (2021):** WAF provides *virtual patches* for A01–A10; it is a compensating control, not a replacement for secure coding.
- **Core metrics to remember:**
  - $\text{DR} = T_p / (T_p + F_n)$
  - $\text{FPR} = F_p / (F_p + T_n)$
  - $\text{Pre} = T_p / (T_p + F_p)$
  - $F_1 = 2 \cdot \text{Pre} \cdot \text{DR} / (\text{Pre} + \text{DR})$
  - Industry benchmark: $F_1 \geq 0.85$ for production.
- **Mandatory PCI DSS reference:** PCI DSS v4.0 Requirement 6.4.1 mandates a WAF for any public-facing web application handling cardholder data.
- **Latency budget:** < 10 ms for inline proxy mode is the industry expectation.
- **Limitations:** Cannot stop volumetric DDoS, cannot fix code-level vulnerabilities, can be bypassed via encoding/TLS offloading, cannot detect insider abuse.
- **Defense-in-depth principle:** WAF + Network Firewall + IDS/IPS + SIEM + Secure SDLC = holistic security posture.
- **Exam mantra:** "WAF is a Layer 7 compensating control that virtually patches OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities using signature, anomaly, and behavioral detection, with actions governed by a severity-based policy decision point."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Database Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Database Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — DATABASE SECURITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> **Database Security** is the discipline, methodology, and set of controls, tools, and techniques employed to protect a Database Management System (DBMS) from unauthorized access, malicious threats, data leakage, integrity violations, and availability disruptions. It encompasses the triad of **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA)** of data stored within relational, NoSQL, distributed, and cloud-based databases, while simultaneously enforcing authentication, authorization, auditing, encryption, and inference control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Database Security in the PECCT524 syllabus bridges classical database theory (access control matrices, TCB) with modern adversarial attack surfaces (SQLi, inference attacks, insider threats). Students must master both **preventive** and **detective** security mechanisms.

### 1.2 The CIA Triad in Database Context

| Pillar | Database Meaning | Threat Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Only authorized users read sensitive records | SQL Injection data exfiltration |
| **Integrity** | Data is not altered by unauthorized actors | Privilege escalation update attack |
| **Availability** | DBMS is online when business needs it | Ransomware, DDoS, log flooding |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault

Imagine a bank's **vault room** containing thousands of safety deposit boxes:

- **Database Server** = The vault room itself.
- **Tables / Rows** = Individual safety deposit boxes.
- **Users** = Customers holding keys to specific boxes.
- **DBA (Database Administrator)** = Bank manager with master keys.
- **Authentication** = Verifying your identity at the entrance (ID check).
- **Authorization** = Checking which boxes your key actually opens.
- **SQL Injection** = A thief who manipulates the *request slip* to fool the clerk into opening the wrong box.
- **Encryption** = Each safety deposit box is wrapped in an unbreakable code that only the intended owner can decipher.
- **Audit Trail** = The CCTV camera that records every single box opening, including time, person, and box ID.
- **Inference Attack** = A spy who never directly opens a box but, by observing who visits which boxes, deduces the contents.

> [!NOTE]
> A secure database must protect the data **at rest** (stored on disk), **in transit** (over the network), and **in use** (during queries/operations). This is the **three-state data protection principle**.

### 1.4 Why Database Security is Critical in Industry

- **Regulatory Compliance:** GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, India's DPDP Act 2023 mandate strict DB controls.
- **Insider Threats:** **68%** of breaches (per Verizon DBIR) involve human elements including DB credentials.
- **Average Breach Cost (2024):** **USD 4.88 million** — IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report.
- **Ransomware** specifically targets database files (.mdf, .ibd, .dbf) for encryption extortion.
- **Critical Infrastructure:** SCADA/ICS systems in power plants and manufacturing rely on historian databases; compromise = physical damage.

### 1.5 KTU-Relevant Scope of Module 2

Per KTU 2024 PECCT524 syllabus, the expected depth of Module 2 covers:
1. Database access control models (DAC, MAC, RBAC).
2. SQL Injection (SQLi) — types, mechanics, countermeasures.
3. Inference and statistical database security.
4. Encryption methods for databases (column-level, TDE, homomorphic).
5. Database auditing and recovery (logical & physical).
6. Multilevel secure databases (Bell-LaPadula adapted to DBs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Database Security Threat Taxonomy

Database threats are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Insider vs. Outsider** — Authorized users abusing privileges vs. external attackers.
2. **Active vs. Passive** — Modifying data/querying silently.
3. **Intentional vs. Accidental** — Malicious actors vs. human error.
4. **Direct vs. Indirect** — Direct query manipulation vs. inference from aggregated queries.

### 2.2 Database Access Control Models (Detailed)

Access Control answers: **"Who can do what to which data object, under what conditions?"**

#### 2.2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

- Owner of a database object decides who gets access.
- Implemented via **GRANT** and **REVOKE** statements in SQL.
- Flexible but vulnerable to **Trojan horse** attacks propagating privilege abuse.

#### 2.2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

- Central security policy enforced by the DBMS; users *cannot* override.
- Each subject (user) and object (table/row) has a **security label / clearance**.
- Based on **Bell-LaPadula** model:
  - **No Read Up (NRU):** Subject clearance ≥ Object label.
  - **No Write Down (NWD):** Subject clearance ≤ Object label (prevents leakage to lower-level subjects).
- Used in defense, government (e.g., Oracle Label Security).

#### 2.2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

- Permissions are bundled into **roles**; users are assigned roles.
- Supports **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** and **Separation of Duties (SoD)**.
- Industry standard (NIST RBAC standard, ANSI INCITS 359).

#### 2.2.4 Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Property | DAC | MAC | RBAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Policy Authority | Data owner | Central policy | Administrator |
| Flexibility | High | Low | Medium |
| Trojan Resistance | Weak | Strong | Medium |
| Implementation Cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Granularity | Row/Column | Label-based | Role-based |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High | High | High |

### 2.3 SQL Injection — The #1 Web Database Threat

**SQL Injection (SQLi)** is a code injection technique where an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is later concatenated into a dynamic query executed by the database.

#### 2.3.1 Anatomy of a SQLi Attack

A vulnerable login form backend code:
```sql
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + input + "' AND password='" + pwd + "'"
```

Attacker supplies:
- Username: `admin' --`
- Password: *(anything)*

Resulting query:
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' --' AND password='anything'
```
The `--` comments out the password check → **unauthenticated access as admin**.

#### 2.3.2 SQLi Classification (OWASP Standard)

1. **In-band SQLi** (Error-based, Union-based)
2. **Blind/Inferential SQLi** (Boolean-based, Time-based)
3. **Out-of-band SQLi** (DNS, HTTP exfiltration)
4. **Second-order SQLi** (Stored payload triggered later)

### 2.4 Inference Attack & Statistical Database Security

An **inference attack** occurs when a user deduces confidential information by combining **non-sensitive aggregated queries**.

Example:
- Query 1: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM patients WHERE disease='Cancer' AND city='Kochi'` → returns 1
- Query 2: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM patients WHERE disease='Cancer' AND city='Kochi' AND name='Ravi'`
- If both return 1, attacker deduces Ravi has cancer.

Defenses:
- **Query restriction** (minimum set size k-anonymity).
- **Cell suppression** (hiding small aggregates).
- **Auditing** combined with **randomization**.
- **Differential Privacy** (adding Laplace noise).

### 2.5 Database Encryption Layers

| Layer | Scope | Performance Impact | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Disk-level (TDE)** | Entire DB files | Low | Compliance baseline |
| **Column-level** | Sensitive columns (SSN, PAN) | Medium | GDPR/HIPAA |
| **Row-level** | Specific records | Medium-High | Multi-tenant SaaS |
| **Field-level** | Individual fields | High | Crypto-secure DBs |
| **Homomorphic** | Computation on ciphertext | Very High | Privacy-preserving ML |
| **Tokenization** | Replace with surrogate | Low | PCI-DSS card data |

### 2.6 Auditing, Backup & Recovery

- **Audit Log:** Immutable record of `WHO did WHAT, WHEN, FROM WHERE, SUCCESS/FAIL`.
- **Logical Backup:** Dumps schema + data (e.g., `mysqldump`, `pg_dump`).
- **Physical Backup:** Raw filesystem snapshot.
- **Recovery Models:** Simple, Full, Bulk-Logged (SQL Server terminology).
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Max acceptable data loss window.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Max acceptable downtime.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas and metrics are high-yield for KTU ESE numericals and derivations. The probability/security calculus is directly examinable.

#### 2.7.1 Risk & Threat Metrics

| Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Classical risk triangle | Qualitative scores |
| $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | SLE = Single Loss Expectancy, ARO = Annualized Rate of Occurrence |
| $SLE = Asset\_Value \times Exposure\_Factor$ | Single Loss Expectancy | EF in fraction (0–1) |
| $ROSI = \frac{(ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}) - Cost\_of\_Control}{Cost\_of\_Control}$ | Return on Security Investment | Pure USD metric |
| $k\text{-anonymity: } \vert Q(R) \vert \geq k$ | Query result minimum size | k = anonymity threshold |

#### 2.7.2 Cryptographic Formulas Relevant to DB

| Concept | Formula / Relation |
|---|---|
| AES key sizes | 128, 192, 256 bits |
| AES rounds | $N_r = 6 + \max(\lceil \log_2(N_b) \rceil, \lceil \log_2(N_k) \rceil)$ where $N_b = 4$, $N_k \in \{4,6,8\}$ |
| RSA encryption | $C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$ |
| RSA decryption | $M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ |
| RSA key relation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| Euler totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ for $n = p \cdot q$ |
| SHA-256 output | 256-bit fixed digest |
| HMAC | $HMAC(K,M) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ |
| Entropy (Shannon) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ bits |
| Password entropy bits | $E = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ where L=length, R=character pool size |

#### 2.7.3 Bell-LaPadula & Biba Integrity Rules

| Model | Rule | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| BLP — NRU (Simple Security) | Subject reads only if $\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)$ | Confidentiality |
| BLP — NWD (*-property) | Subject writes only if $\lambda(S) \leq \lambda(O)$ | Prevent leakage |
| Biba — No Read Down | Subject reads only if $\lambda(S) \leq \lambda(O)$ | Integrity |
| Biba — No Write Up | Subject writes only if $\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)$ | Integrity |

Where $\lambda(S)$ is the subject's clearance and $\lambda(O)$ is the object's classification level.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Risk Calculation for a Sample Database Asset

> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark analytical question):**  
> A financial database stores ₹50,00,000 worth of customer PII. A SQL injection attack is estimated to compromise **20%** of the asset per successful incident. The attack is expected to occur **3 times per year**. The proposed WAF + parameterised queries solution costs ₹2,00,000/year and is expected to reduce ARO to 0.5/year. Compute the **ROSI** and recommend deployment.

### Step 1: Compute SLE (Single Loss Expectancy)

$$
SLE = Asset\_Value \times Exposure\_Factor
$$

$$
SLE = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Logic:** Each successful attack damages 20% of the ₹50 Lakh asset = ₹10 Lakh per incident.

### Step 2: Compute ALE before control

$$
ALE_{before} = SLE \times ARO_{before}
$$

$$
ALE_{before} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

### Step 3: Compute ALE after control

$$
ALE_{after} = SLE \times ARO_{after}
$$

$$
ALE_{after} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Risk Reduction (Annual Savings)

$$
\Delta ALE = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}
$$

$$
\Delta ALE = 30{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

### Step 5: Compute ROSI

$$
ROSI = \frac{\Delta ALE - Cost\_of\_Control}{Cost\_of\_Control}
$$

$$
ROSI = \frac{25{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
ROSI = \frac{23{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 11.5
$$

**Interpretation:** A ROSI of 11.5 means every ₹1 invested returns ₹11.5 in net risk reduction → **STRONGLY RECOMMEND deployment.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Defending Against SQLi

### Step-by-step walkthrough of how a parameterised query neutralises an SQLi attack.

| Step | Vulnerable Code (Concatenation) | Parameterised Code |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | User input `admin' --` accepted as string | Input bound to variable `:username` |
| 2 | Concatenated into raw SQL | Driver treats it strictly as data |
| 3 | Query structure altered | Query structure preserved |
| 4 | Attacker logs in as admin | Auth fails / No record matched |
| 5 | Data exfiltration begins | Attack neutralised |

### Worked example — Boolean Blind SQLi time analysis

Suppose the attacker injects:
```sql
' AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(password,1,1) FROM users WHERE username='admin')='a' --
```

If the response time is **T** for each test character and the page loads in time **T** for the *true* branch and 2T for the *false* branch, then the **time complexity** of extracting a length-L password over charset C is:

$$
T_{extract} = \mathcal{O}(L \cdot \vert C \vert \cdot T_{request})
$$

For a 12-character password with charset size 95 (printable ASCII):
$$
T_{extract} = 12 \times 95 = 1140 \text{ requests}
$$

This shows why **rate limiting + WAF + parameterised queries** form a layered defense — each layer multiplies attacker cost.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inference Attack on Statistical Database

### Scenario: Hospital database with 1000 patients, k-anonymity requirement k = 5.

| Attacker Query 1 | Result | Inference |
|---|---|---|
| `COUNT(*) WHERE ward='ICU' AND gender='F'` | 12 | Population exists |
| `COUNT(*) WHERE ward='ICU' AND gender='F' AND age=72` | 1 | **ATTACK SUCCEEDS** — single person identified |

### Defense using cell suppression:

$$
\text{Display}(Q) = \begin{cases} Q & \text{if } Q \geq k \\ \text{“Suppressed} \leq k\text{”} & \text{if } Q < k \end{cases}
$$

Setting k = 5 → the second query returns "Suppressed (≤ 5)" instead of "1", preventing individual identification while preserving aggregate utility.

### Tracker attack defense formula (Denning's approach):

For a tracker query that combines multiple legitimate queries to isolate one record, the defense is to detect the linear dependency:

$$
Q_{tracker} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i Q_i
$$

Where coefficients $c_i \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ and the result is 1. The DBMS monitors these and denies any query that is a **linear combination** of previously issued queries forming a tracker.

---

## 3.4 Access Control Matrix Formalisation

The **Access Control Matrix (ACM)** is a formal Lampson model:

$$
ACM: S \times O \rightarrow 2^{\{r, w, x, d, *\}}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of subjects (users, processes)
- $O$ = set of objects (tables, columns, rows)
- $r$ = read, $w$ = write, $x$ = execute, $d$ = delete, $*$ = ownership

The matrix is implemented in practice by:
- **Authorization Table** (decomposed ACM into rows)
- **Capability List** (decomposed into columns)

The TCB (Trusted Computing Base) of a secure DBMS is responsible for **enforcing** the matrix on every operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Database Security Architecture — Layered Defense (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA["External User"]:::user
    userB["Internal DBA"]:::user
    attacker["Threat Actor"]:::threat

    fw["Perimeter Firewall / WAF"]:::defense
    ids["Intrusion Detection System"]:::defense
    auth["Authentication Layer"]:::defense

    enc1["Network Encryption TLS 1.3"]:::defense
    enc2["Column Level Encryption"]:::defense
    enc3["Transparent Data Encryption TDE"]:::defense

    acm["Access Control Matrix DAC MAC RBAC"]:::policy
    audit["Audit Log and SIEM Feed"]:::detective
    backup["Backup and Recovery System"]:::recovery

    db["Database Engine Oracle PostgreSQL MSSQL"]:::data
    data["At Rest Data Files"]:::data

    attacker --> fw
    userA --> fw
    userB --> fw
    fw --> ids
    ids --> auth
    auth --> enc1
    enc1 --> acm
    acm --> enc2
    enc2 --> enc3
    enc3 --> db
    db --> data
    db --> audit
    audit --> backup

    classDef user fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef threat fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef defense fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a6638,color:#000
    classDef policy fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef detective fill:#e2d6f0,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000
    classDef recovery fill:#fde2c5,stroke:#8b4513,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows from the top (users/attackers) downward through successive security gates. Each gate is a checkpoint that may DENY, ALLOW, or LOG the request. Backup & Recovery acts as the *last line of defense* once prevention has failed.

---

## 4.2 SQL Injection Attack & Defense Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Att as Attacker
    participant Web as Web Application
    participant DB as Database Server

    Note over Att,DB: PHASE 1: Reconnaissance
    Att->>Web: Submit input: test'
    Web->>DB: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id='test''
    DB-->>Web: SQL Syntax Error exposed
    Web-->>Att: Error page reveals SQL dialect

    Note over Att,DB: PHASE 2: Exploitation
    Att->>Web: Submit: ' OR 1=1 --
    Web->>DB: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id='' OR 1=1 --'
    DB-->>Web: Returns entire users table
    Web-->>Att: Authentication bypassed

    Note over Att,DB: DEFENSE: Parameterised Query
    Att->>Web: Submit: ' OR 1=1 --
    Web->>DB: PREPARE stmt FROM 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?'
    Web->>DB: EXECUTE stmt USING ' OR 1=1 --'
    DB-->>Web: Returns 0 rows (treated as literal string)
    DB-->>Web: No data leak
```

---

## 4.3 Access Control Decision Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    req["Incoming Request"] --> authn{"Authenticated?"}
    authn -->|No| deny1["DENY - Log Anomaly"]
    authn -->|Yes| authz{"Authorized for object?"}
    authz -->|No| deny2["DENY - Audit Trail"]
    authz -->|Yes| macCheck{"MAC Label Check"}
    macCheck -->|NRU Violation| deny3["DENY - MAC Block"]
    macCheck -->|Pass| rbacCheck{"RBAC Role Active?"}
    rbacCheck -->|No| deny4["DENY - Role Inactive"]
    rbacCheck -->|Yes| timeCheck{"Business Hours?"}
    timeCheck -->|No| deny5["DENY - Temporal Policy"]
    timeCheck -->|Yes| allow["ALLOW - Execute and Log"]
```

---

## 4.4 Database Security Operational Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1. Identify Assets"] --> B["2. Classify Data"]
    B --> C["3. Assess Threats"]
    C --> D["4. Design Controls"]
    D --> E["5. Implement"]
    E --> F["6. Monitor and Audit"]
    F --> G{"Incident Detected?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["7. Respond and Forensics"]
    H --> I["8. Recover from Backup"]
    I --> F
    G -->|No| F
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC) in databases. Give one example of where each is used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

| Aspect | DAC | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| Policy Authority | Owner of the data object decides access | Central security policy/labels enforced by TCB |
| Flexibility | High (grants can be cascaded) | Low (no user override) |
| Example | University student portal where each faculty can grant TA access to their course | Military intelligence database where clearance levels (Confidential/Secret/Top Secret) are system-enforced |

**Award Marks:** Definition (1) + Example with distinction (1) + Correct comparison (1).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **What is SQL Injection? List any FOUR prevention mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SQL Injection is a code injection attack where malicious SQL statements are inserted into application input fields, which are then executed by the backend database, allowing unauthorized data access or manipulation.

**Four Prevention Mechanisms (0.5 each, total 2 marks):**

1. **Parameterised Queries / Prepared Statements** — separate code from data.
2. **Stored Procedures** — encapsulate SQL in precompiled routines.
3. **Input Validation / Whitelisting** — reject unexpected characters.
4. **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** — pattern-based filtering.
5. **Principle of Least Privilege** — DB user accounts with minimal rights.
6. **ORM Frameworks** — abstract raw SQL.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - For 14-mark questions, students who write *only bullet points* without sub-headings lose 2-3 marks.
> - Always include **diagrams** for access control / SQLi questions (min. 1 diagram = +1 mark).
> - For numericals (e.g., ROSI), show **all formula substitutions** — missing the substitution step costs 2 marks.
> - Avoid generic statements like "databases are important" — examiners look for *specific technical terms* (RBAC, TDE, k-anonymity, etc.).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the different types of **SQL Injection attacks** with examples. Discuss **any SEVEN** countermeasures in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A healthcare database stores ₹2 Crore worth of patient records. A ransomware attack has a 25% exposure factor and is expected 4 times per year. A proposed endpoint-DLP + immutable backup solution costs ₹5 Lakh/year and is expected to reduce ARO to 1 per year. Compute the **ROSI** and recommend. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** SQL Injection (SQLi) is a code injection technique classified by OWASP as a top-10 web risk. Attackers insert malicious SQL into input fields.

**Types of SQLi (3 marks, 0.75 each):**

1. **In-band SQLi (Error-based):** Attacker receives error messages revealing DB schema.
   Example: `' AND 1=CONVERT(int, (SELECT TOP 1 table_name FROM information_schema.tables))--`

2. **In-band SQLi (Union-based):** Attacker uses `UNION SELECT` to append results.
   Example: `' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users--`

3. **Blind Boolean SQLi:** Attacker infers data from TRUE/FALSE page differences.
   Example: `' AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1))='M'--`

4. **Blind Time-based SQLi:** Uses `SLEEP()` or `WAITFOR DELAY` to infer via response time.
   Example: `'; IF (1=1) WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--`

5. **Out-of-band SQLi:** Uses DNS/HTTP exfiltration via `xp_dirtree`, `UTL_HTTP`.

6. **Second-order SQLi:** Payload stored, triggered by another function/process later.

**Seven Countermeasures (3 marks, ~0.4 each):**

1. **Parameterised Queries** — precompile SQL with placeholders.
2. **Stored Procedures** — SQL defined in DB, input passed as parameters.
3. **Whitelisting Input Validation** — only known good characters accepted.
4. **Escaping User Input** — DB-specific escape functions.
5. **WAF (Web Application Firewall)** — blocks known SQLi patterns.
6. **Least Privilege DB Accounts** — separate read-only, write, admin accounts.
7. **Disable Error Messages in Production** — no SQL errors to client.
8. **Regular Security Patching** — DB engine + application framework.
9. **Static & Dynamic Code Analysis (SAST/DAST)** — automated detection.
10. **Database Activity Monitoring (DAM)** — real-time query auditing.

**Conclusion (Optional, 0 marks):** Layered defense is essential — no single control is foolproof.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Asset Value $AV = ₹2,00,00,000$
- Exposure Factor $EF = 0.25$
- $ARO_{before} = 4$
- $ARO_{after} = 1$
- $Cost\_of\_Control = ₹5,00,000$

**Step 1: Compute SLE (1 mark)**

$$
SLE = AV \times EF = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2: Compute ALE before (1 mark)**

$$
ALE_{before} = SLE \times ARO_{before} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 4 = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3: Compute ALE after (1 mark)**

$$
ALE_{after} = SLE \times ARO_{after} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 1 = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 4: Annual Savings (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta ALE = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 5: ROSI Computation (2 marks)**

$$
ROSI = \frac{\Delta ALE - Cost\_of\_Control}{Cost\_of\_Control}
$$

$$
ROSI = \frac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = \frac{1{,}45{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 29
$$

**Step 6: Recommendation (1 mark):** A ROSI of 29 indicates ₹29 return per ₹1 invested. The solution is **highly recommended** for deployment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Bell-LaPadula model** as applied to multilevel secure databases. Discuss its limitations in ensuring integrity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is an **inference attack** in statistical databases? Explain with a hospital database example. Discuss **k-anonymity, l-diversity, and differential privacy** as defenses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):** Mandatory — Draw subject-object lattice with security labels (Unclassified < Confidential < Secret < Top Secret). Mark the *No Read Up* and *No Write Down* arrows.

**Bell-LaPadula Rules (3 marks, 1.5 each):**

1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU):** A subject at clearance level $\lambda(S)$ can read an object at level $\lambda(O)$ only if $\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)$. Prevents unauthorized disclosure.

2. **Star Property (*-property / No Write Down — NWD):** A subject can write to an object only if $\lambda(S) \leq \lambda(O)$. Prevents a high-clearance subject from writing sensitive data to a lower-level object (which a low-clearance subject could then read).

**Tranquility:** Strong tranquility (labels never change at runtime) vs. Weak tranquility (labels cannot change in a way that violates security).

**Limitations Regarding Integrity (2 marks):**

1. BLP focuses purely on **confidentiality** — it has no concept of *integrity levels* (Trusted vs. Untrusted data).
2. A low-clearance user can still **corrupt** low-level data without detection.
3. This motivated the **Biba integrity model** (No Read Down / No Write Up) and the **Clark-Wilson** model for commercial DB integrity.

**Conclusion:** BLP is necessary but not sufficient — modern secure DBMS combine BLP + Biba + Clark-Wilson (e.g., Oracle Label Security, PostgreSQL with SE-Linux MLS).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Subject: Analyst (Secret)"] -->|Reads| O2["Object: Secret Report"]
    S1 -->|Cannot Read Up| O3["Object: Top Secret Memo"]
    S2["Subject: Junior (Confidential)"] -->|Reads| O1["Object: Confidential File"]
    S2 -->|Cannot Write Down| O1
    S1 -.->|NRU Check OK| O2
    S1 -.->|NRU Fails| O3
    S2 -.->|NWD Fails| O1
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** An inference attack is an indirect violation of confidentiality where a user pieces together confidential information from the responses to multiple non-sensitive statistical queries.

**Hospital Database Example (2 marks):**

- Total patients: 1,000
- Q1: `COUNT(*) WHERE disease='Leukemia' AND city='Kochi' AND age_group='60-70'` → 1
- Q2: `COUNT(*) WHERE disease='Leukemia' AND city='Kochi' AND age_group='60-70' AND name='Mr. X'` → 1

The attacker now knows Mr. X has leukemia — inference succeeded.

**Defense 1 — k-anonymity (1.5 marks):**
A query result must reflect at least $k$ indistinguishable records.
$$
\forall r \in R, \vert Q(r) \vert \geq k
$$
Setting $k = 5$ → any aggregate fewer than 5 is suppressed.

**Defense 2 — l-diversity (1 mark):**
Strengthens k-anonymity by requiring at least $l$ *distinct sensitive values* within each equivalence class. Prevents homogeneity attacks.

**Defense 3 — Differential Privacy (1.5 marks):**
Adds calibrated Laplace noise to query results:
$$
M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)
$$
Where $\Delta f$ is query sensitivity and $\epsilon$ is the privacy budget. Smaller $\epsilon$ → stronger privacy, more noise.

**Conclusion:** Modern statistical DB security combines k-anonymity + l-diversity + differential privacy for robust protection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Database Security: Final Revision Bullets**

- ✅ **CIA Triad** is the foundation: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ✅ **DAC** = owner-controlled; **MAC** = system-policy; **RBAC** = role-based — exam favorite.
- ✅ **Bell-LaPadula** = confidentiality (NRU + NWD); **Biba** = integrity (No Read Down + No Write Up).
- ✅ **SQLi** types: In-band (Error/Union), Blind (Boolean/Time), Out-of-band, Second-order.
- ✅ SQLi defenses: parameterised queries (BEST), stored procedures, WAF, input validation, least privilege, error suppression.
- ✅ **Inference attack** = deducing secrets from non-secret aggregates; defenses are k-anonymity, l-diversity, differential privacy.
- ✅ **Encryption layers**: Disk (TDE), Column, Row, Field, Homomorphic, Tokenization.
- ✅ **Auditing** must log: WHO, WHAT, WHEN, WHERE, SUCCESS/FAIL.
- ✅ **RPO** = max data loss; **RTO** = max downtime — backup strategy depends on these.
- ✅ **ROSI formula**:
$$
ROSI = \frac{\Delta ALE - Cost\_of\_Control}{Cost\_of\_Control}
$$
- ✅ **SLE = AV × EF**; **ALE = SLE × ARO** — memorize these three for any numerical.
- ✅ **AES rounds**: 10 (128-bit key), 12 (192-bit), 14 (256-bit).
- ✅ **RSA**: $C = M^e \bmod n$, $M = C^d \bmod n$, $ed \equiv 1 \bmod \phi(n)$.
- ✅ **Password entropy**: $E = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ — 8-char alphanumeric password = $\sim 47.6$ bits.
- ✅ **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** of DBMS enforces the Access Control Matrix.
- ✅ **Multilevel Secure DBs** use labels + BLP/Biba combined policies.
- ✅ **Industry regulations**: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, India's DPDP Act 2023.
- ✅ For **14-mark KTU questions**, always include: Definition + Diagram + Comparison Table + Numerical Working + Conclusion.
- ✅ Examiner deducts marks for: missing diagrams, undefined abbreviations, skipping substitution steps, generic non-technical statements.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Protection Controls: Data Classification and Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — DATA PROTECTION CONTROLS: DATA CLASSIFICATION AND HANDLING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Data Classification** is the systematic process of organizing data assets into relevant categories based on their sensitivity, value, regulatory requirements, and the impact level of unauthorized disclosure, modification, or destruction. **Data Handling** refers to the suite of administrative, technical, and physical controls applied to data throughout its lifecycle (creation, storage, usage, sharing, archival, and destruction) in alignment with its classification.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library of Secrets"

Imagine a massive corporate library where every document is a piece of information. Without a system, anyone could walk in and read the CEO's salary slip, the company's secret recipe, or a customer's medical record. **Data Classification** is like creating a card-catalog system that sorts books into four zones:

1. **The Open Stacks** (Public) — free to read.
2. **The Staff Reading Room** (Internal) — only for employees.
3. **The Restricted Vault** (Confidential) — requires a special badge.
4. **The Inner Sanctum** (Restricted) — only the CEO and two bodyguards may enter.

**Data Handling** is the set of **rules of conduct** inside that library: how to carry the books, whether you can photocopy them, how long you can keep them, and how they must be shredded when obsolete. Together, they form a **Governance Shield** ensuring information is protected proportional to its sensitivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Industry Metric (NIST SP 800-60):** Data sensitivity is graded on a **4-tier scale** with corresponding **CIA Triad weights**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.

### 1.2 Why Classification Matters in Industrial Cyber Security

In Industrial Control Systems (ICS) and OT environments, data classification is the **foundation of the Purdue Model's Layer 3.5 (DMZ) and Layer 0-1 (Process) data governance**. Without classification:

- **SCADA telemetry** (low sensitivity, high availability) cannot be distinguished from **engineering firmware signing keys** (high sensitivity, low availability).
- **Regulatory penalties** under GDPR (up to **€20 million or 4% of annual global turnover**) become unavoidable.
- **Insider threat** risk is amplified, as 60% of data breaches originate from internal actors (Verizon DBIR 2023).

### 1.3 The Four Canonical Sensitivity Tiers (Industry Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU High-Yield Classification Matrix (NIST + ISO 27001 Hybrid)**

| Tier | Label | Typical Examples | Encryption Standard | Access Model |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T1** | **Public** | Marketing brochures, press releases, public website | TLS 1.3 in transit; optional at rest | Unrestricted |
| **T2** | **Internal** | Org charts, internal memos, generic SOPs | AES-128 at rest | Employee-only |
| **T3** | **Confidential** | PII, financial reports, customer contracts, PLC ladder source | AES-256 + DLP enforcement | Role-Based (RBAC) |
| **T4** | **Restricted** | Trade secrets, cryptographic keys, OT signing keys, M&A data | AES-256 + HSM + MFA + Just-in-Time access | Need-to-Know + Zero Trust |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Data Classification Lifecycle (Six-Phase Model)

The classification process is **not a one-time event**; it is a continuous lifecycle aligned with the **ISO 27001 ISMS** and **NIST SP 800-60** frameworks.

1. **Discovery & Inventory** — Identify data assets via automated scanners (e.g., Microsoft Purview, Varonis, BigID).
2. **Categorization** — Assign a sensitivity tier (T1–T4) based on **business impact analysis (BIA)**.
3. **Labeling & Tagging** — Apply metadata headers, watermarks, visual markings, and DLP fingerprints.
4. **Handling Rules** — Define how data may be stored, transmitted, processed, shared, and destroyed.
5. **Monitoring & Auditing** — SIEM-driven anomaly detection on access patterns; quarterly classification accuracy audits.
6. **Re-classification & Disposal** — Recurring trigger events (mergers, regulatory change, project end) trigger review; end-of-life data is securely destroyed (NIST SP 800-88 *Clear/Purge/Physical Destroy*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad Mapping in OT Environments:**
> - **SCADA Real-Time Data** → High **Availability**, Low Confidentiality.
> - **Engineering Workstation Configs** → High **Integrity**, Medium Confidentiality.
> - **Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Credentials** → High **Confidentiality**, High **Integrity**.

### 2.2 Data Handling Controls — The Five Pillars

| Pillar | Control Family | Example Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Creation** | Author tagging, template enforcement | Mandatory classification drop-down in MS Office/Azure Information Protection |
| **Storage** | Encryption, segmentation, geo-fencing | AES-256, separate VLANs, India-region-only for DPDP Act 2023 |
| **Transmission** | TLS, VPN, secure file transfer | SFTP, MFT (Managed File Transfer), signed PGP |
| **Usage** | DLP, CASB, watermarking | Microsoft Purview DLP blocking "send credit card # outside org" |
| **Destruction** | NIST SP 800-88 sanitization | Cryptographic erase, degaussing, shredding (DIN 66399 P-6) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Data Risk & Valuation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Quantitative Formulas (Data Protection Engineering)**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset Value (AV)** | $AV = F \times (C + I + A)$ | F = functional value, C/I/A = CIA impact (0–5) |
| **Exposure Factor (EF)** | $EF = \dfrac{\text{Value Lost if Breach Occurs}}{AV}$ | Expressed as percentage (0% to 100%) |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Monetary loss from a single breach event |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | Tabulated from threat intel | Expected breaches per year |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected yearly financial loss — the **central decision metric** |
| **Data Classification Score (DCS)** | $DCS = w_C C + w_I I + w_A A + w_R R$ | Weighted sum: w = weight, R = regulatory exposure |
| **Handling Cost Multiplier (HCM)** | $HCM = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i$ | $\alpha_i$ = cost premium of each control (e.g., HSM = 0.40) |
| **Data Lifecycle Protection Index (DLPI)** | $DLPI = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{7} P_j}{7}$ | $P_j \in [0,1]$ = protection score per lifecycle phase |

> [!WARNING]
> **No Vertical Pipes Allowed:** All absolute-value separators in this sheet use `\vert` (rendered as single bar). Example: $x \vert y$ instead of $x \vert y$ in raw text to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Control

- **Why encrypt T3/T4 at rest?** A stolen laptop with AES-256 disk encryption has near-zero data extraction cost to the attacker, removing the breach's regulatory trigger under GDPR Art. 32.
- **Why DLP on email?** 62% of confidential data exits the perimeter through email channels (Symantec 2023 ISTR).
- **Why HSM for T4 keys?** Hardware Security Modules are FIPS 140-2 Level 3/4 certified, ensuring keys **never exist in plaintext outside tamper-resistant silicon**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **smart grid** or **refinery DCS**, classification distinguishes between:
- **Public-facing** outage notification data (T1) — free to publish.
- **SCADA operational telemetry** (T2) — internal only.
- **Process control setpoints and alarm thresholds** (T3) — engineering access only.
- **SIS bypass passwords and cryptographic firmware-signing keys** (T4) — dual-control, vaulted, audited.

This tiering directly drives **firewall rule sets, VLAN segmentation, IEC 62443 Security Level Targets (SL-T 1–4)**, and **incident response playbooks**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) for a Misclassified Data Asset

**Problem Context:** A mid-size BPO in Kerala processes EU customer credit-card data. The asset is **misclassified as T2 (Internal)** instead of **T3 (Confidential)**. Compute the ALE differential and show the cost of misclassification.

**Step 1 — Establish Asset Value (AV)**

The functional value of the dataset is **₹50,00,000**. CIA scores (0–5 scale) from a BIA workshop:
- Confidentiality impact $C = 5$ (PCI-DSS exposure, GDPR fines)
- Integrity impact $I = 4$ (corrupt records cause wrong billing)
- Availability impact $A = 3$ (3-day tolerable downtime)

$$
\begin{aligned}
AV &= F \times (C + I + A) \\
  &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times (5 + 4 + 3) \\
  &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 12 \\
  &= \text{₹}6{,}00{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The functional value is **amplified** by cumulative CIA impact because damage in any one CIA dimension triggers cascading regulatory, reputational, and operational loss.

**Step 2 — Exposure Factor (EF) Under Correct vs. Misclassification**

- Under **correct T3 classification** (with DLP + AES-256 + tokenization), the breach blast-radius is contained: $EF_{\text{T3}} = 0.10$ (10%).
- Under **misclassified T2 treatment** (no DLP, AES-128 only), the blast-radius expands: $EF_{\text{T2}} = 0.70$ (70%).

**Step 3 — Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) for Each Case**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE_{\text{T3}} &= AV \times EF_{\text{T3}} = 6{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000 \\
SLE_{\text{T2}} &= AV \times EF_{\text{T2}} = 6{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 = \text{₹}4{,}20{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)**

From Verizon DBIR industry data, organizations with mature T3 controls experience $ARO_{\text{T3}} = 0.15$ breaches/year. Misclassified environments: $ARO_{\text{T2}} = 0.80$ breaches/year.

**Step 5 — Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE_{\text{T3}} &= SLE_{\text{T3}} \times ARO_{\text{T3}} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = \text{₹}9{,}00{,}000 \\
ALE_{\text{T2}} &= SLE_{\text{T2}} \times ARO_{\text{T2}} = 4{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = \text{₹}3{,}36{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost of Misclassification (CoM)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoM &= ALE_{\text{T2}} - ALE_{\text{T3}} \\
    &= 3{,}36{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= \text{₹}3{,}27{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A single tier-misclassification increases the annualized expected loss by **~37×** — a **3.27 Crore ₹/year** penalty. This quantifies the *business case* for accurate classification and the ROI of DLP investment.

### 3.2 Derivation: Data Classification Score (DCS) Weighted Decision

**Problem:** Classify a dataset containing **employee Aadhaar numbers and salary slips** using the DCS formula.

**Step 1 — Score each axis on 0–5 scale**

| Axis | Score | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality (C) | 5 | Direct PII, DPDP Act 2023 penalty |
| Integrity (I) | 4 | Salary tampering = legal + financial damage |
| Availability (A) | 3 | 2-day acceptable downtime |
| Regulatory (R) | 5 | DPDP Act + IT Act §43A + ISO 27701 |

**Step 2 — Apply weights (org-defined)**

$$
w_C = 0.35, \quad w_I = 0.20, \quad w_A = 0.15, \quad w_R = 0.30
$$

**Step 3 — Compute DCS**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCS &= w_C C + w_I I + w_A A + w_R R \\
    &= (0.35 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 5) \\
    &= 1.75 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 1.50 \\
    &= 4.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Map to Classification Tier**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{DCS Range} & \textbf{Tier} & \textbf{Label} \\
\hline
0.00 \text{ to } 1.50 & \text{T1} & \text{Public} \\
1.51 \text{ to } 2.50 & \text{T2} & \text{Internal} \\
2.51 \text{ to } 4.00 & \text{T3} & \text{Confidential} \\
4.01 \text{ to } 5.00 & \text{T4} & \text{Restricted} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Result:** DCS = **4.50** → **T4 — Restricted**, with mandatory HSM, MFA, and audit trail.

### 3.3 Derivation: Data Lifecycle Protection Index (DLPI)

**Problem:** Compute DLPI for a T3 financial-report dataset across its 7 lifecycle phases.

Protection scores $P_j$ from a control-assessment workshop:

| Phase | $P_j$ |
|---|---|
| 1. Creation | 0.90 |
| 2. Storage | 0.85 |
| 3. Transmission | 0.80 |
| 4. Usage | 0.75 |
| 5. Archival | 0.70 |
| 6. Sharing | 0.65 |
| 7. Destruction | 0.95 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
DLPI &= \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{7} P_j}{7} \\
     &= \frac{0.90 + 0.85 + 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.70 + 0.65 + 0.95}{7} \\
     &= \frac{5.60}{7} \\
     &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** DLPI = 0.80 indicates **80% lifecycle coverage** — a healthy state. A DLPI below 0.60 triggers mandatory remediation under the **NIST CSF Protect function**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define data classification. List the four standard sensitivity tiers with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Data Classification** is the process of organizing data into categories based on its sensitivity, value, legal/regulatory requirements, and the impact of its unauthorized disclosure, modification, or destruction. The four standard tiers are:
>
> 1. **T1 — Public:** Press releases, public website content. No restriction.
> 2. **T2 — Internal:** Org charts, internal SOPs. Employee-only access.
> 3. **T3 — Confidential:** Customer PII, financial reports. RBAC + DLP enforced.
> 4. **T4 — Restricted:** Trade secrets, cryptographic keys. Need-to-know, HSM-backed, MFA + Just-in-Time access.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the role of Data Loss Prevention (DLP) in data handling with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

> **DLP (Data Loss Prevention)** is a set of tools and processes that ensure sensitive data is not lost, misused, or accessed by unauthorized users. It operates via **content inspection, contextual analysis, and policy enforcement** across endpoints, networks, and cloud (CASB).
>
> **Example:** A DLP policy configured for credit-card numbers (matching the Luhn algorithm + 13–19 digit pattern) blocks any employee from emailing a 16-digit PAN-format string to an external Gmail address, triggering an alert to the InfoSec team and quarantining the message. This enforces **T3 handling rules** automatically, scaling governance without manual review.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B carries **14 marks** with internal choice between two questions, each having sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Solutions must be exhaustive and structured for full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Discuss the Data Classification Lifecycle in detail. Explain each phase with its corresponding ISO 27001 / NIST control reference.*

**Model Solution:**

The Data Classification Lifecycle is a **six-phase continuous loop** ensuring that data is identified, categorized, protected, and disposed of in alignment with organizational risk appetite. It is mandated by **ISO 27001 Annex A.5.12 (Classification of Information)** and **NIST SP 800-60 Vol. 1**.

**Phase 1 — Discovery & Inventory:**
Automated and manual identification of data assets across endpoints, servers, cloud (AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCP), and OT historians. Tools: Microsoft Purview, Collibra, Varonis. Output: a *data inventory register* mapped to data owners.

**Phase 2 — Categorization:**
Each asset is assigned a sensitivity tier (T1–T4) via a **Business Impact Analysis (BIA)**. The DCS formula is applied, considering confidentiality, integrity, availability, and regulatory exposure weights.

**Phase 3 — Labeling & Tagging:**
Visual labels (headers, footers, watermarks) and machine-readable metadata (MIP labels, Microsoft Sensitivity Labels) are embedded. This enables downstream policy enforcement.

**Phase 4 — Handling Rules:**
Per-tier rules are defined for **storage, transmission, usage, sharing, archival, and destruction**. Example: T4 = AES-256 + HSM + MFA + no email + no USB.

**Phase 5 — Monitoring & Auditing:**
SIEM/SOAR systems log all data access events. Quarterly **classification accuracy audits** validate that data is labeled correctly. Anomalies trigger re-classification.

**Phase 6 — Re-classification & Disposal:**
Trigger events (M&A, regulatory change, project end) force a re-evaluation. End-of-life data is destroyed per **NIST SP 800-88** sanitization standards (Clear, Purge, Physical Destroy).

> The lifecycle is **continuous**, not linear — Phase 6 often loops back to Phase 2.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]**
*A BPO in Kochi handles EU credit-card data. The asset is currently misclassified as T2. Using the ALE framework, compute the financial impact of correct (T3) versus misclassified (T2) treatment. Assume AV = ₹5 Crore, EF(T3) = 0.10, EF(T2) = 0.70, ARO(T3) = 0.15, ARO(T2) = 0.80.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute SLE for both cases.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE_{\text{T3}} &= AV \times EF_{\text{T3}} = 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000 \\
SLE_{\text{T2}} &= AV \times EF_{\text{T2}} = 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 = \text{₹}3{,}50{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE for both cases.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE_{\text{T3}} &= SLE_{\text{T3}} \times ARO_{\text{T3}} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = \text{₹}7{,}50{,}000 \\
ALE_{\text{T2}} &= SLE_{\text{T2}} \times ARO_{\text{T2}} = 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = \text{₹}2{,}80{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cost of Misclassification (CoM).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoM &= ALE_{\text{T2}} - ALE_{\text{T3}} \\
    &= 2{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}50{,}000 \\
    &= \text{₹}2{,}72{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A single tier misclassification costs the BPO **₹2.725 Crore per year** in expected losses. The ROI of investing in DLP, tokenization, and AES-256 to achieve T3 compliance is recovered in **< 1 quarter**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the five pillars of data handling controls. Describe at least two encryption standards applicable to T3 and T4 data.*

**Model Solution:**

The **Five Pillars of Data Handling Controls** ensure end-to-end protection of data across its lifecycle.

**1. Creation:**
Mandatory classification at point of creation via dropdowns in MS Office, Azure Information Protection (AIP) labels, or templated forms. Auto-applies encryption, watermarks, and metadata.

**2. Storage:**
Encryption at rest is mandatory for T3/T4. **AES-256** (Advanced Encryption Standard, 256-bit key, FIPS 197 approved) is the global benchmark. **AES-128** is acceptable for T2 only.

**3. Transmission:**
TLS 1.3 for web/API traffic. SFTP/FTPS for file transfer. PGP/GPG for email. IPSec VPNs for site-to-site. **End-to-End Encryption (E2EE)** mandated for T4.

**4. Usage:**
**DLP (Data Loss Prevention)** scans content/context to block unauthorized exfiltration. **CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker)** enforces policies on SaaS. **Watermarking** deters screenshot leaks.

**5. Destruction:**
**NIST SP 800-88** defines three levels:
- **Clear:** Overwrite with zeros/ones (software).
- **Purge:** Cryptographic erase or degaussing.
- **Physical Destroy:** Shredding (DIN 66399 P-6/P-7), incineration, disintegration.

**Encryption Standards for T3/T4:**

- **T3 — AES-256-GCM:** Authenticated encryption with associated data; protects both confidentiality and integrity. Used in TLS 1.3, IPsec, full-disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault).
- **T4 — AES-256 inside HSM (FIPS 140-2 Level 3/4):** Keys never leave tamper-resistant hardware. Examples: Thales Luna HSM, AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]**
*A dataset of employee Aadhaar numbers and salary slips is to be classified. CIA scores: C=5, I=4, A=3, R=5. Weights: w_C=0.35, w_I=0.20, w_A=0.15, w_R=0.30. Compute the DCS and state the resulting tier with its mandatory controls.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute values into DCS formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCS &= w_C C + w_I I + w_A A + w_R R \\
    &= (0.35 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 5) \\
    &= 1.75 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 1.50 \\
    &= 4.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Map to tier using the standard threshold table.**

Since $DCS = 4.50 \in [4.01, 5.00]$, the dataset is classified as **T4 — Restricted**.

**Step 3 — Mandatory T4 controls:**

- **Encryption:** AES-256 inside FIPS 140-2 Level 3+ HSM.
- **Authentication:** Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) mandatory; phishing-resistant FIDO2 preferred.
- **Access Model:** Need-to-Know + Just-in-Time (JIT) elevation; Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA).
- **DLP:** Strict blocking — no email, no USB, no unauthorized cloud upload.
- **Audit:** Every access logged immutably; quarterly access review.
- **Destruction:** NIST SP 800-88 *Purge* (cryptographic erase) or *Physical Destroy* (DIN P-6 shred).
- **Regulatory:** DPDP Act 2023 consent collection, breach notification within **72 hours**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the loop-back:** Many students describe the classification lifecycle as linear. The correct answer must emphasize the **continuous nature** (Phase 6 → Phase 2 feedback).
> 2. **Confusing encryption standards:** T3 = AES-256 software; T4 = AES-256 inside HSM. Mixing these loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping unit declarations in numericals:** Always state **₹** or **per year** explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting regulatory references:** Citing DPDP Act 2023, GDPR Art. 32, PCI-DSS v4.0, or ISO 27001 A.5.12 elevates answers from "good" to "full marks".
> 5. **Not computing both SLE and ALE:** Half-marked numericals are common when students skip Step 2 of the ALE derivation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam)**

- **Four tiers:** T1 Public, T2 Internal, T3 Confidential, T4 Restricted.
- **CIA Triad** is the foundation; **Regulatory exposure** is the fourth axis in DCS.
- **Lifecycle is continuous**, not linear — six phases, loop-back from disposal to categorization.
- **DLP** = Content + Context inspection; operates at endpoint, network, and cloud (CASB).
- **T4 keys MUST reside in HSM** — never as plaintext files on disk.
- **NIST SP 800-88** governs destruction: Clear / Purge / Physical Destroy.
- **ALE = SLE × ARO** is the **central decision metric** for security investment.
- **DCS thresholds:** ≤1.5 T1, ≤2.5 T2, ≤4.0 T3, >4.0 T4.
- **DPDP Act 2023** breach notification: **72 hours**.
- **GDPR fine ceiling:** **€20 M or 4% global turnover**, whichever is higher.
- **Purdue Model** is classification in space — L0 telemetry (T2) vs L1 SIS keys (T4).
- **IEC 62443 SL-T 1–4** mirrors T1–T4 — Security Level Target per zone/conduit.
- **Re-classification triggers:** M&A, regulatory change, project end, breach incident.
- **Insider threat** accounts for ~60% of breaches — classification is the first defense.
- **Quantum-readiness:** T4 data with >10 year retention should already be considering **post-quantum cryptography (PQC)** — CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium.
- **Audit frequency:** Quarterly classification accuracy review; annual full BIA refresh.
- **Metadata labeling** is machine-readable (MIP labels) — enables **automated DLP enforcement**.

> **End of Module 2 — Data Protection Controls: Data Classification and Handling**
