topics.title: Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Loss Prevention (DLP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Loss Prevention (DLP)** is a comprehensive set of strategies, policies, processes, and technological controls designed to **identify, monitor, classify, protect, and prevent the unauthorized disclosure, exfiltration, or leakage of sensitive data** — whether in motion (network traffic), in use (endpoint operations), or at rest (storage repositories) — across an organization's entire information ecosystem.

In the context of **Industrial Cyber Security (ICS/SCADA/OT environments)**, DLP extends beyond traditional IT boundaries to include **operational data** such as **Process & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)**, **SCADA configurations**, **PLC ladder logic programs**, **historian databases**, and **proprietary manufacturing recipes** — all of which constitute *critical industrial intellectual property* and *safety-critical operational parameters*.

### The Three States of Data (KTU 2024 Highlight)

| State | Description | Industrial Example |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Data in Motion (DIM)** | Data traversing networks | SCADA telemetry sent to vendor cloud |
| **Data in Use (DIU)** | Data being actively processed | Engineer editing PLC program on HMI |
| **Data at Rest (DAR)** | Data stored in repositories | Process historian database on plant server |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of DLP as a "Security Custom House" for your Organization.**

Imagine your company is a fortified **medieval castle** storing priceless treasures (sensitive data). The castle has:
- **Guards at the gates (Network DLP)** inspecting every merchant's cart (network packet) entering or leaving.
- **Guards patrolling inside the castle (Endpoint DLP)** watching servants (employees) to ensure no one pockets silverware (downloads customer data to USB).
- **Treasurers auditing the vault inventory (Storage DLP)** ensuring gold bars (encrypted files) are correctly counted and labelled.
- **Royal scribes writing the laws (DLP Policies)** stating what *can* and *cannot* leave the castle, and what punishments apply.

In an **industrial plant (ICS/OT)**, the "treasure" includes:
- Reactor temperature curves 📈
- Turbine calibration parameters ⚙️
- Proprietary catalyst formulas 🧪
- Network architecture diagrams 🗺️

If a disgruntled engineer emails the *ammonia synthesis recipe* to a competitor, or a malware-infected USB transfers PLC code to a vendor laptop, DLP is the system that **detects, blocks, and logs** that action.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024)

- **DLP Coverage (C)**: Percentage of endpoints/storage systems governed by DLP agents.
  
  $$C = \frac{N_{\text{DLP-governed}}}{N_{\text{Total assets}}} \times 100\%$$

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** for data exfiltration attempts.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)**: Critical metric — must remain **< 5%** in production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 2 mandates understanding of *content inspection*, *contextual analysis*, and *policy enforcement mechanisms* across **IT-OT converged environments**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The DLP Workflow (Step-by-Step)

The DLP system operates in a continuous **5-stage pipeline**:

1. **Data Discovery & Classification**
   - **How**: Crawls file shares, databases, emails, cloud repositories using *agent-based* and *agentless* scanners.
   - **Why**: You cannot protect what you don't know exists. Industrial plants often have *shadow data* — orphaned historian backups on engineering workstations.
   - **Techniques**: Fingerprinting (exact match), Regex pattern matching, Exact Data Matching (EDM), Machine Learning classifiers.

2. **Policy Definition & Rule Engine Configuration**
   - **How**: Security architects create rule sets — *"Block any file containing 'SSN' pattern from being emailed to external domains."*
   - **Why**: Provides the governance backbone. Policies translate *business risk* into *technical enforcement*.

3. **Content Inspection & Analysis**
   - **How**: Two complementary methods:
     - **Regex / Pattern Matching** → for structured data (credit cards, IP addresses).
     - **Contextual Analysis** → for unstructured data (source code, design documents).
   - **Why**: Insider threats often disguise data (e.g., base64-encoding P&IDs in image files).

4. **Enforcement Action**
   - **How**: Upon violation, the DLP agent executes: **Block**, **Quarantine**, **Encrypt**, **Notify**, or **Audit-only**.
   - **Why**: Granular response prevents business disruption while securing data.

5. **Monitoring, Reporting & Incident Response**
   - **How**: SIEM integration, dashboards, forensic logs.
   - **Why**: Enables continuous improvement and regulatory compliance audit trails.

---

## 2.2 Classification of DLP Solutions (KTU High-Yield Framework)

| **DLP Type** | **Deployment Location** | **What It Protects** | **Industrial Use Case** |
|--------------|------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Network DLP (NDLP)** | At network egress/ingress points (mail gateways, proxies, firewalls) | Email, HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, IM traffic | Blocks exfiltration of SCADA config over SMTP |
| **Endpoint DLP (EDLP)** | Installed on user devices (laptops, engineering workstations, HMI PCs) | USB, Bluetooth, clipboard, local print, screenshots | Prevents copying ladder logic to removable media |
| **Cloud DLP** | SaaS-based or CASB-integrated | OneDrive, SharePoint, Google Drive, AWS S3 | Stops uploading OT network diagrams to personal Dropbox |
| **Storage DLP** | Scans file servers, databases, SharePoint, Historians | Data at rest on servers | Identifies unencrypted P&IDs in engineering drives |
| **Email DLP** | Specialized gateway integration | Outgoing/incoming emails and attachments | Flags emails containing reactor schematics to external domains |

---

## 2.3 Content Inspection Techniques (Deep Dive)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a KTU 2024 high-weightage area — expect a 7-mark question.**

### A. **Pattern-Based Detection (Descriptor Matching)**
- Uses **Regular Expressions (Regex)** to detect structured identifiers.
- Example: Credit card pattern `\d{4}-\d{4}-\d{4}-\d{4}`, SSN pattern `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}`.
- **Limitation**: Easily bypassed by data obfuscation (e.g., inserting spaces, encoding).

### B. **Fingerprinting**
- Creates a unique cryptographic hash of a *known sensitive file* (e.g., a finalized PLC program).
- DLP compares all outbound files against the fingerprint database.
- **Strength**: Near-zero false positives for exact matches.

### C. **Exact Data Matching (EDM)**
- Maintains a database of *actual sensitive records* (e.g., full customer list).
- Performs row-level matching.
- **Industrial use**: Detects unauthorized export of **complete asset inventory** databases.

### D. **Statistical Analysis & Machine Learning**
- Trains models on document *features* (word frequency, n-grams) to classify sensitivity.
- Effective for **unstructured data** like R&D reports, process flow narratives.

### E. **Contextual Analysis**
- Evaluates **metadata**: sender, recipient, file size, time of day, geolocation.
- Example: A 500 MB file sent at 2 AM to a personal Gmail account → high-risk context.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Framework Sheet

| **Framework Element** | **Definition / Formula** | **Application** |
|----------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **DLP Policy Hierarchy** | `Global Policy → Department Policy → User-Level Policy` | Cascading governance |
| **Risk Score Formula** | $R = (C \times S) + (V \times T)$ where C=Confidentiality, S=Sensitivity, V=Volume, T=Threat exposure | Prioritizing violations |
| **Detection Coverage** | $\text{DC} = \frac{\text{Violations Detected}}{\text{Actual Violations}} \times 100\%$ | DLP effectiveness metric |
| **Policy Enforcement Modes** | *Monitor → Warn → Block* | Phased rollout |
| **Insider Threat Indicator (ITI)** | $\text{ITI} = w_1 \cdot B + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot T$ (Behaviour, Access, Time anomaly) | UEBA integration |
| **Data Classification Labels** | `Public → Internal → Confidential → Restricted → Top Secret` | KTU standard 5-tier model |

> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip**: Always cite the *three pillars of DLP* in answers: **Visibility, Protection, Governance**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)**, DLP protects:
- **Digital Twins** — proprietary 3D plant models.
- **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** — production schedules & batch records.
- **IIoT Telemetry** — vibration, temperature, pressure curves used for predictive maintenance.
- **Vendor proprietary algorithms** — ML models predicting equipment failure.

A single DLP violation in a **pharma plant** could leak a $2 billion drug formulation. In **power generation**, leaking turbine control setpoints could enable **grid destabilization attacks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed DLP Architecture (Step-by-Step Decomposition)

The industrial-grade DLP architecture follows a **layered, defense-in-depth** model. Below is the exhaustive functional decomposition.

### Step 1: Data Identification & Discovery
The DLP engine initiates by performing **deep packet inspection (DPI)** and **agent-based file system crawls** to enumerate all data assets.

$$\text{Assets Discovered} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Endpoints}_i \cup \text{Storage}_i \cup \text{Network Flows}_i)$$

**Logic**: We construct a union set of all monitored data sources across the IT-OT stack — including engineering workstations, historians, and outbound SMTP traffic.

### Step 2: Classification Engine
Each discovered asset is processed by the classification engine, which applies a **multi-factor scoring function**:

$$\text{Score}(d) = w_1 \cdot P(d) + w_2 \cdot F(d) + w_3 \cdot C(d) + w_4 \cdot M(d)$$

Where:
- $P(d)$ = **Pattern match score** (regex/fingerprint hit count, normalized 0–1)
- $F(d)$ = **File type sensitivity weight** (e.g., CAD file = 0.8, text file = 0.3)
- $C(d)$ = **Contextual risk** (recipient domain, time, geolocation)
- $M(d)$ = **ML classifier probability** of sensitivity
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = tunable weights, with constraint $\sum w_i = 1.0$

**Classification Decision** (using threshold $\tau$):

$$\text{Class}(d) = \begin{cases} \text{Restricted} & \text{if } \text{Score}(d) \geq 0.85 \\ \text{Confidential} & \text{if } 0.65 \leq \text{Score}(d) < 0.85 \\ \text{Internal} & \text{if } 0.40 \leq \text{Score}(d) < 0.65 \\ \text{Public} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 3: Policy Matching & Decision
The classified document is matched against the **active policy rule set**. The decision function is:

$$D(d, a, u) = \begin{cases} \text{Block} & \text{if } \text{Policy}_{\text{deny}}(d) \land \neg \text{Exception}(u) \\ \text{Encrypt} & \text{if } \text{Policy}_{\text{protect}}(d) \land \text{Action} = \text{External Share} \\ \text{Warn} & \text{if } \text{Policy}_{\text{advisory}}(d) \\ \text{Allow} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $d$ = document/data object
- $a$ = attempted action (email, USB copy, upload)
- $u$ = user identity & role
- $\text{Exception}(u)$ = role-based override (e.g., Security Admin)

### Step 4: Enforcement & Response
Based on decision $D$, the enforcement module executes the corresponding action. The **response latency** must satisfy:

$$t_{\text{enforcement}} \leq t_{\text{exfiltration}}$$

If enforcement time exceeds exfiltration time, DLP is ineffective — this drives the **real-time inline architecture** of Network DLP.

### Step 5: Logging & Feedback Loop
Every event is logged with a tuple: $(t, u, d, a, D, \text{hash}(d))$

This feeds the SIEM and supports the continuous tuning equation:

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} + \eta \cdot (\text{FP} - \text{FN})$$

Where $\eta$ is the learning rate, and FP/FN are false positive/negative counts — implementing a **closed-loop ML refinement**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DLP vs Adjacent Technologies

| **Feature** | **DLP** | **CASB** | **DAM (Database Activity Monitoring)** | **FIM (File Integrity Monitoring)** |
|-------------|---------|----------|----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Primary Focus** | Data confidentiality across states | Cloud service governance | Database queries & access | File change detection |
| **Scope** | All data (files, emails, packets) | SaaS apps only | DBs only | Files only |
| **Insider Threat Defense** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |
| **OT/ICS Suitability** | High (with OT-aware policies) | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Real-time Blocking** | Yes | Yes | Alerts mostly | Alerts mostly |
| **Content Inspection Depth** | Deep (regex + ML) | Medium | Query-level | Hash-level |

---

## 3.3 Industrial DLP Implementation Roadmap (7-Step Framework)

1. **Asset Inventory** — Map all data flows in the Purdue Model (Levels 0–5).
2. **Data Classification** — Tag P&IDs, PLC code, batch records with sensitivity labels.
3. **Policy Definition** — Collaborate with OT, IT, Legal, Compliance teams.
4. **Pilot Deployment** — Start in *monitor-only mode* to baseline false positives.
5. **Phased Enforcement** — Move to *warn → block* over 3–6 months.
6. **SIEM/SOC Integration** — Feed alerts to the Security Operations Center.
7. **Continuous Tuning** — Quarterly policy review, ML model retraining.

> **Critical Engineering Insight**: In OT environments, **never deploy endpoint DLP agents directly on Level 0–1 controllers (PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers)**. This violates real-time determinism and may cause process instability. Deploy on **Level 2–3** (HMI, Historian, Engineering Workstations) only.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DLP Architecture — IT/OT Converged Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Threat Actors] --> B[Perimeter Firewall]
    B --> C[Network DLP Engine]
    C --> D{Core Network Switch}
    D --> E[IT Zone Servers]
    D --> F[OT DMZ Historian]
    F --> G[Level 3 Site Operations]
    G --> H[Level 2 HMI Stations]
    H --> I[Level 1 PLCs and RTUs]
    I --> J[Level 0 Field Sensors]
    
    K[Endpoint DLP Agent] -.-> H
    K -.-> F
    K -.-> E
    
    L[Storage DLP Scanner] -.-> E
    L -.-> F
    
    M[Cloud DLP CASB] -.-> N[SaaS Apps]
    M -.-> O[Cloud Storage]
    
    C --> P[SIEM SOC Dashboard]
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    
    P --> Q[Incident Response Team]
    Q --> R[Policy Tuning Engine]
    R -.-> C
    R -.-> K
    R -.-> L
    R -.-> M
```

> **Diagram Interpretation**: This block diagram shows the **multi-layer DLP deployment** across the Purdue Model. Notice that DLP agents are **deployed at Levels 2–3 only** (HMI, Historian, Engineering Workstations) to avoid disrupting real-time Level 0–1 operations. All DLP events funnel into a central SIEM for SOC analysis.

---

## 4.2 DLP Policy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([User Action Detected]) --> Capture[Capture Data Object and Context]
    Capture --> Classify{Classification Engine}
    Classify -->|Score >= 0.85| Restricted[Restricted Label]
    Classify -->|0.65 to 0.85| Confidential[Confidential Label]
    Classify -->|0.40 to 0.65| Internal[Internal Label]
    Classify -->|Below 0.40| Public[Public Label]
    
    Restricted --> PolicyCheck{Policy Match}
    Confidential --> PolicyCheck
    Internal --> PolicyCheck
    Public --> Allow1[Action: Allow]
    
    PolicyCheck -->|External Destination| Block[Action: Block and Alert]
    PolicyCheck -->|Internal Destination| Allow2[Action: Allow with Log]
    PolicyCheck -->|Exception Role| Audit[Action: Audit Only]
    
    Block --> Log[(Central Log and SIEM)]
    Allow2 --> Log
    Audit --> Log
    
    Log --> SOC[SOC Review and Forensics]
    SOC --> Tune[Policy Tuning Feedback]
    Tune -.-> PolicyCheck
```

---

## 4.3 DLP Data Lifecycle Coverage Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Creation] --> B[Data Classification]
    B --> C[Data Storage]
    C --> D[Data Sharing]
    D --> E[Data Archival]
    E --> F[Data Destruction]
    
    G[Storage DLP] -.-> C
    H[Endpoint DLP] -.-> D
    I[Network DLP] -.-> D
    J[Cloud DLP] -.-> D
    K[Discovery DLP] -.-> B
    L[Erasure DLP] -.-> F
```

> **Block Architecture Note**: This functional flow illustrates the **six-stage data lifecycle** and the corresponding DLP module that provides protection at each stage. KTU 2024 examiners frequently use such lifecycle diagrams in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define Data Loss Prevention (DLP). List any two types of DLP solutions.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Data Loss Prevention (DLP) refers to a comprehensive set of **strategies, policies, procedures, and tools** designed to **identify, monitor, protect, and prevent the unauthorized access, use, transmission, or storage of sensitive data** across an organization. DLP ensures that confidential information — including intellectual property, financial records, personally identifiable information (PII), and in industrial contexts, **OT operational data and SCADA configurations** — does not leave the corporate environment through unauthorized channels.

**Two Types (1 Mark):**
1. **Network DLP (NDLP)** — Monitors data in motion at network egress points (email gateways, proxies).
2. **Endpoint DLP (EDLP)** — Monitors data in use on user devices (laptops, HMI workstations), preventing USB transfers, print, clipboard copy.

*(Valid alternatives: Cloud DLP, Storage DLP, Email DLP — any two accepted)*

---

### **Q2. What is the difference between 'Data in Motion' and 'Data at Rest' in the context of DLP?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Data in Motion (DIM) | Data at Rest (DAR) |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Data actively traveling across networks | Data stored on persistent media |
| **DLP Module** | Network DLP, Email DLP | Storage DLP, Discovery DLP |
| **Inspection Point** | Inline at firewalls, proxies, mail gateways | Scheduled file system crawls, database scans |
| **Example** | SCADA telemetry sent over MQTT to vendor cloud | Historian database archive on plant server |
| **Protection Technique** | Stream inspection, SSL/TLS interception | File fingerprinting, regex scanning, encryption-at-rest verification |

**Conclusion (Closing line for 3 marks):** DLP systems must address **both** states simultaneously to provide comprehensive coverage, as attackers may stage exfiltration by first *collecting* at-rest data and then *transmitting* it in-motion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU Pattern**

> **Q: (a)** Explain the three states of data with respect to DLP. Discuss the various **content inspection techniques** used in DLP systems. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a Network DLP system** deployed in an Industrial Control System (ICS) environment. Highlight how it differs from a pure IT deployment. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
DLP systems protect sensitive data across its entire lifecycle. Data exists in three primary states, each requiring distinct protection mechanisms.

**The Three States (3 Marks):**

1. **Data in Motion (DIM):** Data traversing the network — emails, HTTP traffic, file transfers, SCADA telemetry. Protected via **inline Network DLP** appliances that perform **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**. Example: An engineer emailing a PLC program to an external vendor.

2. **Data in Use (DIU):** Data being actively accessed, modified, or processed by applications/users. Protected by **Endpoint DLP agents** that monitor clipboard, USB, print, and screen capture operations. Example: A contractor copying ladder logic from an HMI to a USB drive.

3. **Data at Rest (DAR):** Data stored on disks, databases, file shares, cloud storage. Protected by **Storage DLP scanners** that perform scheduled crawls and fingerprinting. Example: Unencrypted CAD drawings on an engineering workstation's D: drive.

**Content Inspection Techniques (3 Marks):**

| Technique | Mechanism | Strength | Limitation |
|-----------|-----------|----------|------------|
| **Pattern Matching (Regex)** | Detects structured identifiers (SSN, CC numbers) | Fast, low overhead | Easily bypassed by obfuscation |
| **Fingerprinting** | Hash-based exact file matching | Zero false positives | Detects only known files |
| **Exact Data Matching (EDM)** | Row-level DB comparison | Catches partial leaks | High storage overhead |
| **Statistical / ML** | n-gram, word frequency analysis | Detects novel data | Requires training data |
| **Contextual Analysis** | Evaluates metadata (sender, recipient, time) | Catches behavioural anomalies | High false positives alone |

**Conclusion (Closing sentence for full marks):** Modern enterprise DLP combines **all five techniques** in a layered, weighted scoring engine to balance detection accuracy with operational feasibility.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**ICS-Specific Network DLP Architecture (5 Marks):**

A Network DLP system in an ICS environment is deployed at strategic chokepoints within the **Purdue Model**, specifically:

- **Level 3.5 (OT DMZ)** — Between the corporate IT network and plant OT network.
- **Level 4 (Site Business Planning)** — Mail gateways, web proxies.
- **Egress to Internet** — Where OT data could leak to external cloud services.

**Block Diagram Description (in text since diagram is in Section 4):**

```
[Corporate IT] → [Mail Gateway + Network DLP] → [Firewall] → [OT DMZ] → [Plant Historian] → [HMI] → [PLC]
```

**Key Architectural Features:**

1. **Inline Bridge Mode** — DLP sits in-line, inspecting every packet in real-time.
2. **Protocol-Aware Parsing** — Understands ICS protocols (Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA, MQTT).
3. **Asset Whitelisting** — Allows legitimate vendor remote access (RSA, VPN tunnels) without false alarms.
4. **Air-gap Preservation** — DLP enforces directional flow rules (IT → OT for patches, OT → IT for telemetry only).

**Differences from Pure IT Deployment (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | IT DLP | ICS/OT DLP |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| **Protocols** | HTTP, SMTP, FTP | Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA, MQTT |
| **Data Types** | PII, financial records | P&IDs, ladder logic, batch recipes |
| **Update Cycle** | Frequent signature updates | Minimized to avoid process disruption |
| **Latency Tolerance** | ~100 ms acceptable | <10 ms (real-time control) |
| **Deployment** | Centralized | Segmented per plant zone |

**Conclusion:** ICS DLP prioritizes **availability and safety** over strict confidentiality, following the **ISA/IEC 62443** safety priority hierarchy: *Safety > Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality*.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q: (a)** Discuss the **five-stage DLP workflow** from data discovery to incident response. Explain how **policy enforcement modes** (Monitor, Warn, Block) are used in phased deployment. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Describe **Insider Threat detection** using DLP in an industrial environment. Compare **Endpoint DLP vs Network DLP** for detecting malicious insiders. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Five-Stage DLP Workflow (5 Marks):**

1. **Discovery** — Agentless scanners crawl file shares (SMB, NFS), databases (SQL), and cloud repositories (OneDrive, SharePoint) to enumerate sensitive data. **OT context:** Discovery must include historians, engineering shares, and version control repos containing PLC code.

2. **Classification** — Each file is scored using a weighted formula combining regex hits, fingerprint matches, and ML classifier confidence. Labels assigned: *Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted*.

3. **Policy Definition** — Security teams author rules: *"Block outbound emails containing 'Confidential' files to non-corporate domains."* Policies may be **content-based**, **context-based**, or **user/role-based**.

4. **Enforcement** — The DLP agent intercepts the action and applies the rule. Possible actions: Allow, Warn, Quarantine, Block, Encrypt.

5. **Monitoring & Response** — Logs flow to SIEM. SOC analysts triage alerts, conduct forensics, and update policies.

**Phased Deployment — Policy Enforcement Modes (2 Marks):**

| Mode | Behavior | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Monitor (Audit-Only)** | Logs violations but does not block | Initial 30–60 day baseline period to tune false positives |
| **Warn** | Notifies user of policy violation but allows action | Soft launch to raise employee awareness |
| **Block** | Hard-stops the action; quarantine or deny | Full production enforcement after policy maturity |

**Conclusion:** The phased rollout is **critical in OT environments** where a wrongly blocked file transfer could disrupt operations (e.g., blocking a calibration file needed for shift handover).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Insider Threat in Industrial Environments (2 Marks):**

Insiders (employees, contractors, vendors with authorized access) pose the **#1 threat to OT data confidentiality**. Categories:
- **Malicious insiders** — Disgruntled employees stealing IP for competitors.
- **Negligent insiders** — Well-meaning engineers emailing schematics to personal accounts.
- **Compromised insiders** — Credentials stolen by external attackers (e.g., phishing).

**Endpoint DLP vs Network DLP for Insider Detection (5 Marks):**

| Detection Vector | Endpoint DLP | Network DLP |
|-----------------|--------------|-------------|
| **USB data theft** | ★★★★★ (full visibility into removable media) | ★ (only sees traffic, not local copies) |
| **Email exfiltration** | ★★★ (if agent monitors Outlook) | ★★★★★ (SMTP gateway inspection) |
| **Cloud upload** | ★★★ (browser activity) | ★★★★ (HTTPS proxy) |
| **Print screen capture** | ★★★★★ (can block print-screen API) | ★ (no visibility) |
| **Encrypted exfiltration** | ★★★ (can intercept pre-encryption) | ★★ (limited by SSL pinning) |
| **OT context (air-gapped)** | ★★★★★ (operates offline) | ★★ (requires network tap) |

**Conclusion:** For **insider threat defense in OT**, **Endpoint DLP is more effective** because it operates at the source, captures offline activity, and has visibility into local actions (USB, print, screenshots) that Network DLP cannot see. The optimal industrial deployment uses **both**, with Endpoint DLP on engineering workstations and Network DLP at the OT DMZ egress.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in DLP Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DLP with firewalls or antivirus** — DLP is **data-centric**, not network/host-centric. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Ignoring the three states of data** — Always mention DIM, DIU, DAR. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Not stating false-positive trade-off** — KTU 2024 expects awareness that strict DLP = business disruption.
> 4. **Forgetting OT-specific concerns** — Real-time latency, Level 0–1 restrictions, ISA/IEC 62443.
> 5. **Writing only definitions in 7-mark sub-parts** — Examiner expects **diagrams + examples + comparison tables**.
> 6. **No closing conclusion** — Always end with a summarizing line ("Thus, DLP is essential for...").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DLP**

- ✅ **Definition**: Strategies, tools, and policies to prevent unauthorized data exfiltration.
- ✅ **Three States**: Data in Motion, Data in Use, Data at Rest.
- ✅ **Five DLP Types**: Network, Endpoint, Cloud, Storage, Email.
- ✅ **Five Inspection Techniques**: Regex, Fingerprinting, EDM, ML, Contextual.
- ✅ **Five-Stage Workflow**: Discovery → Classification → Policy → Enforcement → Monitoring.
- ✅ **Three Enforcement Modes**: Monitor → Warn → Block (phased rollout).
- ✅ **Five-Tier Classification**: Public → Internal → Confidential → Restricted → Top Secret.
- ✅ **OT/ICS Rules**: Deploy only at Purdue Levels 2–3. Never on Level 0–1.
- ✅ **Standards**: ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53 (Rev 5), ISA/IEC 62443.
- ✅ **Key Metrics**: Detection Coverage, False Positive Rate (<5%), MTTD.
- ✅ **Industrial Data Types Protected**: P&IDs, PLC code, Historian records, SCADA configs, batch recipes, digital twin models.
- ✅ **Top 3 Vendors (Industry Awareness)**: Symantec (Broadcom), Microsoft Purview DLP, Forcepoint DLP, Digital Guardian.
- ✅ **Integration Points**: SIEM (Splunk, QRadar), SOAR, UEBA, EDR, CASB.
- ✅ **Formula to Memorize**: 
  - $R = C \times S + V \times T$ (Risk Score)
  - $C = N_{\text{DLP-governed}} / N_{\text{Total}} \times 100\%$ (Coverage)
  - $t_{\text{enforcement}} \leq t_{\text{exfiltration}}$ (Real-time requirement)

> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In 14-mark answers, structure as **Definition → Diagram → Detailed Explanation → Comparison Table → OT/Industrial Context → Conclusion**. This 6-element structure consistently scores 12–14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encryption at Rest and In Transit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Encryption at Rest and In Transit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encryption** is the cryptographic process of transforming readable plaintext (P) into unreadable ciphertext (C) using a mathematical algorithm (cipher) and a secret key (K), such that the original data can only be recovered by an authorized party possessing the correct decryption key.

In the context of **Advanced Industrial Cyber Security**, encryption is classified into two fundamental operational states based on the **data lifecycle phase** it protects:

### A. Encryption at Rest
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Encryption at Rest refers to the cryptographic protection of data while it is **persistently stored** on physical or logical media — such as hard drives, SSDs, databases, backup tapes, cloud storage buckets, or USB devices — when the data is **not actively moving** through networks.

The data is in a "stationary" or "dormant" state. The threat model assumes an attacker has gained **physical access** to the storage medium (e.g., stolen laptop, compromised server, unauthorized database dump) and is attempting to read the raw bytes directly.

### B. Encryption in Transit (a.k.a. Encryption in Motion / In Flight)
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Encryption in Transit refers to the cryptographic protection of data while it is **actively being transmitted** across communication channels — such as LANs, WANs, the public Internet, wireless networks, or industrial bus protocols — between a source endpoint and a destination endpoint.

The data is in a "moving" or "in-flight" state. The threat model assumes an adversary is performing **eavesdropping (passive attack)** or **man-in-the-middle (active attack)** on the communication path.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Parchment" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Students:**

Imagine you are sending a **highly confidential industrial process recipe** (e.g., a chemical formula for pharmaceutical manufacturing) from your factory's main office to a partner facility overseas.

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | Technical Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Drafting the formula** at your desk | You write the formula on a parchment and **lock it inside a steel safe** before leaving the office for the night. Even if a thief breaks into your office and steals the safe, they cannot open it without the combination. | **Encryption at Rest** — data is encrypted on the disk/server before storage. |
| **Shipping the formula** overseas | You place the parchment inside a **tamper-proof, armoured courier pouch** that is sealed with a unique wax stamp. The courier physically carries it across countries. Nobody can peek inside during transit. | **Encryption in Transit** — data is encrypted inside TLS/IPsec tunnels during network travel. |
| **Receiving at destination** | The receiver **verifies the wax seal**, breaks it open, and reads the formula. | **Decryption at the destination endpoint** using the negotiated session key. |

> The steel safe protects the parchment **at rest** in your office. The armoured pouch protects it **in transit** across the world. **Both are necessary** — losing either protection compromises the confidentiality of the recipe.

---

## 1.3 Why Both Are Required in Industrial Cyber Security

Industrial Control Systems (ICS) and SCADA environments handle **Crown Jewel Assets** — proprietary process formulas, PLC ladder logic, HMI configurations, and operational telemetry. The **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** strategy mandated by standards like **IEC 62443** and **NIST SP 800-53** explicitly requires encryption in **both states**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Note:** A common student misconception is that "encryption in transit alone is sufficient." This is **FALSE**. If data is transmitted encrypted (TLS) but stored unencrypted on a server's hard disk, an attacker who breaches the server (via SQL injection, ransomware, or insider threat) can read **all** the data in plaintext. Conversely, if data is stored encrypted but transmitted in plaintext (HTTP, FTP, Telnet), a network sniffer (Wireshark, tcpdump) can intercept the entire stream. **Both layers are mandatory.**

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Metric / Constant | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **AES Key Size** | **128, 192, or 256 bits** | NIST-approved symmetric block cipher standard (FIPS 197) |
| **RSA Key Size** | **2048 or 4096 bits** | Asymmetric cipher minimum for industrial use (post-2020) |
| **TLS 1.3 Cipher Suites** | **AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305** | Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) |
| **NIST Key Management Lifecycle** | **5 phases** | Pre-operational, Operational, Post-operational, Destroyed, Archived |
| **FIPS 140-2/140-3 Levels** | **Level 1 to 4** | Cryptographic module validation rigor (Level 4 = highest) |
| **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** | **Mandatory in TLS 1.3** | Each session uses ephemeral (DHE/ECDHE) keys |
| **IKEv2** | **RFC 7296** | IPsec key exchange standard for VPN tunnels |
| **MTU Overhead for IPsec** | **50–73 bytes** | Reduces effective payload due to ESP header/trailer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Encryption at Rest — Theoretical Framework

Encryption at Rest protects data against threats such as **physical theft of devices**, **unauthorized access to backup media**, **insider threats with OS-level access**, and **cloud storage exfiltration**.

### 2.1.1 Layered Architecture of Encryption at Rest

The protection is applied at **four distinct layers** of the storage stack:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — Full Disk Encryption (FDE) / Self-Encrypting Drives (SED)**
> - Encrypts **every sector** of a disk uniformly using a symmetric key (typically AES-256-XTS).
> - Examples: **BitLocker** (Windows), **FileVault 2** (macOS), **LUKS** (Linux), **OPAL-compliant SSDs** (hardware SED).
> - **Key Storage:** Disk Encryption Key (DEK) is stored in the TPM (Trusted Platform Module) chip or HSM.
> - **Authentication:** Pre-boot PIN, smart card, or TPM-measured PCR values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 2 — File-Level Encryption (FLE)**
> - Encrypts **individual files** or directories (e.g., EFS — Encrypting File System on NTFS, PGP/GPG for documents).
> - Allows **granular access control** (per-user, per-group).
> - Useful when full disk encryption is impractical (e.g., shared network drives).

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 — Database Encryption**
> - **Transparent Data Encryption (TDE):** Encrypts the entire database at the file level (data files, log files, backups). The encryption/decryption is transparent to applications.
> - **Column-Level Encryption:** Specific sensitive columns (e.g., SSN, credit card numbers) are encrypted with application-managed keys.
> - **Field-Level Encryption (FLE in DB context):** Each field has its own encryption key envelope.
> - Examples: **SQL Server TDE**, **Oracle Transparent Data Encryption**, **MongoDB Encrypted Storage Engine**, **AWS RDS Encryption**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 4 — Application-Level / Object Encryption**
> - The **application itself** encrypts sensitive data before persisting it (e.g., AWS S3 SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C).
> - Cryptographic operations occur in the **application's memory space** before data is written to disk.

### 2.1.2 Key Management for Encryption at Rest

The single most important sub-system in encryption at rest is **Key Management**. The cryptographic keys themselves become the crown jewels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Management Service (KMS) / Hardware Security Module (HSM) Architecture:**
> - **Key Generation:** Keys are generated inside **FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or 4** HSMs using cryptographically secure random number generators (CSPRNG) seeded from hardware entropy sources.
> - **Key Storage:** Keys never leave the HSM in plaintext. They are used to wrap/unwrap other keys via envelope encryption.
> - **Key Rotation:** Periodic (typically **90 days** for data keys, **1 year** for master keys) to limit blast radius of compromise.
> - **Key Revocation:** Compromised keys are added to CRLs or marked as disabled in the KMS; data is re-encrypted with new keys.
> - **Key Custodianship:** Dual control (M-of-N) split-knowledge scheme to prevent single-actor key misuse.
> - **Industry Examples:** AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, Google Cloud KMS, HashiCorp Vault, Thales Luna HSM.

### 2.1.3 Envelope Encryption Pattern (Critical Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Envelope Encryption** is the industry-standard approach where data is encrypted with a **Data Encryption Key (DEK)**, and the DEK itself is encrypted with a **Key Encryption Key (KEK)** stored in the HSM/KMS.

$$C_{\text{data}} = E_{K_{\text{DEK}}}(P_{\text{data}})$$

$$C_{\text{DEK}} = E_{K_{\text{KEK}}}(K_{\text{DEK}})$$

The **encrypted DEK** is stored alongside the ciphertext. The plaintext DEK **never touches disk or memory** in unprotected form. This pattern enables efficient key rotation (only the DEK needs re-wrapping, not the entire dataset).

---

## 2.2 Encryption in Transit — Theoretical Framework

Encryption in Transit protects data against **eavesdropping, tampering, and impersonation** during network movement.

### 2.2.1 Protocol Stack Position

Encryption in Transit can be applied at **different layers of the OSI/TCP-IP model**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 7 (Application Layer) — TLS/SSL**
> - The most common form. **TLS 1.3** (RFC 8446) is the current standard, deprecating TLS 1.0/1.1 and SSL 3.0.
> - Wraps protocols like HTTPS, SMTPS, IMAPS, LDAPS, FTPS, SFTP.
> - Uses **X.509 digital certificates** for server (and optionally client) authentication.
> - **Cipher Suite Negotiation:** Client offers supported suites; server selects one (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`).
> - **Handshake:** ECDHE key exchange + ECDSA/RSA certificate authentication → derive session keys via HKDF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 (Network Layer) — IPsec**
> - **Mode 1: Transport Mode** — Encrypts only the payload of the IP packet (used for host-to-host).
> - **Mode 2: Tunnel Mode** — Encrypts the **entire original IP packet** and adds a new IP header (used for site-to-site VPN, e.g., gateway-to-gateway).
> - **Protocols:** ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload, RFC 4303) provides confidentiality + integrity + anti-replay; AH (Authentication Header, RFC 4302) provides integrity + authentication only.
> - **Key Exchange:** IKEv2 (Internet Key Exchange version 2, RFC 7296) negotiates SAs (Security Associations) using pre-shared keys or certificates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 4 (Transport Layer) — DTLS, SSH Tunneling**
> - **DTLS (Datagram TLS, RFC 6347):** TLS over UDP for real-time protocols (VoIP, video, industrial real-time telemetry).
> - **SSH (Secure Shell, RFC 4253):** Provides encrypted terminal sessions, port forwarding (SSH tunneling), and SFTP/SCP for file transfer.

### 2.2.2 TLS 1.3 Handshake — The Modern Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TLS 1.3 Improvements over TLS 1.2:**
> - **1-RTT Handshake** (down from 2-RTT) — faster connection establishment.
> - **Mandatory Forward Secrecy** — DHE/ECDHE is the **only** key exchange mechanism (no static RSA key exchange).
> - **AEAD-only ciphers** — AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 (no CBC mode, no RC4, no MD5, no SHA-1).
> - **Encrypted Extensions** — SNI (Server Name Indication) is encrypted, preventing traffic analysis.
> - **0-RTT Resumption** — for previously connected clients (with replay risk).

### 2.2.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) for In-Transit Encryption

> [!NOTE]
> **PKI Components:**
> - **Certificate Authority (CA):** Trusted issuer of X.509 digital certificates (root CA → intermediate CA → leaf/end-entity cert).
> - **Registration Authority (RA):** Verifies identity before CA issues certificate.
> - **Certificate Revocation List (CRL) / OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol):** Real-time revocation checking.
> - **Trust Store:** Operating system / browser pre-installed root CA certificates.
> - **Certificate Pinning:** Client pins expected server certificate (or public key) to detect MITM attacks.
> - **Industrial PKI:** Per IEC 62443, ICS devices use **device certificates** issued by an internal Plant PKI for mutual authentication.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Key Equations and Constants**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard / Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric Encryption (Block Cipher)** | $C = E_K(P)$, $\quad P = D_K(C)$ | NIST FIPS 197 (AES) |
| **Asymmetric Encryption** | $C = E_{K_{\text{pub}}}(P)$, $\quad P = D_{K_{\text{priv}}}(C)$ | RSA (RFC 8017), ECC |
| **AES Block Size** | $B = 128 \text{ bits}$ | FIPS 197 |
| **AES Key Sizes** | $k \in \{128, 192, 256\} \text{ bits}$ | FIPS 197 |
| **AES Rounds** | $N_r = 10 \text{ (AES-128)}, 12 \text{ (AES-192)}, 14 \text{ (AES-256)}$ | FIPS 197 |
| **RSA Security** | $k \geq 2048 \text{ bits (NIST 2024)}$ | NIST SP 800-131A |
| **Diffie-Hellman Modulus** | $p \geq 2048 \text{ bits (IKEv2 Group 14+)}$ | RFC 3526 |
| **Elliptic Curve Key Size** | $k \geq 256 \text{ bits (secp256r1) equivalent to RSA 3072}$ | NIST SP 800-186 |
| **HMAC-SHA256 Output** | $\text{HMAC} = H((K \oplus opad) \vert\vert H((K \oplus ipad) \vert\vert M))$ | RFC 2104 |
| **HKDF Extract** | $PRK = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(salt, IKM)$ | RFC 5869 |
| **HKDF Expand** | $OKM = T(1) \vert\vert T(2) \vert\vert \ldots \vert T(N)$ where $T(i) = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(PRK, T(i-1) \vert\vert info \vert\vert i)$ | RFC 5869 |
| **XOR Operation** | $C = P \oplus K$, $\quad P = C \oplus K$ | Foundation of stream ciphers |
| **Digital Signature** | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{\text{priv}}}(H(M))$ | FIPS 186-5 (DSA, RSA, ECDSA) |
| **Hash Function Output** | $h = H(M)$, $\quad \vert h \vert = 256 \text{ bits (SHA-256)}$ | FIPS 180-4 |
| **TLS 1.3 Cipher Suite** | $\text{TLS\_AES\_256\_GCM\_SHA384}$ | RFC 8446 |
| **Entropy Requirement** | $\geq 128 \text{ bits of true entropy for AES-256 key}$ | NIST SP 800-90A |
| **Key Rotation Period (DEK)** | $T_{\text{rot}} \leq 90 \text{ days}$ | Industry best practice |
| **Master Key Rotation** | $T_{\text{mk}} \leq 365 \text{ days}$ | NIST SP 800-57 |
| **MTU Overhead (IPsec ESP)** | $\Delta_{\text{MTU}} \approx 50\text{–}73 \text{ bytes}$ | RFC 4303 |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{\text{payload}}}{L_{\text{payload}} + \Delta_{\text{MTU}}}$ | Engineering rule |
| **FIPS 140-2/3 Levels** | $\text{Level} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ | FIPS 140-2/3 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Industry Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **Power & Utilities (Smart Grid)** | IPsec VPN between substations and control center; TDE for SCADA historian databases |
| **Oil & Gas (Pipelines)** | TLS 1.3 for RTU-to-Master telemetry; hardware SED for PLC program storage |
| **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** | TDE on MES databases protecting proprietary batch recipes; column-level encryption on PII |
| **Automotive (Connected Vehicles)** | TLS for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication; HSM for immobilizer key storage |
| **Healthcare IoMT** | AES-256 at rest for patient records (HIPAA); TLS for device-to-cloud telemetry |
| **Manufacturing 4.0** | OPC UA over TLS for machine-to-machine (M2M) communication in smart factories |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation — Encryption at Rest

### 3.1.1 AES-256 Single Block Encryption Pipeline

The **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is the dominant symmetric cipher for data at rest. AES-256 operates on **128-bit blocks** with a **256-bit key** across **14 rounds**.

**AES Round Transformations (Single Round):**

> [!NOTE]
> **The four sub-operations executed in each AES round:**

1. **SubBytes (S-Box substitution):** Each byte in the 4×4 state matrix is replaced via a non-linear 16×16 lookup table:

$$S = \text{S-Box}(S) = \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0}' & s_{0,1}' & s_{0,2}' & s_{0,3}' \\ s_{1,0}' & s_{1,1}' & s_{1,2}' & s_{1,3}' \\ s_{2,0}' & s_{2,1}' & s_{2,2}' & s_{2,3}' \\ s_{3,0}' & s_{3,1}' & s_{3,2}' & s_{3,3}' \end{bmatrix}$$

where each $s'_{i,j} = \text{S-Box}(s_{i,j})$ over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ with the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.

2. **ShiftRows (Row-wise cyclic rotation):** Row $i$ is rotated left by $i$ positions:

$$S' = \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} & s_{1,0} \\ s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} & s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} \\ s_{3,3} & s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} \end{bmatrix}$$

3. **MixColumns (Column-wise linear diffusion):** Each column vector $\mathbf{c}$ is multiplied by a fixed MDS matrix in $\text{GF}(2^8)$:

$$\mathbf{c}' = M_{\text{mix}} \cdot \mathbf{c} \pmod{m(x)}$$

where

$$M_{\text{mix}} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix}$$

4. **AddRoundKey (XOR with round key):** The round key $K_r$ is derived from the original key $K$ via the **Key Expansion** algorithm:

$$S_{\text{out}} = S' \oplus K_r$$

**Full AES-256 Encryption (14 Rounds):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \text{AddRoundKey}(\text{State}, K_0) \\
&\circ \ \text{SubBytes} \circ \text{ShiftRows} \circ \text{MixColumns} \circ \text{AddRoundKey}(K_1) \\
&\circ \ \text{SubBytes} \circ \text{ShiftRows} \circ \text{MixColumns} \circ \text{AddRoundKey}(K_2) \\
&\quad \vdots \\
&\circ \ \text{SubBytes} \circ \text{ShiftRows} \circ \text{AddRoundKey}(K_{14})
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step-by-step explanation:**
> - **Step 1:** The plaintext block (16 bytes = 128 bits) is loaded into a 4×4 state matrix.
> - **Step 2:** Initial round key $K_0$ is XORed with the state (whitening step).
> - **Steps 3-15:** Rounds 1 through 13 each perform SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey.
> - **Step 16:** Final round (14th) **omits MixColumns** (a design choice to simplify the inverse cipher) — only SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey is performed.
> - **Step 17:** The final state is emitted as the ciphertext block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on AES-256 key expansion:** Generates **15 round keys** ($K_0$ through $K_{14}$), each 128 bits, for a total of **15 × 128 = 1920 bits** of round key material derived from the 256-bit master key.

---

### 3.1.2 Envelope Encryption — Full Mathematical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A SCADA historian database at a power plant contains **2 TB of time-series process data** that must be encrypted at rest. Performance is critical — symmetric encryption is used for bulk data, while the symmetric key is protected by an asymmetric operation.

**Step 1 — Data Encryption Key (DEK) Generation:**

$$K_{\text{DEK}} = \text{CSPRNG}(256 \text{ bits})$$

*Generated inside the HSM using NIST SP 800-90A DRBG (Deterministic Random Bit Generator) seeded by hardware entropy.*

**Step 2 — Bulk Data Encryption (Symmetric):**

$$C_i = E_{K_{\text{DEK}}}^{\text{AES-256-GCM}}(P_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, N \text{ blocks}$$

*Each plaintext block $P_i$ (e.g., 4 KB page) is encrypted using AES-256-GCM. GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) provides AEAD — both confidentiality and authenticity in one operation.*

**Step 3 — DEK Envelope (Asymmetric Wrap):**

$$C_{\text{DEK}} = E_{K_{\text{KEK}}}^{\text{RSA-2048}}(K_{\text{DEK}})$$

*The DEK itself is encrypted with the Key Encryption Key (KEK), which never leaves the HSM. The encrypted DEK ($C_{\text{DEK}}$) is stored in the database header alongside the ciphertext.*

**Step 4 — Storage Layout:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Database File: historian.db.enc             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Header:                                      │
│   Version: 1.0                               │
│   Algorithm: AES-256-GCM                     │
│   Wrapped DEK: C_DEK = [512 bytes]           │
│   IV: [12 bytes]                             │
│   Auth Tag: [16 bytes per block]             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Encrypted Data Blocks:                       │
│   [Block 1 ciphertext + auth tag]            │
│   [Block 2 ciphertext + auth tag]            │
│   ...                                        │
│   [Block N ciphertext + auth tag]            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 5 — Decryption (Reverse Process):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5a:} \quad & K_{\text{DEK}} = D_{K_{\text{KEK}}}^{\text{RSA-2048}}(C_{\text{DEK}}) \\
& \text{(Unwrap the DEK using KEK inside HSM)} \\
\\
\text{Step 5b:} \quad & K_{\text{DEK}} \text{ used only in HSM memory, never persisted} \\
\\
\text{Step 5c:} \quad & P_i = D_{K_{\text{DEK}}}^{\text{AES-256-GCM}}(C_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, N \\
& \text{(Auth tag verified; tampered blocks rejected)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Key Engineering Insight:** Notice that the **DEK is regenerated or re-wrapped** for every encryption operation, but the **KEK remains stable** for a long period (typically 1 year). This is why **rotating the KEK is expensive** (entire dataset must be re-encrypted), but rotating the DEK is **cheap** (only re-wrapped). This asymmetry is the architectural beauty of envelope encryption.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Foundation — Encryption in Transit

### 3.2.1 RSA Key Generation (Asymmetric Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **RSA is the historical foundation of TLS key exchange (TLS 1.2 and below). Although TLS 1.3 uses ECDHE, understanding RSA is essential for KTU exams.**

**Step 1 — Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$:**

$$p, q \leftarrow \text{CSPRNG} \text{ with } \vert p \vert \approx \vert q \vert \approx 1024 \text{ bits (for 2048-bit RSA)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the modulus:**

$$n = p \times q$$

For a 2048-bit RSA key, $n$ is exactly **2048 bits**.

**Step 3 — Compute Euler's totient:**

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Choose public exponent:**

$$e = 65537 \quad (0x10001)$$

This is the F4 prime — a near-universal standard because it is prime, small (fast modular exponentiation), and has no known structural weaknesses.

**Step 5 — Compute private exponent:**

$$d = e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

This means $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, computed via the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.

**Step 6 — Publish public key, secure private key:**

$$K_{\text{pub}} = (n, e), \quad K_{\text{priv}} = (n, d)$$

**Encryption and Decryption Operations:**

$$C = M^e \pmod{n}$$

$$M = C^d \pmod{n}$$

> **Verification of correctness** (Euler's theorem):

$$C^d \pmod{n} = (M^e)^d \pmod{n} = M^{ed} \pmod{n} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \pmod{n} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^k \pmod{n} = M \cdot 1^k = M$$

> The last equality holds because $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ (Euler's theorem) for $\gcd(M, n) = 1$.

---

### 3.2.2 TLS 1.3 Handshake — Step-by-Step Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) achieves a 1-RTT handshake with mandatory forward secrecy.**

```
CLIENT                                              SERVER
  |                                                    |
  |  1. ClientHello (key_share, signature_algorithms, |  (0-RTT data possible)
  |     supported_versions, psk_key_exchange_modes)    |
  |------------------------------------------------->  |
  |                                                    |
  |                          2. ServerHello (key_share, |
  |                             selected cipher suite) |
  |  <-------------------------------------------------|
  |                                                    |
  |  3. EncryptedExtensions                              |
  |     Certificate (server cert chain)                  |
  |     CertificateVerify (signature over handshake)    |
  |     Finished (MAC over handshake transcript)        |
  |  <-------------------------------------------------|
  |                                                    |
  |  4. Certificate (client cert, if mutual auth)       |
  |     CertificateVerify (client signature)            |
  |     Finished (client MAC)                            |
  |------------------------------------------------->  |
  |                                                    |
  |  5. Application Data (encrypted with derived keys)  |
  | <================================================> |
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

**Round 1 (Client → Server):**
- The client sends a **ClientHello** containing its **ephemeral ECDHE public key** (e.g., X25519) in the `key_share` extension.
- This key is **freshly generated per connection** — never reused. This is the source of **Forward Secrecy**.
- The client also advertises supported AEAD cipher suites (e.g., TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256).

**Server Processing:**
- Server picks a cipher suite and generates its own ephemeral ECDHE key pair.
- **ECDH shared secret computation** (Diffie-Hellman over the elliptic curve secp256r1 or X25519):

$$Z = x_{\text{client ECDH}} \cdot Y_{\text{server ECDH}} = y_{\text{server ECDH}} \cdot X_{\text{client ECDH}}$$

This point $Z$ (an element of the elliptic curve group) is the same on both sides due to the commutativity of scalar multiplication on an elliptic curve.

**Key Derivation (HKDF):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ECDH Shared Secret} \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Handshake Secret} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Master Secret} \\
\text{HKDF-Extract:} \quad PRK &= \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{salt}, \text{ECDH secret}) \\
\text{HKDF-Expand:} \quad \text{Handshake Secret} &= \text{HKDF-Expand}(PRK, \text{label}, \text{context}) \\
\text{Master Secret} &= \text{HKDF-Expand}(PRK_{\text{handshake}}, \text{"master secret"}, \text{context}) \\
\text{Application Traffic Keys} &= \text{HKDF-Expand}(PRK_{\text{master}}, \text{"app traffic keys"})
\end{aligned}$$

**Round 2 (Server → Client):**
- The server sends **ServerHello** (with its ephemeral key), followed by encrypted handshake messages.
- **EncryptedExtensions** carries the SNI confirmation and ALPN (Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation) result.
- **Certificate** presents the server's X.509 chain.
- **CertificateVerify** contains a digital signature over the entire handshake transcript, binding the server's identity to the session keys:

$$\sigma_{\text{server}} = \text{Sign}_{K_{\text{priv, server}}}(\text{Transcript Hash of handshake so far})$$

- **Finished** message contains a **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** over the entire handshake transcript using a key derived from the handshake secret.

**Round 3 (Client → Server):**
- Client verifies server certificate (chain, revocation, hostname).
- Client verifies the CertificateVerify signature.
- Client verifies the Finished MAC.
- Client sends its own Certificate (if mutual TLS is configured) and CertificateVerify.
- Client sends its own **Finished** message.

**Secure Channel Established:**
- Both sides now possess matching **application traffic keys** derived via HKDF.
- All subsequent application data is encrypted with **AES-256-GCM** or **ChaCha20-Poly1305**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Forward Secrecy Matters:** Even if the server's long-term private key is compromised **years later**, an attacker who recorded the ciphertext cannot decrypt it. This is because the session keys were derived from **ephemeral ECDHE keys** that were never persisted and were destroyed after the session ended. The attacker would need to break the discrete logarithm problem on the elliptic curve, which is computationally infeasible for 256-bit curves.

---

### 3.2.3 IPsec ESP Tunnel Mode — Encryption in Transit (Network Layer)

> [!NOTE]
> **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload, RFC 4303) in Tunnel Mode** is the standard for site-to-site industrial VPN tunnels (e.g., between a power plant SCADA control center and a remote substation).

**Original IPv4 Packet (Plaintext):**

```
┌──────────────────────────┐
│  Original IP Header      │  (e.g., 192.168.10.5 → 10.20.30.7)
│  TCP Header (20 bytes)   │
│  Application Payload     │  (e.g., MODBUS/TCP register read)
└──────────────────────────┘
```

**ESP Tunnel-Mode Encapsulation:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ New IP Header (gateway IPs, e.g., GW1 → GW2)             │  ← Outer routing
│ ESP Header (SPI, Seq#)                                    │  ← 8 bytes
│ Original IP Header                                        │  ← Encrypted
│ TCP Header                                               │  ← Encrypted
│ Application Payload                                       │  ← Encrypted
│ ESP Trailer (Padding, Pad Length, Next Header)            │  ← Encrypted
│ ESP Auth Data (HMAC-SHA256 over entire ESP packet)        │  ← Integrity only
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Engineering Observation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTU Overhead} &= \text{New IP Header} + \text{ESP Header} + \text{ESP Trailer} + \text{ESP Auth Data} \\
&= 20 + 8 + 2 + 12 + 16 \\
&= 58 \text{ bytes (IPv4)} \\
\text{MTU Overhead (IPv6 + AES-GCM ICV)} &= 73 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

If the underlying link has an MTU of 1500 bytes, the effective payload is reduced to **1500 − 73 = 1427 bytes**, requiring **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD)** or **TCP MSS clamping** to avoid fragmentation.

---

## 3.3 Side-by-Side Analytical Matrix

| Dimension | Encryption at Rest | Encryption in Transit |
|---|---|---|
| **Data State** | Persisted, stationary | In motion, in flight |
| **Primary Cipher** | AES-256-GCM, AES-256-XTS | AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 |
| **Key Type** | Symmetric (DEK) wrapped by KEK | Symmetric session keys derived via ECDHE |
| **Key Lifecycle** | Long-lived (90 days – 1 year) | Ephemeral (session-only) |
| **Key Storage** | HSM, KMS, TPM | Volatile memory (RAM) only |
| **Authentication** | TPM pre-boot, login credentials, MFA | X.509 certificates, PSK, mutual TLS |
| **Integrity Protection** | AEAD tag (GCM) or HMAC | AEAD tag (GCM) — built into TLS 1.3 |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Not required (data is static) | Mandatory (TLS 1.3 enforces ECDHE) |
| **Performance Bottleneck** | Disk I/O + AES-NI hardware acceleration | Network latency + handshake RTT |
| **Typical Standards** | FIPS 197, FIPS 140-2/3, NIST SP 800-38E (XTS) | RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), RFC 4303 (IPsec ESP) |
| **Threat Mitigated** | Physical theft, stolen backup, insider dump | Eavesdropping, MITM, replay, traffic analysis |
| **Common Vulnerabilities** | Key exposure, side-channel (TPM sniffing), cold boot | Stripping attack (downgrade), weak cert validation, BEAST/POODLE (legacy TLS) |
| **Industrial Example** | SCADA historian database TDE | Substation-to-control-center IPsec VPN |

## 4.1 Encryption at Rest — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

    subgraph CRYPTO["CRYPTOGRAPHIC LAYER"]
        B1["CSPRNG inside HSM / TPM"]
        B2["Key Management Service KMS"]
        B3["Key Encryption Key KEK stored in HSM"]
        B4["Data Encryption Key DEK generated per dataset"]
        B5["AES-256-GCM Engine"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["STORAGE LAYER"]
        C1["Encrypted File Block 1 with auth tag"]
        C2["Encrypted File Block 2 with auth tag"]
        C3["Encrypted File Block N with auth tag"]
        C4["Wrapped DEK stored in file header"]
        C5["Physical Media SSD HDD Tape Cloud Bucket"]
    end

    subgraph ACCESS["ACCESS CONTROL LAYER"]
        D1["TPM Pre-Boot PIN or Smart Card"]
        D2["OS-Level Access Control ACL"]
        D3["Audit Log for Key Operations"]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B5
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    B5 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C2 --> C5
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    D1 --> B2
    D2 --> B2
    D2 --> D3
    B2 --> D3
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
- The **Application Layer** produces plaintext industrial data.
- The **Cryptographic Layer** is where the magic happens: an HSM-protected KEK wraps a freshly generated DEK, and the DEK encrypts the data using AES-256-GCM.
- The **Storage Layer** persists the ciphertext blocks, the auth tags, and the wrapped DEK — but **never the plaintext DEK or KEK**.
- The **Access Control Layer** ensures only authorized principals (with valid TPM credentials and ACL permissions) can request unwrapping of the DEK from the HSM. Every key operation is logged for audit compliance (e.g., NERC CIP for power utilities).

## 4.2 Encryption in Transit — TLS 1.3 1-RTT Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT<br>Industrial HMI
    participant N as NETWORK<br>LAN / WAN / Internet
    participant S as SERVER<br>SCADA Historian
    participant PKI as PKI<br>Certificate Authority
    participant HSM as HSM<br>Server Private Key

    Note over C,S: TLS 1.3 Handshake (1-RTT)

    C->>C: Generate ephemeral X25519 key pair<br>(x_client, X_client = x_client * G)

    C->>N: ClientHello<br>{key_share: X_client, ciphers: AES-256-GCM, supported_versions: TLS 1.3}
    N->>S: Forward ClientHello

    S->>S: Generate ephemeral X25519 key pair<br>(y_server, Y_server = y_server * G)

    S->>PKI: Request server certificate chain
    PKI-->>S: Return leaf + intermediate certs

    S->>S: Compute shared secret:<br>Z = y_server * X_client = x_client * Y_server
    S->>S: Derive keys via HKDF:<br>Handshake Secret, Master Secret, App Traffic Keys

    S->>N: ServerHello {key_share: Y_server, cipher: TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384}
    S->>N: EncryptedExtensions
    S->>N: Certificate [server chain]
    S->>HSM: Sign handshake transcript with private key
    HSM-->>S: Return signature sigma_server
    S->>N: CertificateVerify [sigma_server]
    S->>N: Finished [MAC over transcript]

    N->>C: Forward ServerHello + encrypted handshake

    C->>C: Verify server cert chain against trust store
    C->>C: Compute shared secret:<br>Z = x_client * Y_server
    C->>C: Derive matching keys via HKDF
    C->>C: Verify Finished MAC

    C->>N: Finished [client MAC]
    N->>S: Forward client Finished

    Note over C,S: SECURE CHANNEL ESTABLISHED

    C->>S: Application Data {encrypted with AES-256-GCM}
    S->>C: Application Data {encrypted with AES-256-GCM}

    Note over C,S: Session ends -> ephemeral keys destroyed -> FORWARD SECRECY
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- The **Client** generates an **ephemeral** X25519 key pair (used only for this session).
- The **Server** does the same.
- Both sides exchange public keys and compute the **same ECDH shared secret** without ever transmitting it.
- The shared secret seeds the **HKDF** key derivation chain, producing:
  - **Handshake Secret** → protects the handshake
  - **Master Secret** → seeds application traffic keys
  - **Application Traffic Keys** → encrypt all subsequent data
- After session end, both sides **destroy** their ephemeral private keys. This guarantees **Forward Secrecy** — even capturing all ciphertext + the server's long-term private key later cannot decrypt past sessions.

---

## 4.3 Industrial Cyber Security — Defense-in-Depth with Encryption

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERIM["PERIMETER LAYER"]
        P1["Industrial Firewall<br>IEC 62443 Zone Boundaries"]
        P2["Intrusion Detection System IDS"]
        P3["DMZ with Jump Server"]
    end

    subgraph INTRANSIT["ENCRYPTION IN TRANSIT"]
        T1["TLS 1.3 for Application Traffic<br>HTTPS OPC UA over TLS"]
        T2["IPsec VPN for Site-to-Site<br>ESP Tunnel Mode with AES-256-GCM"]
        T3["SSH for Administrative Access<br>Public Key Authentication"]
        T4["PKI and X.509 Certificates<br>Device Certificates per IEC 62443"]
    end

    subgraph INTERNAL["INTERNAL NETWORK ZONE"]
        N1["Industrial Control LAN<br>SCADA DCS Network"]
        N2["Historian Database Server"]
        N3["Engineering Workstations"]
        N4["PLCs and RTUs"]
    end

    subgraph ATREST["ENCRYPTION AT REST"]
        R1["Full Disk Encryption FDE<br>Self-Encrypting Drive OPAL"]
        R2["Database TDE<br>Transparent Data Encryption"]
        R3["Column-Level Encryption for PII"]
        R4["Backup Tape Encryption<br>AES-256-XTS"]
    end

    subgraph KEYMGMT["KEY MANAGEMENT SUBSTRATE"]
        K1["Hardware Security Module HSM<br>FIPS 140-2 Level 3+"]
        K2["Key Management Service KMS"]
        K3["Key Rotation Policy 90 days DEK"]
        K4["Audit and Compliance Logging"]
    end

    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T1
    P3 --> T2
    T1 --> N1
    T2 --> N1
    T3 --> N1
    T4 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    N1 --> N4
    N2 --> R1
    N2 --> R2
    N2 --> R3
    N4 --> R1
    N3 --> R4
    R1 --> K1
    R2 --> K1
    R3 --> K1
    R4 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    K2 --> K3
    K2 --> K4
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
This is the **end-to-end industrial cyber security architecture** showing how encryption at rest and in transit work together as complementary layers of the **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** model mandated by IEC 62443-3-3.
- **Perimeter Layer:** Filters malicious traffic before it reaches the industrial zone.
- **Encryption in Transit Layer:** Protects all data flowing between systems, even within the trusted zone (zero-trust principle).
- **Internal Network Zone:** The actual industrial assets (PLCs, historians, workstations).
- **Encryption at Rest Layer:** Protects stored data on every device — from operator workstations to backup tapes.
- **Key Management Substrate:** The **root of trust** — the HSM and KMS that make the entire encryption architecture viable. Without proper key management, all encryption is moot.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is envelope encryption? Why is it preferred over direct encryption of bulk data with a master key?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Envelope encryption** is a cryptographic pattern in which a randomly generated **Data Encryption Key (DEK)** encrypts the bulk data, and the DEK itself is encrypted (wrapped) by a long-term **Key Encryption Key (KEK)** that resides inside an HSM or KMS.

**Why Preferred:**
1. **Performance:** The expensive asymmetric wrap operation is performed **once per dataset** (or per rotation cycle), while the cheap symmetric encryption is performed on every block. Avoids RSA-2048 from being called per block.
2. **Key Rotation Efficiency:** Rotating the KEK only requires **re-wrapping the DEK** — not re-encrypting the entire 2 TB dataset.
3. **HSM Offload:** The master KEK never leaves the HSM; the DEK never exists in plaintext on disk.
4. **Separation of Duties:** Key custodians (KEK holders) are different from data owners (DEK holders).
5. **Scalability:** Cloud KMS services (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault) natively use envelope encryption — every data key call is a single API request.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in ESE carry **14 marks** with **module-level internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** and its role in implementing encryption at rest in an industrial environment. Discuss FIPS 140-2 security levels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **TLS 1.3 handshake** and describe how **Forward Secrecy** is achieved using **ECDHE** (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral).

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**HSM Architecture:**

> **Step 1 — Hardware Tamper-Evident Enclosure:**
> An HSM is a physically hardened, tamper-resistant appliance. It contains:
> - A **secure cryptoprocessor** (e.g., Freescale P2020, Infineon SLE 97).
> - **Hardware True Random Number Generator (TRNG)** based on physical entropy sources (Johnson noise, shot noise, ring oscillators).
> - **Battery-backed RAM** for volatile key storage that is zeroized on tamper detection.
> - **Anti-tamper mesh and intrusion sensors** that trigger immediate key erasure upon physical breach.

> **Step 2 — Cryptographic Accelerator:**
> Dedicated hardware engines for AES, RSA, ECC, SHA-2/3, and HKDF operations. These engines are **FIPS-validated** (FIPS 140-2/140-3 approved algorithms only).

> **Step 3 — Key Custodianship & M-of-N Split Knowledge:**
> HSMs enforce **dual control** — a critical operation (e.g., unwrapping a master key) requires **M-of-N** smart cards to be inserted simultaneously (e.g., 3-of-5).

**Role in Industrial Encryption at Rest:**

1. The **Root Key** of the entire industrial PKI is generated and stored only inside the HSM.
2. All DEKs are wrapped by the HSM-resident KEK.
3. **Audit log** of every key operation is signed and exported to a SIEM (e.g., Splunk, QRadar) for compliance (NERC CIP, IEC 62443).

**FIPS 140-2 Security Levels (4 levels):**

| Level | Tamper Resistance | Identity Authentication | EFP / EFT* | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Minimal (production-grade) | None / basic | Not required | Software crypto module, general-purpose |
| **Level 2** | Tamper-evident coating | Operator role-based | Required | Small office equipment |
| **Level 3** | High — strong tamper resistance + zeroization | **Identity-based** (operator + role) | Required | Financial HSM, cloud KMS |
| **Level 4** | **Highest** — active tamper detection, environmental failure protection | Identity + multi-factor | Required + complete envelope coverage | Military, critical infrastructure (e.g., nuclear plant) |

*EFP = Environmental Failure Protection; EFT = Environmental Failure Testing.

> **Conclusion for industrial use:** Critical infrastructure operators should mandate **FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or higher** HSMs for protecting root keys used in SCADA and historian encryption.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Refer to **Section 3.2.2 (TLS 1.3 Handshake)** above for the complete flow diagram and step-by-step derivation. Key points to include:

1. **ClientHello** contains the client's **ephemeral ECDHE public key** (e.g., X25519: 32-byte public key).
2. **ServerHello** contains the server's **ephemeral ECDHE public key**.
3. Both compute the **ECDH shared secret** $Z$ using the elliptic curve scalar multiplication, which is identical on both sides due to group commutativity.
4. The shared secret feeds **HKDF** to derive the handshake secret → master secret → application traffic keys.
5. Server signs the handshake transcript with its long-term private key (RSA-2048 or ECDSA P-256) to prove its identity.
6. The **ephemeral nature** of the ECDHE keys means they are **destroyed at session end** — this is the source of **Forward Secrecy (FS)** / **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)**.

**Diagrammatic Representation (Student should redraw in exam):**
- 1-RTT handshake (1.5 round trips total)
- Show ephemeral key generation on both sides
- Show ECDH shared secret computation
- Show HKDF key derivation
- Show session key destruction on disconnect

**Conclusion:** Forward Secrecy ensures that compromise of a long-term private key **cannot** retroactively decrypt recorded past sessions — a critical property for long-lived industrial data (e.g., 7-year retention of pharmaceutical batch records).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **symmetric and asymmetric encryption**. Explain how **AES-256-GCM** is used to encrypt data at rest in a database, including the **key management lifecycle** as per NIST SP 800-57.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **IPsec ESP Tunnel Mode** for site-to-site VPN with a packet diagram. Discuss the **MTU overhead** issue and how it impacts industrial SCADA networks.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption (Comparison Table):**

| Parameter | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (RSA / ECC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Keys** | 1 shared secret key | Key pair (public + private) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (GB/s with AES-NI) | Slow (1000× slower for RSA-2048) |
| **Key Distribution** | Hard (must share secret) | Easy (public key is published) |
| **Use Case** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |
| **Key Size for Equivalent Security** | AES-256 (128-bit security) | RSA-3072 or ECC P-256 |
| **Example Algorithms** | AES, 3DES (deprecated), ChaCha20 | RSA, DH, ECDH, ECDSA, Ed25519 |

**AES-256-GCM for Database Encryption (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Key Generation:** A 256-bit DEK is generated inside the HSM using CSPRNG.
2. **Initialization Vector (IV):** A 96-bit (12-byte) IV is generated for each encryption operation. GCM **must** have a unique IV per key to maintain security (NIST SP 800-38D).
3. **Plaintext Division:** The database page (e.g., 4 KB) is treated as the plaintext $P$.
4. **Authenticated Encryption:**

$$C, T = \text{AES-256-GCM}_{K_{\text{DEK}}}(\text{IV}, P, \text{AAD})$$

where $C$ is the ciphertext, $T$ is the 128-bit authentication tag, and **AAD** (Additional Authenticated Data) is metadata like the row ID or block address (protects against block-swap attacks).

5. **Storage:** The database writes $\{ \text{IV}, C, T \}$ to disk. The DEK $K_{\text{DEK}}$ is wrapped by the KEK and stored in the database header.

**Key Management Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5):**

> **Phase 1 — Pre-Operational:** Key is generated, but not yet active. Algorithm, key type, and intended use are documented.
> **Phase 2 — Operational:** Key is used for cryptographic operations (encryption, decryption, signing). Key is **activated** and available.
> **Phase 3 — Post-Operational:** Key is no longer used for new operations, but may be retained for **verification of past signatures** or **decryption of legacy data** until re-encrypted.
> **Phase 4 — Destroyed (End-of-Life):** Key is **cryptographically erased** — its plaintext is securely overwritten and cannot be recovered. The wrapped form may be retained for audit, but is rendered useless.
> **Phase 5 — Archived (Optional):** Key is stored in long-term archival storage (e.g., offline HSM in a bank vault) for compliance decryption of historical records.

> **Key Rotation Policy (Industry Standard):**
> - **DEK:** Rotate every **90 days** OR on suspected compromise, whichever is earlier.
> - **KEK:** Rotate every **1 year**.
> - **Root Key (HSM):** Rotate every **2–3 years** with cryptographic ceremony (M-of-N quorum).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Refer to **Section 3.2.3 (IPsec ESP Tunnel Mode)** above for the full packet diagram and MTU calculation. Key points:

1. **IPsec ESP Tunnel Mode** wraps the **entire original IP packet** (header + payload) inside a new IP packet, encrypting the inner packet and providing confidentiality + integrity + anti-replay.

2. **Packet Structure (to be redrawn by student):**
   - New IP Header → ESP Header → **Encrypted (Original IP Header + TCP Header + Payload)** → ESP Trailer → ESP Auth Data

3. **Protocol Operation:**
   - **IKEv2** negotiates the Security Association (SA) — algorithms, keys, lifetime.
   - **ESP** encrypts each packet using AES-256-GCM (or AES-256-CBC + HMAC-SHA256 in older configurations).
   - **Anti-Replay:** A 32-bit or 64-bit sequence number + sliding window rejects duplicate packets.

4. **MTU Overhead Calculation:**

$$\Delta_{\text{MTU}} = \text{New IP Header (20B)} + \text{ESP Header (8B)} + \text{ESP Trailer (2B)} + \text{IV (8–16B)} + \text{ICV (12–16B)} \approx 50\text{–}73 \text{ bytes}$$

5. **Impact on Industrial SCADA:**
   - Standard Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**.
   - After IPsec overhead, effective payload = **~1427 bytes**.
   - **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD)** must be enabled to avoid silent fragmentation.
   - **TCP MSS Clamping** on the VPN gateway sets the maximum segment size to **MSS = MTU − 40 − ESP overhead ≈ 1380 bytes**.
   - For **legacy SCADA protocols** (MODBUS/TCP, DNP3 over TCP) that do not support PMTUD, the gateway must pre-fragment or use **tunnel MTU of 1400 bytes**.

6. **Why it matters in Industrial Networks:**
   - Real-time protocols (DNP3, IEC 61850 GOOSE) have tight latency budgets (4 ms for GOOSE in IEC 61850).
   - IPsec encryption adds **~10–50 µs latency** per hop — acceptable for most control loops but must be measured.
   - **Hardware-accelerated IPsec** (Intel AES-NI, Cavium Nitrox) is **mandatory** for industrial VPN throughput.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing symmetric with asymmetric:** A common mistake is recommending "RSA encryption for the database" — RSA-2048 can encrypt at most 245 bytes per operation and is **1000× slower** than AES. Always use **hybrid encryption** (RSA for key wrap + AES for data).
> 2. **Forgetting IV uniqueness in GCM:** AES-GCM with a reused IV catastrophically breaks both confidentiality and authenticity. The IV must be unique per key per operation.
> 3. **Ignoring Forward Secrecy:** TLS configurations that use **static RSA key exchange** (TLS 1.2 RSA-only mode) provide **no forward secrecy**. Always use **ECDHE**.
> 4. **Wrong MTU assumptions:** Assuming the IPsec tunnel does not affect packet size. **Always account for ESP overhead** in network design.
> 5. **Confusing hashing with encryption:** SHA-256 is a **one-way hash** — it cannot be "decrypted." Only encryption algorithms (AES, RSA) are reversible.
> 6. **Misnaming protocols:** Writing "SSL" when the standard is **TLS** (SSL is deprecated and has multiple known vulnerabilities — POODLE, DROWN). The current standard is **TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446)**.
> 7. **Skipping key management:** In exam answers, students often write "encrypt with AES-256" and stop. The **key management lifecycle** is worth **2–3 marks** on its own — never omit it.

---
