## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- **Encryption at Rest** protects stationary data (disks, databases, backups, cloud storage) using **AES-256** with **envelope encryption** (DEK wrapped by HSM-resident KEK).
- **Encryption in Transit** protects moving data (network traffic) using **TLS 1.3** (application layer) or **IPsec ESP** (network layer).
- **AES** operates on **128-bit blocks** with key sizes of **128, 192, or 256 bits** in **10, 12, or 14 rounds** respectively.
- **TLS 1.3** enforces **1-RTT handshake** and **mandatory Forward Secrecy** via **ECDHE** (X25519, secp256r1).
- **Forward Secrecy (PFS)** = ephemeral session keys destroyed at session end → past sessions remain secure even if long-term keys leak.
- **Envelope Encryption** is the **gold standard** for cloud and industrial data at rest (data encrypted with DEK, DEK wrapped by KEK in HSM).
- **HSM** must be **FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or higher** for industrial critical infrastructure.
- **Key Rotation:** DEK every 90 days; KEK every 1 year; Root Key every 2–3 years.
- **AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)** are mandatory in modern encryption — they provide both confidentiality and integrity in one operation.
- **PKI & X.509 Certificates** are the trust anchor for TLS — managed by a **Certificate Authority (CA)** with **OCSP/CRL** for revocation.
- **IPsec ESP Tunnel Mode** wraps the entire IP packet → **MTU overhead of 50–73 bytes** → must be accounted for in industrial network design.
- **NIST SP 800-57** defines 5 key lifecycle phases: Pre-Operational, Operational, Post-Operational, Destroyed, Archived.
- **IEC 62443** mandates encryption for all data flows in industrial zones (Zone & Conduit model).
- **Common Pitfall:** TLS 1.0/1.1 and SSL 3.0 are **deprecated** — never recommend them in 2024 answers.
- **Common Pitfall:** "Hashing" ≠ "Encryption" — SHA-256 is one-way; AES/RSA are reversible.
- **Industrial Relevance:** SCADA historians use **TDE**; substation-to-control-center links use **IPsec VPN**; V2X uses **TLS 1.3**; HSMs protect root keys for entire plant.
- **Key Lengths to Memorize (2024 Standard):** RSA ≥ 2048, ECC ≥ 256, AES ≥ 128, SHA ≥ 256, DH Group ≥ 14 (2048-bit MODP).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backup and Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Backup and Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Backup** is the process of creating and storing copies of critical data, system configurations, and operational states at a secure secondary location to enable restoration in the event of data loss, corruption, cyber-attack (e.g., ransomware), hardware failure, or natural disaster.

**Recovery** is the coordinated set of procedures, policies, and tools used to restore IT/OT systems, data, and services to a predefined operational state following a disruption, governed by measurable Service Level Objectives (SLOs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):** A backup is a *defense-in-depth* control under the **CIA Triad — Availability**, while recovery is the *resilience mechanism* that translates backup artefacts back into live, trustworthy services within Industrial Control System (ICS) / SCADA environments.

---

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Cyber-Physical Resilience

| Pillar | Goal | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| **Backup** | Preserve data & system states | IT/OT Administrator |
| **Recovery** | Restore services & operations | Incident Response Team |
| **Continuity (BCP/DRP)** | Sustain business during disruption | Management + Security Officer |

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Family Photo Album and the Fireproof Safe"**
>
> Imagine your most valuable family photographs. The **backup** is making duplicate copies of those photographs. You don't just put both copies in the same drawer (that would be foolish). Instead, you keep one copy in your house, one copy in a bank locker (offsite), and perhaps one copy in a cloud album. Now suppose a fire destroys the original. You walk to the bank locker, take out the duplicate, and place it in a new frame. That act of *taking the duplicate and putting it back into use* is the **recovery**.
>
> The **3-2-1 rule** is exactly this: **3** copies, on **2** different media, with **1** copy **offsite**.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in Industrial Cyber Security

In an Industrial Cyber Security context, the consequences of failed backup/recovery are not merely *lost documents* — they translate to:

- **Physical Safety Risks:** Loss of PLC set-points leading to unsafe actuator behaviour.
- **Production Downtime:** Direct revenue loss (e.g., a refinery losing $1M/hour).
- **Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Compromise:** Inability to validate safety logic post-ransomware.
- **Regulatory Non-Compliance:** Violations of **IEC 62443**, **NIST SP 800-82**, and **NIS2 Directive**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Highlight:** A question framing could be — *"Why is backup alone insufficient without a tested recovery procedure?"* The KTU-recommended answer references the **Untested Backup Paradox**: ~58% of organisations discover their backups are corrupt *only during a real disaster*.

---

### 1.5 Industry-Standard Metrics (Must Memorise)

- **RPO — Recovery Point Objective:** *Maximum acceptable data loss*, measured in time.
- **RTO — Recovery Time Objective:** *Maximum acceptable downtime*, measured in time.
- **MTTR — Mean Time To Recovery:** Average time to restore service after a failure.
- **MTBF — Mean Time Between Failures:** Average operational time between two consecutive failures.

$$Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> Confusing RPO with RTO is the **single most common error** in KTU valuation. RPO is a **data-loss** metric; RTO is a **downtime** metric.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Backup Types

#### A. By Data Selection Method

| Backup Type | What is Copied | Storage Cost | Restore Speed | RPO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Backup** | Entire dataset | **High** (100%) | **Fastest** | Large (next full cycle) |
| **Incremental Backup** | Only changes since *last backup of any type* | **Low** | Slow (chain restoration) | Small |
| **Differential Backup** | Changes since *last full backup* | Medium | Moderate | Medium |
| **Mirror Backup** | Exact 1:1 real-time copy | High | Instant | Near-zero |
| **Synthetic Full Backup** | Engineered full backup from prior full + incrementals | Medium | Fast (one file) | Small |
| **Snapshot (VSS-based)** | Point-in-time block-level copy | Low | Very fast | Near-zero |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Tip:** In SCADA environments, **point-in-time snapshots** of Engineering Workstations are preferred over file-based incremental backups because PLC project files have *atomic consistency* requirements.

#### B. By Operational Readiness (Disaster Recovery Sites)

| Site Type | Hardware Ready? | Data Current? | Cost | RTO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Site** | No (must procure/install) | No | **Low** | Days–Weeks |
| **Warm Site** | Partially (pre-configured) | Partially (periodic sync) | Medium | Hours |
| **Hot Site** | Yes (always running) | Yes (real-time replication) | **High** | Minutes |
| **Active-Active (Multi-Site)** | Yes, load-balanced | Yes, synchronous | Highest | Near-zero |

---

### 2.2 The 3-2-1-1-0 Modern Backup Rule (KTU-Favoured)

The classic **3-2-1 rule** has been extended for ransomware resilience:

- **3** copies of data
- **2** different media types
- **1** copy offsite
- **1** copy immutable/air-gapped
- **0** errors in recovery (verified, tested backups)

---

### 2.3 Grandfather-Father-Son (GFS) Rotation Strategy

A classic tape/disk rotation scheme:

| Tier | Period | Retention |
|---|---|---|
| **Son** (Daily) | Mon–Sat | 1 week |
| **Father** (Weekly) | Friday | 1 month |
| **Grandfather** (Monthly) | Last Friday | 1 year |

This balances *storage cost*, *retention window*, and *recovery granularity*.

---

### 2.4 Industrial-Specific Considerations (ICS/SCADA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why OT Backup ≠ IT Backup:**
> 1. **24×7 Process Availability:** Cannot stop a blast furnace to back up.
> 2. **Deterministic Networks:** Backup traffic cannot jitter control loops.
> 3. **Legacy Equipment:** Many PLCs (Siemens S7-300, Allen-Bradley SLC-500) lack modern backup APIs — require serial/ethernet gateway.
> 4. **Safety-Critical Validation:** Restored PLC logic must be **validated against engineering baseline** before re-commissioning.
> 5. **Air-Gapping:** Many OT networks are deliberately isolated → backups must use *sneakernet* or unidirectional data diodes.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | System uptime percentage | Expressed as **%** (e.g., 99.999% = "five nines") |
| 2 | $Downtime_{yr} = (1 - Availability) \times 8760$ | Annual downtime in hours | 8760 = hours/year |
| 3 | $SLA_{credit} = \dfrac{Actual\_Downtime - Allowed\_Downtime}{Allowed\_Downtime}$ | SLA penalty calculation | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Backup\_Window = T_{start} + \sum T_{copy} + T_{verify}$ | Time to complete a backup job | Hours |
| 5 | $Storage_{req} = D \times (1 + r)^{n} \times R$ | Growth-adjusted storage | $D$=current data, $r$=growth rate, $n$=years, $R$=redundancy factor |
| 6 | $RPO$ | Recovery Point Objective | Time (minutes/hours) |
| 7 | $RTO$ | Recovery Time Objective | Time |
| 8 | $MTTD$ | Mean Time To Detect | Time |
| 9 | $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Recover | Time |
| 10 | $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Safety Note:** The absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ uses `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent breaking markdown table parsing in your study notes.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance:** RPO of *near-zero* requires synchronous mirroring (e.g.,两地三中心 pattern in Chinese banking regulations).
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Recovery must be tested *quarterly*; air-gapped immutable backups are mandatory.
- **Power & Utilities (NERC CIP):** Recovery plans for BES (Bulk Electric System) assets must be reviewed *annually*.
- **Manufacturing (IEC 62443-2-4):** Service providers must demonstrate SR 7.3 — *Backup* and SR 7.4 — *System Recovery*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Availability and Annual Downtime

**Given:**
- A SCADA server has $MTBF = 3000$ hours and $MTTR = 1$ hour.
- Find: (a) Availability, (b) Annual downtime.

#### Step 1 — Write the availability formula
$$Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute the given values
$$Availability = \dfrac{3000}{3000 + 1}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the denominator
$$Availability = \dfrac{3000}{3001}$$

#### Step 4 — Convert to percentage
$$Availability = 0.999666... \times 100\% \approx 99.967\%$$

#### Step 5 — Compute annual downtime using the conversion formula
$$Downtime_{yr} = (1 - Availability) \times 8760$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute the value
$$Downtime_{yr} = (1 - 0.999667) \times 8760$$

#### Step 7 — Compute the difference
$$Downtime_{yr} = 0.000333 \times 8760$$

#### Step 8 — Final result
$$Downtime_{yr} \approx 2.92 \text{ hours/year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This corresponds to a **"three nines and a half"** availability class — typical for industrial SCADA servers (vs. five nines for telecom).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Backup Window Calculation for a Hybrid Strategy

**Scenario:** A factory has 4 TB of project data. Strategy: *Weekly full + Daily incremental*.

**Given parameters:**
- $T_{full} = 2.5$ hours per 1 TB → full backup of 4 TB = 10 hours
- $T_{incremental} = 0.25$ hours per 1 TB → for an avg. 200 GB daily change
- $T_{verify} = 0.5$ hours

#### Step 1 — Calculate full backup time
$$T_{full,total} = 2.5 \times 4 = 10 \text{ hours}$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate daily incremental backup time
$$T_{inc,total} = 0.25 \times 0.2 = 0.05 \text{ hours}$$

#### Step 3 — Add verification time
$$T_{verify} = 0.5 \text{ hours}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the backup window formula
$$Backup\_Window = T_{full,total} + T_{inc,total} + T_{verify}$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute values
$$Backup\_Window = 10 + 0.05 + 0.5$$

#### Step 6 — Final result
$$Backup\_Window = 10.55 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the maintenance window in this factory is only **8 hours** on Sundays, the schedule is **infeasible**. Solutions: (a) synthetic full backups, (b) deduplication, or (c) snapshot-based differentials.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Backup Strategy

| Scenario | Critical Data | RPO | RTO | Recommended Strategy | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLC Program Files** | Low volume, high integrity | 1 h | 4 h | Daily full + versioned snapshots | Atomic restore of engineering baseline |
| **HMI Historical Logs** | High volume, append-only | 15 min | 2 h | Continuous incremental + weekly full | Balance cost and granularity |
| **SCADA Server OS Image** | Full system state | 24 h | 8 h | Weekly full image + daily differential | Enables bare-metal restore |
| **ERP Database (SAP)** | Mission-critical, transactional | ~0 (synchronous) | 30 min | Hot-standby + log shipping | Near-zero RPO via synchronous replication |
| **Engineering Drawings (CAD)** | Large, infrequently changed | 24 h | 24 h | Daily incremental, weekly full | Storage-efficient |
| **Safety Logic (SIS)** | Cannot be hot-restored | Manual re-validation | N/A | Air-gapped offline + paper master | Requires manual SIL re-certification |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: RPO / RTO Trade-off Cost Analysis

**Problem:** An MES database must achieve $RPO \le 5$ minutes and $RTO \le 30$ minutes. Compare two strategies:

| Strategy | RPO Achieved | RTO Achieved | Annual Cost (INR) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A: Daily backup + tape** | 24 h ❌ | 12 h ❌ | ₹2,00,000 | Reject |
| **B: Hourly incremental to DR site** | 1 h ⚠️ | 2 h ⚠️ | ₹18,00,000 | Marginal |
| **C: Synchronous mirroring + automated failover** | ~0 s ✅ | ~3 min ✅ | ₹45,00,000 | Accept |

> [!NOTE]
> The cost escalates **non-linearly** as RPO/RTO approach zero — a classic engineering trade-off curve examined in KTU viva questions.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Storage Requirement Growth

$$Storage_{req} = D \times (1 + r)^{n} \times R$$

- $D = 10$ TB (current data)
- $r = 30\%$ annual growth
- $n = 3$ years
- $R = 2$ (RAID-1 mirroring)

#### Step 1 — Apply formula
$$Storage_{req} = 10 \times (1 + 0.3)^{3} \times 2$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the growth exponent
$$Storage_{req} = 10 \times (1.3)^{3} \times 2$$

#### Step 3 — Compute $1.3^3$
$$Storage_{req} = 10 \times 2.197 \times 2$$

#### Step 4 — Final result
$$Storage_{req} = 43.94 \text{ TB}$$

**Conclusion:** Procurement teams must budget for **~4.4× growth** in 3 years under this scenario.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Backup & Recovery Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Systems - PLC, HMI, SCADA, ERP] --> B[Identify Critical Data and Assets]
    B --> C[Classify by CIA and RPO/RTO]
    C --> D[Select Backup Strategy - Full, Incremental, Differential]
    D --> E[Execute Backup Job - Scheduled or Event Driven]
    E --> F[Encrypt and Hash - SHA-256 or AES-256]
    F --> G[Store on Tiered Media - Disk, Tape, Cloud]
    G --> H{Storage Type}
    H --> I[Onsite Primary - Hot Tier]
    H --> J[Offsite Secondary - Warm Tier]
    H --> K[Immutable Air Gapped - Cold Tier]
    I --> L[Verify Integrity - Checksum Validation]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Verification Passed?}
    M -- No --> N[Trigger Alert and Re-backup]
    M -- Yes --> O[Update Backup Catalogue]
    O --> P[Periodic DR Drill - Tabletop or Live]
    P --> Q[Document RTO and RPO Metrices]
    Q --> R[Continuous Improvement - Post Incident Review]
```

### 4.2 RPO vs RTO Decision Matrix (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Disaster Strikes] --> B[Time Zero - Event Occurs]
    B --> C[Last Successful Backup Point]
    C --> D[Data Loss Window - RPO]
    B --> E[Recovery Initiated]
    E --> F[Service Restored to Users]
    D --> G[Acceptable Data Loss in Time]
    F --> H[Acceptable Downtime in Time]
    G --> I[RPO Target]
    H --> J[RTO Target]
    I --> K[Choose Backup Frequency and Replication]
    J --> L[Choose DR Site Type and Automation]
    K --> M[Final Backup and Recovery Architecture]
    L --> M
```

### 4.3 Industrial (OT) Backup Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OT Network - Level 1 to 3] --> A1[Field Sensors and Actuators]
    A --> A2[PLCs and RTUs]
    A --> A3[SCADA Servers and HMI]
    A --> A4[Engineering Workstations]
    A3 --> B[OT Backup Server with Industrial Connector]
    A4 --> B
    B --> C{Data Sensitivity and Air Gap Policy}
    C -- Critical Engineering Data --> D1[Unidirectional Data Diode]
    C -- Process Historian Data --> D2[Encrypted TLS Tunnel to IT DMZ]
    D1 --> E1[Air Gapped Immutable Storage - Industrial NAS]
    D2 --> E2[Corporate Backup Repository]
    E1 --> F[Cloud Cold Storage with Object Lock]
    E2 --> G[Cloud DR Vault]
    F --> H[Quarterly DR Drill and Recovery Test]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Audit and Compliance Reporting - IEC 62443, NIST 800-82]
```

### 4.4 3-2-1-1-0 Rule — Visual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Production Data] --> B1[Copy 1 - Onsite Disk]
    A --> B2[Copy 2 - Onsite Tape or NAS]
    A --> B3[Copy 3 - Offsite Cloud]
    B3 --> C1[Copy 4 - Immutable Air Gapped with Object Lock]
    C1 --> D[Verification Step - Zero Errors Confirmed]
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
```

### 4.5 Disaster Recovery Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detected] --> B{Type of Incident}
    B -- Hardware Failure --> C1[Activate Hardware Spare or Hot Site]
    B -- Ransomware Attack --> C2[Isolate Network, Restore from Immutable Backup]
    B -- Natural Disaster --> C3[Activate Offsite DR Site]
    B -- Data Corruption --> C4[Restore from Last Known Good Snapshot]
    C1 --> D[RTO Less than 1 Hour]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Validate Restored Systems]
    E --> F{Validation Passed}
    F -- Yes --> G[Resume Operations and Notify Stakeholders]
    F -- No --> H[Re-initiate Recovery from Older Backup Set]
    H --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:** All node IDs in the above diagrams follow the alpha-prefix rule (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `C2`) and labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown bold or italics inside the double-quoted labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q: Differentiate between RPO and RTO with a suitable industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** is the *maximum acceptable data loss* measured in time — i.e., how far back you must rewind. In a SCADA historian, an RPO of 15 minutes means you may lose at most the last 15 minutes of process data. (1.5 marks)
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** is the *maximum acceptable downtime* — i.e., how long the system can be offline. For the same SCADA system, an RTO of 2 hours means operations must resume within 2 hours of an outage. (1.5 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q: List and briefly explain the components of the 3-2-1 backup rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **3 copies** of critical data (production + 2 backups). (1 mark)
2. **2 different media types** (e.g., disk + tape, or disk + cloud) to avoid correlated failure. (1 mark)
3. **1 copy offsite** to survive site-level disasters (fire, flood, earthquake). (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Notice (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT524 Module 2]**
> *(a)* Explain the various **types of backup strategies** with their relative advantages, disadvantages, and storage overhead. Compare at least four strategies using a tabular format. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* An industrial plant operates 24×7 and cannot tolerate more than **30 minutes of downtime** or **5 minutes of data loss**. Design a **disaster recovery architecture** that meets these RTO/RPO targets, justifying your choice of primary, secondary, and tertiary storage tiers, replication mode (synchronous vs asynchronous), and the use of air-gapping. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Backup Strategies (7 marks):**

The major backup strategies are:

1. **Full Backup:** Copies the *entire dataset* every time.
   - ✅ Advantage: Fastest restore, simplest to manage.
   - ❌ Disadvantage: Highest storage cost, longest backup window.
   - Storage overhead: **100%** of source size per cycle.

2. **Incremental Backup:** Copies only data changed since the *last backup of any type*.
   - ✅ Advantage: Lowest storage and time cost.
   - ❌ Disadvantage: Slowest restore (must chain full + all incrementals in order).
   - Storage overhead: **~5–10%** of source per cycle.

3. **Differential Backup:** Copies all data changed since the *last full backup*.
   - ✅ Advantage: Restore needs only full + latest differential.
   - ❌ Disadvantage: Grows over time until next full backup.
   - Storage overhead: **Cumulative** — up to 100% just before full cycle.

4. **Mirror Backup:** Real-time 1:1 copy (e.g., RAID-1, synchronous replication).
   - ✅ Advantage: Near-zero RPO, instant failover.
   - ❌ Disadvantage: If source is corrupted, mirror is also corrupted (no version history).
   - Storage overhead: **100%** constant.

5. **Snapshot Backup (VSS/ZFS/Btrfs):** Block-level point-in-time copy using copy-on-write.
   - ✅ Advantage: Near-instant, minimal storage growth.
   - ❌ Disadvantage: Usually stored on same array — vulnerable to array failure.

6. **Synthetic Full Backup:** Server-side composition of a full backup from full + incrementals.
   - ✅ Advantage: Fast restore, low production-side I/O.
   - ❌ Disadvantage: Server-side resource intensive.

**Comparison Table (for 7-mark full credit):**

| Strategy | Backup Speed | Restore Speed | Storage Cost | RPO Granularity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full | Slow | Fastest | High (100%) | Coarse (next cycle) | Small datasets, weekly anchor |
| Incremental | Fastest | Slowest | Lowest | Fine | Large append-only logs |
| Differential | Medium | Medium | Cumulative | Medium | Mid-size DBs |
| Mirror | Real-time | Instant | High (100%) | Near-zero | Mission-critical primary |
| Snapshot | Near-instant | Very fast | Low | Near-zero | Virtualised environments |
| Synthetic Full | Medium (offloaded) | Fast | Medium | Fine | High-RTO services |

---

**Part (b) — DR Architecture Design (7 marks):**

**Target requirements (restated):** $RTO \le 30$ min, $RPO \le 5$ min, **24×7 operation**.

**Recommended Architecture — Three-Tier Synchronous + Asynchronous Hybrid:**

**Tier 1 — Hot Standby (Synchronous Replication):**
- A **hot-standby SCADA server** in the *same site* (or a nearby DR site) receives data via **synchronous replication** (e.g., SAN mirroring, storage-level replication).
- Synchronous mode guarantees that every write to the primary is acknowledged only after it is committed to the standby → **RPO ≈ 0 seconds**.
- Automated failover via clustering software (e.g., Windows Failover Cluster, Pacemaker for Linux) achieves **RTO in 1–3 minutes**.

**Tier 2 — Warm Asynchronous Offsite Copy:**
- A **warm DR site** at 50–100 km distance receives **asynchronous** replication every 1–2 minutes.
- Protects against site-level disasters (fire, flood).
- **RPO ≈ 1–2 minutes** (well within the 5-minute target).
- RTO contribution: 30–60 minutes (hardware is racked, software is pre-installed, data is current).

**Tier 3 — Cold Immutable Air-Gapped Copy:**
- A **daily snapshot** is exported via **unidirectional data diode** to an **air-gapped, immutable object store** (e.g., AWS S3 with Object Lock, Azure Immutable Blob).
- This protects against **ransomware** that may compromise both primary and DR sites.
- RPO: 24 hours (acceptable as a *last-resort* recovery point).
- RTO: 4–8 hours (cold restoration + full system validation).

**Justification Summary (for full marks):**
- **Synchronous replication** satisfies the strict RPO target.
- **Hot-standby clustering** satisfies the strict RTO target.
- **Asynchronous offsite** protects against site failure.
- **Air-gapped immutable tier** protects against cyber-attack (ransomware) — aligning with **IEC 62443 SR 7.3 / 7.4** and **NIST CSF 2.0 — Recover (RC)** function.
- The architecture satisfies the **3-2-1-1-0 rule**: 3 copies (primary, standby, offsite), 2 media (SAN + object store), 1 offsite (warm DR), 1 immutable (air-gapped), 0 errors (quarterly DR drills validate recovery).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT524 Module 2]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **disaster recovery process** in an Industrial Control System (ICS). Discuss the role of **cold, warm, and hot sites** and justify which is most appropriate for a *Level-2 SCADA control server*. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Define **RPO, RTO, MTBF, and MTTR**. A system has $MTBF = 5000$ hours and $MTTR = 2$ hours. Compute its availability and annual downtime. If the business requirement is *99.99% availability*, determine the **maximum allowable MTTR**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — DR Process & Site Types (7 marks):**

**Disaster Recovery Process (sequential steps):**
1. **Incident Detection** — SIEM, IDS, or operator reports an outage.
2. **Incident Classification** — Tier 1 (minor), Tier 2 (major), Tier 3 (catastrophic/site loss).
3. **Activation of DR Plan** — Incident commander declares disaster, notifies DR team.
4. **Failover Initiation** — Production traffic redirected to DR site.
5. **Data Synchronisation Verification** — Confirm DR site data is consistent.
6. **Service Restoration** — Applications brought online in priority order (P1 → P2 → P3).
7. **Validation & Testing** — Functional and security tests before declaring operational.
8. **Failback** — Once primary site is restored, return operations gradually.
9. **Post-Incident Review** — Document lessons learned, update DR plan.

**Site Type Comparison (for full marks):**

| Parameter | Cold Site | Warm Site | Hot Site |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power & HVAC | Available | Available | Available & running |
| Hardware | Not pre-installed | Partially installed | Fully installed |
| Network connectivity | Basic | Pre-configured | Live & mirrored |
| Data currency | Stale (days) | Near-current (mins–hours) | Real-time |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 5–10× | 15–30× |
| RTO achievable | Days–Weeks | Hours | Minutes |
| Best for | Archives, non-critical | Mid-tier business apps | Mission-critical |

**Justification for Level-2 SCADA Server (Hot Site):**
- A SCADA control server is a **real-time process** — loss of visibility or control may trigger **safety instrumented system (SIS) shutdowns** or process instability.
- The cost of unplanned downtime in process industries can reach **₹10–50 lakh/hour**, justifying the high cost of a hot site.
- A hot site with **synchronous data replication** ensures that the failover server has identical state to the primary, eliminating data loss and enabling restoration within minutes.
- **Verdict:** Hot site is the only acceptable option for Level-2 SCADA control servers.

---

**Part (b) — Metrics Computation (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Maximum tolerable data loss in time.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Maximum tolerable downtime in time.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average time between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Recovery):** Average time to restore service after a failure.

**Given:** $MTBF = 5000$ hours, $MTTR = 2$ hours.

**Step 1 — Apply the availability formula**
$$Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values**
$$Availability = \dfrac{5000}{5000 + 2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator**
$$Availability = \dfrac{5000}{5002}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage**
$$Availability = 0.9996 \times 100\% = 99.96\%$$

**Step 5 — Annual downtime using the formula**
$$Downtime_{yr} = (1 - Availability) \times 8760$$

**Step 6 — Substitute**
$$Downtime_{yr} = (1 - 0.9996) \times 8760$$

**Step 7 — Final result**
$$Downtime_{yr} = 0.0004 \times 8760 = 3.504 \text{ hours/year}$$

---

**Now find maximum MTTR for 99.99% availability:**

**Step 8 — Set up the equation for 99.99%**
$$0.9999 = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR_{max}}$$

**Step 9 — Cross-multiply**
$$0.9999 \times (MTBF + MTTR_{max}) = MTBF$$

**Step 10 — Expand**
$$0.9999 \cdot MTBF + 0.9999 \cdot MTTR_{max} = MTBF$$

**Step 11 — Isolate MTTR**
$$0.9999 \cdot MTTR_{max} = MTBF - 0.9999 \cdot MTBF$$

**Step 12 — Factor**
$$0.9999 \cdot MTTR_{max} = MTBF \times (1 - 0.9999)$$

**Step 13 — Substitute MTBF = 5000**
$$0.9999 \cdot MTTR_{max} = 5000 \times 0.0001$$

**Step 14 — Compute right side**
$$0.9999 \cdot MTTR_{max} = 0.5$$

**Step 15 — Solve for MTTR**
$$MTTR_{max} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.9999} \approx 0.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 16 — Convert to minutes**
$$MTTR_{max} \approx 30 \text{ minutes}$$

**Conclusion:** To meet 99.99% ("four nines") availability, the recovery process must complete in **at most 30 minutes**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing RPO with RTO** — RPO is *data loss*, RTO is *downtime*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to hours/year** — Students often write *3.504* without the unit. Always specify units.
> 3. **Using $|x|$ notation incorrectly** — When writing *absolute value* in maths sections, use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables.
> 4. **Skipping the 3-2-1-1-0 justification** — A DR design that omits the air-gap/immutable tier loses marks in 2024-scheme questions emphasising ransomware resilience.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — When solving numericals, explicitly state: *Assume one failure cycle per MTBF period* etc.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Backup** = defence-in-depth control for **Availability** under the CIA Triad.
- ✅ **Recovery** = restoring services within **RTO** with at most **RPO** data loss.
- ✅ **3-2-1-1-0 Rule:** 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite, 1 immutable/air-gapped, 0 verification errors.
- ✅ **RPO ≠ RTO** — RPO = data loss window; RTO = downtime window.
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- ✅ **Annual downtime:** $D_{yr} = (1 - A) \times 8760$ hours.
- ✅ **"Five nines" = 99.999% = 5.26 min/year downtime.**
- ✅ **"Four nines" = 99.99% = 52.6 min/year downtime.**
- ✅ **Full backup** = simplest restore, highest cost.
- ✅ **Incremental** = lowest cost, slowest restore.
- ✅ **Differential** = medium cost, medium restore speed.
- ✅ **Snapshot** = near-zero RPO, same-array vulnerability.
- ✅ **Mirror** = real-time, no version history.
- ✅ **Cold site** = days/weeks RTO, low cost.
- ✅ **Warm site** = hours RTO, medium cost.
- ✅ **Hot site** = minutes RTO, high cost — only choice for Level-2 SCADA.
- ✅ **GFS rotation:** Son (daily), Father (weekly), Grandfather (monthly).
- ✅ **OT-specific:** Cannot stop process → prefer **snapshots** over file backups.
- ✅ **Ransomware defence:** Use **immutable object storage** + **air gap** + **unidirectional diode**.
- ✅ **IEC 62443 SR 7.3** = Backup requirement; **SR 7.4** = System Recovery.
- ✅ **NIST CSF 2.0 Recover (RC) function** governs backup/recovery planning.
- ✅ **Untested backups are not backups** — quarterly DR drills are mandatory.
- ✅ **Cost grows non-linearly** as RPO/RTO → 0.
- ✅ **Storage growth formula:** $S = D(1+r)^n \cdot R$.
- ✅ **PLC program files** require *atomic* restore + engineering baseline validation.
- ✅ **SIS logic** cannot be hot-restored — requires manual SIL re-certification.
- ✅ **Blockchain / WORM storage** is used for tamper-evident audit trails in OT.
- ✅ **Synchronous replication** = near-zero RPO but adds latency (avoid on control loops).
- ✅ **Asynchronous replication** = small RPO penalty, no latency penalty.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identity Lifecycle Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Identity Lifecycle Management ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Identity Lifecycle Management (ILM)** is the disciplined, policy-driven framework that governs the **creation, maintenance, governance, and revocation of digital identities** (human users, service accounts, machine identities, and IoT/ICS devices) across an enterprise — from the moment an identity is provisioned until it is securely de-provisioned. In the context of **Industrial Cyber Security (ICS/OT environments)**, ILM is extended to manage the identities of: - Plant operators and engineers - Third-party maintenance contractors - Vendor remote-access accounts - PLC, RTU, HMI, and Engineering Workstation service identities - Machine-to-machine (M2M) communication certificates > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT524 – Module 3):** > *Identity Lifecycle Management refers to the comprehensive, automated management of digital identities throughout their operational existence — encompassing provisioning, authentication, authorization, periodic recertification, and de-provisioning — to enforce least-privilege access and maintain the CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) across Industrial Control Systems (ICS) and Enterprise IT.* --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large multi-floor pharmaceutical manufacturing plant** in Kochi. Every person who walks in receives a **color-coded, time-bound access badge**: | Badge Color | Allowed Areas | Validity | |---|---|---| | 🟢 **Green** | Lobby, Cafeteria, Office | 1 year | | 🟡 **Yellow** | Production Floor, QC Lab | 6 months | | 🔴 **Red** | Reactor Hall, DCS Control Room | Project-based | | 🔵 **Blue (Visitor)** | Lobby + Escorted zones only | 1 day | When a **new chemical engineer joins** → HR raises a request → The Security Office prints a Yellow badge with restricted zones. When the engineer is **transferred from QC to the Reactor team** → The badge is **upgraded (mover event)** and zone permissions are re-issued. When the engineer **resigns** → The badge is **deactivated within 24 hours (leaver event)** and all zones are revoked. **This is exactly how ILM works digitally — but for logins, certificates, SSH keys, and OT credentials, not just physical badges.** > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** In OT environments, an *un-deprovisioned* contractor account can become a **backdoor** for ransomware groups (e.g., the 2017 TRITON/Trisis attack exploited stolen contractor credentials). Identity hygiene is therefore a **safety issue**, not just an IT issue. --- ### 1.3 Core Identity Lifecycle Stages (The JML Model) The JML model is the de-facto framework recognized by NIST SP 800-53 (AC-2) and IEC 62443-3-3: 1. **J – Joiner (Provisioning):** Identity is created. Default least-privilege rights are assigned. 2. **M – Mover (Modification):** Role/department change triggers re-permissioning. 3. **L – Leaver (Deprovisioning):** Identity is disabled → archived → purged. 4. **G – Governance (Continuous):** Periodic recertification, audit, and compliance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Industrial Metrics (must-know for KTU):** > - **Time-to-Provision (TPP):** < **2 hours** (best practice) > - **Time-to-Revoke (TTR):** < **15 minutes** for high-privilege accounts > - **Orphan Account Rate (OAR):** Should be **< 0.5%** > - **Recertification Cycle:** **Every 90 days** (privileged), **180 days** (standard) --- ### 1.4 Why ILM is Mission-Critical in ICS | Threat Vector | ILM Control That Mitigates It | |---|---| | Stale contractor credentials | Automated JML + TTR SLA | | Privilege creep | Periodic recertification | | Insider misuse | Segregation of Duties (SoD) | | Lateral movement to DCS | Zone-based identity segmentation | | Credential stuffing | MFA + adaptive authentication | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The JML Operational Framework — Step-by-Step #### **Stage 1: Joiner (Provisioning)** 1. **Identity Source-of-Truth (SoT)** — HR system (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors) raises a *New Hire* event. 2. **Identity Provisioning Engine** (e.g., SailPoint, Saviynt, One Identity) intercepts the event via SCIM 2.0 or LDAP. 3. **Birthright Role Assignment** — Default role (e.g., *Operator_L1*) is applied based on **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** rules. 4. **Credential Issuance** — Password, MFA token, smart card (PIV), or X.509 certificate is generated. 5. **Account Activation** — User logs in for the first time; MFA binding is enforced. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Birthright Access** = *Default entitlements auto-assigned based on attributes like Job Title, Department, Location, Employee Type.* No human approval is required for low-risk roles. This is mandated by **NIST SP 800-53 AC-2(2)**. #### **Stage 2: Mover (Modification / Up-provisioning & Down-provisioning)** 1. HR raises a *Transfer* event (e.g., Engineer moves from Unit-A to Unit-B). 2. The provisioning engine **evaluates the delta**: - **Add** permissions specific to Unit-B. - **Remove** permissions specific to Unit-A. 3. **Approval Workflow** — A role-owner (e.g., Plant Manager) must approve the new entitlements. 4. **Access Review** — Old accounts (email, VPN, badge) are flagged for cleanup. 5. **Effective Date Enforcement** — Permissions activate/deactivate on a specific date to avoid privilege gaps. #### **Stage 3: Leaver (Deprovisioning)** 1. HR raises a *Termination* event. 2. The engine immediately: - **Disables** the account (login blocked). - **Revokes** all OAuth tokens, SAML assertions, Kerberos tickets. - **Archives** the account (data preserved for 7 years per IT Act 2000/India). - **Purges** after the retention period. 3. For **ICS contractor accounts**, all VPN sessions, jump-server tunnels, and RDP sessions are force-terminated. #### **Stage 4: Governance (Continuous Compliance)** - **Access Reviews (Recertification):** Every 90/180 days, the role owner must *certify* that the user still needs the entitlements. - **Segregation of Duties (SoD):** No user can hold conflicting roles (e.g., *Requester* + *Approver* in the same workflow). - **Privileged Access Management (PAM):** Vaulting, just-in-time (JIT) elevation, and session recording for admin/root accounts. - **Identity Governance & Administration (IGA):** The umbrella discipline that orchestrates all of the above. --- ### 2.2 Access Control Models — Comparative Analysis | Model | Full Form | How It Works | ICS Use Case | Weakness | |---|---|---|---|---| | **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Permissions assigned to *roles*; users assigned to roles | Operator, Engineer, Supervisor roles | Role explosion in large plants | | **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Permissions based on *attributes* (user, resource, env, action) | Time-of-day, zone, shift-based DCS access | Complex policy authoring | | **PBAC** | Policy-Based Access Control | Externalized policies evaluated by a PDP/PEP | NIST 800-162 standard for OT | Performance overhead | | **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | Sensitivity labels (Unclassified → Top Secret) | Military/government ICS | Inflexible | | **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Owner decides who accesses | Legacy engineering workstations | Weakest in OT | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Favourite Question:** *"Compare RBAC and ABAC with respect to ICS deployments."* — Use the table above as your answer skeleton. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **mathematically rigorous, NIST/IEEE-aligned**, and frequently appear in KTU calculations. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Orphan Account Rate (OAR)** | $$OAR = \frac{N_{\text{orphan}}}{N_{\text{total active}}} \times 100\%$$ | $N_{\text{orphan}}$ = # of accounts with no valid owner; $N_{\text{total}}$ = # of active identities | KPI for governance; should be < 0.5% | | 2 | **Time-to-Provision (TPP)** | $$TPP = T_{\text{approval}} + T_{\text{create}} + T_{\text{activate}}$$ | All in **hours** | Best practice < 2 hrs | | 3 | **Time-to-Revoke (TTR)** | $$TTR = T_{\text{leaver event}} - T_{\text{disable timestamp}}$$ | In **minutes** | Privileged accounts < 15 min | | 4 | **Provisioning Automation Ratio (PAR)** | $$PAR = \frac{N_{\text{auto-provisioned}}}{N_{\text{total provisions}}} \times 100\%$$ | Count of accounts | Industrial benchmark: 85–95% | | 5 | **Segregation of Duties Conflict Density (SoD)** | $$SoD = \frac{C_{\text{conflicts}}}{R_{\text{total}}}$$ | $C$ = conflicting role pairs, $R$ = total role assignments | Lower is better | | 6 | **Mean Time to Detect Orphan (MTTDO)** | $$MTTDO = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{detect}_i} - T_{\text{created}_i})$$ | In **days** | Indicates governance maturity | | 7 | **RBAC Cardinality Bound** | $$\vert U \vert \cdot \vert R \vert + \vert R \vert \cdot \vert P \vert \leq \vert UA \vert + \vert PA \vert$$ | $U$=users, $R$=roles, $P$=permissions, $UA$=user-role assignments, $PA$=role-permission assignments | From ANSI INCITS 359 | | 8 | **Identity Risk Score (IRS)** | $$IRS = w_1 P + w_2 S + w_3 T + w_4 A$$ | $P$=privilege, $S$=sensitivity, $T$=tenure anomaly, $A$=activity anomaly; $w_i$ = weights with $\sum w_i = 1$ | Used in UEBA systems | | 9 | **Recertification Coverage (RC)** | $$RC = \frac{A_{\text{reviewed}}}{A_{\text{subject}}} \times 100\%$$ | Reviewed vs. subject accounts | Target = 100% | | 10 | **JIT Privilege Window (JIT-PW)** | $$\Delta T_{\text{elev}} = T_{\text{revoke}} - T_{\text{grant}}$$ | Minutes | PAM best practice: < 60 min | > **Note on notation:** The vertical bar $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **cardinality** (count of set elements), not absolute value. This distinction is important for RBAC. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility - **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Every sensor, robot, and AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) needs a **machine identity** — managed via ILM + X.509 certificates. - **Pharma / FDA Compliance:** 21 CFR Part 11 mandates **electronic signature identity management** — ILM ensures only authorized chemists can sign batch records. - **Power Grids (IEC 62443):** Zone & Conduit model relies on **identity-based segmentation** between Levels 0–3 of the Purdue Model. - **Remote Vendor Access:** 68% of OT breaches (per Dragos 2024 report) involve **third-party credentials** — ILM with vendor risk scoring is the first line of defense. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost of Identity Lifecycle (TCO-ILM) **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A Kerala-based petrochemical plant has **4,200 identities** managed by ILM. Annual costs are: - Helpdesk calls for password reset: ₹120 per call - Manual provisioning labor: ₹800 per account - Audit & compliance: ₹6,00,000 per year (fixed) - Orphan account remediation: ₹2,500 per orphan - PAM license: ₹45 lakhs per year (fixed) - Identity governance tool: ₹18 lakhs per year (fixed) Current data: 4,200 users, 38% require password resets per year, 12% are provisioned manually, orphan rate = 1.8%, automation target = 90%. **Derive:** (a) Current TCO. (b) TCO after ILM automation. (c) % savings. --- #### **Step 1: Compute the Current Annual Costs** **Password Reset Cost:** $$C_{\text{reset}} = N_{\text{users}} \times f_{\text{reset}} \times C_{\text{per call}}$$ $$C_{\text{reset}} = 4200 \times 0.38 \times 120$$ $$C_{\text{reset}} = 1{,}596 \times 120 = \text{₹ } 1{,}91{,}520$$ **Manual Provisioning Cost:** $$C_{\text{provision}} = N_{\text{users}} \times f_{\text{manual}} \times C_{\text{labor}}$$ $$C_{\text{provision}} = 4200 \times 0.12 \times 800$$ $$C_{\text{provision}} = 504 \times 800 = \text{₹ } 4{,}03{,}200$$ **Orphan Remediation Cost:** $$N_{\text{orphan}} = N_{\text{users}} \times OAR = 4200 \times 0.018 = 75.6 \approx 76 \text{ accounts}$$ $$C_{\text{orphan}} = 76 \times 2500 = \text{₹ } 1{,}90{,}000$$ **Fixed Costs:** $$C_{\text{fixed}} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 45{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 69{,}00{,}000$$ **Total Current TCO:** $$\begin{aligned} TCO_{\text{current}} &= C_{\text{reset}} + C_{\text{provision}} + C_{\text{orphan}} + C_{\text{fixed}} \\ &= 1{,}91{,}520 + 4{,}03{,}200 + 1{,}90{,}000 + 69{,}00{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ } 76{,}84{,}720 \end{aligned}$$ --- #### **Step 2: Compute TCO After ILM Automation** **Assumption:** 90% password resets are self-service (cost = ₹10/call), 90% provisioning is automated (cost = ₹80/account, mostly for license amortization), orphan rate drops to 0.2% (best-in-class). **New Password Reset Cost:** $$C_{\text{reset}}^{\text{new}} = (4200 \times 0.38 \times 0.90 \times 10) + (4200 \times 0.38 \times 0.10 \times 120)$$ $$C_{\text{reset}}^{\text{new}} = 14{,}364 + 19{,}152 = \text{₹ } 33{,}516$$ *Logic: 90% of calls become self-service at ₹10; 10% remain manual at ₹120.* **New Provisioning Cost:** $$C_{\text{provision}}^{\text{new}} = 4200 \times 0.12 \times 0.90 \times 80 + 4200 \times 0.12 \times 0.10 \times 800$$ $$C_{\text{provision}}^{\text{new}} = 36{,}288 + 40{,}320 = \text{₹ } 76{,}608$$ *Logic: 90% of new provisions are auto-handled at ₹80; 10% still need manual intervention at ₹800.* **New Orphan Remediation Cost:** $$N_{\text{orphan}}^{\text{new}} = 4200 \times 0.002 = 8.4 \approx 9 \text{ accounts}$$ $$C_{\text{orphan}}^{\text{new}} = 9 \times 2500 = \text{₹ } 22{,}500$$ **Fixed Costs** (unchanged, since licenses are already paid): ₹ 69,00,000. **Total Automated TCO:** $$\begin{aligned} TCO_{\text{automated}} &= 33{,}516 + 76{,}608 + 22{,}500 + 69{,}00{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ } 70{,}32{,}624 \end{aligned}$$ --- #### **Step 3: Compute Percentage Savings** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Savings} &= TCO_{\text{current}} - TCO_{\text{automated}} \\ &= 76{,}84{,}720 - 70{,}32{,}624 = \text{₹ } 6{,}52{,}096 \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{6{,}52{,}096}{76{,}84{,}720} \times 100 \approx 8.48\%$$ **Conclusion:** Even with conservative estimates, ILM automation yields **~8.5% direct cost savings**, plus significant *indirect* benefits (reduced breach risk: ₹4–10 Cr saved per avoided OT incident). --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Lifecycle Risk Score per Stage | Stage | Typical Risk | Root Cause | ILM Mitigation | Residual Risk | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Joiner** | Medium | Stale role templates | Birthright RBAC + SCIM | Low | | **Mover** | High | Privilege creep | Periodic recertification | Medium | | **Leaver** | **Critical** | Account not disabled | Automated deprovisioning | Low | | **Governance** | Medium | Manual reviews | IGA dashboards | Low | | **PAM / JIT** | High | Standing admin rights | Just-in-Time elevation | Low | --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Identity Risk Score (IRS) Worked Example **Given (KTU-style):** A contractor's account has: - Privilege score $P = 0.9$ (admin on DCS) - Sensitivity $S = 0.85$ (touches safety system) - Tenure anomaly $T = 0.6$ (active 3 months past contract end) - Activity anomaly $A = 0.7$ (login from unrecognized geo-location) Weights: $w_1 = 0.35,\ w_2 = 0.25,\ w_3 = 0.20,\ w_4 = 0.20$ (must sum to 1: ✓) **Compute IRS:** $$\begin{aligned} IRS &= (0.35 \times 0.9) + (0.25 \times 0.85) + (0.20 \times 0.6) + (0.20 \times 0.7) \\ &= 0.315 + 0.2125 + 0.12 + 0.14 \\ &= 0.7875 \approx 0.79 \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** $IRS \geq 0.7$ → **HIGH RISK** → Trigger automatic account suspension + SOC investigation. > [!NOTE] > **Decision thresholds (NIST-aligned):** > - $IRS < 0.3$ → Low (routine) > - $0.3 \leq IRS < 0.7$ → Medium (MFA challenge) > - $IRS \geq 0.7$ → High (block + alert) --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Full Identity Lifecycle Flow (JML + Governance) ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Identity Request Initiated]) --> B{Joiner Event?} B -- Yes --> C[HR SoT emits New Hire] C --> D[Provisioning Engine via SCIM 2.0] D --> E[Birthright RBAC Role Assigned] E --> F[MFA Enrollment and PIV Card Issue] F --> G[Account Activated] G --> H[Continuous Monitoring Begins] B -- No --> I{Mover Event?} I -- Yes --> J[HR emits Transfer] J --> K[Delta Permission Evaluation] K --> L[Role Owner Approval] L --> M[Old Access Revoked] M --> N[New Access Granted] N --> H I -- No --> O{Leaver Event?} O -- Yes --> P[HR emits Termination] P --> Q[Disable Account Immediate] Q --> R[Revoke All OAuth and SAML Tokens] R --> S[Force Terminate VPN and RDP Sessions] S --> T[Archive Account for 7 Years] T --> U[Purge After Retention Period] H --> V{Recertification Due?} V -- Yes --> W[Quarterly Access Review] W --> X{Access Still Needed?} X -- Yes --> Y[Recertify and Continue] X -- No --> Z[Trigger Deprovisioning] Z --> P V -- No --> H H --> AA[UEBA Anomaly Detection] AA --> AB{IRS Greater Than 0.7} AB -- Yes --> AC[Block and SOC Alert] AB -- No --> H ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: ICS Zone-Based Identity Architecture (Purdue-Aligned) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Level5["Level 5: Enterprise Zone"] E1[Active Directory] E2[HR System SoT] end subgraph DMZ["Industrial DMZ"] D1[ICCP and Historian Gateway] D2[PIM and Bastion Host] D3[Jump Server with MFA] end subgraph Level3["Level 3: Site Operations"] S1[Engineering Workstation] S2[SCADA Server] S3[HMI Console] end subgraph Level2["Level 2: Area Supervisory"] L2A[PLC] L2B[RTU] end subgraph Level1["Level 1: Local Control"] L1A[Sensors] L1B[Actuators] end subgraph Level0["Level 0: Physical Process"] P1[Motor and Valve] end E1 -->|SCIM Sync| D2 E2 -->|Employee Lifecycle| E1 D2 --> D3 D3 -->|JIT Elevation| S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 -->|Modbus TCP| L2A L2A --> L1A L1A --> P1 L2B --> L1B subgraph IdentityPlane["Identity Plane Cross Cutting"] IDP1[SCIM 2.0 Provisioning] IDP2[RBAC and ABAC Engine] IDP3[PAM Vault] IDP4[SIEM and UEBA] end E1 -.-> IDP1 D2 -.-> IDP2 D3 -.-> IDP3 S2 -.-> IDP4 ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Federated Identity Authentication Flow (SAML + OIDC) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant SP as Service Provider DCS Portal participant IdP as Identity Provider AD FS or Okta participant PAM as PAM Vault CyberArk U->>SP: 1 Request access to DCS Console SP->>U: 2 Redirect to IdP with SAML AuthnRequest U->>IdP: 3 Present Credentials and MFA IdP->>U: 4 SAML Assertion signed U->>SP: 5 Forward SAML Assertion SP->>PAM: 6 Check JIT Privilege and Role PAM-->>SP: 7 Grant Temporary Token 30 min SP->>U: 8 Establish Session with Session Recording U->>SP: 9 Perform Action on Engineering Workstation SP-->>PAM: 10 Log Session to Vault Note over U,PAM: Token auto expires and JIT elevation revoked ``` --- ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (ILM System Stack) ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Layer 1: Identity Source of Truth
HR System, Contractor DB, CMDB] --> L2 L2[Layer 2: Identity Governance and Administration
SailPoint, Saviynt, One Identity] --> L3 L2 --> L4 L3[Layer 3: Authentication Services
Active Directory, Azure AD, LDAP, RADIUS] --> L5 L4[Layer 4: Authorization Services
RBAC Engine, ABAC PDP, OAuth 2.0 Server] --> L5 L5[Layer 5: Privileged Access Management
CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Thycotic] --> L6 L6[Layer 6: Target Systems
SCADA, DCS, Historians, PLCs, Engineering Workstations] L1 --> L7[Layer 7: Audit and Analytics
SIEM, UEBA, Compliance Reports] L3 --> L7 L4 --> L7 L5 --> L7 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this architecture matters:** In a **Zero Trust** model, identity is the **new perimeter**. The ILM stack replaces the old "castle-and-moat" firewall approach with continuous verification at every layer. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define Identity Lifecycle Management. List its four main stages.** **Model Answer:** Identity Lifecycle Management (ILM) is the policy-driven process of creating, managing, maintaining, and revoking digital identities for users, services, and devices throughout their operational existence within an organization, ensuring least-privilege access and regulatory compliance. **Four Main Stages (JML + G):** 1. **Joiner (Provisioning):** Identity creation and birthright role assignment. 2. **Mover (Modification):** Role/department change triggers permission update. 3. **Leaver (Deprovisioning):** Account disable, token revocation, archive, and purge. 4. **Governance:** Continuous recertification, access review, SoD enforcement, and audit. *(Self-explanatory, 3 marks)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** **Distinguish between RBAC and ABAC. Give one ICS-relevant example for each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | RBAC | ABAC | |---|---|---| | **Decision basis** | Static role membership | Dynamic attribute evaluation | | **Flexibility** | Low–Medium | High | | **Complexity** | Simple to implement | Complex policy authoring | | **ICS Example** | "Operators in the *Reactor_Operator* role can start Pump P-101" | "Operators **in the *Reactor_Operator* role, during shift 06:00–14:00, and physically present in the Reactor Hall**, can start Pump P-101" | RBAC is suitable for **stable, well-defined OT roles**; ABAC suits **context-aware, dynamic ICS policies** (e.g., shift, zone, certification validity). --- ### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** Explain the **JML (Joiner-Mover-Leaver) framework** in detail, with reference to identity provisioning in an Industrial Control System (ICS). Discuss the role of **SCIM 2.0** and **birthright access**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe **Privileged Access Management (PAM)** for OT environments. Explain **Just-in-Time (JIT) elevation**, **session brokering**, and **credential vaulting** with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — JML Framework (7 Marks)** The **JML framework** is the de-facto model for managing identity states throughout an employee's tenure, codified in NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 (AC-2 family). **1. Joiner Stage (Provisioning) — 2 Marks** - Trigger: HR raises a *New Hire* event in the system of record (e.g., SAP HR). - The **SCIM 2.0 (System for Cross-domain Identity Management)** protocol — an IETF standard (RFC 7643, RFC 7644) — pushes the identity to downstream systems (AD, Azure AD, OT directory, PAM vault). - **Birthright access** automatically assigns a default role (e.g., *Plant_Operator_L1*) based on attributes like Job Title, Department, Employee Type. - Default-deny principle: If no role matches, no access is granted. - MFA is enrolled; PIV/CAC smart card issued for OT zones. **2. Mover Stage (Modification) — 2 Marks** - Trigger: HR raises a *Transfer* event (e.g., Engineer moves from Unit-A to Unit-B). - Provisioning engine performs **delta evaluation**: adds Unit-B permissions, removes Unit-A permissions. - Role-owner approval is mandatory for sensitive entitlements. - **Effective dating** ensures a clean handover without privilege gaps. **3. Leaver Stage (Deprovisioning) — 2 Marks** - Trigger: HR raises a *Termination* event. - Account is **disabled immediately** (login blocked across IT and OT). - All **OAuth 2.0 tokens, SAML assertions, Kerberos TGTs, X.509 certificates** are revoked. - For **ICS contractor accounts**: VPN sessions, RDP tunnels, jump-host sessions are **force-terminated**. - Account is **archived** (preserved for audit) and **purged** after retention period (typically 7 years per regulatory mandates). **4. Why JML is critical in ICS — 1 Mark** OT environments (per IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1, SR 2.1) mandate that identities be uniquely identified, authenticated, and authorized before accessing safety-critical systems. JML directly addresses the **TRITON, Colonial Pipeline, and Oldsmar** attack patterns where stale credentials were the initial vector. --- #### **Part (b) — Privileged Access Management in OT (7 Marks)** **PAM** is a security discipline that controls, monitors, and audits *elevated* access to critical systems. **1. Credential Vaulting — 2 Marks** - All privileged credentials (root, admin, service accounts) are stored encrypted in a **centralized vault** (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust). - **No human ever knows the actual password** — the PAM broker injects it on demand. - Credentials are **auto-rotated** after every checkout (typically every 1–24 hours). **2. Just-in-Time (JIT) Elevation — 2 Marks** - Default state: User has **zero admin rights**. - When a task requires elevation, the user raises a **JIT request** with justification. - A policy engine (e.g., based on ABAC) evaluates: time-of-day, asset sensitivity, ticket reference, change-window. - If approved, the privilege is **granted for a bounded window** (e.g., 30–60 minutes) and **auto-revoked** thereafter. - Formula (from Section 2.3): $\Delta T_{\text{elev}} = T_{\text{revoke}} - T_{\text{grant}}$ — best practice: < 60 min. **3. Session Brokering & Recording — 2 Marks** - The user connects through a **PAM proxy/jump server** (Bastion host). - All keystrokes, screen activity, and commands are **recorded in full motion video (FMV)**. - Recordings are immutable (WORM storage) and used for forensic analysis and compliance audits (PCI-DSS, NERC-CIP, IEC 62443). **4. Diagram — 1 Mark** *(Refer to Section 4.3 — Federated Identity + PAM flow diagram.)* **Conclusion:** JIT + Vaulting + Session Recording forms the **"Zero Standing Privilege"** model, the gold standard for OT privileged access. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice (Alternative to Question A)* **(a)** Compare **RBAC, ABAC, and PBAC** access control models. Discuss which is most suitable for a **multi-vendor smart factory** with 5,000+ users. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A power utility has **12,000 active OT/IT identities**. Current statistics: - Orphan Account Rate: **2.4%** - Time-to-Provision: **36 hours** - Time-to-Revoke: **4 days** - Annual OT breach attempts: **17** **Calculate:** (i) Number of orphan accounts. (ii) Required Time-to-Revoke to meet the **15-minute SLA** for privileged accounts. (iii) Estimated cost savings if 90% of password resets are self-serviced. Assume: 38% reset rate, ₹120 per manual reset, ₹10 per self-service reset. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Comparison of Access Control Models (7 Marks)** *(Refer to the comparison table in Section 2.2; expand with examples.)* | Criterion | RBAC | ABAC | PBAC | |---|---|---|---| | **Standard** | ANSI INCITS 359 | NIST SP 800-162 | NIST SP 800-162 (Policy-based) | | **Decision input** | User→Role mapping | User/Resource/Env/Action attributes | Externalized XACML policies | | **Granularity** | Medium | High | Very High | | **Scalability** | Role explosion issue | Scales well | Scales with PDP performance | | **ICS suitability** | Stable plant roles | Dynamic shift/zone logic | Federated multi-vendor | | **Example** | "Engineer role can view HMI" | "Engineer on Day Shift in Zone-3 with valid cert can view HMI" | "Per policy PLC-Auth-7: any certified engineer from approved vendor can HMI if in maintenance window" | **Best fit for a multi-vendor smart factory: ABAC** — because vendors have different attribute sets (vendor ID, contract validity, certification), and access must be context-aware (shift, zone, ticket linkage). PBAC (using XACML) is preferred when policies must be centrally auditable and shareable across vendor domains. --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)** **(i) Number of orphan accounts: (2 Marks)** $$N_{\text{orphan}} = N_{\text{total}} \times OAR = 12{,}000 \times 0.024 = 288 \text{ accounts}$$ **(ii) Required TTR for 15-minute SLA: (2 Marks)** Current TTR = 4 days = $4 \times 24 \times 60 = 5760$ minutes. Required TTR = **15 minutes**. **Reduction factor:** $$\text{Reduction} = \frac{5760}{15} = 384\times$$ This requires **fully automated deprovisioning** triggered by HRIS events via SCIM/webhook — manual ticket-based deprovisioning is insufficient. **(iii) Cost savings on self-service password resets: (3 Marks)** Number of password resets per year: $$N_{\text{reset}} = 12{,}000 \times 0.38 = 4{,}560 \text{ resets}$$ Cost at 100% manual (current scenario): $$C_{\text{manual}} = 4560 \times 120 = \text{₹ } 5{,}47{,}200$$ Cost with 90% self-service, 10% manual: $$C_{\text{new}} = (4560 \times 0.90 \times 10) + (4560 \times 0.10 \times 120)$$ $$C_{\text{new}} = 41{,}040 + 54{,}720 = \text{₹ } 95{,}760$$ **Savings:** $$\text{Savings} = 5{,}47{,}200 - 95{,}760 = \text{₹ } 4{,}51{,}440 \text{ per year}$$ **Savings %:** $$\frac{4{,}51{,}440}{5{,}47{,}200} \times 100 \approx 82.5\%$$ **Conclusion:** Self-service automation alone yields ~**82.5% reduction** in password reset costs. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing RBAC vs ABAC:** Many students write *"RBAC is more secure than ABAC"* — this is **wrong**. ABAC is more *granular*; RBAC is more *manageable*. Security depends on *implementation*. > 2. **Forgetting the 'G' in JML:** Students list only J, M, L and miss **Governance** (recertification). This loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Units confusion:** TTR is in **minutes**, not hours or days. Always state units. > 4. **Not relating to ICS:** Generic IT answers score lower. Always anchor your answer to **OT, SCADA, DCS, Purdue Model, IEC 62443**. > 5. **Skipping diagrams:** In 14-mark questions, **a labeled diagram fetches at least 1–2 extra marks**. Always include a JML flow or RBAC architecture diagram. > 6. **Formulas without variable definitions:** When writing OAR, TTR, IRS — **define every variable** before substituting. Examiners deduct marks for undefined symbols. --- ### 📋 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** - ✅ **ILM** = policy-driven management of identity from creation to deletion. - ✅ **JML + G** = Joiner, Mover, Leaver, Governance — the four pillars. - ✅ **SCIM 2.0** is the IETF-standard protocol for identity provisioning. - ✅ **Birthright access** = auto-assigned default entitlements based on attributes. - ✅ **RBAC** is role-based; **ABAC** is attribute-based; **PBAC** is policy-based (XACML). - ✅ **PAM** = Vaulting + JIT elevation + Session brokering/recording. - ✅ **Zero Standing Privilege (ZSP)** is the modern gold standard. - ✅ **Orphan Account Rate (OAR)** should be **< 0.5%**. - ✅ **Time-to-Revoke (TTR)** for privileged accounts must be **< 15 minutes**. - ✅ **Recertification** every 90 days (privileged) and 180 days (standard). - ✅ **Segregation of Duties (SoD)** prevents toxic role combinations. - ✅ **UEBA** (User & Entity Behavior Analytics) computes **IRS** to detect anomalies. - ✅ In ICS, ILM aligns with **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1, SR 1.3, SR 2.1** and **NIST SP 800-53 AC-2**. - ✅ **Identity is the new perimeter** — Zero Trust demands continuous verification. - ✅ Always **state formulas with units** and **define variables** in exam answers. - ✅ Use **Purdue Model (Levels 0–5)** to contextualize any ICS answer. ---

topics.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Single Sign-On (SSO) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Single Sign-On (SSO)** is a federated authentication architecture and session management scheme that enables a user to authenticate **once** with a single set of credentials (username/password, token, or biometric) and gain seamless, trusted access to multiple — otherwise independent — application systems, domains, and services within a single session, without re-entering credentials at each system. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme and the **PECCT524 – Advanced Industrial Cyber Security** syllabus, SSO is classified under **Identity, Authentication, and Access Management (IAM)** as a **federated identity solution**. It is formally built on the **AAA framework** (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) and the **Zero Trust** paradigm of *verify-always*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Federation** is the formal term used in KTU 2024 scheme modules to describe the trust relationship between an **Identity Provider (IdP)** and a **Service Provider (SP)** that enables SSO to function across organizational boundaries. ### 1.2 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Standard) | Acronym | Full Form | Functional Role | |---------|-----------|-----------------| | **IdP** | Identity Provider | Authenticates the user and issues assertions/tokens | | **SP** | Service Provider | The target application the user wants to access | | **SAML** | Security Assertion Markup Language | XML-based open standard for exchanging auth data | | **OIDC** | OpenID Connect | Identity layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 | | **TGT** | Ticket Granting Ticket | Kerberos session token issued by KDC | | **STS** | Security Token Service | Issues security tokens in WS-Trust / OAuth flows | | **AAL** | Authenticator Assurance Level | NIST SP 800-63B strength metric (**AAL1, AAL2, AAL3**) | --- ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The Airport Boarding Pass Imagine an international traveller clearing a multi-leg journey: 1. At the **first check-in counter**, the passenger shows a **passport** (single credential) and receives a **boarding pass** (assertion/token). 2. At the **security gate**, the same boarding pass is verified against the airline's trusted database (IdP) — no re-presentation of the passport. 3. At every subsequent **lounge access**, **duty-free shop**, and **boarding gate**, the same boarding pass is honored without re-identification. In this analogy: - The **passport** = the user's primary credential. - The **airline database** = the Identity Provider (IdP). - The **boarding pass** = the SAML Assertion / OAuth Token / Kerberos TGT. - The **gates, lounges, shops** = Service Providers (SPs). - The **airline-to-airline trust agreement** = the **Federation Trust**. > [!TIP] > **Why this matters in Industry:** In a typical enterprise with 50+ SaaS applications (SAP, Salesforce, Office 365, AWS, GitHub), without SSO, employees manage 50+ passwords, leading to **password fatigue**, **credential reuse**, and **phishing susceptibility**. SSO collapses this surface area to **one strong credential**, drastically reducing the human-factor attack vector — a core concern of **PECCT524 Module 3**. --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Architecture — How SSO Works (Step-by-Step Logic) The operational flow of an SSO transaction is decomposed into **7 logical phases**. The KTU board examiner typically awards marks for correctly identifying these phases. ### Phase 1 — Service Request Initiation - The user (User Agent / Browser) attempts to access a protected resource at the **Service Provider (SP)**. - The SP has **no local session** for the user, so it generates a **SAML AuthnRequest** (or OIDC authorization request). ### Phase 2 — Redirect to Identity Provider - The SP returns an **HTTP 302 Redirect** to the user's browser, pointing to the IdP's **Single Sign-On Service (SSOS) URL**. - The redirect carries the request as a **Base64-encoded query parameter** (`SAMLRequest=...`). ### Phase 3 — Credential Presentation - The IdP identifies the user via: - An existing **IdP session cookie** (the true "single sign" — if absent, prompt for credentials). - Or a fresh credential submission (password + MFA). ### Phase 4 — Assertion Generation - Upon successful authentication, the IdP constructs a **signed assertion** containing: - Subject (NameID of the user) - Audience (intended SP) - NotOnOrAfter (validity window — typically **5 minutes**) - Authentication context (password / MFA / X.509) - Attribute statements (role, group, email) ### Phase 5 — Token/Assertion Delivery - The IdP issues an **HTTP POST** containing the signed assertion to the SP's **Assertion Consumer Service (ACS)** endpoint. ### Phase 6 — Token Validation - The SP validates the assertion by: - Verifying the **XML Digital Signature** using the IdP's public certificate. - Checking the **AudienceRestriction** matches its own entityID. - Validating the **NotOnOrAfter** timestamp (replay protection). ### Phase 7 — Local Session Establishment - The SP creates a local session (cookie) for the user, granting access to the requested resource. --- ## 2.2 The "Why" — Engineering Rationale | Engineering Goal | SSO Mechanism | |------------------|---------------| | Reduce **password reuse** | One credential governs N applications | | Centralize **audit logging** | All auth events funnel through the IdP | | Enforce **MFA uniformly** | MFA is added once at the IdP, inherited by all SPs | | Implement **Just-In-Time provisioning** | IdP pushes user attributes to SP at first login | | Support **Zero Trust** | Continuous verification via short-lived tokens | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize this table.** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Constant | |---|---------|----------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Assertion Validity Window** | $\Delta t = t_{\text{NotOnOrAfter}} - t_{\text{IssueInstant}}$ | $\Delta t \le 5\,\text{minutes}$ (SAML 2.0 best practice) | | 2 | **Token Replay Window** | $t_{\text{skew}} \le 30\,\text{seconds}$ | NIST SP 800-63B clock skew tolerance | | 3 | **Authenticator Assurance Level** | $\text{AAL} \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | AAL1 = single factor, AAL3 = hardware crypto + MFA | | 4 | **OAuth 2.0 Access Token Lifetime** | $t_{\text{access}} \le 1\,\text{hour}$ | RFC 6749 recommendation | | 5 | **Refresh Token Rotation** | $t_{\text{refresh}} \le 90\,\text{days}$ | RFC 6749 §6 | | 6 | **Kerberos TGT Lifetime** | $t_{\text{TGT}} \le 10\,\text{hours}$ | RFC 4120 default | | 7 | **Kerberos Ticket Skew Tolerance** | $\delta t \le 5\,\text{minutes}$ | Configurable per realm policy | | 8 | **Federation Trust Equation** | $\text{Trust}_{A \leftrightarrow B} = f(\text{Sig}_{A}, \text{Sig}_{B}, \text{Audit}_{A}, \text{Audit}_{B})$ | Boolean — established or rejected | | 9 | **Identity Assurance Level** | $\text{IAL} \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | NIST SP 800-63A | | 10 | **Federation Assurance Level** | $\text{FAL} \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | NIST SP 800-63C | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **AAL** (Authenticator strength) with **IAL** (Identity proofing strength). Memorize the distinction: **AAL = how you prove you are you**, **IAL = how strongly your identity was verified at enrollment**. --- ## 2.4 SSO Protocol Family — Comparative Matrix | Protocol | Layer | Token Format | Best Suited For | KTU Weight | |----------|-------|--------------|-----------------|------------| | **SAML 2.0** | XML/SOAP/HTTP | SAML Assertion (XML) | Enterprise B2B federation, browser SSO | **High** | | **OAuth 2.0** | HTTP/JSON | Bearer Access Token | API authorization (not authentication!) | **High** | | **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** | HTTP/JSON | ID Token (JWT) + Access Token | Modern web/mobile SSO, consumer apps | **High** | | **Kerberos** | TCP/UDP | TGT, Service Ticket | On-premise Windows/Active Directory | **Medium** | | **WS-Federation** | SOAP/XML | SAML-like token | Microsoft AD FS legacy environments | **Low** | | **LDAP** | TCP/389/636 | Bind operation | Directory access, not strictly SSO | **Low** | --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Mathematical Modeling of SSO Token Validation The validation of an SSO assertion (SAML, OIDC, or Kerberos) can be expressed as a **decision function** that the SP evaluates. We derive this formally. ### 3.1.1 Inputs to the Validation Function Let the assertion be denoted as a tuple: $$A = (S, R, I, N, \sigma, P_{IdP})$$ where each element is defined below. $$S = \text{Subject identifier (NameID or 'sub' claim)}$$ $$R = \text{Recipient / Audience URI of the target SP}$$ $$I = \text{IssueInstant timestamp at which the IdP generated the assertion}$$ $$N = \text{NotOnOrAfter — assertion expiry timestamp}$$ $$\sigma = \text{XML Digital Signature or JWS signature over the assertion body}$$ $$P_{IdP} = \text{Public key certificate of the issuing Identity Provider}$$ ### 3.1.2 Validation Function Definition The SP's validation function $\mathcal{V}(A)$ returns a Boolean decision. $$\mathcal{V}(A) = \mathbb{1}\Big[\text{VerifySig}(\sigma, P_{IdP}) \;\wedge\; R = \text{self} \;\wedge\; t_{\text{now}} \le N \;\wedge\; t_{\text{now}} \ge I - \delta t\Big]$$ Here the indicator function $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ evaluates to **1** (accept) only if **all four conjunctive conditions** hold true. ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Expansion of Each Conjunct **Step 1 — Signature Verification:** $$\text{VerifySig}(\sigma, P_{IdP}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{Hash}(A_{\text{body}}) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Decrypt}_{P_{IdP}}(\sigma) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ This step guarantees **integrity** and **authenticity** of the assertion. The hash algorithm is typically **SHA-256** in modern deployments. **Step 2 — Audience Restriction Match:** $$R \stackrel{?}{=} \text{self} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R \in \text{AudienceList}_{SP}$$ This prevents **token redirection attacks** where an assertion for SP-A is replayed against SP-B. **Step 3 — Temporal Validity (Freshness Check):** $$t_{\text{now}} \le N$$ The current server time must not exceed the NotOnOrAfter timestamp. Otherwise, the token has **expired** and is rejected. **Step 4 — Anti-Replay Skew Window:** $$t_{\text{now}} \ge I - \delta t$$ This ensures the assertion was issued recently and was not replayed from a captured earlier session. $\delta t$ is the clock-skew tolerance, typically **30 seconds** per NIST guidance. ### 3.1.4 Final Decision Rule $$\mathcal{V}(A) = 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{4} C_i(A) = 1$$ If $\mathcal{V}(A) = 1$, the SP establishes a local session $S_{\text{local}}$ for the user. $$S_{\text{local}} = \text{CreateCookie}(S, t_{\text{expiry}} = t_{\text{now}} + \Delta t_{\text{session}})$$ where $\Delta t_{\text{session}}$ is the SP-side session timeout, typically **8 hours** for enterprise apps and **30 minutes** for high-security apps. --- ## 3.2 Kerberos Authentication — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation Kerberos is the **canonical SSO protocol** in industrial and on-premise enterprise environments. The KTU board frequently asks for a description of its phases. ### Phase 1 — AS-REQ (Authentication Service Request) The client $C$ sends its identity to the **Authentication Server (AS)** within the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**: $$C \rightarrow AS : \{ID_C, ID_{TGS}, N_1\}$$ where $ID_C$ is the client ID, $ID_{TGS}$ is the requested TGS service ID, and $N_1$ is a **nonce** for replay protection. ### Phase 2 — AS-REP (Authentication Service Reply) The AS verifies $ID_C$ against its principal database, then issues a **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)** encrypted with the TGS's secret key $K_{TGS}$: $$AS \rightarrow C : \{K_{C,TGS}, ID_{TGS}, TS_1, \text{Lifetime}_1\}K_C \,\|\, \{ID_C, K_{C,TGS}, TS_1, \text{Lifetime}_1\}K_{TGS}$$ The double-ticket structure here is critical: the **first part** is encrypted with the client's secret key $K_C$ (derived from the password), and the **second part** is the TGT itself, encrypted with $K_{TGS}$. ### Phase 3 — TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Service Request) The client decrypts the first part, extracts the session key $K_{C,TGS}$, then requests access to a target service $V$: $$C \rightarrow TGS : \{ID_V, TGT, \text{Authenticator}_1\}$$ where the **authenticator** is constructed as: $$\text{Authenticator}_1 = \{ID_C, AD_C, TS_2\}K_{C,TGS}$$ and $AD_C$ is the client's network address. ### Phase 4 — TGS-REP (Ticket Granting Service Reply) The TGS decrypts the TGT using $K_{TGS}$, recovers $K_{C,TGS}$, decrypts the authenticator, verifies $TS_2$ is current, and issues a **Service Ticket (ST)**: $$TGS \rightarrow C : \{K_{C,V}, ID_V, TS_3, \text{Lifetime}_3\}K_{C,TGS} \,\|\, \{ID_C, K_{C,V}, AD_C, TS_3, \text{Lifetime}_3\}K_V$$ ### Phase 5 — AP-REQ (Application Request) The client presents the service ticket to the target application server $V$: $$C \rightarrow V : \{ST, \text{Authenticator}_2\}$$ where $\text{Authenticator}_2 = \{ID_C, TS_4\}K_{C,V}$. ### Phase 6 — AP-REP (Mutual Authentication, optional) The server proves its identity to the client: $$V \rightarrow C : \{TS_4 + 1\}K_{C,V}$$ This **mutual authentication** step defeats man-in-the-middle attacks and is a defining feature of Kerberos. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Protocol > [!TIP] > **High-yield for KTU 2024 ESE — Part B questions often test protocol selection.** | Scenario | Recommended Protocol | Justification | |----------|---------------------|---------------| | Cross-organization SaaS federation | **SAML 2.0** | Mature, well-supported, browser-based | | Mobile/native app API access | **OAuth 2.0 + OIDC** | JSON-friendly, lightweight, JWT-based | | Internal Windows Active Directory | **Kerberos** | Native, ticket-based, mutual auth | | Microservices in Kubernetes | **OAuth 2.0 + mTLS** | Service-to-service, short-lived tokens | | Legacy mainframe integration | **SAML 2.0** or **WS-Federation** | XML/SOAP compatibility | | Consumer-facing login (Google/Facebook) | **OIDC** | Standardized, JWT, easy to implement | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level SSO Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB UserAgent["USER AGENT
Browser or Native App"] SP1["SERVICE PROVIDER A
Salesforce"] SP2["SERVICE PROVIDER B
SAP"] SP3["SERVICE PROVIDER C
Office 365"] IdP["IDENTITY PROVIDER
Okta / Azure AD / ADFS"] AuthDB[("USER DIRECTORY
LDAP / Active Directory")] MFA["MFA ENGINE
TOTP / FIDO2 / Push"] UserAgent -->|1 Access Request| SP1 SP1 -->|2 Redirect to SSO| UserAgent UserAgent -->|3 SAMLRequest| IdP IdP -->|4 Credential Check| AuthDB IdP -->|5 Challenge| MFA MFA -->|6 Verified| IdP IdP -->|7 Signed Assertion| UserAgent UserAgent -->|8 POST Assertion| SP1 SP1 -.->|9 Subsequent Access| SP2 SP1 -.->|9 Subsequent Access| SP3 SP2 -.->|Trust Anchor| IdP SP3 -.->|Trust Anchor| IdP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Notice how the user authenticates **once** at the IdP (steps 1–8), and then subsequent access to SP-B and SP-C (step 9) is **silent** because the IdP's session cookie is still valid.
