This is the **"single sign"** in action. --- ## 4.2 SAML 2.0 SP-Initiated Flow (Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant U as User Browser participant SP as Service Provider participant IdP as Identity Provider participant DS as User Directory U->>SP: GET /protected-resource SP-->>U: HTTP 302 Redirect (SAMLRequest) U->>IdP: GET /SSO?SAMLRequest=... IdP->>U: Login Page (if no session) U->>IdP: POST credentials + MFA token IdP->>DS: Bind / Lookup user DS-->>IdP: User attributes IdP-->>U: HTTP 200 + Auto-POST form (SAMLResponse) U->>SP: POST /ACS SAMLResponse (signed) SP->>SP: Validate signature, audience, timestamps SP-->>U: HTTP 200 + Session cookie (access granted) Note over U,SP: Subsequent SPs reuse IdP session cookie - no re-auth ``` --- ## 4.3 Kerberos Ticket Exchange (State Diagram) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unauthenticated Unauthenticated --> AS_REQ: Client sends ID_C to AS AS_REQ --> AS_REP: AS verifies and returns TGT AS_REP --> TGT_Received: Client decrypts with K_C TGT_Received --> TGS_REQ: Request service ticket for V TGS_REQ --> TGS_REP: TGS issues service ticket ST TGS_REP --> AP_REQ: Client presents ST to server V AP_REQ --> AP_REP: Mutual auth TS_4+1 AP_REP --> Authenticated: Session established Authenticated --> [*]: Session expires or logout ``` --- ## 4.4 OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow with OIDC ```mermaid flowchart LR A["User
Resource Owner"] -->|1 Login| B["Client App
RP / SP"] B -->|2 Auth Code Request| C["Authorization Server
OIDC Provider"] C -->|3 Login + Consent| A A -->|4 Approve| C C -->|5 Auth Code| B B -->|6 Auth Code + Client Secret| C C -->|7 ID Token + Access Token + Refresh Token| B B -->|8 Access Token in Authorization Header| D["Resource Server
API"] D -->|9 Protected Resource| B ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Distinction:** The **ID Token** in OIDC proves the user's *identity* (authentication). The **Access Token** grants the client permission to call APIs (authorization). Conflating these is a **common 2-mark deduction** in the KTU valuation key. --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PECCT524]** > Define **Single Sign-On (SSO)**. List any **four advantages** of implementing SSO in an enterprise environment. ### Model Answer (3 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** Single Sign-On (SSO) is an authentication mechanism that allows a user to log in **once** with a single set of credentials and gain access to multiple independent applications or systems within the same session, **without re-entering credentials** at each application. **Four Advantages (½ mark each):** 1. **Reduced Password Fatigue** — Users manage one strong password instead of dozens, lowering the risk of weak/reused passwords. 2. **Centralized Authentication Control** — IT administrators enforce policies (MFA, password complexity, lockout) at a single IdP, gaining unified visibility. 3. **Improved Security Posture** — Eliminates credential stuffing and phishing across federated apps; integrates seamlessly with MFA. 4. **Enhanced User Productivity** — Faster access, fewer helpdesk password-reset tickets, seamless cross-application navigation. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PECCT524]** > Differentiate between **SAML 2.0** and **OpenID Connect (OIDC)**. State **two** distinguishing features of each. ### Model Answer (3 Marks) | Aspect | **SAML 2.0** | **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** | |--------|--------------|---------------------------| | Token Format | **XML-based** SAML Assertion | **JSON-based** ID Token (JWT) | | Transport | **HTTP Redirect / POST binding** (browser-centric) | **HTTPS + JSON** (REST-friendly) | | Primary Use Case | Enterprise **B2B federation** | Modern **web / mobile / SPA** apps | | Year / Standard | OASIS, 2005 | OpenID Foundation, 2014 | **Two distinguishing features of SAML 2.0 (½ + ½ = 1 mark):** - Uses **XML Digital Signatures** for assertion integrity. - Designed for **browser-based SSO** with the SP and IdP exchanging signed XML assertions. **Two distinguishing features of OIDC (½ + ½ = 1 mark):** - Built as a **thin identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0**. - Issues a **JWT ID Token** signed using **JWS (JSON Web Signature)**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT524, Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain the **architecture of Single Sign-On (SSO)** with a neat block diagram. Identify the role of the **Identity Provider (IdP)**, **Service Provider (SP)**, and the **user agent**. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **complete SAML 2.0 SP-Initiated SSO flow** with all six phases, including the contents of the SAML Assertion. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — SSO Architecture (7 Marks) The SSO architecture follows a **federated identity model** consisting of three principal actors and an underlying trust layer. **1. User Agent (1 mark):** The **User Agent** (web browser, mobile app, or thick client) acts on behalf of the **end user**. It is the carrier of HTTP requests, redirects, and the SAML/OIDC tokens between the IdP and SP. It does not interpret the cryptographic contents of the assertion — it merely transports them. **2. Service Provider – SP (2 marks):** The SP is the **target application** (e.g., Salesforce, Workday, GitHub) that the user wishes to access. It: - **Protects resources** by checking for a valid local session. - On session absence, **redirects** the user to the IdP's SSO endpoint. - **Validates** the assertion received from the IdP (signature, audience, timestamps). - **Establishes a local session** upon successful validation. - Maintains a **federation trust** with the IdP (shared certificate or metadata exchange). **3. Identity Provider – IdP (2 marks):** The IdP (e.g., Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, Ping Identity) is the **central authentication authority**. It: - Maintains the **user directory** (LDAP, AD, SQL). - **Authenticates** the user (password + MFA). - **Generates and signs** the SAML/OIDC assertion. - Manages **user attributes, group memberships, and roles** transmitted via assertions. - Issues **federated identity claims** to all trusted SPs. **4. Federation Trust (1 mark):** The IdP and SP establish a **mutual trust relationship** by exchanging **metadata documents** containing: - Entity IDs - Public-key certificates (X.509) - SSO endpoint URLs - ACS (Assertion Consumer Service) URLs This trust is the **cryptographic root** of SSO — without it, no SP will accept an IdP's assertion. **5. Neat Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 above for the complete Mermaid block diagram.)* --- #### Part (b) — SAML 2.0 SP-Initiated Flow (7 Marks) **Phase 1 — Resource Request (1 mark):** The user attempts to access a protected URL on the SP, e.g., `https://sp.example.com/dashboard`. The SP detects no local session and constructs a **SAML AuthnRequest** XML document containing the SP's entityID and an autogenerated `ID` and `IssueInstant`. **Phase 2 — Redirect to IdP (1 mark):** The SP returns an **HTTP 302 Redirect** to the browser with the AuthnRequest **Base64-encoded** as a URL parameter: ``` https://idp.example.com/SSOS?SAMLRequest=&RelayState= ``` **Phase 3 — Authentication at IdP (1 mark):** The IdP checks for an existing IdP session cookie. If absent, it presents a login page. The user submits credentials + MFA token. The IdP validates them against the user directory. **Phase 4 — Assertion Generation (2 marks):** The IdP constructs a **SAML Response** containing a **SAML Assertion** with the following elements: - `` — the IdP's entityID - `` — the user's unique identifier - `` — validity window - `` — the SP's entityID - `` — authentication context (time, method) - `` — optional user attributes (role, email) - **XML Digital Signature** using the IdP's private key **Phase 5 — POST to ACS (1 mark):** The IdP returns an **auto-submitting HTML form** that POSTs the Base64-encoded SAMLResponse to the SP's **Assertion Consumer Service (ACS)** endpoint. **Phase 6 — Validation and Session (1 mark):** The SP: - Verifies the **XML Digital Signature** using the IdP's public certificate. - Confirms the **Audience** matches its own entityID. - Checks the **NotOnOrAfter** timestamp (replay protection). - Extracts user attributes and creates a **local session cookie**. The user is then granted access to the protected resource. Subsequent accesses to other SPs within the same IdP session require **no re-authentication**. --- ### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT524, Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain the **Kerberos authentication protocol** in detail with a sequence diagram. List the **six phases** and describe the role of the **KDC, TGT, and Service Ticket**. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Compare and contrast **SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, and OpenID Connect** as SSO protocols. Provide a **suitability matrix** and discuss **two security threats** specific to SSO implementations and their mitigations. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Kerberos Authentication (7 Marks) **Overview (1 mark):** Kerberos is a **ticket-based symmetric-key authentication protocol** designed for **on-premise enterprise networks** (e.g., Windows Active Directory). It provides **mutual authentication** — both client and server prove their identities to each other — and is the **canonical SSO protocol** for internal corporate environments. **Key Components (1 mark):** - **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** — combines the AS (Authentication Server) and TGS (Ticket Granting Server). - **Principal** — a uniquely named client or service. - **Realm** — the administrative domain governed by a KDC. - **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)** — a ticket encrypted with the TGS's secret key, used to request service tickets. - **Service Ticket (ST)** — a ticket encrypted with the target service's secret key, used to access that service. **Six Phases (5 marks — distributed):** **Phase 1 — AS-REQ:** The client $C$ sends $(ID_C, ID_{TGS}, N_1)$ to the AS. **Phase 2 — AS-REP:** The AS replies with a response encrypted using the client's secret key $K_C$ (derived from the user's password), containing a session key $K_{C,TGS}$ and a **TGT** encrypted with the TGS's secret key $K_{TGS}$. **Phase 3 — TGS-REQ:** The client presents the TGT to the TGS along with the requested service ID $ID_V$ and an **authenticator** $\{$`ID_C, AD_C, TS_2`$\}K_{C,TGS}$. **Phase 4 — TGS-REP:** The TGS verifies the TGT and authenticator, then issues a **Service Ticket** encrypted with the target service's key $K_V$, plus a new session key $K_{C,V}$ encrypted with $K_{C,TGS}$. **Phase 5 — AP-REQ:** The client sends the service ticket and a fresh authenticator to the target server $V$. **Phase 6 — AP-REP (Mutual Auth):** The server returns $\{TS_4 + 1\}K_{C,V}$ to prove it knows $K_{C,V}$, completing **mutual authentication**. **Sequence Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to the Kerberos state diagram in Section 4.3 above.)* --- #### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis & Security Threats (7 Marks) **SAML 2.0 vs OAuth 2.0 vs OpenID Connect (4 marks):** | Feature | **SAML 2.0** | **OAuth 2.0** | **OpenID Connect** | |---------|--------------|---------------|---------------------| | **Purpose** | Authentication & federation | Authorization (delegation) | Authentication (built on OAuth 2.0) | | **Token Format** | XML (SAML Assertion) | Opaque string / JWT | JWT (ID Token) | | **Transport** | Browser HTTP Redirect / POST | HTTPS REST | HTTPS REST | | **Initiator** | SP-initiated or IdP-initiated | Client-initiated | Client-initiated | | **Best For** | Enterprise B2B SaaS | API authorization | Modern web/mobile SSO | | **Standard Body** | OASIS | IETF RFC 6749 | OpenID Foundation | | **Mutual Auth** | Via XML Signature | Not native | Via JWS signature | **Suitability Matrix (1 mark):** | Use Case | Recommended Protocol | |----------|---------------------| | Enterprise cross-org federation | **SAML 2.0** | | Mobile app login with social provider | **OIDC** | | Microservices / API access | **OAuth 2.0** | | Windows AD domain login | **Kerberos** | **Two Security Threats & Mitigations (2 marks — 1 each):** 1. **Threat: SAML Signature Wrapping Attack (XSW).** - **Description:** Attacker submits a malformed SAML response with the legitimate signed assertion placed in a different XML element, while inserting a malicious unsigned assertion that the SP accepts. - **Mitigation:** Use **strict XML schema validation**, validate the assertion **ID reference**, and employ updated IdP libraries that enforce canonicalized XML signature checks. 2. **Threat: Single Point of Failure (SPoF) — IdP Compromise.** - **Description:** If the IdP is compromised, attackers gain federated access to **all** downstream SPs. - **Mitigation:** Deploy the IdP in **High-Availability (HA) clustered mode**, enforce **hardware-backed MFA (FIDO2 / WebAuthn)** at the IdP, enable **continuous authentication / risk-based access**, and apply the principle of **least privilege** with short-lived tokens. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on SSO Questions:** > 1. **Conflating OAuth 2.0 with OIDC:** OAuth 2.0 is an **authorization** framework; it does NOT authenticate users. OIDC adds the identity layer. Mixing these up costs **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Forgetting Mutual Authentication in Kerberos:** Many students stop at the AP-REQ. Mentioning the **AP-REP (TS+1) step** earns the final mark. > 3. **Omitting the Federation Trust:** An SSO architecture explanation that does not mention **metadata exchange / certificate trust** between IdP and SP is **incomplete** — KTU awards 1 mark specifically for this. > 4. **Drawing SP and IdP as the same entity:** They are **distinct**; merging them on the diagram loses **1 mark** in the architecture question. > 5. **Skipping the `NotOnOrAfter` timestamp:** Always state the validity window in SAML flow answers. Default is **5 minutes** unless specified. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single Sign-On (SSO)** - **Core Idea:** Authenticate **once**, access **many** — reduces password fatigue and centralizes IAM. - **Three Actors:** **User Agent** (carrier), **Service Provider** (resource), **Identity Provider** (authenticator & assertion issuer). - **Federation Trust:** The cryptographic foundation — established via **metadata + X.509 certificates**. - **SAML 2.0:** XML-based, enterprise B2B, browser-centric, **5-minute** token validity. - **OAuth 2.0:** Authorization framework, **NOT** authentication; uses bearer tokens for API access. - **OpenID Connect (OIDC):** Identity layer on OAuth 2.0, uses **JWT ID Tokens**, modern web/mobile standard. - **Kerberos:** Ticket-based, **mutual authentication**, on-premise Windows AD, default TGT lifetime **10 hours**, clock skew **5 minutes**. - **Validation Function:** $\mathcal{V}(A) = \mathbb{1}[\text{Sig} \wedge \text{Audience} \wedge \text{Freshness} \wedge \text{Skew}]$ — all four conditions must hold. - **Key Threats:** SAML Signature Wrapping, IdP compromise (SPoF), token replay, session hijack. - **Mitigations:** HA IdP, hardware MFA, short-lived tokens, strict XML/JWT validation, continuous authentication. - **NIST Standards:** AAL (1–3) for auth strength, IAL (1–3) for identity proofing, FAL (1–3) for federation. - **Distinctions to Memorize:** - **AAL** = authenticator strength - **IAL** = identity proofing strength - **FAL** = federation assertion strength - **OAuth 2.0 ≠ OIDC ≠ SAML** — three different protocols with overlapping use cases - **Industrial Relevance:** Mandatory in **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)**, **Industry 4.0 OT/IT convergence**, and **cloud-native IAM stacks** (Okta, Azure AD, Ping). - **Token Lifetimes to Memorize:** SAML = **5 min**, OAuth access token = **1 hour**, Kerberos TGT = **10 hours**, clock skew = **30 sec (NIST) / 5 min (Kerberos)**. ---

topics.title: Federation and Trust Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Federation and Trust Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Federation (Identity Federation):** A standardized framework that enables the secure sharing of identity information and authentication credentials across multiple autonomous, heterogeneous, and administratively independent security domains (organizations), allowing users to use a single set of credentials (Single Sign-On, SSO) to access resources held by different service providers.

**Trust Model:** The formally defined set of rules, cryptographic protocols, policy agreements, and assertion mechanisms that govern the verification, acceptance, and reliance of a *Relying Party* (Service Provider) on the identity assertions issued by an *Asserting Party* (Identity Provider) regarding an authenticated *Subject* (User/Entity).

In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus for Advanced Industrial Cyber Security, federation and trust models form the cornerstone of **Zero Trust Architectures (ZTA)**, **Industrial Identity Management**, and **Cross-Domain Authentication** in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) environments.

### 1.2 Key Actors in a Federated Trust Framework

| Actor | Formal Role | Industrial Analogy |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Subject (User)** | The entity requesting access to a protected resource. | An operator/engineer in a plant needing SCADA dashboard access. |
| **Identity Provider (IdP)** | Issues authentication assertions after verifying the subject's credentials. | Corporate LDAP/Active Directory server in a plant. |
| **Relying Party / Service Provider (RP/SP)** | Consumes the assertion to grant access to its resources. | Cloud-based Historian, ERP, or remote HMI server. |
| **Attribute Authority (AA)** | Issues verified attribute claims about the subject (e.g., role, clearance). | HR system issuing "Shift Supervisor" or "Plant Manager" role. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The International Passport System"**
>
> Think of Federation like an **international airport trust system**:
> - You get a **passport** (your digital identity) from your country (your **Identity Provider**).
> - When you land in a foreign country (a **Service Provider** like an industrial cloud), the immigration officer does NOT call your home country to verify you. Instead, they **trust the issuing authority** (your government/embassy) because of pre-established treaties and standards (the **Trust Framework**).
> - The **passport stamp** is the **assertion token** (e.g., SAML token, JWT, OAuth access token) that conveys verified claims about you.
>
> This is exactly how a federated industrial system works: a power plant operator logs in once to their corporate IdP and seamlessly accesses multiple vendor systems (Siemens, GE, ABB dashboards) without re-authenticating, because those vendors trust the corporate IdP's assertions.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Boundaries

- **Assertion Validity Period (Token TTL):** Typically **300 seconds (5 minutes)** to **3600 seconds (1 hour)** for SAML/OAuth tokens.
- **Federation Overhead:** Token exchange round-trip latency should be **less than 200 ms** in industrial real-time systems.
- **Identity Assurance Level (IAL):** Defined by **NIST SP 800-63-3** as **IAL1, IAL2, IAL3** (1 = self-asserted, 3 = in-person verification).
- **Authentication Assurance Level (AAL):** **AAL1** (single-factor), **AAL2** (multi-factor), **AAL3** (hardware cryptographic + multi-factor).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Federation Exists

In modern industrial enterprises, the security perimeter has dissolved. A single engineer at a refinery in Kerala may need to access:
1. Local **DCS (Distributed Control System)** dashboards
2. Cloud-based **Predictive Maintenance AI** (AWS/Azure)
3. Vendor remote support portals (Siemens, Honeywell)
4. Corporate **ERP** (SAP)
5. Mobile **EHS (Environment, Health, Safety)** applications

Without federation, this requires **5 different logins**, creating credential fatigue, password reuse vulnerabilities, and shadow IT risks. Federation solves this through **Single Sign-On (SSO)**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Flow

The operational flow of a federated trust transaction follows these steps:

1. **User Initiation:** The subject (operator) attempts to access a protected resource at the **Service Provider (SP)**.
2. **Authentication Redirect:** The SP detects no valid session and redirects the user to the **Identity Provider (IdP)** with an **AuthnRequest** (in SAML 2.0) or an authorization request (in OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect).
3. **Credential Verification:** The IdP authenticates the user via credentials (password, smart card, biometric, MFA).
4. **Assertion Generation:** The IdP generates a digitally signed **assertion/token** containing:
   - Subject identifier (NameID / sub claim)
   - Attributes (role, group, clearance level)
   - Validity period (NotBefore, NotOnOrAfter)
   - Audience restriction (intended SP)
5. **Token Delivery:** The assertion is delivered to the SP via HTTP POST binding (SAML) or redirect (OAuth code flow).
6. **Trust Verification:** The SP validates the assertion's **digital signature** against the IdP's pre-shared **public key certificate** (typically exchanged out-of-band via federation metadata).
7. **Access Grant:** Upon successful validation, the SP establishes a local session and grants access.

### 2.3 Core Trust Models — Hierarchical Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Fundamental Trust Models in Federation:**

#### **Model A: Direct Trust (Bilateral)**
- **How it works:** SP and IdP have a **one-to-one** pre-configured trust relationship.
- **Strength:** Maximum control, explicit policy.
- **Weakness:** **O(n²) complexity** — for n partners, you need n(n-1)/2 trust agreements.
- **Example:** A single refinery and a single vendor in a closed OT network.

#### **Model B: Hierarchical Trust (PKI-based)**
- **How it works:** A **Root Certification Authority (CA)** acts as a trusted third party. All IdPs and SPs hold certificates issued by this root.
- **Strength:** **O(n) complexity** — one root trust anchor serves all parties.
- **Weakness:** Single point of failure; if the root CA is compromised, the entire federation collapses.
- **Example:** Industrial PKI deployments using **IEC 62443-3-3** certificate hierarchies.

#### **Model C: Federated Trust (Web of Trust / Broker Model)**
- **How it works:** A **Federation Operator / Trust Broker** (e.g., eduGAIN, Kantara, SAFE-BioPharma) issues federation metadata and policy frameworks that all members agree to.
- **Strength:** **Scalable**, policy-driven, supports dynamic trust.
- **Weakness:** Requires governance overhead; trust is transitive (A trusts B, B trusts C, therefore A trusts C — Chain of Trust).
- **Example:** Cross-organizational industrial consortiums (e.g., NAMUR Open Architecture).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Formulas and Engineering Relationships for Federation & Trust Models**

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units / Standard | Engineering Application |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Federation Trust Complexity** | $T(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ (Direct Trust) | $n$ = number of federation partners | Measures scaling cost of bilateral agreements. |
| **Hierarchical Trust Complexity** | $T(n) = n$ (Single Root CA) | $n$ = number of entities | Optimal scaling for large OT/IT federations. |
| **Token Validity Window** | $W = t_{NotOnOrAfter} - t_{NotBefore}$ | Seconds (s) | Limits replay attack window. |
| **Assertion Trust Probability** | $P_{trust} = P_{sig} \cdot P_{CA} \cdot P_{policy}$ | Dimensionless [0, 1] | Composite trust score from signature, CA validity, and policy compliance. |
| **Session Risk Score (Zero Trust)** | $R = w_1 \cdot A + w_2 \cdot D + w_3 \cdot C$ | $A$=anomaly, $D$=device trust, $C$=context | Where $\sum w_i = 1$; used in dynamic trust re-evaluation. |
| **MFA Assurance Multiplier** | $AAL = f(\text{factors}) \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | NIST SP 800-63B | Determines IdP-to-SP trust elevation. |
| **Token Replay Window** | $t_{replay} = \frac{W}{2}$ (avg. detection time) | Seconds (s) | Industrial control token rotation cadence. |
| **Federation Latency Budget** | $L_{total} = L_{DNS} + L_{TLS} + L_{authn} + L_{assert} + L_{SP}$ | Milliseconds (ms) | Must be < 200 ms for industrial HMI response. |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used in **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) systems** to compute **dynamic trust scores** for federated industrial sessions, enabling **just-in-time access revocation** in SCADA environments.

### 2.5 Cryptographic Trust Anchors

The entire federation security model rests on **asymmetric cryptography**:

- **Signing Key (Private Key):** Held exclusively by the IdP, used to sign assertions.
- **Verification Key (Public Key):** Published in federation metadata (SAML Metadata XML, OIDC JWKS endpoint), used by SPs to verify signatures.
- **Algorithm Standard:** Minimum **RSA-2048** or **ECDSA P-256** as of 2024 KTU/CNSS guidelines. **SHA-256** is the minimum acceptable hash function.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **encryption** with **signing**. In federation, assertions are **signed (integrity + non-repudiation)** but typically **NOT encrypted** (the SP needs to read them). TLS provides transport encryption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Composite Trust Score for Federated Session

In an **Advanced Industrial Cyber Security** context, a Zero Trust framework dynamically evaluates session trust. Let us derive the composite trust score formula step-by-step.

#### **Step 1: Define the Base Identity Trust**

The foundational trust in a federated assertion comes from three cryptographically verifiable components:

$$
T_{base} = T_{signature} \cdot T_{certificate} \cdot T_{policy}
$$

**Where:**
- $T_{signature} \in \{0, 1\}$: 1 if the assertion's digital signature verifies against the IdP's published public key.
- $T_{certificate} \in \{0, 1\}$: 1 if the IdP's certificate chain is valid (not expired, not revoked via **CRL** or **OCSP**).
- $T_{policy} \in \{0, 1\}$: 1 if the assertion complies with the SP's policy (e.g., audience restriction, authn context class).

**Conversion logic:** This is a Boolean AND operation across three independent trust gates. All must pass for $T_{base} = 1$.

#### **Step 2: Apply Assurance Level Multiplier**

Based on **NIST SP 800-63B**, the authentication strength multiplies the base trust:

$$
T_{authn} = T_{base} \cdot \alpha_{AAL}
$$

**Where:**

$$
\alpha_{AAL} = \begin{cases} 0.6, & \text{AAL1 (single-factor)} \\ 0.85, & \text{AAL2 (multi-factor)} \\ 0.98, & \text{AAL3 (hardware crypto + MFA)} \end{cases}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Higher assurance levels (more authentication factors, hardware-backed cryptography) increase the multiplier, reflecting greater confidence in the identity claim.

#### **Step 3: Incorporate Contextual Trust Signals**

Industrial federations require context-aware trust. Define context vectors:

$$
T_{context} = w_1 \cdot T_{device} + w_2 \cdot T_{location} + w_3 \cdot T_{behavior}
$$

**Where:**
- $T_{device} \in [0, 1]$: Device posture score (patch level, EDR status, jailbreak detection).
- $T_{location} \in [0, 1]$: Network location trust (e.g., 1.0 if in plant secure zone, 0.3 if remote VPN).
- $T_{behavior} \in [0, 1]$: Behavioral analytics score (UEBA — User and Entity Behavior Analytics).
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$: Weights with $\sum w_i = 1$.

**Conversion logic:** This is a **weighted linear combination** (convex combination), ensuring the result remains bounded in [0, 1].

#### **Step 4: Final Composite Trust Score**

$$
T_{composite} = T_{authn} \cdot T_{context}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The final trust is the **product** (not sum) of authentication trust and contextual trust. This is intentional: a **single failure** in either dimension collapses the entire trust (zero-trust principle — "never trust, always verify").

#### **Step 5: Access Decision Threshold**

The SP makes an access decision based on a threshold $\theta$:

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{GRANT}, & \text{if } T_{composite} \geq \theta \\ \text{CHALLENGE (Step-up MFA)}, & \text{if } \theta - 0.2 \leq T_{composite} < \theta \\ \text{DENY}, & \text{if } T_{composite} < \theta - 0.2 \end{cases}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A **three-state decision** is more nuanced than binary grant/deny, allowing for **step-up authentication** in industrial environments where connectivity may be intermittent.

### 3.2 Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** An industrial federation has the following parameters:
> - Signature valid: $T_{signature} = 1$
> - Certificate valid (not revoked): $T_{certificate} = 1$
> - Policy compliant: $T_{policy} = 1$
> - AAL3 used: $\alpha_{AAL} = 0.98$
> - Device: $T_{device} = 0.9$, $w_1 = 0.4$
> - Location: $T_{location} = 1.0$, $w_2 = 0.3$
> - Behavior: $T_{behavior} = 0.8$, $w_3 = 0.3$
> - Threshold: $\theta = 0.75$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $T_{base}$:

$$
T_{base} = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 1
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $T_{authn}$:

$$
T_{authn} = 1 \cdot 0.98 = 0.98
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $T_{context}$:

$$
T_{context} = (0.4)(0.9) + (0.3)(1.0) + (0.3)(0.8)
$$

$$
T_{context} = 0.36 + 0.30 + 0.24 = 0.90
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $T_{composite}$:

$$
T_{composite} = 0.98 \cdot 0.90 = 0.882
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with threshold:

$$
T_{composite} = 0.882 \geq \theta = 0.75 \implies \text{GRANT}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SAML vs OAuth 2.0 vs OpenID Connect

| Feature | **SAML 2.0** | **OAuth 2.0** | **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** |
|---------|--------------|---------------|---------------------------|
| **Primary Purpose** | Authentication + Authorization | Authorization (delegation) | Authentication (identity layer on OAuth 2.0) |
| **Token Format** | XML (SAML Assertion) | Opaque access token / JWT | **ID Token (JWT)** + Access Token |
| **Year Introduced** | **2005** | **2012 (RFC 6749)** | **2014** |
| **Transport Binding** | HTTP POST / Redirect | HTTP Redirect (Auth Code) | HTTP Redirect (Auth Code) + JSON over HTTPS |
| **Best For** | Enterprise SSO, B2B federation | API authorization, mobile apps | Modern web/mobile SSO, social login |
| **Signature** | XMLDSig (XML Signature) | Optional (JWT bearer) | **JWS (mandatory for ID token)** |
| **Industrial Use** | Legacy OT systems, gov/defense | Cloud-to-cloud IIoT APIs | Modern cloud-native industrial dashboards |
| **Identity Claims** | Rich attribute statements | Scopes only | Standardized `claims` (sub, email, profile) |
| **Attack Surface** | XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) | Token theft, open redirector | Token substitution, mix-up attacks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Federated Trust Architecture — Industrial IIoT Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER_ZONE["User Zone (Operator Workstation)"]
        OP1[Plant Operator]
        OP2[Remote Engineer]
    end

    subgraph IDP_ZONE["Identity Provider Zone (Corporate HQ)"]
        IDP1[Enterprise IdP - Azure AD]
        IDP2[Legacy IdP - LDAP]
        MFA1[MFA Service - Okta]
    end

    subgraph FEDERATION_ZONE["Federation Trust Broker"]
        FB1[Trust Framework - Kantara / SAFE-BioPharma]
        FB2[Federation Metadata Exchange]
    end

    subgraph SP_ZONE["Service Provider Zone (Plant + Cloud)"]
        SP1[SCADA HMI Dashboard]
        SP2[Cloud Historian - AWS]
        SP3[Vendor Remote Support - Siemens]
        SP4[ERP System - SAP]
    end

    OP1 -->|1 Login Request| FB1
    OP2 -->|1 Login Request| FB1
    FB1 -->|2 Authn Redirect| IDP1
    FB1 -->|2 Authn Redirect| IDP2
    IDP1 -->|3 MFA Challenge| MFA1
    IDP2 -->|3 MFA Challenge| MFA1
    MFA1 -->|4 Signed Assertion| IDP1
    IDP1 -->|5 SAML Token| FB1
    FB2 -->|6 Token Relay with Trust Policy| SP1
    FB2 -->|6 Token Relay with Trust Policy| SP2
    FB2 -->|6 Token Relay with Trust Policy| SP3
    FB2 -->|6 Token Relay with Trust Policy| SP4
```

### 4.2 SAML 2.0 Authentication Flow — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User (Subject)
    participant SP as Service Provider
    participant IDP as Identity Provider
    participant DS as Directory Service (LDAP/AD)

    U->>SP: 1. GET /protected-resource
    SP->>U: 2. 302 Redirect to IdP (AuthnRequest)
    U->>IDP: 3. POST SAML Request via Browser
    IDP->>DS: 4. LDAP Bind - Verify Credentials
    DS-->>IDP: 5. User Attributes + Group Membership
    IDP->>IDP: 6. Generate Signed SAML Assertion
    IDP-->>U: 7. Browser auto-POST SAML Response (Artifact/POST binding)
    U->>SP: 8. POST SAML Response to ACS URL
    SP->>SP: 9. Verify XML Signature against IdP Cert
    SP->>SP: 10. Check NotOnOrAfter, Audience, Replay
    SP-->>U: 11. Set Session Cookie - Grant Access
    SP->>U: 12. Return Protected Resource
```

### 4.3 Trust Model Hierarchy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1["Level 1 - Direct Trust"]
        A1[Org A] ---|Bilateral SAML| B1[Org B]
    end

    subgraph L2["Level 2 - Hierarchical PKI Trust"]
        R1[Root CA] --> I1[Intermediate CA]
        R1 --> I2[Intermediate CA]
        I1 --> C1A[Org A IdP Cert]
        I1 --> C1B[Org A SP Cert]
        I2 --> C2A[Org B IdP Cert]
        I2 --> C2B[Org B SP Cert]
    end

    subgraph L3["Level 3 - Federated Broker Trust"]
        FB[Trust Broker - Federation Operator]
        FB -->|Policy Agreement| M1[Org A Metadata]
        FB -->|Policy Agreement| M2[Org B Metadata]
        FB -->|Policy Agreement| M3[Org C Metadata]
    end

    L1 -.evolves to.-> L2
    L2 -.evolves to.-> L3
```

### 4.4 Zero Trust Decision Engine — Federation Trust Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Incoming Federated Assertion] --> V1{Signature Valid?}
    V1 -->|No| DENY1[DENY - Invalid Signature]
    V1 -->|Yes| V2{Certificate Revoked?}
    V2 -->|Yes| DENY2[DENY - Revoked Cert]
    V2 -->|No| V3{Audience Match?}
    V3 -->|No| DENY3[DENY - Wrong Audience]
    V3 -->|Yes| CTX[Evaluate Context Vector]
    CTX --> CTX1[Device Posture Score]
    CTX --> CTX2[Location Trust Score]
    CTX --> CTX3[Behavior Analytics Score]
    CTX1 --> COMP[Compute Composite Trust]
    CTX2 --> COMP
    CTX3 --> COMP
    COMP --> TH{Score >= Threshold?}
    TH -->|Yes| GRANT[GRANT - Issue Session]
    TH -->|Borderline| STEPUP[STEP-UP MFA Required]
    TH -->|No| DENY4[DENY - Insufficient Trust]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
