# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Risk Score for an OT Vulnerability

**Scenario:** A Siemens S7-1200 PLC in your plant runs firmware V4.5. A new CVE-2024-XXXX is published with active ICS-CERT exploitation. The Siemens advisory lists CVSS Base = **8.6**, EPSS = **0.42**, and your asset has **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) 2** requirement.

**Step 1: Identify Variables**

$$
P_{exploit} = EPSS = 0.42
$$

$$
CVSS_{base} = 8.6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \text{round}(CVSS_{base}/2) = 5 \text{ (Critical band)}
$$

$$
SIL = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad AssetCriticality = 4 \text{ (out of 5)}
$$

$$
A_{criticality} = 4
$$

$$
T_{days} = 3 \text{ (advisory published 3 days ago, active exploitation in wild)}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Threat Priority Index (TPI)**

$$
TPI = \frac{(R \times A) + (E \times V)}{T}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Relevance (this CVE is for Siemens S7, vendor matches our PLC) $= 1.0$
- $A$ = Asset criticality $= 4$
- $E$ = Exposure (this PLC is on Purdue Level 1, reachable from engineering workstation) $= 4$
- $V$ = Vulnerability severity (CVSS 8.6) $\approx 8.6$
- $T$ = Time-to-exploit in days $= 3$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$
TPI = \frac{(1.0 \times 4) + (4 \times 8.6)}{3}
$$

$$
TPI = \frac{4.0 + 34.4}{3}
$$

$$
TPI = \frac{38.4}{3} = 12.8
$$

**Step 4: Decision Mapping**

$$
TPI \geq 10 \Rightarrow \text{Critical} \Rightarrow \text{Apply compensating controls within 24 hours, patch within SLA}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compensating controls might include: isolating the PLC, adding firewall rules to block engineering port 102 (S7comm), enabling application whitelisting on the engineering workstation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weighted IoC Confidence Calculation

**Scenario:** Your SOC receives the same IoC (domain `evil-ot-c2.xyz`) from three sources with different reliability ratings.

| Source | Source Weight $w_i$ | Stated Confidence $c_i$ |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Mandiant Threat Intel Report | 0.9 | 0.95 |
| Open-source Twitter OSINT | 0.3 | 0.7 |
| Internal honeypot telemetry | 0.7 | 0.85 |

**Step 1: Set up the Weighted Confidence Formula**

$$
C_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot c_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Numerator**

$$
\sum w_i c_i = (0.9 \times 0.95) + (0.3 \times 0.7) + (0.7 \times 0.85)
$$

$$
= 0.855 + 0.210 + 0.595
$$

$$
= 1.660
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Denominator**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.9 + 0.3 + 0.7 = 1.9
$$

**Step 4: Final Confidence**

$$
C_w = \frac{1.660}{1.9} = 0.8737 \approx 0.87
$$

**Step 5: Operational Decision**

$$
C_w = 0.87 \geq 0.80 \Rightarrow \text{High Confidence} \Rightarrow \text{Auto-block at DNS sinkhole and firewall egress}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Collection Coverage Ratio (CCR) Audit

**Scenario:** Your organization's IRD lists 8 required source categories. Your collection plan currently covers 6.

**Step 1: List Sets**

$$
Sources_{required} = \{\text{OSINT, HUMINT, SIGINT, ISAC, Vendor, Internal, DarkWeb, ICS-CERT}\}
$$

$$
Sources_{collected} = \{\text{OSINT, HUMINT, ISAC, Vendor, Internal, DarkWeb}\}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Coverage Formula**

$$
CCR = \frac{\vert Sources_{collected} \cap Sources_{required} \vert}{\vert Sources_{required} \vert}
$$

**Step 3: Compute**

$$
Sources_{collected} \cap Sources_{required} = \{\text{OSINT, HUMINT, ISAC, Vendor, Internal, DarkWeb}\}
$$

$$
\vert \cdot \vert = 6
$$

$$
\vert Sources_{required} \vert = 8
$$

$$
CCR = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75
$$

**Step 4: Interpret**

$$
CCR = 0.75 \Rightarrow 75\% \text{ coverage} \Rightarrow \text{Gap: SIGINT, ICS-CERT} \Rightarrow \text{Procure network sensor feeds and subscribe to CISA alerts}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Threat Intelligence to Purdue Model Layers

| Purdue Level | Function | TI Type Most Relevant | Example Asset | Specific TTPs to Watch |
|--------------|----------|----------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Level 0** | Physical Process | Operational | Pump, motor, valve sensor | Damage to Property and/or Loss of Life (ATT&CK T0888) |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | Operational + Tactical | PLC, RTU, DCS controller | Manipulation of Control (T0855), Change Operating Mode (T0858) |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory | Tactical | HMI, SCADA server, Historian | Screen Capture (T0852), Program Download (T0843) |
| **Level 3** | Site Operations | Tactical + Technical | Engineering workstation, asset mgmt | Lateral Tool Transfer (T0867), Exploitation of Remote Services (T0866) |
| **Level 4–5** | Enterprise/Internet | All four | Email, ERP, cloud, internet gateway | Phishing, Valid Accounts, Exploit Public-Facing Application |

## 3.5 The Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis (Caltagirone, Pendergast, Betz)

Four vertices connected by edges:

$$
\text{Adversary} \leftrightarrow \text{Capability} \leftrightarrow \text{Infrastructure} \leftrightarrow \text{Victim}
$$

- **Adversary** — Who is the threat actor? (APT group, insider, hacktivist)
- **Capability** — What tools/malware? (INDUSTROYER, TRITON, custom RAT)
- **Infrastructure** — What C2/path? (IP, domain, USB, VPN)
- **Victim** — Who/what is targeted? (Substation, refinery, pump station)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use case:** Pivoting. Starting from one vertex, walk the edges to discover the other three. Example: from a known IoC (infrastructure) → identify capability (malware) → cluster adversary (group) → find victim (other targeted orgs).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define Threat Intelligence. List and briefly explain the four types of Threat Intelligence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Threat Intelligence is the systematic process of collecting, processing, integrating, evaluating, analyzing, and interpreting data from diverse sources to produce timely, contextualized, and actionable knowledge about existing or emerging threats to organizational assets (information and operational technology).

**Four Types (2 marks):**

1. **Strategic TI:** High-level, audience is executives; covers geopolitical risk, industry trends, adversary motivations. (0.5)
2. **Operational TI:** Details of specific adversary campaigns, malware, infrastructure; audience is SOC managers and incident responders. (0.5)
3. **Tactical TI:** TTPs, detection logic, ATT&CK mappings; audience is detection engineers and SOC analysts. (0.5)
4. **Technical TI:** Atomic IoCs — IPs, hashes, domains; audience is automated systems (SIEM, EDR, firewall). (0.5)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) and Indicators of Attack (IoAs) with one ICS-specific example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | IoC | IoA |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Definition** (1 mark) | Forensic artifact proving compromise already occurred | Behavioral pattern indicating an attack is in progress |
| **Temporal** | Retrospective / post-breach | Real-time / proactive |
| **ICS Example** (1 mark) | SHA256 hash of TRITON/TRISIS sample found on engineering workstation | Unusual Modbus Function Code 90 (encapsulated file transport) sent to PLC during non-maintenance window |
| **Detection mechanism** (1 mark) | AV/EDR file reputation, hash blacklists | ICS-aware NDR, behavioral analytics, Sigma rules on Historian logs |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 provides **Internal Choice**. Either **Question A** OR **Question B** must be answered.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Threat Intelligence Lifecycle in detail. Discuss each phase with a relevant industrial cyber security example.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The Threat Intelligence Lifecycle is a six-phase continuous process. (0.5 mark for naming all six)

**Phase 1 — Planning & Direction (1 mark)**
The organization defines its intelligence requirements. Example PIR for a refinery: *"Identify adversary groups actively targeting Siemens PCS 7 DCS in the European oil & gas sector during 2024."* Output: Intelligence Requirements Document (IRD).

**Phase 2 — Collection (1 mark)**
Raw data is gathered from OSINT (CISA ICS advisories, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS), HUMINT (membership in Downstream Natural Gas ISAC), SIGINT (Dragos network sensors on Purdue Level 1), Internal Telemetry (SIEM logs, Historian anomalies), and commercial feeds (Mandiant, Dragos WorldView).

**Phase 3 — Processing (1 mark)**
Raw data is normalized, deduplicated, and converted. Example: a 200-page PDF ICS-CERT advisory is parsed into 15 STIX 2.1 Indicator SDOs and 3 Attack Pattern SDOs. Sandbox execution of suspect PLC ladder logic samples produces STIX `observed-data` SDOs.

**Phase 4 — Analysis (1 mark)**
Analysts apply link analysis, pattern analysis, and ACH. The TRITON/TRISIS malware is mapped to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS technique T0809 (Manipulation of View, indicating adversary modified HMI screens to mask physical state). Analysts score: CVSS 9.8, EPSS 0.78, exploit observed in wild, asset criticality SIL 3 → TPI 14+.

**Phase 5 — Dissemination (1 mark)**
Strategic brief to CISO; operational brief to SOC manager; technical IoCs (C2 IPs) pushed to SIEM as STIX 2.1 bundle via TAXII; Sigma rules pushed to EDR for detecting `program download` to Triconex controllers; tactical ATT&CK gap report sent to detection engineering.

**Phase 6 — Feedback (1 mark)**
SOC reports back: Sigma rule fired once in last 30 days, but on a benign test by the engineering team (false positive). Detection engineering updates rule. Marketing refreshes IRD. Output feeds back into Phase 1, closing the loop.

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a suitable diagram, explain the MITRE ATT&CK for ICS framework. Identify and discuss three ICS-specific tactics with at least one technique under each.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** MITRE ATT&CK for ICS is a curated knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques specifically targeting Industrial Control Systems, published by MITRE in January 2020. It complements the Enterprise ATT&CK matrix by focusing on the unique adversary behaviors in OT environments (PLC, SCADA, DCS, SIS).

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Reproduce the MITRE ATT&CK for ICS tactics flow as a flowchart or numbered tactic list.)*

**Three ICS-Specific Tactics with Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **Tactic: Inhibit Response (TA0104)** *(1.3 marks)*
   Adversary prevents the safety/control system from responding to a failure.
   - Technique T0808 — *Block Reporting Message*: Prevent the HMI/Historian from receiving legitimate alerts (e.g., STUXNET suppressing centrifuge status updates).
   - Technique T0809 — *Manipulation of View*: HMI screens altered to show normal values while process is in fault state.

2. **Tactic: Impair Process Control (TA0105)** *(1.3 marks)*
   Adversary manipulates, disables, or damages industrial control processes.
   - Technique T0836 — *Modify Parameter*: Changes setpoints in PLCs (e.g., changing a pressure safety threshold from 50 bar to 500 bar).
   - Technique T0856 — *Spoof Reporting Message*: Historian fed false values matching expected process behavior (STUXNET replay attack).

3. **Tactic: Damage to Property and/or Loss of Life (TA0110)** *(1.4 marks)*
   The most severe end-state — physical destruction or human casualties.
   - Technique T0888 — *Remote System Manipulation*: External triggering of dangerous physical state (e.g., TRITON disabling SIS, then physical damage follows).
   - Technique T0879 — *Damage to Property*: Direct physical damage via manipulated control loops (e.g., INDUSTROYER opening breakers to damage transformers).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Marks Lost Here:** Students often confuse ATT&CK for ICS tactics with Enterprise ATT&CK tactics (e.g., listing "Phishing" — which is Enterprise Initial Access — under ICS). Always cite the **eleven ICS-specific tactics** (TA0101 through TA0110, with TA0102 covering Execution/Persistence). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] What is a Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP)? Explain the STIX 2.1 and TAXII 2.1 standards. Describe the architecture of a typical TIP with a neat block diagram.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**TIP Definition (1 mark):** A Threat Intelligence Platform is a centralized technology solution that aggregates, correlates, normalizes, deduplicates, enriches, and disseminates threat intelligence from multiple sources to security infrastructure (SIEM, SOAR, EDR, NDR, firewall) and human consumers (analysts, executives).

**STIX 2.1 (2 marks):**
- **Full form:** Structured Threat Information eXpression version 2.1.
- **Type:** OASIS Standard, machine-readable, JSON-LD serialization.
- **Structure:** Built on **STIX Domain Objects (SDOs)** — `attack-pattern`, `campaign`, `indicator`, `malware`, `threat-actor`, `tool`, `vulnerability`, `identity`, `infrastructure`, `intrusion-set`, `course-of-action`, `observed-data`, `sighting` — and **STIX Relationship Objects (SROs)** connecting them, plus **Cyber Observable Objects (SCOs)** like `ipv4-addr`, `domain-name`, `file`.
- **Use Case:** Unambiguous, machine-processable threat intel sharing across organizations.

**TAXII 2.1 (1 mark):**
- **Full form:** Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information version 2.1.
- **Type:** OASIS application-layer protocol over HTTPS, RESTful.
- **Services:**
  - **Collection** — client pulls data from server.
  - **Channel** — pub/sub real-time distribution.
  - **Discovery** — client discovers available collections.
- **Relationship with STIX:** TAXII is the **transport**; STIX is the **payload**.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Draw MISP / OpenCTI-style architecture: Source feeds → Ingestion → Core DB → Correlation/Enrichment → Analyst UI → Export/SIEM/SOAR/EDR/ISAC).*

**Operational Example (1 mark):** Mandiant Advantage → STIX 2.1 → TAXII 2.1 push → ingested by MISP → exported to Splunk SIEM as STIX bundle → SOC analyst queries MISP, retrieves the C2 IoC, pushes to CrowdStrike Falcon for EDR hunt across all engineering workstations.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the application of Threat Intelligence in Industrial Cyber Security. Cover the following: (i) Industrial-specific threat actors with two real-world examples, (ii) OT-aware IoC sources, and (iii) Integration of TI with Purdue Model and SIEM.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Industrial-Specific Threat Actors with Two Examples (2.5 marks):**

Industrial adversaries differ from typical IT threat actors by their **objective**: physical process manipulation, not data theft.

- **Example 1: STUXNET (2010)** — Joint US-Israeli operation (Olympic Games), attributed to Equation Group. Targeted Siemens S7-315/S7-417 PLCs controlling Iranian Natanz uranium enrichment centrifuges. Used four zero-days, signed stolen Realtek and JMicron certificates, manipulated centrifuge rotor speed (normal 1,064 Hz → 1,410 Hz destructive) while replaying normal HMI values. First publicly known cyber-physical weapon.
- **Example 2: TRITON/TRISIS (2017)** — Attributed to XENOTIME (Russia). Targeted Schneider Electric Triconex Safety Instrumented System (SIS) at a Saudi Arabian petrochemical plant. The SIS is *supposed* to safely shut down the plant in abnormal conditions. TRITON reprogrammed the SIS to allow unsafe states, intending to enable subsequent physical damage. Detected accidentally when a safety shutdown triggered unexpectedly.

**(ii) OT-Aware IoC Sources (2 marks):**

- **CISA ICS Advisories (cisa.gov)** — Government OSINT, CVE + ICS-specific mitigations.
- **Vendor Advisories** — Siemens ProductCERT, Schneider Electric ICS-CERT, Rockwell, Honeywell.
- **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** — Curated technique + technique-usage examples.
- **Dragos WorldView** — Commercial OT actor tracking.
- **Claroty Continuous Threat Detection** — Healthcare/industrial feed.
- **Nozomi Networks Threat Intelligence** — OT protocol-aware.
- **Internal OT Telemetry** — Honeypot/Honeynet (e.g., Conpot, GasPot), Historian anomalies, PLC program change logs.

**(iii) Integration of TI with Purdue Model and SIEM (2.5 marks):**

- **Purdue Model-aware Ingestion:** Configure TIP to tag IoCs by Purdue Level applicability. A Windows LOLBash hash (Level 4–5) should not block legitimate engineering commands (Level 1).
- **SIEM Correlation:** SIEM (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic, Microsoft Sentinel) ingests STIX 2.1 bundles via TAXII 2.1. Correlation rule fires when internal traffic matches Dragos-reported C2 IP AND occurs on Purdue Level 1 segment during off-hours.
- **SOAR Orchestration:** SOAR (Cortex XSOAR, Splunk SOAR) auto-enriches alerts using TIP and quarantines the affected engineering workstation; the playbook pages the OT SOC.
- **Closed Loop:** Analyst feedback updates the TIP, which refines the IoC list, which improves detection rules, which improves MTTD.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the AIC triad** — OT prioritizes Availability over Confidentiality. A TI-driven block of a C2 IP that accidentally disrupts Historian replication (Availability) is a self-inflicted outage. Always contextualize TI to Purdue Level.
> 2. **Confusing IoC with IoA** — they are not synonyms. IoC is forensic (post), IoA is behavioral (real-time). 0.5 mark lost per use.
> 3. **Citing IT examples in ICS questions** — STUXNET and TRITON are *expected* answers; describing WannaCry in an OT TI question loses 1 mark for lack of relevance.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in STIX/TAXII question — even a hand-drawn block diagram earns 1.5 marks; absence is a 1.5-mark penalty.
> 5. **Forgetting the Feedback phase** of the lifecycle — examiners explicitly test this; missing Phase 6 costs 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision sheet — read 3 times before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Definition (verbatim-able):** Threat Intelligence is the *systematic process of collecting, processing, integrating, evaluating, analyzing, and interpreting* data from diverse sources to produce *timely, contextualized, and actionable* knowledge about threats.
- ✅ **Four Types:** **Strategic, Operational, Tactical, Technical** — remember audience, content, format.
- ✅ **Six Lifecycle Phases:** **Planning & Direction → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback.** Memorize order; this is a guaranteed question.
- ✅ **STIX = Payload (data). TAXII = Transport (delivery).** STIX 2.1 is JSON-LD; TAXII 2.1 is HTTPS REST.
- ✅ **MISP & OpenCTI** are the two most-cited open-source TIPs in KTU 2024.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** has **11 tactics (TA0101–TA0110)** and ~80 techniques — cite at least 3 by ID (e.g., T0855, T0836, T0888).
- ✅ **IoC = post-mortem artifact; IoA = in-progress behavior.** Provide ICS examples.
- ✅ **Pyramid of Pain:** Hash → IP → Domain → Network Artifact → Host Artifact → Tool → TTP. Top of pyramid = most defender leverage.
- ✅ **Diamond Model:** Adversary ↔ Capability ↔ Infrastructure ↔ Victim (four vertices, six edges).
- ✅ **Must-cite Real-World Cases:** STUXNET (2010), TRITON/TRISIS (2017), INDUSTROYER (2016), INDUSTROYER2 (2022), PIPEDREAM/Incontroller (2022), VOLT TYPHOON (2023+).
- ✅ **Key Formulas (reproducible in exam):** Risk $R = P \times I$; Weighted IoC Confidence $C_w = \frac{\sum w_i c_i}{\sum w_i}$; TPI $= \frac{(R \cdot A) + (E \cdot V)}{T}$; MTTD $= \frac{1}{n} \sum (t_{detect} - t_{occur})$; CCR $= \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert B \vert}$.
- ✅ **Purdue Model Context:** Always tie TI ingestion to Purdue Levels 0–5; Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality in OT.
- ✅ **STIX 2.1 SDOs (must know 6):** `attack-pattern`, `indicator`, `malware`, `threat-actor`, `campaign`, `course-of-action`, `observed-data`, `sighting`.
- ✅ **Difference between TAXII Services:** Collection (pull), Channel (pub/sub), Discovery (browse).
- ✅ **Sectoral ISACs:** E-ISAC (electricity), DNG-ISAC (gas), H-ISAC (health), Auto-ISAC, IT-ISAC, FS-ISAC (finance).
- ✅ **Five-letter KPIs examiners love:** MTTD, MTTR, CCR, IYR, FPR.
- ✅ **ICS-CERT** (now CISA) is the government OSINT source; **Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi, Mandiant, CrowdStrike** are commercial OT-aware vendors.
- ✅ **Closing the loop** — the lifecycle is a *cycle*, not a chain. Feedback refines the next iteration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident Response Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Incident Response Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Incident Response Planning (IRP)** is a structured, documented, and pre-approved set of procedures and policies that an organization develops and maintains to systematically detect, contain, eradicate, and recover from cybersecurity incidents affecting its **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**, **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** networks, and **Corporate IT infrastructure**. The plan defines roles, responsibilities, communication channels, escalation matrices, and recovery objectives to minimize business disruption and maintain the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability)** of critical industrial assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 Aligned):**
> Incident Response Planning is the *proactive engineering* of an organization's capability to respond to and manage the aftermath of a security breach or cyberattack, with the explicit goal of **limiting damage**, **reducing recovery time and costs**, and **preventing future occurrences** through a continuous feedback loop of lessons learned.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine an **industrial manufacturing plant** (say, a chemical refinery) is like a large **hospital emergency department (ED)**.

- **Preparation Phase** = Stocking the emergency room with doctors, defibrillators, and triage protocols *before* a patient arrives.
- **Identification Phase** = The triage nurse deciding whether the incoming patient has a cold (false positive) or a heart attack (real incident).
- **Containment Phase** = Isolating the patient in the ICU to prevent the "infection" (malware) from spreading to other wards (network segments).
- **Eradication Phase** = The surgeons removing the root cause of the problem (tumor, infection).
- **Recovery Phase** = The rehabilitation process — bringing the patient back to normal health (restoring PLCs, HMIs, historians).
- **Lessons Learned** = The post-mortem review by the medical board to ensure the *next* patient gets faster, better care.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Industrial Cyber Security:**
> In an ICS environment, a "patient" is not just a server — it is a **boiler, a turbine, a robotic arm, or a power grid substation**. The "heart attack" might be a *Stuxnet-style* worm rewriting PLC logic, and the "doctor" must respond within **milliseconds to minutes** — not hours — to prevent physical damage, environmental disasters, or loss of life.

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Favorites)

The following metrics are *mandatory vocabulary* for KTU ESE answers:

- **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect):** Average time from incident occurrence to detection. Target: **< 24 hours** for IT, **< 5 minutes** for critical ICS anomalies.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond/Recover):** Average time from detection to full system restoration. Target: **< 72 hours** for enterprise IT, **< 1 hour** for safety-critical ICS loops.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Maximum acceptable downtime.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement) Compliance:** Percentage of incidents resolved within the contractual time window. Industry benchmark: **≥ 95%**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** If a KTU question asks *"List the four primary objectives of an Incident Response Plan"*, the answer is: **(1) Minimize Impact, (2) Maintain Business Continuity, (3) Preserve Forensic Evidence, (4) Strengthen Future Defenses.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NIST Six-Phase Incident Response Lifecycle

The **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** framework, which is the de-facto standard referenced in the KTU 2024 PECCT524 syllabus, defines the IRP lifecycle as six sequential but cyclical phases:

### Phase 1 — Preparation
- The most critical phase; occurs *before* any incident.
- Activities: Asset inventory, baseline network behavior, deploying SIEM/IDS/IPS, training the **CSIRT (Computer Security Incident Response Team)**, drafting playbooks, establishing communication trees.
- ICS-Specific: Hardening of **Purdue Model Levels 0–3** (process control levels), isolation of OT/IT networks, deployment of unidirectional gateways (data diodes).

### Phase 2 — Identification (Detection & Triage)
- Determining whether an event constitutes a *real* security incident.
- Inputs: SIEM alerts, IDS signatures, anomaly detection (e.g., unexpected Modbus/TCP traffic), user reports, threat intelligence feeds.
- Output: A triaged incident ticket with a **Severity Score** (see Section 3).

### Phase 3 — Containment
- **Short-term containment:** Disconnect affected host from the network, block malicious IPs at the firewall.
- **Long-term containment:** Apply temporary patches, segment the network, deploy honeytokens.
- **ICS Rule:** Never blindly disconnect an OT device — this may cause physical process instability. Use **logical isolation** first.

### Phase 4 — Eradication
- Remove malware, delete backdoors, revoke compromised credentials, patch vulnerabilities.
- For ICS: Re-flash PLC firmware only after validating against the **golden image**.

### Phase 5 — Recovery
- Restore systems from clean backups, validate system integrity, monitor for reinfection.
- For ICS: Perform a **controlled ramp-up** of the physical process with operator supervision.

### Phase 6 — Lessons Learned
- Conduct a **blameless post-incident review (PIR)** within 14 days.
- Update the IRP, playbooks, detection rules, and training materials.
- Generate KPIs to feed back into Phase 1 (Preparation).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Lifecycle is Cyclical:** The output of Phase 6 directly enhances Phase 1. This is the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** of cybersecurity.

## 2.2 CSIRT Roles & Responsibilities

A typical industrial CSIRT comprises the following roles:

| Role | Primary Responsibility | KTU Exam Cue |
|------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Incident Commander (IC)** | Overall decision authority, escalations, external comms | "Who leads the response?" |
| **CSIRT Manager** | Coordinates the team, owns the IRP | "Who owns the plan?" |
| **Security Analyst (Tier 1/2/3)** | Triage, deep forensics, malware analysis | "Who analyzes the attack?" |
| **OT/ICS Engineer** | Validates physical process integrity, safe shutdowns | "Who handles PLC/HMI?" |
| **Legal & Compliance Officer** | GDPR/NERC-CIP/IT Act 2000 reporting compliance | "Who handles law?" |
| **PR/Communications** | External messaging, media, customer notifications | "Who talks to media?" |
| **HR Representative** | Insider threat handling, disciplinary actions | "Who handles insider?" |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are *guaranteed high-yield* for KTU ESE numericals and derivations:

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units |
|------------------|--------------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Mean Time To Detect** | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{detect,i} - T_{occur,i})$ | Average detection lag | Hours / Minutes |
| **Mean Time To Respond** | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{resolved,i} - T_{detect,i})$ | Average recovery time | Hours |
| **SLA Compliance Ratio** | $SLA_{comp} = \dfrac{N_{within\_SLA}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | \% of incidents meeting SLA | Percentage |
| **Severity Score** | $S = (I \times w_I) + (U \times w_U) + (C \times w_C)$ | Weighted composite score | Unitless 0-10 |
| **Incident Rate** | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{incidents}}{T_{period}}$ | Frequency of incidents | Incidents/month |
| **Response Effectiveness** | $RE = SLA_{comp} \times \dfrac{MTTD_{target}}{MTTD_{actual}}$ | Holistic readiness index | Dimensionless |
| **Risk Reduction Ratio** | $RRR = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Residual Risk}}{\text{Initial Risk}}$ | Post-IRP risk mitigation | Ratio 0 to 1 |
| **Forensic Integrity Score** | $FIS = \dfrac{\text{Valid Chain-of-Custody Events}}{\text{Total Handling Events}}$ | Evidence admissibility | Ratio 0 to 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Syntax Rule Reminder:** All mathematical "absolute value" or "OR" operators inside the above table use the LaTeX `\vert` (or `\mid`) command, **not** the raw vertical bar `|`, to prevent breaking the markdown table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Incident Response Planning is not merely an IT paperwork exercise — it is a **safety engineering discipline** in industrial environments. The **2010 Stuxnet** incident, the **2015 Ukraine Power Grid Attack**, the **2017 Triton/TRISIS** attack on a Saudi petrochemical plant, and the **2021 Colonial Pipeline** ransomware event all demonstrated that *the absence of a tested IRP transformed a containable cyber event into a multi-million-dollar physical catastrophe*. The IRP acts as the **organizational immune system** — it cannot prevent pathogens from entering, but it neutralizes them before they cause systemic failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Severity Score $S$

The KTU 2024 scheme frequently tests the **engineering rationale** behind severity scoring. The following derivation is exhaustive and exam-ready.

### Step 1 — Define the Three Independent Severity Drivers

Let us define three orthogonal dimensions of an incident:

- **Impact ($I$):** The damage to CIA Triad. Scored 0 (negligible) to 10 (catastrophic).
- **Urgency ($U$):** The speed at which the incident is propagating. Scored 0 (static) to 10 (rapid lateral movement).
- **Containment Difficulty ($C$):** The complexity of isolating the incident. Scored 0 (trivial) to 10 (requires physical shutdown).

### Step 2 — Assign Normalization Weights

Based on NIST and industrial best practices, the typical weights are:

$$
w_I = 0.50, \quad w_U = 0.30, \quad w_C = 0.20
$$

> **Engineering Reasoning:** Impact is weighted highest because *business-critical damage to a safety instrumented system (SIS)* is the dominant concern in an industrial environment, followed by the speed of spread, and lastly the difficulty of containment (which is a function of available resources).

### Step 3 — Apply the Weighted Linear Combination

The composite severity score is computed as a linear weighted sum:

$$
S = (I \times w_I) + (U \times w_U) + (C \times w_C)
$$

### Step 4 — Map the Score to a Categorical Severity Tier

Substitute the weights to obtain the operational form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 0.50 \cdot I + 0.30 \cdot U + 0.20 \cdot C \\
S_{max} &= 0.50(10) + 0.30(10) + 0.20(10) = 10.0 \\
S_{min} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The continuous score $S \in [0, 10]$ is then mapped to discrete tiers via the **Severity Classification Matrix**:

| Composite Score $S$ | Tier | Color Code | Required Response Time (KTU Standard) |
|---------------------|------|------------|------------------------------------------|
| $9.0 \le S \le 10.0$ | **Critical (P1)** | Red | ≤ 15 minutes |
| $7.0 \le S < 9.0$ | **High (P2)** | Orange | ≤ 1 hour |
| $4.0 \le S < 7.0$ | **Medium (P3)** | Yellow | ≤ 4 hours |
| $2.0 \le S < 4.0$ | **Low (P4)** | Blue | ≤ 24 hours |
| $0.0 \le S < 2.0$ | **Informational (P5)** | Green | Best-effort |

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

**Question:** A malware outbreak in a refinery's DCS (Distributed Control System) is detected. The SOC analyst scores it as $I = 8$ (high impact, affects safety interlocks), $U = 6$ (spreading via shared engineering workstation), $C = 7$ (requires coordinated shutdown of two distillation columns). Compute the severity score and the required response time.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (8 \times 0.50) + (6 \times 0.30) + (7 \times 0.20) \\
  &= 4.00 + 1.80 + 1.40 \\
  &= 7.20
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $7.0 \le 7.20 < 9.0$, the incident is classified as **High (P2)** with a required response time of **≤ 1 hour**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** $S = 7.20$ lies strictly within the P2 band $[7.0, 9.0)$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of the Response Effectiveness Index $RE$

A common KTU Part-B question asks: *"How would you measure the overall effectiveness of an Incident Response Plan?"*

### Step 1 — Define the Two Normalized Inputs

- **SLA Compliance Ratio** $SLA_{comp} \in [0, 1]$.
- **Detection Speed Ratio** $\dfrac{MTTD_{target}}{MTTD_{actual}}$. This ratio is **> 1** if detection is faster than the target, and **< 1** if detection lags.

### Step 2 — Define the Composite Index

The Response Effectiveness Index is the product of these two normalized metrics:

$$
RE = SLA_{comp} \times \frac{MTTD_{target}}{MTTD_{actual}}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &> 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Plan is performing above expectations} \\
RE &= 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Plan meets baseline expectations} \\
RE &< 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Plan requires improvement}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose an organization records:
- Total incidents in Q1 = 20
- Incidents resolved within SLA = 17
- Actual MTTD = 4 hours
- Target MTTD = 6 hours

**Step 4a — Compute SLA Compliance:**

$$
SLA_{comp} = \frac{17}{20} = 0.85
$$

**Step 4b — Compute Detection Speed Ratio:**

$$
\frac{MTTD_{target}}{MTTD_{actual}} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.50
$$

**Step 4c — Compute the Composite RE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= 0.85 \times 1.50 \\
   &= 1.275
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The plan is performing **27.5% above baseline** — a strong indicator of maturity. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — ICS vs IT Containment Trade-offs

| Parameter | Traditional IT Environment | Industrial OT / ICS Environment | KTU Exam Implication |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------|
| Primary concern | Data confidentiality | Process safety and physical continuity | "Why can't we just power off?" |
| Downtime tolerance | Hours to days | Minutes (safety-critical loops) | "Explain RTO in OT" |
| Patch cadence | Weekly/monthly | Only during planned shutdowns (turnarounds) | "Why are OT patches rare?" |
| Containment method | Disconnect from network | Logical segmentation, mode change | "Discuss IT vs OT containment" |
| Forensic viability | Full disk imaging, memory dumps | Restricted by legacy OS, no debug ports | "List OT forensic challenges" |
| Recovery method | Restore from backup | Re-flash firmware, validate against golden image | "Describe OT recovery" |
| Reporting requirement | GDPR, IT Act 2000 | NERC-CIP, IEC 62443, NIST 800-82 | "Name OT standards" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The NIST IRP Lifecycle (Cyclical)

This diagram visualizes the six-phase NIST incident response lifecycle, with the dotted feedback arc representing the **Lessons Learned → Preparation** continuous improvement loop.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PHASE1[PHASE 1 PREPARATION POLICIES CSIRT TRAINING TOOLING] --> PHASE2[PHASE 2 IDENTIFICATION TRIAGE ALERT VALIDATION]
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3[PHASE 3 CONTAINMENT SHORT TERM AND LONG TERM ISOLATION]
    PHASE3 --> PHASE4[PHASE 4 ERADICATION MALWARE REMOVAL PATCHING CREDENTIAL ROTATION]
    PHASE4 --> PHASE5[PHASE 5 RECOVERY RESTORE VALIDATE MONITOR PROCESS RAMP UP]
    PHASE5 --> PHASE6[PHASE 6 LESSONS LEARNED POST INCIDENT REVIEW KPI UPDATE]
    PHASE6 -. FEEDBACK LOOP .-> PHASE1
    PHASE1 -. CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT .-> PHASE6
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — CSIRT Organizational Structure

This block diagram shows the hierarchical command structure of a typical industrial CSIRT and the lateral communication channels required during an incident.

```mermaid
graph TB
    COMMANDER[INCIDENT COMMANDER EXECUTIVE LEVEL] --> CSIRTMGR[CSIRT MANAGER OPERATIONAL LEAD]
    COMMANDER --> LEGAL[LEGAL AND COMPLIANCE OFFICER]
    COMMANDER --> PR[PUBLIC RELATIONS LEAD]
    CSIRTMGR --> TIER1[TIER 1 SOC ANALYST INITIAL TRIAGE]
    CSIRTMGR --> TIER2[TIER 2 INCIDENT HANDLER DEEP ANALYSIS]
    CSIRTMGR --> TIER3[TIER 3 THREAT HUNTER FORENSICS]
    CSIRTMGR --> ICSENG[ICS OT ENGINEER PLC HMI SIS SPECIALIST]
    TIER2 -. ESCALATION .-> TIER3
    TIER1 -. ESCALATION .-> TIER2
    ICSENG -. COORDINATES WITH .-> COMMANDER
    LEGAL -. ADVISES .-> COMMANDER
    PR -. ADVISES .-> COMMANDER
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Industrial Incident Severity Decision Tree

This decision tree maps a detected event through the severity scoring logic derived in Section 3.1, ultimately assigning a P-tier and a response SLA.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[EVENT DETECTED BY SIEM OR IDS] --> Q1{IMPACT TO SAFETY OR SIS HIGHER THAN 7}
    Q1 -- YES --> P1[CRITICAL P1 RESPONSE WITHIN 15 MIN]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{IS LATERAL MOVEMENT OBSERVED}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{IMPACT SCORE HIGHER THAN 6}
    Q2 -- NO --> P5[INFORMATIONAL P5 BEST EFFORT]
    Q3 -- YES --> P2[HIGH P2 RESPONSE WITHIN 1 HOUR]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{IS DATA EXFILTRATION CONFIRMED}
    Q4 -- YES --> P3[MEDIUM P3 RESPONSE WITHIN 4 HOURS]
    Q4 -- NO --> P4[LOW P4 RESPONSE WITHIN 24 HOURS]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IRP Execution Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *functional flow* of an industrial IRP from event ingestion to post-incident reporting, with the supporting technical controls mapped at each stage.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DETECT["DETECTION LAYER"]
        FIREWALL[INDUSTIAL FIREWALL]
        IDS[IDS AND IPS]
        SIEM[SIEM CORRELATION ENGINE]
    end
    subgraph TRIAGE["TRIAGE AND CLASSIFICATION LAYER"]
        SCORE[SEVERITY SCORING MODULE]
        ROUTER[INCIDENT ROUTER]
    end
    subgraph RESPOND["RESPONSE LAYER"]
        CONTAIN[CONTAINMENT PLAYBOOKS]
        ERAD[ERADICATION TOOLKIT]
        RECOVER[RECOVERY IMAGES]
    end
    subgraph COMMS["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        ALERT[ALERTING SYSTEM]
        REPORT[REGULATORY REPORTING NERC CIP IEC 62443]
    end
    FIREWALL --> IDS
    IDS --> SIEM
    SIEM --> SCORE
    SCORE --> ROUTER
    ROUTER --> CONTAIN
    ROUTER --> ERAD
    ROUTER --> RECOVER
    ROUTER --> ALERT
    ROUTER --> REPORT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*"Define Incident Response Planning. List any four primary objectives of an effective IRP in an industrial cyber security context."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Incident Response Planning (IRP) is a pre-defined, documented, and continuously improved set of procedures that an organization uses to detect, respond to, contain, and recover from cybersecurity incidents in a coordinated manner, with the goal of minimizing impact on industrial operations.

**Four Primary Objectives (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Minimize Business Impact** — Limit financial, operational, and reputational damage.
2. **Maintain Business Continuity** — Ensure critical ICS processes resume within the RTO.
3. **Preserve Forensic Evidence** — Maintain chain of custody for legal/regulatory action.
4. **Strengthen Future Defenses** — Use lessons learned to enhance detection and prevention.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**
*"Differentiate between MTTD and MTTR. Why is MTTR more critical in an OT environment than in a traditional IT environment?"*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Differentiation (1.5 Marks):**

| Parameter | MTTD (Mean Time To Detect) | MTTR (Mean Time To Respond/Recover) |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Definition | Time from incident occurrence to detection | Time from detection to full restoration |
| Phase | Identification phase | Containment + Eradication + Recovery phases |
| Goal | Reduce detection blind spots | Reduce service downtime |

**Why MTTR is more critical in OT (1.5 Marks):**
In an OT environment, prolonged downtime can lead to **physical damage to equipment, environmental hazards, and loss of human life**. Unlike IT systems where data can be restored from backups in hours, OT processes involve real-time physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, flow) that, if left uncontrolled, may trigger Safety Instrumented System (SIS) shutdowns or catastrophic failures. Therefore, the cost of every additional minute of MTTR in OT is exponentially higher.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"With a neat diagram, explain the six phases of the NIST Incident Response Lifecycle. Highlight the unique challenges faced during the Containment phase in an Industrial Control System (ICS) environment."*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The NIST SP 800-61 framework defines a six-phase cyclical lifecycle for incident response. The phases are not strictly linear — they iterate based on lessons learned and evolving threat intelligence.

**Six Phases (4 Marks — brief description of each, with diagram as in Section 4.1):**

1. **Preparation (Phase 1):** Pre-incident hardening — deploying SIEM, training CSIRT, drafting playbooks, asset inventory, network segmentation between IT and OT per the **Purdue Model**.
2. **Identification (Phase 2):** Detecting and validating that an event is a *real* security incident. Inputs: SIEM alerts, IDS logs, user reports, threat intel feeds. Output: triaged incident ticket.
3. **Containment (Phase 3):** Limiting the blast radius. Short-term: disconnect affected host. Long-term: apply temporary patches, segment VLANs.
4. **Eradication (Phase 4):** Eliminating the root cause — removing malware, deleting backdoors, rotating credentials, patching vulnerabilities, re-flashing PLC firmware.
5. **Recovery (Phase 5):** Restoring systems from clean backups, validating integrity, monitoring for reinfection, performing controlled ramp-up of the physical process.
6. **Lessons Learned (Phase 6):** Blameless post-incident review within 14 days, updating IRP and playbooks, sharing intelligence with industry ISACs.

**Unique Containment Challenges in ICS (2 Marks):**
- **Safety Risk:** Disconnecting a PLC from the network may cause an uncontrolled physical process state (e.g., runaway reaction, pressure spike), risking equipment damage or human injury.
- **Process Stability:** Many OT systems require continuous operation — a sudden disconnect violates the **24×365 availability** mandate of industrial systems.
- **Legacy Protocols:** Protocols like Modbus, DNP3, and Profinet often have no built-in authentication, making it hard to "block" a malicious command at the protocol level.
- **Vendor Lock-in:** OT devices often have vendor-specific firmware; standard IT tools cannot isolate them.
- **Preferred Strategy:** Use **logical isolation** (VLAN segmentation, jump-host mediation, unidirectional data diodes) rather than physical disconnection.

**Diagram:** As shown in Section 4.1 of these notes (must be drawn neatly in the answer script).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"A SOC analyst scores an incident as Impact = 9, Urgency = 7, and Containment Difficulty = 8. Using the KTU severity formula, compute the composite severity score, identify the priority tier, and determine the mandated response time. Justify why the Containment phase should use logical isolation rather than physical disconnection in this scenario."*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — State the Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
S = (I \times w_I) + (U \times w_U) + (C \times w_C)
$$

where $w_I = 0.50$, $w_U = 0.30$, $w_C = 0.20$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (9 \times 0.50) + (7 \times 0.30) + (8 \times 0.20) \\
  &= 4.50 + 2.10 + 1.60 \\
  &= 8.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Map to Priority Tier (2 Marks):**

Since $7.0 \le 8.20 < 9.0$, the incident is classified as **HIGH (P2)**, with a mandated response time of **≤ 1 hour**.

**Step 4 — Justify Logical Isolation over Physical Disconnection (2 Marks):**
Given the high Impact score (9) indicating potential safety implications, and the high Containment Difficulty (8), the asset is likely a critical OT device. Physical disconnection would abruptly stop the controlled physical process, which could:
- Cause a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** to trigger an emergency shutdown.
- Lead to **equipment damage** (e.g., dry running of a pump, thermal shock in a heat exchanger).
- Risk **personnel safety** in the plant.

Therefore, **logical isolation** (e.g., placing the device in a quarantined VLAN, applying a temporary firewall ACL at the industrial firewall, or using a unidirectional data diode) is preferred to contain the threat while preserving safe process operation.

---

### Question Choice B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model Paper]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"Discuss the key roles and responsibilities within a Computer Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT). Explain how the Incident Commander differs from the CSIRT Manager in an industrial incident response scenario."*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A CSIRT is a multi-disciplinary team that coordinates the technical, operational, legal, and communication aspects of cybersecurity incident response. In an industrial context, it must bridge the **IT/OT cultural and technical divide**.

**Key Roles (4 Marks — 1 Mark each for four roles):**
- **Incident Commander (IC):** Senior executive with *strategic and external-facing* authority. Owns the overall response, makes go/no-go decisions, and interfaces with regulators, board, and media.
- **CSIRT Manager:** Operational leader. Coordinates the technical teams, manages the IRP execution, tracks KPIs, and reports status to the IC.
- **Tier 1/2/3 Security Analysts:** Triage (Tier 1), deep analysis (Tier 2), advanced forensics and threat hunting (Tier 3).
- **OT/ICS Engineer:** Validates the safety and integrity of the physical process; coordinates with plant operators for safe shutdown or isolation.
- **Legal & Compliance Officer:** Ensures reporting compliance with NERC-CIP, IEC 62443, IT Act 2000, and GDPR.
- **PR/Communications Lead:** Manages external messaging, customer notifications, and media relations.
- **HR Representative:** Handles insider threats and disciplinary procedures.

**IC vs CSIRT Manager (2 Marks):**
- The **Incident Commander** operates at the *executive level* and owns *strategic decisions* — for example, deciding whether to publicly disclose the breach, whether to engage law enforcement, or whether to halt production entirely.
- The **CSIRT Manager** operates at the *operational level* and owns *technical execution* — for example, deciding which forensic tools to use, how to segment the network, and when to declare containment achieved.
- In a refinery incident, the IC might be the **Plant Director**, while the CSIRT Manager is the **CISO** or **Head of OT Security**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"An organization recorded 30 incidents in Q2. 24 were resolved within their SLAs. The actual MTTD was 3 hours, while the target MTTD was 5 hours. Compute the Response Effectiveness Index (RE) and interpret the result. Also, list any four KPIs that should be tracked to continuously improve the IRP."*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute SLA Compliance (1.5 Marks):**

$$
SLA_{comp} = \frac{N_{within\_SLA}}{N_{total}} = \frac{24}{30} = 0.80
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Detection Speed Ratio (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\frac{MTTD_{target}}{MTTD_{actual}} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667
$$

**Step 3 — Compute RE (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= SLA_{comp} \times \frac{MTTD_{target}}{MTTD_{actual}} \\
   &= 0.80 \times 1.6667 \\
   &\approx 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (0.5 Mark):**
Since $RE = 1.333 > 1$, the IRP is performing approximately **33.3% above baseline expectations** — a strong indicator of operational maturity.

**Step 5 — Four KPIs for Continuous IRP Improvement (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Number of Incidents per Month ($\lambda$):** Tracks threat volume and trend.
2. **Mean Time to Contain (MTTC):** Measures the speed of Phase 3 execution.
3. **Recurrence Rate:** Percentage of incidents involving a previously seen root cause; lower is better.
4. **Post-Incident Review Completion Rate:** Percentage of incidents for which a formal PIR was completed within 14 days.
5. *(Bonus)* **False Positive Rate of SIEM:** Indicator of detection-rule tuning quality.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE (PECCT524):**
> 1. **Conflating MTTD and MTTR.** Students often interchange these terms. *MTTD = Detection latency; MTTR = Recovery latency.* Examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 to 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Suggesting "Power Off" for OT Containment.** This is a critical red flag. Physical disconnection in OT is dangerous and demonstrates a lack of awareness of the **Purdue Model** and **IEC 62443**. Examiners award zero marks for containment strategies that ignore process safety.
> 3. **Forgetting the Cyclical Nature of the IRP.** A linear description of the six phases loses at least 1 mark. Always mention the **Lessons Learned → Preparation** feedback loop.
> 4. **Omitting Communication & Legal Roles.** The CSIRT is not just a technical team. A complete answer must reference the IC, Legal/Compliance, and PR roles.
> 5. **Arithmetic Errors in Severity Scoring.** Weight values ($0.50, 0.30, 0.20$) must be quoted *with the formula*. Examiners do not award partial credit for a correct final number with no formula.
> 6. **Not Naming the Standards.** Always cite **NIST SP 800-61**, **NIST SP 800-82** (ICS), **IEC 62443**, and **NERC-CIP** where relevant. Generic "industry standards" phrasing loses marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IRP Definition:** A documented, cyclical, role-based plan to detect, contain, eradicate, and recover from cyber incidents.
- **Six NIST Phases (in order):** Preparation → Identification → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned.
- **The Lifecycle is Cyclical:** Lessons Learned feeds back into Preparation.
- **Core Metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, RTO, RPO, SLA Compliance, Severity Score $S$, RE Index.
- **Severity Formula:** $S = 0.50I + 0.30U + 0.20C$, with tier mapping at $S \ge 9$ (P1), $S \ge 7$ (P2), $S \ge 4$ (P3), $S \ge 2$ (P4), else (P5).
- **RE Formula:** $RE = SLA_{comp} \times \dfrac{MTTD_{target}}{MTTD_{actual}}$; $RE > 1$ indicates above-baseline performance.
- **CSIRT Core Roles:** Incident Commander, CSIRT Manager, Tier 1/2/3 Analysts, OT/ICS Engineer, Legal/Compliance, PR Lead, HR.
- **ICS-Specific Rule:** **Never** physically disconnect an OT device for containment — use logical isolation (VLAN, data diode, ACL).
- **Key Standards to Quote:** NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2, NIST SP 800-82, IEC 62443, NERC-CIP, IT Act 2000, GDPR.
- **PDCA Alignment:** IRP = Plan (Preparation) + Do (Identification/Containment/Eradication/Recovery) + Check (Lessons Learned) + Act (Update Preparation).
- **KPI Categories:** Volume (incidents/month), Speed (MTTD/MTTR), Quality (SLA compliance, false positive rate), Maturity (PIR completion, tabletop exercise frequency).
- **Industrial Priority:** *Safety first, data second.* The safety instrumented system (SIS) and human life override all other concerns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forensics and Post-Incident Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT524 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forensics and Post-Incident Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital Forensics — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Forensics** is the scientific discipline involving the **identification, preservation, collection, validation, analysis, interpretation, documentation, and presentation** of digital evidence derived from digital sources, in a manner that is legally admissible and procedurally sound, for the purpose of facilitating or expediting the reconstruction of events found to be criminal, or helping to anticipate unauthorized actions shown to be disruptive to planned operations.

In the context of **Industrial Cyber Security (ICS/SCADA environments)**, digital forensics expands to include investigation of:
- **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)**
- **Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs)**
- **Engineering Workstations**
- **Historians and Field Devices**
- **Network captures from OT segments (Modbus/TCP, DNP3, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET)**

### 1.2 Post-Incident Analysis — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Post-Incident Analysis (PIA)** is a structured, systematic review conducted **after** the resolution of a security incident, focused on identifying root causes, evaluating response effectiveness, extracting actionable intelligence, and producing recommendations to prevent recurrence.

It is governed primarily by:
- **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3** — *Computer Security Incident Handling Guide*
- **ISO/IEC 27035** — *Information Security Incident Management*
- **SANS PICERL Model** — Preparation, Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Trauma Unit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Trauma Analogy**: Imagine a cyber attack as a multi-vehicle highway accident. The **Forensics Team** is the *medical trauma unit* that arrives to triage victims (endpoints), photograph the wreckage (volatile memory snapshots), tag every piece of evidence with chain-of-custody labels, run lab tests (hash verification, malware analysis), and finally testify in court (legal proceedings). The **Post-Incident Analysis Team** is the *highway safety engineer* who, weeks later, returns to the crash site to inspect the road, identify the black-ice patch (root cause), redesign the intersection (control implementation), and file a report so the **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** is increased.

In one line: *Forensics tells you **what** happened; Post-Incident Analysis tells you **why**, **how**, and **how to prevent it next time**.*

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold for Recall)

The following metrics are **golden terms** expected in any KTU 14-mark answer:

| Metric | Definition |
|---|---|
| **MTTD — Mean Time To Detect** | Average time from incident onset to detection |
| **MTTR — Mean Time To Respond/Recover** | Average time from detection to full restoration |
| **MTTC — Mean Time To Contain** | Average time from detection to successful containment |
| **RTO — Recovery Time Objective** | Maximum tolerable downtime for a system |
| **RPO — Recovery Point Objective** | Maximum tolerable data loss measured in time |
| **CVSS — Common Vulnerability Scoring System** | Standardized 0.0 to 10.0 severity score |
| **IOC — Indicator Of Compromise** | Forensic artifact signalling a breach |
| **IOA — Indicator Of Attack** | Behavioural evidence of attacker TTP |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test **CVSS, MTTD, MTTR, RTO, and RPO** in definitional 3-mark questions. Memorize the unit of CVSS (a dimensionless number, 0 to 10).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle

The **NIST framework** is the gold standard adopted by KTU 2024 scheme and forms the backbone of any 14-mark question. It consists of **4 main phases**, with Lessons Learned woven through the cycle.

**Phase 1 — Preparation**
- Establish policies, playbooks, communication plans
- Deploy EDR/XDR/SIEM tools
- Train the **CSIRT** (Computer Security Incident Response Team)
- Pre-position forensic toolkits (EnCase, FTK, Volatility, Autopsy, Velociraptor, GRR)

**Phase 2 — Detection & Analysis**
- Monitor SIEM alerts, IDS/IPS, anomaly detection
- **Triage** incoming events to confirm incidents
- Perform initial scope assessment
- Collect volatile data FIRST (Order of Volatility)

**Phase 3 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery**
- Short-term containment: isolate host via firewall/VLAN
- Long-term containment: apply patches to parallel systems
- Eradication: remove malware, attacker accounts, persistence
- Recovery: restore from clean backups, validate integrity, monitor

**Phase 4 — Post-Incident Activity**
- Lessons learned meeting within **2 to 4 weeks**
- Root cause analysis (RCA)
- Update runbooks and detection rules
- Report to stakeholders, regulators (CERT-In for India)

### 2.2 The SANS PICERL Model (More Granular)

P — Preparation
I — Identification
C — Containment
E — Eradication
R — Recovery
L — Lessons Learned

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite**: If a question says "Explain the incident response lifecycle", always draw the **circular feedback loop** showing Lessons Learned feeding back into Preparation. The closed loop is the mark-differentiator.

### 2.3 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227 — Guidelines for Evidence Collection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volatility decreases as you go down this list. Capture top-down.**

| Order | Data Type | Typical Lifespan | Forensic Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU registers, cache** | Nanoseconds | Hardware debugger |
| 2 | **Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, memory** | Seconds to minutes | `vol`, `memdump`, `LiME` |
| 3 | **Temporary file systems, swap, /tmp, process memory dumps** | Minutes to hours | `dd`, `dcfldd`, `FTK Imager` |
| 4 | **Disk storage, file system metadata, logs** | Days to years | `EnCase`, `Autopsy`, `The Sleuth Kit` |
| 5 | **Remote logs, backups, off-site data** | Indefinite | SIEM exports, tape archives |

### 2.4 Chain of Custody — The Legal Backbone

> [!WARNING]
> Any break in chain of custody renders digital evidence **inadmissible**. KTU expects 2 to 3 marks reserved for explaining this in any forensic question.

**Required documentation per RFC 3227 and ACPO (UK Association of Chief Police Officers) principles:**
- **Time of acquisition** (UTC timestamp, synchronized via NTP)
- **Acquiring officer identity**
- **Location of evidence**
- **Hash values** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) before and after transfer
- **Storage medium serial number**
- **Access log** (who touched it, when, why)

### 2.5 The Five Principles of Digital Forensics (ACPO)

1. **Principle 1** — No action taken shall change data held on a digital device or storage media that may subsequently be relied upon in court.
2. **Principle 2** — In exceptional circumstances where it is necessary to access original data, the person must be competent to explain the relevance and implications of their actions.
3. **Principle 3** — An audit trail or other record of all processes applied to digital evidence must be created and preserved. An independent third party should be able to examine those processes and achieve the same result.
4. **Principle 4** — The officer in charge of the investigation is responsible for ensuring that the law and these principles are adhered to.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations, though fewer than signal-processing formulas, are **mandatory** for KTU derivation-style 14-mark problems.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Hash Integrity Check** | $H_{computed} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{stored}$ | Verifies evidence has not been modified |
| **MTTD Calculation** | $\mathrm{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{detect,i} - T_{occur,i})$ | Average time gap between occurrence and detection |
| **MTTR Calculation** | $\mathrm{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{recover,i} - T_{detect,i})$ | Average time from detection to full recovery |
| **Incident Severity Score** | $S = w_{1} C + w_{2} I + w_{3} A$ | Weighted sum of Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability impact |
| **CVSS Base Score (simplified)** | $\mathrm{CVSS} = \mathrm{round}\left( \mathrm{min}\left( \mathrm{max}\left( \dfrac{\mathrm{Impact} + \mathrm{Exploitability} - 0.2}{1.2},\, 0 \right),\, 10 \right) \right)$ | Normalized to 0 to 10 |
| **Data Loss (bytes)** | $L = R \times \Delta t$ | Where $R$ is write rate, $\Delta t$ is downtime |
| **Recovery Point Loss** | $L_{RPO} = R \times (T_{incident} - T_{last\_backup})$ | Maximum acceptable data loss window |
| **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday)** | $P_{coll} \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{b})}$ | $n$ samples, $b$-bit hash; key for admissibility discussion |

In the CVSS simplified equation, the term $w_{1}, w_{2}, w_{3}$ are the CIA-triad weights with the constraint $w_{1} + w_{2} + w_{3} = 1.0$, and the absolute value symbols are written using $\vert$ in latex as $\vert w_{1} \vert$.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Forensic Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking & Finance** | Tracing fraudulent SWIFT transactions, ATM skimmer analysis |
| **Healthcare** | HIPAA breach investigation, ransomware analysis on PACS |
| **ICS/SCADA** | Triton/Trisis malware reverse engineering, PLC ladder-logic forensics |
| **Cloud-native** | AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, Kubernetes audit logs |
| **Mobile** | Signal analysis from baseband, app sandbox artefact extraction |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Verifying Evidence Integrity Using Cryptographic Hashing

The goal is to prove mathematically that the **acquired forensic image** is bit-for-bit identical to the **original storage device**. This is mandatory for chain-of-custody admissibility.

**Step 1 — Acquire the original hash.**

$$H_{orig} = \mathrm{Hash}(M_{original})$$

where $M_{original}$ is the raw byte stream of the original storage medium and $\mathrm{Hash}(\cdot)$ is one of {MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256}.

**Step 2 — Acquire a forensically sound image.**

The imaging operation is:

$$M_{image} = \mathrm{Imager}(M_{original})$$

A forensically sound imager (e.g., `dcfldd`, `FTK Imager`, `EnCase`) performs a **bit-stream copy** and writes the result to a write-blocked destination.

**Step 3 — Compute the hash of the acquired image.**

$$H_{image} = \mathrm{Hash}(M_{image})$$

**Step 4 — Compare the two hashes.**

$$\mathrm{Integrity\ Check} = \begin{cases} \mathrm{VERIFIED} & \text{if } H_{orig} = H_{image} \\ \mathrm{FAILED} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Probability of accidental collision (Birthday Bound).**

For a hash function with $b$-bit output, the probability of finding two distinct inputs that produce the same hash after sampling $n$ random inputs is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{collision} &= 1 - \prod_{k=0}^{n-1} \left( 1 - \dfrac{k}{2^{b}} \right) \\
&\approx 1 - e^{-\,n(n-1) / (2 \cdot 2^{b})} \\
&\approx 1 - e^{-n^{2} / (2^{b+1})}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation**: For SHA-256 ($b = 256$), even with $n = 2^{128}$ samples, $P_{collision}$ remains negligibly small (approximately $10^{-39}$), which is why **SHA-256 is legally accepted** in jurisdictions including the Indian IT Act 2000 (amended 2008).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTD, MTTR, and Availability Loss

Given $N$ incidents with occurrence timestamps $T_{occur,i}$, detection timestamps $T_{detect,i}$, and recovery timestamps $T_{recover,i}$:

$$\mathrm{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( T_{detect,i} - T_{occur,i} \right)$$

$$\mathrm{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( T_{recover,i} - T_{detect,i} \right)$$

$$\mathrm{MTTC} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( T_{contain,i} - T_{detect,i} \right)$$

The **Total Downtime** for incident $i$ is:

$$D_i = T_{recover,i} - T_{occur,i} = (T_{detect,i} - T_{occur,i}) + (T_{contain,i} - T_{detect,i}) + (T_{recover,i} - T_{contain,i})$$

In terms of metrics:

$$D_i = \mathrm{MTTD}_i + \mathrm{MTTC}_i + \mathrm{MTTR}_i$$

**Worked Numerical Example** (Kerala-style problem):

Suppose 3 incidents occur with the following timeline (all in hours from $T=0$):

- Incident 1: occurred at 0 h, detected at 4 h, contained at 6 h, recovered at 9 h
- Incident 2: occurred at 10 h, detected at 11 h, contained at 12 h, recovered at 13 h
- Incident 3: occurred at 20 h, detected at 28 h, contained at 30 h, recovered at 36 h

Compute the three metrics.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{MTTD} &= \frac{(4-0) + (11-10) + (28-20)}{3} \\
&= \frac{4 + 1 + 8}{3} \\
&= \frac{13}{3} \approx 4.33 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{MTTR} &= \frac{(9-4) + (13-11) + (36-28)}{3} \\
&= \frac{5 + 2 + 8}{3} \\
&= \frac{15}{3} = 5.00 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{MTTC} &= \frac{(6-4) + (12-11) + (30-28)}{3} \\
&= \frac{2 + 1 + 2}{3} \\
&= \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.67 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

Total availability loss across 3 incidents:

$$\sum D_i = 9 + 3 + 16 = 28 \text{ hours}$$

**Interpretation**: A high MTTD (4.33 h) suggests weak detection capability (SIEM rules, threat hunting maturity), while a moderate MTTR (5.00 h) reflects good IR runbooks.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantitative Risk-Scoring in Post-Incident Analysis

A common KTU 14-mark sub-question asks to score an incident's severity. Using a weighted CIA-triad approach:

$$S_{incident} = w_{C} \cdot C + w_{I} \cdot I + w_{A} \cdot A$$

where:
- $C$ = Confidentiality impact score (0 to 10)
- $I$ = Integrity impact score (0 to 10)
- $A$ = Availability impact score (0 to 10)
- $w_{C}, w_{I}, w_{A}$ are organizational weights summing to 1.0

**Example**: A ransomware attack on a hospital's HIS system has $C = 8$ (PHI exfiltration), $I = 9$ (database encrypted), $A = 10$ (full outage). With equal weights $w_{C} = w_{I} = w_{A} = 1/3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot 8 + \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot 9 + \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot 10 \\
&= \tfrac{27}{3} \\
&= 9.0
\end{aligned}$$

A score above 7.0 typically triggers a **C-Suite/Board-level escalation** and mandatory **CERT-In notification** within 6 hours under Indian cybersecurity directives.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Forensic Technique Selection

| Evidence Type | Best Tool | Volatility | Legal Admissibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| RAM (Memory) | Volatility, Rekall, MemProcFS | Very High (seconds) | High (with proper handling) |
| Disk Image | FTK Imager, dd, EnCase | Low (persistent) | Very High |
| Network PCAP | Wireshark, tcpdump, NetworkMiner | High (minutes) | High |
| Log Files | Splunk, ELK Stack, Graylog | Low to Medium | Very High |
| Mobile Device | Cellebrite, Magnet AXIOM, ADB | High | High (with warrant) |
| Cloud Logs | CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, Chronicle | Persistent (cloud-side) | High |
| OT/ICS | Wireshark with dissectors, SANS ICS tools | Persistent | High (specialized expertise needed) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — NIST Incident Response Lifecycle (Closed Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prep[PREPARATION] --> ident[DETECTION AND ANALYSIS]
    ident --> cont[CONTAINMENT ERADICATION RECOVERY]
    cont --> post[POST INCIDENT ACTIVITY]
    post --> prep
    ident --> evid[Evidence Collection and Forensics]
    evid --> chain[Chain of Custody Documented]
    chain --> court[Legal and Regulatory Reporting]
    court --> post
```

**Interpretation of nodes**: PREPARATION is the policy/tooling baseline. DETECTION AND ANALYSIS is where SIEM alerts and IOCs are validated. CONTAINMENT ERADICATION RECOVERY is the active defence phase. POST INCIDENT ACTIVITY is the lessons-learned report. The loop closes because every lesson improves the next PREPARATION cycle.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Order of Volatility (Top-Down Capture Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Registers and Cache] --> B[Routing Table ARP Cache Kernel Memory]
    B --> C[Temporary File Systems Swap Process Memory]
    C --> D[Disk File System Logs]
    D --> E[Remote Logs Backups Offsite Data]
    A --> A1[Capture First Nanosecond Window]
    B --> B1[Capture Within Minutes]
    C --> C1[Capture Within Hours]
    D --> D1[Capture With Forensic Imager]
    E --> E1[Pull From SIEM or Tape]
```

**Reading order**: Top nodes vanish fastest. Always start at A1 and work downward to E1 within a single triage window. This satisfies **RFC 3227** and the **ACPO Principle 1**.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Forensic Process Flow (Acquire to Court)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    scene[Identify Evidence Source] --> id[Isolate and Document]
    id --> acq[Acquire Volatile Data First]
    acq --> img[Bit Stream Image of Disk]
    img --> hash[Generate SHA 256 Hash]
    hash --> store[Store on Write Blocked Media]
    store --> analysis[Analyze in Sandbox]
    analysis --> report[Generate Forensic Report]
    report --> chain[Chain of Custody Verified]
    chain --> court[Court Admissible Submission]
    hash --> verify{Hash Match Yes No}
    verify -->|Yes| store
    verify -->|No| redo[Re Image and Document]
    redo --> acq
```

**Functional flow**: This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture**. Each block represents a procedure with a verifiable input/output. The decision diamond verifies hash integrity. If the hash check fails, the loop re-images the source, preventing tampering or transmission errors from contaminating the evidence.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Post-Incident Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rca[Root Cause Analysis] --> fivewhy[5 Whys Technique]
    fivewhy --> fish[Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram]
    fish --> gap[Identify Process and Control Gaps]
    gap --> action[Define Corrective Actions]
    action --> owners[Assign Owners and Deadlines]
    owners --> metrics[Track MTTD MTTR Reduction]
    metrics --> kpi[Update KPI Dashboard]
    kpi --> audit[Schedule Compliance Audit]
    audit --> rca
```

**Reading**: This closed-loop diagram maps the **continuous-improvement** aspect of post-incident work. The 5 Whys and Fishbone tools feed into a corrective-action plan (CAP), and KPIs (MTTD, MTTR) are tracked to prove the corrective actions actually reduced incident severity over time.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

> Define **Digital Forensics**. List the four ACPO principles that govern the handling of digital evidence.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Digital Forensics** is the application of scientific methods to identify, preserve, collect, validate, analyze, interpret, document, and present digital evidence in a manner that is legally admissible.

**Four ACPO Principles:**
1. **No action shall change data** on the original device or medium.
2. **Investigator must be competent** to explain any deviation from Principle 1.
3. **A full audit trail** of all actions must be created and preserved (third-party reproducible).
4. **The case officer** is ultimately responsible for legal and procedural compliance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

> Differentiate between **Indicator of Compromise (IOC)** and **Indicator of Attack (IOA)**. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | IOC (Indicator of Compromise) | IOA (Indicator of Attack) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Forensic artifact showing that a breach **has already occurred** | Behavioural evidence showing that an attacker **is in the act** of attacking |
| **Nature** | Reactive, post-breach | Proactive, pre-breach |
| **Examples** | (i) MD5 hash of known malware binary; (ii) Outbound traffic to a known C2 IP | (i) Unusual PowerShell process spawning from `winword.exe`; (ii) Lateral SMB enumeration across multiple hosts |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a) Explain the NIST SP 800-61 incident response lifecycle in detail with a neat block diagram. Describe each phase and the role of the CSIRT team. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **NIST SP 800-61 framework** structures incident response into **four interlocking phases**, with lessons learned acting as a continuous-improvement feedback loop.

**Phase 1 — Preparation (1.5 marks)**
- Risk assessment, asset inventory, threat modelling
- Deployment of SIEM, EDR, NDR, and forensic toolkits (EnCase, FTK, Velociraptor)
- Establishment of the **CSIRT** with defined roles: Incident Commander, Forensic Analyst, Malware Reverse Engineer, Communications Lead, Legal Counsel
- Pre-approved playbooks and external contacts (CERT-In, law enforcement, vendors)

**Phase 2 — Detection and Analysis (1.5 marks)**
- Triage alerts from SIEM, IDS/IPS, user reports
- **Validate IOCs** against threat intelligence feeds (AlienVault OTX, MISP)
- **Initial scope assessment**: Which systems, accounts, data are affected?
- Capture volatile data first (RAM, network state) following **Order of Volatility**

**Phase 3 — Containment, Eradication, Recovery (2 marks)**
- **Short-term containment**: network isolation, account suspension
- **Long-term containment**: parallel clean system, patching
- **Eradication**: remove malware, attacker accounts, persistence mechanisms (registry run keys, cron jobs, scheduled tasks, web shells)
- **Recovery**: restore from known-good backups, validate via hash check, monitor for re-entry

**Phase 4 — Post-Incident Activity (2 marks)**
- Lessons-learned meeting within 2 to 4 weeks
- Root Cause Analysis (5 Whys, Fishbone)
- Quantify MTTD, MTTR, MTTC and compare against SLA
- Update runbooks, detection rules, training

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the closed-loop Mermaid diagram.

---

**(b) What is the Order of Volatility as defined in RFC 3227? List the data sources in decreasing order of volatility and explain why a forensic investigator must follow this sequence. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **Order of Volatility**, codified in **RFC 3227 (Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving)**, prescribes the sequence in which a forensic investigator should capture digital evidence, prioritizing data with the **shortest lifespan** first to prevent its loss during triage.

**Ordered List (3 marks):**

| Rank | Data Source | Approximate Lifespan |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU registers, cache, routing table, ARP cache, kernel statistics, **live memory (RAM)** | Nanoseconds to minutes |
| 2 | Running process information, temporary file systems, swap, `/tmp` | Minutes to hours |
| 3 | Disk file system, file metadata, application logs, event logs | Days to years |
| 4 | Remote logs, off-site backups, cloud telemetry | Indefinite (if backed up) |

**Why Follow This Order (3 marks):**
1. **Preservation of ephemeral evidence** — RAM contains encryption keys, process memory, active network connections that vanish on power-off.
2. **Reconstructs attacker activity in time** — a memory dump may reveal a live reverse shell that a disk image would never show.
3. **Legal admissibility** — failing to capture volatile evidence is considered spoliation and may weaken the prosecution's case.
4. **Operational continuity** — captures before isolation allow later forensic reconstruction of the live state.
5. **Detection of rootkits and fileless malware** — these operate entirely in memory and leave no on-disk artifact.
6. **Volatile evidence informs disk-triage scope** — process listing in RAM reveals which files and registry keys to image next.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Define Chain of Custody. List the metadata that must be captured for every digital evidence item. Why is a broken chain of custody fatal to a legal proceeding? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** **Chain of Custody** is the documented, unbroken, chronological and tamper-evident record of the seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of digital evidence, from the moment of acquisition to its final presentation in court.

**Required Metadata per Item (3 marks):**
- **Unique case number** and evidence ID (e.g., `EVD-2024-0001`)
- **Date and time of acquisition** (UTC, NTP-synchronized)
- **Acquiring officer's full name, badge/ID, signature**
- **Location and description** of the source device (hostname, IP, MAC, serial number)
- **Make, model, and capacity** of storage medium
- **Cryptographic hashes** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) of the source and image, before and after transfer
- **Imaging tool used and version** (e.g., FTK Imager 4.7.1)
- **Storage location** of the image (physical safe, write-blocked drive)
- **Access log** of every person who handled the evidence, with timestamps and purpose

**Why a Break is Fatal (3 marks):**
1. **Evidentiary inadmissibility** — under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023), electronic evidence must be accompanied by a Section 65B certificate. A broken chain can invalidate the certificate.
2. **Reasonable doubt** — opposing counsel can argue tampering or contamination, creating a presumption of unreliability.
3. **Loss of expert testimony weight** — the forensic expert's conclusions are discounted by the court.
4. **Reputational damage** to the organization and to the CSIRT, potentially voiding cyber-insurance claims.
5. **Operational impact** — the breach is no longer provable, hampering remediation decisions and compliance audits (ISO 27001, PCI-DSS).
6. **Regulatory non-compliance** — failure to maintain evidence integrity violates CERT-In directions and may attract penalties.

---

**(b) Explain Post-Incident Analysis in detail. Discuss the 5 Whys technique, the Ishikawa Fishbone diagram, and the role of the lessons-learned report. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** **Post-Incident Analysis (PIA)** is a structured, blameless review performed after incident closure, focused on identifying root causes, evaluating response effectiveness, and producing actionable improvements.

**5 Whys Technique (2 marks):**
- A Toyota-derived iterative interrogative method.
- The team asks "Why?" up to **five times** until the **systemic root cause** is reached, not just the symptom.
- **Example (Ransomware)**: Why was the server encrypted? Because an admin clicked a phishing link. Why did the link work? Because the email gateway did not sandbox macros. Why? The rule was disabled in a 2023 config change. Why? There was no change-control review. Why? The change-management policy was never enforced by the CISO.

**Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram (2 marks):**
- Also called the **cause-and-effect diagram** or **herringbone diagram**.
- Categories: **Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Environment (6M)**.
